However, I should be clear that progression beyond the current stage will be subject to approval of the Outline Business Case and the funding available within Translink’s wider capital programme.I will continue to keep this project under review as part of my Department’s ongoing engagement with Translink.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail her Department's position in raising the age of criminal responsibility.
answer_text: I have been clear about my position and commitment to raise the minimum age of criminal responsibility (MACR) in Northern Ireland, to bring us closer to meeting international standards, including those set by the UN Committee on the Rights of the Child.  In Northern Ireland, the MACR is currently set at 10 years old which is already the lowest in Europe, along with England and Wales.  A public consultation seeking views on increasing the MACR to 14 years, which was held in 2022, showed overwhelming support.  83% of respondents agreed that the age should be increased beyond 10 years, with the majority of respondents supporting an increase to 14 years.  This would be my preference, and I have previously written to my Executive colleagues to seek their support for such an increase.  Whilst cross-party support has not been forthcoming, I am hopeful that amendments tabled to the Justice Bill, in relation to increasing the MACR, will be supported at Consideration Stage of the Bill.  Children in contact with the criminal justice system are amongst the most vulnerable in our society.  Many have a range of complex needs and have been exposed to adverse childhood experiences during their short lives.  Raising the MACR would prevent a number of children from being drawn into the formal criminal justice system at a time when they should be offered help and support in order to address any concerning behaviours they may be displaying.  Intervening early in the lives of children, families and communities before problems become entrenched is the most effective route to preventing offending and delivering positive outcomes in the longer-term.  For this reason, my Department supports a welfare-based approach to addressing harmful and concerning behaviour in children – one which seeks to tackle the root causes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department will provide the funding to reopen Bangor custody suite.
To ask the Minister of Health whether any assessment has been made of the impact of the decision to relocate the Minor Injury Unit to the Ulster Hospital from Bangor and Newtownards.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail all services that have been removed from Bangor Community Hospital, in the last ten years.
To ask the Minister of Health whether any issues have been raised about patients not presenting with minor injuries following the closure of the Minor Injuries Unit in Bangor and Newtownards.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the proposed new health and social care centre in Bangor.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail for how long an early years and childcare setting should retain documents for a child, after they have left the setting.
answer_text: While there is discretion within my Department’s Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care for Children under 12 for registered childcare providers to set their own document retention policy, the Implementation Guidance which accompanies the Standards states that those policies should be informed by consultation with the provider’s legal advisers and insurers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he is aware of any issue of staff morale and retention following changes to staff parking permits at the Ulster Hospital.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any guidance issued to early years and childcare settings on the retention of documentation in relation to the care of children.
answer_text: While my Department’s Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care for Children under 12 and associated Implementation Guidance set out the information that should be held by registered childcare providers, guidance on the retention of those records should be sought from the provider’s own legal advisers and insurers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has any plans to strengthen Anti-Social Behaviour Orders to tackle anti-social behaviour.
answer_text: Anti-Social Behaviour (ASB) can have a significant impact on communities.Responses to a joint DoJ and Department for Communities (DfC) public consultation on ASB legislation were published in April 2025 and suggested a need for legislative reform. As was detailed in the consultation report further work has been, and will be, required with respect to any potential amendment to Anti-Social Behaviour Orders (ASBOs). Initially this was taken forward by an ASB Working Group comprised of statutory partners together with representatives from the community and voluntary sector. My officials are now considering all the issues raised throughout this exercise and the recommendations of the working group to inform the final direction of the provisions. At a local level, Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs), which are statutory bodies funded jointly by my Department and the Northern Ireland Policing Board, continue to play a key role in addressing ASB and building confidence locally, through engagement and consultation with communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any services provided at Bangor Community Hospital.
To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the timeline for granting the police additional powers to tackle anti-social behaviour.
answer_text: It remains my intention to bring forward legislative provisions in relation to anti-social behaviour in the next Assembly mandate. This will be subject to Executive and Assembly approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the work of the subject working groups looking at the curriculum.
answer_text: The Curriculum Taskforce is currently finalising the subject frameworks for each subject area.   I expect to receive the draft curriculum framework in the coming weeks and hope to launch a full public consultation before the summer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the public consultation on the full curriculum framework.
answer_text: The Curriculum Taskforce is currently finalising the subject frameworks for each subject area.   I expect to receive the draft curriculum framework in the coming weeks and hope to launch a full public consultation before the summer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail her Department's position in lowering the age of criminal responsibility.
answer_text: I have been clear about my position and commitment to raise the minimum age of criminal responsibility (MACR) in Northern Ireland, to bring us closer to meeting international standards, including those set by the UN Committee on the Rights of the Child.  In Northern Ireland, the MACR is currently set at 10 years old which is already the lowest in Europe, along with England and Wales.  Therefore, I would be opposed to any proposal to lower the MACR further. A public consultation on the issue held in 2022 showed overwhelming support for an increase in the MACR, with over 83% of respondents agreeing that the age should be increased beyond 10 years.  The majority of respondents supported an increase to 14 years.  Children in contact with the criminal justice system are amongst the most vulnerable in our society.  Many have a range of complex needs and have been exposed to adverse childhood experiences during their short lives.  Lowering the MACR would draw even more of these children into the justice system unnecessarily, when instead they should be helped and supported.  Intervening early in the lives of children, families and communities before problems become entrenched is the most effective route to preventing offending and delivering positive outcomes in the longer-term.  For this reason, a welfare-based approach to addressing harmful and concerning behaviour, which seeks to tackle the root causes, provides the most appropriate way forward, rather than criminalising children at an even younger age.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has any plans for reform to allow the public sector to dispose of surplus land more quickly.
answer_text: Following a comprehensive review undertaken by my officials, a refreshed Disposal Policy was published in June 2025. The Disposal of Surplus Public Sector Property Policy (v8, June 2025) is mandatory for all public bodies to which public procurement rules apply, and its comprehensive and dependable framework has also led to many non-mandated bodies adopting it voluntarily. The Disposal Policy provides a clear, transparent and fair process that ensures compliance with established practice and the extant legislation, policy and guidance, including Managing Public Money NI (MPMNI) and the Stormont Act 1933.The Policy sets out defined routes to disposal for asset owning bodies to follow at each stage of the disposal process thus maintaining consistency, fairness and transparency, while safeguarding the public purse, ensuring value for money and maintaining high standards of propriety in disposals.Responsibility for decisions, from declaring an asset surplus through to final disposal, rests with the asset owner operating within this secure and proven framework. Any departure from it increases the risk of inconsistency and potential legal challenge.The Department of Finance (DoF) maintains oversight of transfers of government assets from a financial governance and policy perspective, while Land & Property Services (LPS), through the Strategic Asset Management Unit (SAMU), leads on implementation of the disposal policy. This ensures that the process remains fair, transparent and aligned with the objectives of asset owning bodies, while safeguarding public resources and supporting the proper stewardship of all public sector assets.I have no plans to review this process at this time.A copy of the Disposal Policy is available at: https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/articles/disposal-surplus-public-sector-assets

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail when the Energy Efficiency Capital Grant scheme will reopen.
To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on departmental action to bring empty homes back into use.
answer_text: My Department is currently in the initial planning phase of a targeted pilot exercise designed to gather detailed information on vacancy-related issues across a manageable and representative selection of areas.	The purpose of this pilot is to develop a robust evidence base that can support and inform potential future policy decisions. It will also help to identify practical and effective options for bringing vacant properties back into productive use.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline why the Coastal Forum, which he co-chairs, has only met once since he took up office.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any plans his Department has for a circular economy.
answer_text: While the Circular Economy Strategy is the responsibility of my colleague Dr Caoimhe Archibald, the Minister for the Department for the Economy, circular economy principles underpin my Department’s proposals for waste management in Northern Ireland. 	The recycling proposals within the Departmental Response on ‘Rethinking Our Resources: Measures for Climate Action and a Circular Economy in NI’  aim to increase the quality and quantity of recycling to increase local reprocessing, supporting the creation of local jobs and retaining these materials as an economic resource in Northern Ireland. I am seeking Executive approval to publish this Departmental Response.		The introduction of Extended Producer Responsibility for Packaging (pEPR) in April 2025 and our Deposit Return Scheme for drinks containers to be introduced in October 2027 will also help to implement a local circular economy.		Also, the Resources and Waste Management Strategy, which is currently out for consultation, aims to transition Northern Ireland towards a circular economy and sets out proposals on waste prevention, reuse, recycling, and sustainable resource management.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any plans are in place to ensure that the same recycling options are available to residents across the all local councils.
answer_text: My 2024 consultation on waste collections and recycling reforms contained a number of options to help councils increase the quality and quantity of recycling. The responses to the consultation helped inform the Departmental Response which requires Executive approval for publication.I am seeking the approval of my Executive colleagues to publish the Departmental Response and provide direction and clarity on recycling to councils.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the annual revenue that is expected to be raised by her voluntary contributions scheme for wastewater infrastructure.
answer_text: The voluntary developer contributions scheme for wastewater infrastructure does not provide a fixed or guaranteed level of annual revenue, as participation is entirely voluntary. Any funding generated will depend on developer participation and the level of contribution considered viable on individual sites, and it is therefore not possible at this stage to provide a definitive estimate of annual revenue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any enforcement options available to her Department when a utility company or contractor fails to get the appropriate permission for roads works.
answer_text: The NI Street Works Register and Notification System (NISRANS) provides an essential tool for communication of works notices between the utility companies and the Department. It is a prosecutable offence if a statutory undertaker fails to give the Department notice on NISRANS of its intention to execute street works. A notice is not required for minor works without excavation, in non traffic sensitive situations, as defined in the code of practice.   Alongside these enforcement provisions, the Department takes a collaborative approach to promoting compliance, including guidance and awareness-raising activities with statutory undertakers. However, should a statutory undertaker repeatedly commit the same offence despite regular warnings, prosecution proceedings may be considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 41230/22-27, to detail the reason why the replacement of this sign is not an urgent priority, given the importance of road safety at schools.
answer_text: The Department is currently prioritising a programme of essential road safety and maintenance works in the area, which has meant that contractor resources have not been immediately available to replace the damaged 20mph sign. I am advised that the damage is confined to the lower section of the sign, while the illuminated element remains operational. In addition, advance warning signage in the form of school warning signs and road markings are in place, ensuring that road users continue to be alerted to the presence of the school and the applicable parttime 20mph speed limit.The replacement of this sign has now been included in the programme of works for the Ards and North Down area, and installation is scheduled to take place in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the next roll-out of the part-time 20mph speed limit zones at schools will take place.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. We are acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, particularly around schools.The introduction of part-time 20mph zones at schools across the North is a significant intervention that has helped to ensure that children, parents and staff feel safer as they travel to and from school on a daily basis and in recognition of this intervention I announced a third tranche of 40 schools bringing the total delivered since 2020/21 to 273 schools.This latest tranche focused on rural schools that are located on roads where the national speed limit applies, however, I am aware that there are schools in urban and residential areas that would benefit from a reduction in the speed limit, and I am currently undertaking a consultation on Speed Limits which includes the introduction of permanent 20mph zones.Further consideration on the use of permanent 20mph zones will be taken on completion of the consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any action he is taking to address the number of accountancy related job vacancies across the public sector.
answer_text: The Civil Service is fully committed to the training and development of its staff with a strong focus being on career paths and retention.  The increase in salaries over recent years has helped the Civil Service to become a more attractive employer, offering salaries that are competitive with other public and private sector employers.The Civil Service is creating opportunities for individuals to enter this profession as an Accounting Technician (EO1) with the ability, through excellent training and career opportunities, to progress up through the grades. The Accounting Technician competition is due to be launched in 2026.The Trainee Chartered Accountant scheme was launched in 2024 and is in its third year.  The 2026 competition launched on 23 March and will close to applications on 10 April.  Successful candidates are supported in attaining the chartered accountancy qualification through Chartered Accountants Ireland (CAI). Upon successful completion of the training programme, Trainee Chartered Accountants will be eligible for consideration for promotion to Staff Officer Accountant.A Staff Officer Accountant competition was launched at the start of March and received 88 applications before it closed on the 15 March 2026.  Interviews are due to take place on 30 and 31 March. Staff Officer Accountants can progress to higher grades through the career opportunity and promotion arrangements.Deputy Principal and Grade 7 competitions are scheduled to be launched later in 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether any Barnett consequentials arising from new domestic abuse funding in England will be ring fenced specifically for specialist domestic abuse services in Northern Ireland, including refuge provision, community based support, services for children, early intervention and prevention programmes, and multi agency coordination structures.
answer_text: I refer the Member to AQW 42246/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether the additional domestic abuse funding recently announced for England will generate a Barnett consequential for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I refer the Member to AQW 42247/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will engage with the Minister of Finance to ensure that any Barnett consequentials, relating to domestic abuse funding, will be directed in full to frontline domestic abuse services in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: On 15 December 2025, the UK Government announced an investment of over £1 billion for victims of domestic abuse and violence against women and girls.  The investment will be funded from the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government’s Spending Review outcome and as such does not give rise to additional Barnett consequentials for the Executive.However, even if this decision had resulted in Barnett consequentials, the funding would be unhypothecated, and at the discretion of the Executive as to how to distribute across Northern Ireland departments.Responsibility for funding frontline support services and facilities for victims of domestic abuse and of violence against women and girls falls to several departments.I will continue to engage with the Finance Minister and Executive colleagues to advocate for additional funding for victims of domestic abuse and violence against women and girls, as well as the whole of the justice system, to ensure we have a Justice sector that is funded appropriately now and in future years, which is currently not the case.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any steps that can be taken to ensure that any Barnett consequential relating to domestic abuse funding will be directed to frontline domestic abuse services and not absorbed into general departmental budgets.
answer_text: I refer the Member to AQW 42245/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any support his Department will provide to local councils to enable them to meet their upcoming requirement to collect and to recycle soft plastics.
answer_text: The Department jointly developed and oversees the UK wide Extended Producer Responsibility for packaging (pEPR) programme. This programme transfers the financial burden of managing household packaging waste from taxpayers and councils to the businesses that produce and supply packaging. Councils in Northern Ireland receive annual funding under pEPR to cover the cost of managing household packaging waste, with approximately £52million in additional funding provided in 2025/26. This funding is intended for councils to invest in the provision of efficient and effective household packaging waste collection services, including soft plastics.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting time for financial assessments for care home residents in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust have advised that it does not currently hold statistics on the average waiting time for financial assessments for care home residents. Upon placement, the Trust issues the required documentation to families within 10 working days. Once the completed paperwork has been returned to the Trust Financial Assessments Team who will aim to complete the assessment within 5 working days, provided all required information has been supplied and is in order.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action being taken to ensure that financial assessments for care home residents are carried out as soon as possible.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the new pedestrian crossing on the East Circular Road, Bangor, will be operational.
answer_text: I am pleased to advise that works on the new controlled crossing on East Circular Road, Bangor commenced on Saturday 7 February 2026. It is anticipated to become operational by Friday 27 March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any achievements of the first review of the implementation of the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011.
answer_text: The report on the Review of the Implementation of the Planning Act (NI) 2011 made numerous recommendations/actions covering key elements of the planning system.  Good progress has already been made, including amendments to subordinate legislation enabling councils to bring forward planning application validation checklists; and revised procedures for pre-application community consultation and removal of the mandatory requirement to hold a pre-determination hearing under the Planning (Development Management) Regulations (NI) 2015. The review of the classes and thresholds for development under the Planning (Development Management) Regulations (NI) 2015 is also at an advanced stage.  Enhanced use of digital technology in the planning system has also been aided by the introduction of the Regional Planning IT System (Planning Portal) and the Department and local councils have recently commenced a series of workshops on the ‘ Use of AI and Planning’ to identify the potential for further digital improvements in the planning system.My department has been working closely with statutory consultees and local planning authorities through the Planning Statutory Consultee Forum (PSCF) to streamline statutory consultation processes and improve performance. The most recent full year, 2024/25, recorded 77% of all statutory consultations returned on time, the best result since intensive monitoring began in 2017-18. This encouraging trend has continued into 2025/26 with the year-to-date results for the second quarter standing at 78% for all application types.My department has also introduced three inflationary uplifts to planning fees, with a 12.3% increase in 2023, 4% increase in 2024 and 2.1% increase in 2025 with plans to introduce a further uplift of the planning fees in May 2026 to include new planning fees for non-material change, and discharge of conditions applications to further aid the financial resilience of the planning system.I will continue to review progress with my officials.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to ensure patients are kept informed of arrangements in relation to the search for a new GP contractor at Silverbirch Medical Practice, Bangor.
answer_text: I can confirm that the single-handed GP at Silverbirch Medical Practice has given notice to withdraw from his contract to deliver General Medical Services at the end of the notice period of 3 months.  The contract will end on 31 May 2026.  Officials have now commenced the process to secure alternative arrangements for these services.A number of different options are available to ensure patients are not left without a GP service. The preferred option is to secure a GP contractor, or grouping of GPs, to take over the practice through a formal recruitment process. In some circumstances, Health and Social Care Trusts can take over a GP contract as an interim solution.  I want to reassure patients that the existing Silverbirch Medical Practice will continue to retain the contract to deliver GP services until the 31 May 2026.  Patients at the practice do not need to take any action. They should continue to contact the practice as normal.  Officials have written out to all practice patients notifying them of the situation and will continue to keep them informed throughout the process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement she has had with HM Government, regarding the new pay-per-mile tax for electric vehicles.
answer_text: Vehicle taxation is legally reserved to Westminster. As such, my Department was neither informed of, nor involved in, discussions concerning the proposed introduction of the new pay-per-mile tax (electric Vehicle Excise Duty (eVED)) measures announced in the budget by the British Government on 26th November 2026. Since this time, my officials have engaged with the British Treasury with regard to the implementation of the eVED and are awaiting further clarification regarding the legislative and operational implications here in advance of the proposed implementation date of April 2028. In the interim my officials are monitoring developments following the conclusion of the consultation on the Introduction of Electric Vehicle Excise Duty which closed on18 March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether any funding provided for Coastal Adaptation Pilots in England will be given to Northern Ireland through Barnett consequentials.
answer_text: Coastal Adaptation Pilots in England are funded through DEFRA. The Executive would have received Barnett consequentials on any changes to the DEFRA budget as part of the 2025 Spending Review.However, as Barnett consequentials provided at a Spending Review are identified at Whitehall department level rather than individual programme level it is not possible to identify whether any of these related to the Coastal Adaptation Pilots.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment she has made of the impact that the new pay-per-mile tax for electric vehicles will have on the growth of electric vehicles sales in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Vehicle taxation is a reserved matter and a recent policy consultation by the British Treasury, which closed on 18 March 2026, will inform the future policy in relation to eVED in advance of the proposed implementation date of April 2028. As this is a reserved policy, and no final policy position has been determined, no assessment has been done by my Department regarding the impact of the introduction of eVED will have on the growth of electric vehicles sales here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any contingency plans in place to ensure continued services for patients at Silverbirch Medical Practice, Bangor, in the event that a new GP contractor is not found before the end of the current contract on 31 May 2026.
answer_text: I can confirm that the single-handed GP at Silverbirch Medical Practice has given notice to withdraw from his contract to deliver General Medical Services at the end of the notice period of 3 months.  The contract will end on 31 May 2026.  Officials have now commenced the process to secure alternative arrangements for these services. A number of different options are available to ensure patients are not left without a GP service. The preferred option is to secure a GP contractor, or grouping of GPs, to take over the practice through a formal recruitment process. In some circumstances, Health and Social Care Trusts can take over a GP contract as an interim solution.  Officials have written out to all practice patients notifying them of the situation and will continue to keep them informed throughout the process. I want to reassure patients that the existing Silverbirch Medical Practice will continue to retain the contract to deliver GP services until the 31 May 2026.  Patients at the practice do not need to take any action. They should continue to contact the practice as normal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any actions he is taking to support joint procurement on waste disposal and recycling across the 11 local councils.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, the eleven local councils have statutory responsibility for the collection and disposal of waste. My department does not advise on commercial decisions and/or procurement processes relating to these operations as they are solely the reserve of local councils.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the remaining landfill capacity in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

answer_text: NISRA produces statistics based on the marriage registrations data provided by the General Register Office. The table shows the number of marriages where one or both persons were aged under 18. YearMarriages where at least one partner was under 18202025202147202239202334202438

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any planned reforms for the rental sector.
answer_text: My programme of private rented sector reform is aimed at addressing the key issues of security, affordability, and standards in a way that balances the rights and responsibilities of both landlords and tenants. Work in progress includes: Extending notice to quit periods to strengthen security of tenure for private renters;Amending Landlord Registration Regulations to make the scheme a more meaningful tool in raising property standards; and Establishing Minimum Energy Efficiency Standards to help raise the quality of rental homes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has any plans to give local councils additional powers to address fly-tipping, similar to that recently introduced in England.
answer_text: The Waste and Contaminated land Order 1997 is primary legislation designed, in part, to tackle the illegal dumping of waste and thereby protect the environment. The Waste and Contaminated Land Order 1997 prohibits unauthorised or harmful deposit, treatment or disposal of waste with District councils having the powers to issue fixed penalty notices or pursue convictions through the courts.	Using the powers the 1997 Order provides, my Department is leading a number of initiatives to reduce fly-tipping and littering, including the Fly tipping Protocol which clarifies the responsibilities of local councils and the NI Environment Agency, to ensure fly-tipping incidents are addressed quickly, minimising environmental harm and community impact. As such, I have no plans to amend the 1997 Order at this time.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions he has taken following the outbreak of Meningitis B in Kent.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency is attending UKHSA Incident Management Team meetings to monitor the outbreak in Kent and consider the public health implications for Northern Ireland.  The risk to the NI public is currently low and we are continuing to closely monitor the situation. The suspected case last week relating to a secondary school pupil in Belfast has been confirmed as being negative for meningococcal meningitis.  As a precaution, the PHA has been working with the school and has issued information to parents and guardians. All close contacts have been risk assessed and, where appropriate, received antibiotic prophylaxis. The PHA has issued public health information and the public have been urged to remain vigilant for the signs and symptoms of meningitis and to act fast if they suspect they may be symptomatic.  Further information is available at: PHA statement - meningitis | HSC Public Health Agency and on NI Direct at Meningitis | nidirect

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the reason why the outcome of her Department's Active Travel Delivery Plan consultation has been delayed.
answer_text: I can confirm that ongoing court proceedings related to the A5 Western Transport Corridor have had no bearing on the publication timetable for the Active Travel Delivery Plan (ATDP). Officials provided written evidence to the Committee on 5 November 2025, followed by an oral evidence session on 18 February 2026. The Committee’s feedback, received on 3 March 2026, is currently being considered as officials finalise the Plan for my consideration.Given the importance of the Plan in shaping future investment and delivery of active travel, I will require time to give careful consideration to the final proposals. However, I hope to be in a position to publish the Plan in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the non-charge days for Controlled Parking Zones.
answer_text: The non-charge days for Controlled Parking Zones are New Year’s Day (or day in lieu); St. Patrick’s Day (or day in lieu); Easter Monday; 12th and 13th July (or days in lieu); and 25th and 26th December (or days in lieu).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has any plans to introduce stronger protections for farm animals from dog attacks, similar to the Dogs (Protection of Livestock) (Amendment) Act 2025.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, it remains an offence not to have a dog under control on land where livestock are present, let a dog worry livestock, or set a dog on livestock. If a dog warden has reasonable cause to believe a dog has committed one of these offences, they can place control conditions on the dog licence held by that dog owner, which can include confining the dog to a specified place. Where a person is convicted of letting their dog worry livestock, the penalty is a fine of £1000. For setting a dog on livestock, the penalty is a £2,500 fine. The court can also order that the dog is destroyed. I am aware that Defra has recently amended legislation in relation to livestock worrying. However, I believe that the current legal requirements here, as I have set out, are robust. Given the limited time remaining in the mandate, it is not feasible to conduct a review, or make any amendments, to the legislation in relation to livestock worrying. My focus remains on delivering the reforms set out in my Animal Welfare Pathway, which I set following extensive engagement with stakeholders.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the ongoing issues relating to the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme has had any bearing on the late publication of her Department’s response to the consultation on the Active Travel Delivery Plan.
answer_text: I can confirm that ongoing court proceedings related to the A5 Western Transport Corridor have had no bearing on the publication timetable for the Active Travel Delivery Plan (ATDP). Officials provided written evidence to the Committee on 5 November 2025, followed by an oral evidence session on 18 February 2026. The Committee’s feedback, received on 3 March 2026, is currently being considered as officials finalise the Plan for my consideration.Given the importance of the Plan in shaping future investment and delivery of active travel, I will require time to give careful consideration to the final proposals. However, I hope to be in a position to publish the Plan in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the timetable for her plans to introduce legislation to prohibit the overtaking of school buses at bus stops.
answer_text: My officials are working at pace on the Safer Journeys to School (SJS) programme. The programme is exploring educational, behavioural, infrastructural and legislative measures as part of a whole-system approach to improving safety.Officials are engaging with a wide range of stakeholders including community groups, political representatives, academics, emergency services, public transport operators, and families who have been affected by tragic road traffic incidents.As part of the programme, the Department has considered the use of warning lights and signals on school buses, as well as other measures designed to create a temporary safety zone around stationary buses. Officials have also examined school bus safety measures adopted in other jurisdictions, including the United States of America, Canada and across Europe, to inform policy development.A public consultation on a proposed legislative approach to improving safety around buses during boarding and alighting will shortly be launched. This will provide an opportunity for individuals, families, stakeholders and organisations to share views and evidence to help inform the programme’s next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, as part of her plans to introduce legislation to prohibit overtaking school buses at bus stops, whether her Department has considered any school bus safety measures, that have been adopted in the United States of America and Canada.
answer_text: My officials are working at pace on the Safer Journeys to School (SJS) programme. The programme is exploring educational, behavioural, infrastructural and legislative measures as part of a whole-system approach to improving safety.Officials are engaging with a wide range of stakeholders including community groups, political representatives, academics, emergency services, public transport operators, and families who have been affected by tragic road traffic incidents.As part of the programme, the Department has considered the use of warning lights and signals on school buses, as well as other measures designed to create a temporary safety zone around stationary buses. Officials have also examined school bus safety measures adopted in other jurisdictions, including the United States of America, Canada and across Europe, to inform policy development.A public consultation on a proposed legislative approach to improving safety around buses during boarding and alighting will shortly be launched. This will provide an opportunity for individuals, families, stakeholders and organisations to share views and evidence to help inform the programme’s next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the current average processing time for issuing an updated driving license to drivers who change their surname following marriage.
answer_text: Drivers in the North must advise the Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) of a change of name, which can only be done by a postal application as it requires provision of additional supporting documentation.   The DVA has a target to issue 90% of driving licence applications made by post within 10 working days of receipt, although processing may take longer if health or personal details require additional checks. The latest published figures show performance above target at 93.5%.  The DVA does not record or monitor separate processing times for surname changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any planned NI Water capital works to improve the wastewater infrastructure in North Down.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. The PC21 Price Control includes an investment of approximately £17 million in wastewater infrastructure for this area, with nearly all of the projects completed.  The Ballyreagh Road Sewer project (£4.1m) is scheduled towards the end of the PC21 period. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he is aware that almost 90 per cent of pharmacies were unable to pay their wholesaler bill in January 2026. [R]

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, as part of her plans to introduce legislation to prohibit overtaking school buses at bus stops, whether her Department has considered the use of warning lights on school buses, or other measures that create a temporary safety zone around stationary buses.
answer_text: My officials are working at pace on the Safer Journeys to School (SJS) programme. The programme is exploring educational, behavioural, infrastructural and legislative measures as part of a whole-system approach to improving safety.Officials are engaging with a wide range of stakeholders including community groups, political representatives, academics, emergency services, public transport operators, and families who have been affected by tragic road traffic incidents.As part of the programme, the Department has considered the use of warning lights and signals on school buses, as well as other measures designed to create a temporary safety zone around stationary buses. Officials have also examined school bus safety measures adopted in other jurisdictions, including the United States of America, Canada and across Europe, to inform policy development.A public consultation on a proposed legislative approach to improving safety around buses during boarding and alighting will shortly be launched. This will provide an opportunity for individuals, families, stakeholders and organisations to share views and evidence to help inform the programme’s next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any actions she has taken to support the construction of new dwellings, since she came into post.
answer_text: Although the lead role on housing policy rests with the Department for Communities (DfC), my predecessor and I have ringfenced just over £30 million of additional capital funding allocations to NI Water, which have unlocked wastewater capacity for over 5,300 residential and commercial properties. This is above NI Water’s original plans of 4,500 properties in the current Price Control period (PC21 - 2021-2027).In line with my three-pronged approach to wastewater capacity, I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to secure additional funding for NI Water to unlock capacity to support new developments. This work is being taken forward alongside my new Water, Sustainable Drainage and Flood Management Bill and the consultation on proposals for developer contributions.DFI’s Strategic planning policy has an important enabling role to play in facilitating the delivery of housing on the ground.DfI is also collaborating across Departments through the DfC led Housing Supply Strategy (HSS) Project Board, which takes a whole system, cross government approach to developing and agreeing actions for the first HSS Action Plan, including ensuring that Local Development Plans provide appropriate policy support for housing delivery. I remain fully supportive of the efforts to progress the commitments contained in the HSS. The strategy clearly sets out the progress required to unlock housing supply, including water infrastructure constraints, land use planning, construction skills and land availability.As you will be aware the Department for Infrastructure is not a housing delivery department, however its responsibilities for planning, transport, roads, and wastewater infrastructure are key enablers of residential development.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Education Authority will uplift the rate provided for school transport, operated by the private sector, should petrol prices remain inflated over an extended period.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that it acknowledge the concerns raised by operators providing home to school transport in relation to the recent increase in fuel prices.  At present, the EA has no plans to uplift the contracted rates agreed with private operators. However, it is keeping the position under  review and will continue to monitor developments in fuel costs to assess any potential implications for current home to school transport arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether any assessment has been carried out on the current trend for new dwelling starts and completions in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Councils are responsible for operating effective development management in respect of planning applications for dwellings. They are also responsible for preparing local development plans (LDPs) which are the main vehicle for delivering the construction of dwellings by assessing future housing land requirements and managing housing growth to achieve sustainable patterns of residential development. As part of this process, councils are required to monitor housing land supply (approval of dwellings) which may also include commencements and completions as part of their evidence base. This information will assist in monitoring the implementation of councils housing policies.All councils are currently preparing new LDPs. Once both stages (Plan Strategy and Local Policies Plan) of the process have been completed, councils will be required to submit annual monitoring reports to my Department. Councils will also be required to undertake regular reviews of their LDPs at least every five years. The findings of this review and the annual monitoring report should also be made available to the public.

answer_text: Official Statistics on new dwellings started and completed are published at New dwelling statistics | Department of Finance. Within this link, table 3 shows the annual new dwelling starts and completions from 2005-2025.
