question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any assessment has been made on the length of waiting lists, since he took up office.
answer_text: Reducing the waiting lists in Northern Ireland has always been one of my highest priorities. It demands a long-term collective effort requiring sustained and substantial investment through multi-year budgets, workforce development and system wide reform.Much good work is underway to tackle the backlog of patients waiting, for example through the development of elective care centres, rapid diagnosis centres and Mega Clinics. In addition, my Department is working with Trusts to increase productivity and efficiency through the use of a wide range of best practice service improvements. This ensures we are maximising the capacity available to us.My Department routinely monitors waiting list performance across all HSC Trusts through published waiting time statistics and regular performance reporting. Since taking up office, I have received regular briefings from officials on the current position in relation to waiting lists across elective care, diagnostics and outpatient services.The recently published statistics from 31 December 2025, indicate that, for the first time in years, the numbers waiting across the 5 HSC Trusts for a first consultant outpatient appointment have fallen. We have also seen reductions in the inpatient/daycase and diagnostics lists.While progress is welcome, we cannot become complacent. My Department is working alongside Trusts, clinical teams and managers to continue driving forward system-wide improvement in a challenging environment. Their efforts are to be commended, and I am determined to maintain this momentum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether Health and Social Care Trusts are making any alternative arrangements to contact patients about upcoming appointments, given postal delays have resulted in some patients receiving letters after their appointment date.
answer_text: My Department is committed to working with the Health and Social Care Trusts to continually improve communication methods with all patients, ensuring that they have the best possible experience and timely access to care.To help improve patient communication and ensure that patients are better informed of appointment dates, my Department has provided recurrent funding to Health and Social Care Trusts to support the implementation of a dual notification system for appointment reminders. In conjunction with sending appointment letters to patients, Trusts frequently use text message reminders to notify patients of their upcoming appointments across most services. This approach aims to provide a timely and convenient method of communication, especially when postal services may be delayed. This approach helps to reduce the number of ‘Did Not Attend’ (DNAs) and cancellations by prompting attendance and giving patients the opportunity to cancel or reschedule their appointments, ensuring valuable appointment slots can be offered to other patients.Furthermore, the Encompass system is now fully operational across Northern Ireland, allowing patients and service users to securely access a subset of their health and social care record through the My Care application, providing secure online access to appointment and clinic letters, medication details, and some test results. Patients can also view appointments and track the progress of their treatment.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any engagement they have had with HM Government regarding lowering the cost of transport links across the Irish Sea between Northern Ireland and Great Britain.

To ask the Minister of Finance to detail when he expects to make a decision on the recommendations in the Independent Fiscal Commission's report regarding tax powers that could be devolved to the Executive.
answer_text: The Fiscal Commission have provided a valuable evidence base that is being actively used to inform my department’s work towards a full Fiscal Framework. The Commission’s recommendations informed my paper to Executive Colleagues, outlining my pathway to progressing a full Fiscal Framework, and my officials have also engaged with their Treasury counterparts on the basis of those recommendations, as they considered the scope of a full Fiscal framework. Ultimately, decisions on further fiscal devolution will require agreement at the Executive, and with the British Government. I am fully aware that people will have different views on fiscal devolution. It is my view that it is vital the Executive has the additional financial tools we need to deliver more sustainable public finances and public services. I believe the Executive can work together and agree where greater control over fiscal powers could deliver real benefits. The evidence base provided by the Fiscal Commission will be an ongoing and important part of our considerations as we take this work forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he plans to give councils powers to vary business rates by sector or location.
answer_text: Business rates are set by each of the 11 District Councils, and the Executive, each year. In that regard the Council in which a property is located will determine a property’s rate poundage multiplier. I have no plans to amend powers to allow business rates to be varied at individual District Council level on the basis of sector or location outside of the current statutory framework. My priority remains the Strategic Review of all rate support provisions which I have committed to completing by the end of the 2027/28 rating year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the length of time a road defect remains on the online Report a Fault system.
answer_text: The online Report a Fault service displays a map marker for reported road defects to enable other members of the public to add their details to the same record. Each defect is assigned a unique reference number, which can be used to monitor progress through the separate Track a Fault facility. The map marker is removed once the investigation has been completed, the defect has been repaired, or where the record is more than one month old.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he would consider a public sector pay increase ceiling for senior civil servants.
answer_text: My overall approach to public sector pay is set out in the draft Budget 2026–29, issued for consultation in January, which states that restraint around Chief and Senior Executive pay awards should be applied where remuneration is already relatively high.Pay negotiations for the Civil Service, including senior civil servants, are currently underway with recognised trade unions, and it would be inappropriate to preempt the outcome of those discussions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any actions she is taking to support growth in the defence sector.
answer_text: My Department’s Advanced Manufacturing Sectoral Action Plan continues to support growth across a range of highvalue manufacturing activities.Officials continue to engage with counterparts in the British Government in relation to the proposed Defence Growth Deal. This engagement remains ongoing, and further detail on the nature, scope and next steps for the proposal is anticipated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he is considering a voluntary exit scheme for teachers.
answer_text: There is no current proposal for a voluntary exit scheme for teachers. There are no available finances to fund a voluntary exit scheme.Since 2020-21, voluntary redundancies have been funded in-year from the education budget, and the Department has made an allocation to the Education Authority (EA) as funding authority for all schools, to provide funding for unavoidable redundancies only.  The funding of unavoidable redundancies encourages volunteers to avoid compulsory redundancies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any plans he has to reduce the size of the public sector.
answer_text: Responsibility for workforce management rests with all departments, their Arms’ Length Bodies (ALBs), their respective Accounting Officers, and ultimately their Ministers.  All Departments and ALBs are expected to manage their staffing and resources in line with their budgets, and in recognition of Executive priorities on Reform and Transformation. To support this, my department has been leading on the Departmental 5 Year Plans, where departments are being encouraged to identify efficiencies that can be taken forward over the five-year period, with the aim of driving down costs, improving service delivery, and ensuring that resources are targeted where they are most needed. As part of this work, departments have been encouraged to examine historic workforce trends and consider how to best align future workforce to their strategic direction.In relation to my responsibilities for the Civil Service, the NICS People Strategy 2025–2030, which my department launched in April 2025, sets the overall direction for stronger workforce planning, clearer role structures and better data for NICS.  This will enable departments to take a more planned, evidence-based approach to managing their capacity and capability to become the right size and shape. My department has put in place, core building blocks to embed Strategic Workforce Planning, including: published guidance; an agreed vision; an active Community of Practice; and an agreement for an affordable baseline approach, to ensure departments plan from the same starting point. The Audit Office has recommended that departmental strategic workforce plans should be in place by 31 March 2028 to enable a NICS-wide plan to be in place by 31 March 2029. Departments have been asked to appoint senior owners and resource the capability needed to carry this work forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rationale as to why his Department has not negotiated the over-arching contractual and funding arrangements for community pharmacy services with Community Pharmacy Northern Ireland in the last 15 years. [R]
answer_text: The current community pharmacy contractual arrangements in Northern Ireland centre around a funding envelope determined by the Department in the context of the overall funding available for Health and Social Care (HSC), and services commissioned by SPPG in line with a commissioning plan developed in collaboration with Community Pharmacy NI. The commissioning plan is based on the previously agreed community pharmacy contractual framework that is comprised of: (1) Practice Quality System; (2) Core Services; (3) Additional Services; and (4) Locally Required Services.These arrangements have benefited community pharmacy, with year-on-year increases to recurrent funding, annual pay uplifts aligned with DDRB and exceptional investments such as for National Insurance increases. This has included uplifts to the non-global sum, the application of which is determined in collaboration with Community Pharmacy NI. Government funding for community pharmacy HSC service provision has increased by almost £60m (57%) since April 2018.  I recently approved an inflationary uplift of 4% for 2025/26 which equates to £4.72m and is being paid over three months February to April 2026. In addition to this, in acknowledgement of the recent concerns raised by CPNI in relation to the financial pressures arising from current price fluctuations for a number of medicines, and in order to support the network, I have approved an increase in the special advance payable to contractors. I trust this will be welcomed by community pharmacies.Despite many years of severe financial constraints for my Department due to the rising demand for health services from an ageing population, unprecedented inflationary costs and a significant funding deficit, investment in community pharmacy has been continuous and I see no reason to change the current arrangements at this time.My Department remains committed to continued engagement through existing fora with Community Pharmacy NI to oversee the delivery of commissioned HSC community pharmacy services in line with the available financial envelope and against the agreed commissioning plan in order to ensure the provision of best possible services to patients and the public.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether any additional funding has been allocated to the Community Infrastructure Fund.
answer_text: No additional funding has been allocated to the Northern Ireland Community Infrastructure Fund (NICIF) at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 41226/22-27, to detail the reason why the Coastal Forum has only met once in the last two years.
answer_text: The Coastal Forum, which is co-chaired by Minister Muir and I, meets as necessary when there is a decision to be taken requiring the Forum’s approval or there is significant progress that should be brought to the Forum’s attention.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any revenue raising measures he is considering within his Department’s remit.
answer_text: Due to the nature of my Department’s receipts there is limited scope to increase income from new measures.  However, existing income measures are due to increase in 2026-27 to maintain full cost recovery in line with Managing Public Money Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount spent to date at the Ulster Hospital to comply with the Hospital Parking Charges Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust has invested approximately £0.4 million on preparatory works at the Ulster Hospital site to support the Hospital Parking Charges Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 coming into operation.This investment has funded work to upgrade existing carpark infrastructure and to procure Automatic Number Plate Recognition (ANPR) equipment.In advance of the 2022 Act coming into operation this investment will help prevent the inappropriate use of hospital car parks and will provide management information to improve the usage of the available car parking spaces.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any consultation he had with the Minister for Infrastructure before he announced plans to withdraw the Northern Ireland Environment Agency from the Statement of Regulatory Principles and Intent arrangements.
answer_text: I have met and discussed my department’s proposal to withdraw from the Statement of Regulatory Principles and Intent (SORPI) with the Infrastructure Minister on three occasions. My officials have engaged with NI Water on this matter as part of the Department’s ongoing governance arrangements. Additionally, my department consulted both DfI and NI Water on SORPI between April and July 2025. Both the Minister for Infrastructure and NI Water provided responses to this consultation, which have been carefully considered, and discussed during ongoing engagement with the Infrastructure Minister.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether any assessment has been undertaken by her Department of the impact of Northern Ireland Environment Agency's potential withdrawal from the Statement of Regulatory Principles and Intent will have on house building targets.
answer_text: I have continued to work with Minister Muir on these matters, and I am grateful for his ongoing engagement in respect of same. While I acknowledge that the current SORPI arrangement was intended to be temporary, in my view it is essential that a managed approach is taken to implementation of this proposal and other regulatory changes outlined by Minister Muir. This can ensure that operational impacts for NI Water can be factored into the planning currently underway by both NI Water and the Utility Regulator for the next Price Control period (PC 28, 2028/29 – 2032/33). This approach can also ensure that any cross-cutting impacts for the Executive, including in respect of housing, can be clearly understood.The consideration of a regulatory impact assessment is a matter for the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs as the responsible Department leading on this proposal and other related issues detailed in Minister Muir’s statement to the Assembly of 3rd March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether any assessment has been undertaken by her Department on the impact Northern Ireland Environment Agency's potential withdrawal from the Statement of Regulatory Principles and Intent will have on the ability of NI Water to grant new connections.
answer_text: I have continued to work with Minister Muir on these matters, and I am grateful for his ongoing engagement in respect of same. While I acknowledge that the current SORPI arrangement was intended to be temporary, in my view it is essential that a managed approach is taken to implementation of this proposal and other regulatory changes outlined by Minister Muir. This can ensure that operational impacts for NI Water can be factored into the planning currently underway by both NI Water and the Utility Regulator for the next Price Control period (PC 28, 2028/29 – 2032/33). This approach can also ensure that any cross-cutting impacts for the Executive, including in respect of housing, can be clearly understood.The consideration of a regulatory impact assessment is a matter for the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs as the responsible Department leading on this proposal and other related issues detailed in Minister Muir’s statement to the Assembly of 3rd March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions taken by the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust to expand the capacity of staff parking at the Ulster Hospital, in the last five years.
answer_text: The aim of the Department of Health’s policy on car parking is to ensure the effective provision of car parking across all of the Health Estate for patients, visitors and staff. Decisions on how the Department’s policy is applied is for each Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust to determine, to enable them to take local issues into account.The Ulster Hospital has approximately 2,200 parking spaces, of which 1,300 are designated for permitted staff parking. Capacity is constrained by the geography of the site, planning restrictions and the number of entrances and exits serving the site.  In the last five years, the staff parking permittospace ratio has risen from 1.5 to 1.9 permits per space, which has increased the overall number of staff who have a staff parking permit by more efficiently utilising each space.  The South Eastern HSC Trust also provides over 100 free off-site staff parking spaces at Dundonald Omniplex with a free shuttlebus operating, every 15 minutes at peak times and every 30 minutes at all other times.The Trust realises that car parking is a genuine concern for their staff and is working to provide viable alternatives to single occupancy car use and to increase on and off-site staff car parking capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 39737/22-27, whether the potential for revenue raising was considered in the removal of the Cadastral layer from the Spatial NI dataset.
answer_text: The considerations for the removal of the Cadastral layer from the Spatial NI dataset were noted in my previous AQW response on 26 February which listed the following reasons: Uncertainty following Brexit pending further direction from the Department for Environment Food and Rural Affairs (Defra) and the Westminster Government on future INSPIRE policy and infrastructure. Core software components were nearing supplier end-of-life. Very low platform usage, meaning the data was rarely accessed, further reinforcing the need to pause any further investment until the future direction of INSPIRE is confirmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement that Health and Social Care Trusts have had with the British Polio Fellowship regarding its Optimal Clinical Pathway for polio survivors.
answer_text: Within HSC Trusts, Polio Survivors and individuals with PostPolio Syndrome (PPS) are supported by the appropriate adult community teams, or, where necessary, are assessed by Mental Health, Allied Health Professionals (AHPs) and Disability Services, who then provide treatment, support, or onward signposting based on identified need.HSC Trust representatives attended the British Polio Fellowship event held in the Long Gallery at Stormont on 2 March 2026, and the Optimal Clinical Pathway has since been shared across Trust directorates to help raise awareness and promote good practice principles.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action that is being taken to assist polio survivors who are suffering from post-polio syndrome.
answer_text: Within HSC Trusts, Polio Survivors and individuals with PostPolio Syndrome (PPS) are supported by the appropriate adult community teams, or, where necessary, are assessed by Mental Health, Allied Health Professionals (AHPs) and Disability Services, who then provide treatment, support, or onward signposting based on identified need.HSC Trust representatives attended the British Polio Fellowship event held in the Long Gallery at Stormont on 2 March 2026, and the Optimal Clinical Pathway has since been shared across Trust directorates to help raise awareness and promote good practice principles.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 41230/22-27, whether the part-time 20mph speed limit is still in operation without the appropriate signage.
answer_text: I can confirm that the part-time 20mph speed limit at Ballyvester Primary School will only be enforceable once the appropriate signage is in place on the road network, enabling enforcement by the PSNI.Work to install the required 20mph signage is expected to be completed in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any options available to businesses which, owing to the need for regular access to their work van containing heavy equipment, require to park on double yellow lines or park in a parking bay beyond the limit.
answer_text: Businesses that require a van to load and unload heavy or bulky equipment may use the loading and unloading exemptions that apply to double yellow lines and some on-street restrictions. However, this is only permitted where loading is continuous and the vehicle is not left unattended for longer than the period a Traffic Attendant observes to confirm that a loading and unloading activity is taking place.This exemption does not apply where the vehicle is used for convenience, storage, or ongoing on-site work, and a Penalty Charge Notice may be issued if no continuous loading activity is observed.Where works are planned, such as installation, maintenance or utility works, contractors may apply for a dispensation from parking and waiting restrictions, which may allow controlled parking where it is safe and appropriate.Outside these specific allowances, vehicles must comply with local parking restrictions and businesses should seek alternative permitted parking arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any implications that the December 2023 UK Supreme Court ruling HXA v Surrey County Council has had for survivors of failings in child social services seeking redress from the Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: My thoughts extend to anyone who has experienced abuse, including those whose abuse was impacted upon by the failings of any professional, including Social Services. A person’s experience and legal recourse will each have specific and individual nuances which would need to be considered on a case by case basis. Any such individual and their legal representative should consider the potential implication of the December 2023 UK Supreme Court ruling HXA v Surrey County Council, although I note this ruling was based on the Children Act 1989 as primary legislation which applied to England and Wales.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 41508/22-27, to detail (i) the schools in North Down that currently have Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools (SPiMS); and (ii) any additional schools in North Down expected to have SPiMS for the 2026-2027 academic year.
answer_text: (i) The Education Authority (EA) has advised that there are currently four schools in North Down with Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools  DE RefSchool nameSpecialist provision typeNo. of classes4013023Bloomfield Primary Schoollearning2.04016002Kilcooley Primary SchoolLearning, Social Communication, Special (SLD)9.04250024Priory CollegeSocial Communication4.04.16111Towerview Primary SchoolLearning 1.0In planning for the September 2026 intake, the EA invited all schools in the North Down area to engagement events in November and December 2025 and engaged directly with eight schools in the area.  As of the 9 March, no additional schools have indicated that they are in a position to establish Specialist Provision classes for September 2026.Discussions are ongoing with one of the schools with existing Specialist Provision classes to establish the feasibility of expanding their provision to add one further class for September 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any proposals in place to enable residents to register their interest in establishing a residents’ parking scheme on their street.
answer_text: I am very much aware that parking is an issue of concern for residents in many areas, and along with my officials, I have been carefully considering the best way to address this.I was pleased to announce the launch of a new application process for residents’ parking schemes, which will go-live in April 2026. The new demand-led process will provide a streamlined approach for considering, assessing and prioritising requests for schemes going forward.Further information on Residents’ Parking zones, including guidance on how to apply, can be found on NIDirect at  Residents' parking zones | nidirect

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any revenue raising measures she is considering within her Department’s remit.
answer_text: I continue to explore and consider further revenue raising measures across my Department; however, any proposed uplifts or new charges need to be assessed against the impact on already hard pressed workers and families and delivering modal shift.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the reason for the absence of telephone number for people to query or challenge penalty charge notices, issued by her Department.
answer_text: My Department does not provide a telephone service for querying or challenging Penalty Charge Notices (PCNs), as the statutory process requires challenges to be submitted in writing. This allows the grounds of any challenge, along with any supporting evidence, to be clearly set out and properly considered. My Department provides information on the PCN process on the NI Direct portal and challenges can also be submitted online through the Department’s PCN challenge portal, or by post for those who are unable to access online services, as a written record is required to support the statutory review process and any subsequent appeal to the independent adjudicator.This approach is designed to ensure fairness, transparency and consistency for all road users, and reflects established practice in the operation of parking enforcement in other jurisdictions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any revenue raising measures he is considering within his Department’s remit.
answer_text: My Department has limited scope for revenueraising within its statutory responsibilities. However, in agreement with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury, my Department has submitted a Welfare Fraud and Error “Invest to Save” Business Case.  This includes the potential to share a portion of resulting savings with the Executive.  In line with The Fresh Start Agreement, I will be seeking a 50% share of the savings.My Department continues to assess and explore revenue-generating strategies that align with its statutory responsibilities and policy objectives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any revenue raising measures he is considering within his Department’s remit.
answer_text: Firstly, please note that current legislation enshrines the right to free education for the vast majority of pupils in Northern Ireland.My Department’s recently published Five-Year Education Budget Strategy sets out areas where revenue raising will be explored. The Five-Year Education Budget Strategy is currently out for public consultation, closing on 3 April 2026.In addition to my response above, please also refer to AQW 41597/22-27,  published in the weekly answer booklet w/e 13 March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any revenue raising measures she is considering within her Department’s remit.
answer_text: Within my Department, fees and charges arise in a limited number of areas, including the Insolvency Service, Tourism (in relation to tourist accommodation certification), and the Construction Industry Training Board.These bodies are committed to achieving full cost recovery, where legislation permits, and fees are kept under review in line with Managing Public Money, which does not allow charges to exceed the cost of providing the service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any redress pathways available for survivors of past failings by social services in relation to the care of children that have left lifelong and traumatic consequences into adulthood.
To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any revenue raising measures he is considering within his Department’s remit.
answer_text: My officials are currently undertaking a Strategic Income Review across departments, which will provide a comprehensive overview of income generation opportunities.The Review is not a policy document recommending specific changes, but rather a strategic overview to inform Executive and Ministerial decision-making and will be published in due course.Any revenue raising measures within my own Department form part of that review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the methodology by which non-domestic rates are calculated for licensed premises.
answer_text: Non-domestic rates for licensed premises are assessed using the Receipts and Expenditure method of valuation, in line with established rating and industry practice and the statutory requirement to determine rental value at the valuation date. A detailed explanation of the methodology has been deposited in the Assembly Library under Deposited Paper Number 2034, and is available through the Assembly’s Deposited Papers collection, found here:Deposited Papers

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on measures being taken to tackle benefit fraud.
answer_text: In my statement to the Assembly on 9 February 2026, I committed to accept the recommendations of the Task and Finish Report to drive forward fraud and error work across four key areas.	Enhance and expand current fraud and error activities Strengthen the specialist training and support for staff to double-down on fraud and error, including mistakes made by officials.Maximize technology solutions in conjunction with DWP and other Departments, at Westminster and locally, to aid investigative efforts.Increase public understanding of benefit fraud and raise awareness of our zero-tolerance approach.	Since the Group reported, my Departments Benefit Security has continued to deliver strong results across all areas of fraud and error prevention. By end of 31 January 2026, 560 criminal investigations into benefit fraud had been concluded with 57 prosecutions reported as a direct result of the relentless investigatory work of my Department.  Compliance work within the Department, concluded 2918 cases during the same period, resulting in savings of £7.8million.My Department reviewed over 17,000 Universal Credit cases up to the end January 2026, correcting 3001 UC claims and saving the Department over £11.5m.Over 5900 ESA cases were reviewed within the same period, correcting 758 ESA claims and saving the Department £2.8m.On 9 February 2026, I launched the Department’s new advertising campaign, on radio, newspapers and social media, to highlight our work to identify and root out fraudsters.Benefit Security’s achievements are now being highlighted more effectively through improved internal communications, ensuring that staff across the Department can clearly see the impact of their work and the progress being made.A Fraud Ambassador role has been introduced within the Department to provide support and guidance in recognising, preventing, and responding to fraud and error. These actions form part of a coherent and robust programme of work—enhancing the capability of our investigators, improving the consistency of decisionmaking, and increasing awareness among staff and benefit recipients alike. As we implement the next phase of measures arising from the Task and Finish recommendations, I am determined that we continue to build on this momentum and ensure that public money reaches those who are genuinely entitled to it.Some of these measures are subject to additional funding bids. I welcome the confirmation from the UK Government that they will work with the Northern Ireland Executive over the coming months on ways to tackle welfare fraud and error in Northern Ireland and on different funding options, including the potential to share a portion of resulting savings with the Executive. With the Executive’s support these savings could be reinvested, and directed towards supporting people with barriers to employment, particularly those with disabilities or health conditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 33550/22-27, for an update on the future of Go Succeed.
answer_text: Since its launch in November 2023, the councilled Go Succeed service has become a central pillar of our business support landscape. In less than three years, over 10,000 entrepreneurs have received advice, mentoring, or financial support to start or grow their businesses. However, the future of this service is now at significant risk because of changes introduced by the British Government to the Local Growth Fund. The level of resource funding available through the Local Growth Fund is substantially lower than that provided under its predecessor, the Shared Prosperity Fund. This reduction has created a critical funding shortfall for Go Succeed and a number of other projects that had been supported through Shared Prosperity Fund.Alongside Executive colleagues, I wrote to the Secretary of State for the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government on 22 January to urge him to take immediate action to safeguard the continuity of this important programme.  Regrettably, he is unwilling to reconsider the funding position.   I am continuing to explore with councils all options with regard to the delivery of Go Succeed in 2026/27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any revenue raising measures he is considering within his Department’s remit.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of schools that currently have Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools (SPiMS); and (ii) the number of additional schools expected to have SPiMs for the 2026-2027 academic year.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has provided the following information to the Minister;i) Currently 271 schools including four non-statutory settings have operational specialist provision classes.ii)  It is the view of EA that in principle all mainstream schools should be capable of facilitating Specialist Provision. In advance of the 2026/27 academic year EA has initiated projects to create specialist provision classes in 16 additional schools and is working to extend pathways across 15 existing schools. Furthermore, EA is building on previous engagement to identify schools that are strategically placed to accommodate the current pressure in known areas of high demand to address the challenging projected shortfall of circa 350-400 SEN places for September 2026.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to outline any action it is taking to ensure that Parliament Buildings is compliant with the Supreme Court judgment in the case For Women Scotland Ltd v The Scottish Ministers.
To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the methodology by which non-domestic rates for business is calculated.
answer_text: Nondomestic rates for business properties are based on their net annual value (NAV), which represents the rental value of the property at the statutory valuation date. Land & Property Services determines this rental value using established valuation methods, depending on the type of property and the market evidence available.For most properties, the comparative method is used, drawing on rents from the open market for similar hereditaments. Specialist properties where rental evidence is limited, such as universities and hospitals are assessed using the contractor’s method. For classes of property where trading potential is the best indicator of rental value, such as licensed premises and some leisure uses, the Receipts and Expenditure method is applied.A detailed explanation of the Receipts and Expenditure methodology has been deposited in the Assembly Library under Deposited Paper Number 2034, and is available through the Assembly’s Deposited Papers collection, found here:Deposited PapersRegardless of the valuation method used, the objective is the same: to arrive at a fair and evidencebased rental value in line with statutory requirements. The NAV is multiplied by the relevant regional and district rates to calculate the rates bill.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any legislative requirements regarding (i) school hours; or (ii) days per week that a school must adhere to.
answer_text: The Department’s Circular 2026/10, which issued on 17 February 2026, sets out the statutory minimum requirements that all schools must adhere to in terms of hours of instruction and days of operation. The Circular does not cover new issues, rather it seeks to provide a suite of guidance that sets out clearly the legal requirements and expected practice around school days and hours in one document.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on her Department's plans to redevelop lands at Balloo South Industrial site, Bangor.
answer_text: The land at Balloo South is owned by Invest NI. The remaining available land is actively marketed to potential investors. Invest NI also continues to engage with local stakeholder partners in the area including Ards and North Down Borough Council to explore how Balloo South can best contribute to economic development in the area. Invest NI will also continue to explore development opportunities that support sustainable growth and deliver long-term benefits for the local economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will retain policy to ensure that Councils adopt a town centre first approach to retail, in their Local Development Plans.
answer_text: There are no plans to change the town centre first approach.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the key developments of the Coastal Forum, in the last two years.
answer_text: Key developments of the Coastal Forum in relation to its current Work Programme include:over £2m invested in establishing the baseline, with the evidence that is now available equivalent to, and in some cases, more up to date and of better quality than other jurisdictions. the launch of the Coastal Change Information Tool which provides a scientific interpretation of the patterns of coastal change since the early 1800s;all evidence and research commissioned since 2021 has been made publicly available on the Coastal Observatory website; and €9.6m PEACE PLUS funding awarded to the Coastal Monitoring and Adaptation Planning (CMAP) project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how the Coastal Forum has delivered for North Down, in the last two years.
answer_text: The current Coastal Forum Work Programme has since 2019 focused on creating a baseline of evidence to address the gap in information identified in the 2018 report ‘Baseline Study and Gap Analysis of Coastal Erosion Risk Management NI’, with the studies that have been commissioned covering the complete coastline, including North Down. Sections of the North Down coastline have been included as part of wider studies, for example the Outer Ards coastal cell is included in the NI High Level Coastal Risk Assessment - Phase 2 report which was commissioned by the National Trust to contribute to the Coastal Forum's objective to improve coastal management baseline.Reports prepared by Coastal Forum members are available on the Coastal Observatory site at: https://experience.arcgis.com/experience/b8454f3d62164518817e4c581b5555c8/page/Home

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of times the Coastal Forum has met in the last two years.
answer_text: The Coastal Forum met once in the last two years on 19 November 2025. A further meeting is expected this summer. In addition, the Coastal Forum’s Working Group has met three times during the last two years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of applications submitted at Stage Two of the Community Infrastructure Fund.
answer_text: 521 applications were submitted at Stage Two of the Northern Ireland Community Infrastructure Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when her Department will repair the 20mph speed limit sign at Ballyvester Primary School, Donaghadee.
answer_text: I am advised that the 20mph speed limit signs at Ballyvester Primary School were damaged during the adverse weather conditions in December 2025.  Officials have also confirmed that the damage is limited to the bottom half of the sign, and that the flashing signs remain operational.The repair of these signs is anticipated later in the year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any recommendations the Coastal Forum Working Group has made for the 2026-31 work programme.
answer_text: At the November 2025 meeting of the Coastal Forum, it was agreed that the Coastal Forum’s Working Group should make recommendations for a 2026-2031 Work Programme. A 2026-2031 Work Programme is being drafted and when finalised will be considered at a meeting of the Coastal Forum.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of expressions of interest submitted at Stage One of the Community Infrastructure Fund; and (ii) the number deemed eligible to progress to Stage Two.
answer_text: There were 835 Expressions of Interest submitted at Stage One of the Northern Ireland Community Infrastructure Fund. 752 applicant organisations were subsequently invited to submit a Stage Two application. A number of these were progressed conditionally, pending further clarification on key eligibility criteria.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any support that Bangor Football Club has received from the Northern Ireland Football Fund to date.
answer_text: When the TNIFF Performance Programme was launched in January 2025, all applicants, including Bangor FC, were issued with supporting documentation to guide them through the application process. This information was also made publicly available online via the following link (Performance Programme documents page). In February 2025 Bangor FC availed of an information session that was delivered to applicants to assist in their understanding of the requirements of the TNIFF Performance Programme.In September 2025 officials provided Bangor FC with written clarification on an enquiry regarding the due diligence process.On 5th February 2026 I wrote to Bangor FC, as one of the Performance Programme prioritised cohort applicants, with a Letter of Offer for up to £10,000 to fund an updated Outline Business Case to meet the outcomes of the Performance Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of whether the illegally erected billboard on Department of Infrastructure land, at the Egyptian Arch, Newry, is not a Sinn Fein billboard, despite her Departmental officials writing to Sinn Féin elected representatives to ask for its removal.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the roll-out of LED street lights in North Down.
answer_text: My Department remains committed to replacing outdated sodium street lighting with more energyefficient LED units.I can confirm that any renewal of entire street lighting systems, such as Blair Mayne Road South, Newtownards, which is planned to commence shortly, will incorporate LED technology.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any support her Department provides to businesses at risk of flooding in Donaghadee.
answer_text: As there is no current support provided for businesses that have flooded, I have written to my Executive colleagues to raise the issue of flood risk and the need for a coordinated cross-government response, which officials are taking forward. I am also taking forward a small pilot project with the Minister for Infrastructure around flood protection measures for businesses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures that the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust are taking to ensure that staff who have had their staff parking permit removed under the new criteria at the Ulster Hospital are provided with alternative parking provision.
answer_text: The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT) has confirmed that there is insufficient capacity for all staff to be provided with an on-site car parking space at the Ulster Hospital. As an alternative over 100 free off-site staff parking spaces are provided at Dundonald Omniplex with a free shuttlebus operating every 15 minutes at peak times and every 30 minutes at all other times between 06:45am and 21:15pm. Staff who have had their parking permit removed under the new criteriabased parking permit application process will also automatically receive outofhours, weekend, and bank holiday onsite parking access via their Trust Access ID card.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average time for the provision of a hearing aid, in each Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on reducing the use of agency and locum staff in the Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: A Regional Agency Reduction Implementation and Planning Group has been established to co-ordinate the improvement of staff utilisation, with the aim of reducing unsustainable agency and locum spend. The main function of the Group is to develop and implement a plan, with local and regional commitment and coordination, to safely facilitate, and manage the service impact of, reducing unsustainable agency and locum spend.Significant progress has already been made since the implementation of the new nursing & midwifery framework with expenditure of ‘off-framework’ agency for nurses & midwives reducing by £120m against the base year 2022/23. A new framework aimed at reducing medical & dental locum costs is due to come into effect at the start of March 2026. Based on current expenditure, the costs for both nursing & midwifery and medical & dental locums in 2025/26 is expected to be lower than 2024/25.  This is a positive reversal of the previous trend.Reducing agency spend is a key priority and not just because of the costs involved; HSC staff have been clear that excessive agency use is unfair and impacts on morale and more importantly, having consistent clinical teams which also enhances patient safetyAlongside this work the Department’s strategic approach to workforce development, supported by sustained increased investment in pre-registration education and training, has facilitated a 18.7% (+10,630) increase in whole time equivalent staff in post across the HSC in Northern Ireland between March 2018 (56,803) and September 2025 (67,432).   This includes a 31.7% (+1,331 wte) increase in medical and dental staff, a 19.0% (+2,875 wte) increase in registered nursing and midwifery staff and a 25.9% (+2,104 wte) increase in professional and technical staff, 1,017 (+23.9%) more wte allied health professionals and 971 (+25.8%) more wte social workers, directly employed by the HSC.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions he is taking to reduce the length of time to receive a hearing aid from the Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: As demand, in the form of referrals into Audiology Services in Northern Ireland has increased, the number of audiologists available within HSC has reduced and is restricting capacity. The reasons for this are complex but officials in my Department have been working closely with stakeholders to identify how rising demand can be met in the short, medium and longer term.. Officials are currently scoping the potential of a pilot proposal for an alternative communitybased audiology model to complement existing HSC services. This potential approach would allow a small group of patients to be seen in a new way in the short term, with the intention of gathering evidence to inform a longerterm solution.  The outcome of the scoping exercise will inform the next steps and any associated funding implications of service extension.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there will be any changes to the 45mph restrictions for those that pass a (i) car and trailer, category B and E; and (ii) lorry, category C1, C and CE driving tests, after the Graduated Driver licensing Scheme is introduced on 1 October 2026.
answer_text: From 1 October 2026 newly qualified category B drivers who will have completed GDL training, will be able to drive up to posted speed limits where road conditions allow. New driver speed restrictions will also not apply to category B+E.Speed limits that may apply to those passing category B+E or C1, C, or CE driving tests are based on the size, type of vehicle and type of road being driven on rather than on driver experience. Those speed limits will remain unchanged as a result of the introduction of GDL.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 33345/22-27, for an update on the relocation of Diabetic Eye Screening Clinics from the Ulster Hospital back to the Bangor and Newtownards area.
answer_text: I can confirm that Diabetic Eye Screening Programme (DESP) clinics have been relocated back to the Bangor and Newtownards area. The clinics now take place two days per week in Ards Community Hospital and two days per week in Bangor Hospital.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether any local council has ever used the revocation powers set out in the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, to cancel approval for a planning application.
answer_text: The decision of a council to make an Order under section 68 of the Planning Act (NI) 2011 (the Act) is initiated by the local council. To obtain records of any such Orders, I would advise that you raise this matter directly with the relevant council(s).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the rationale as to why drivers who pass their driving test on 30 September 2026, will be subject to a 45mph restriction for one year, whilst those that pass their test the following day, on the 1 October 2026, will have no such restriction.
answer_text: The introduction of GDL entails the change from the current testing system and post-test requirements to those required under GDL. Such a transition necessitates the drawing of a line beyond which the previous arrangements no longer apply. It is considered appropriate to provide these transitional arrangements to allow for adjustment to the new GDL scheme.   The Road Traffic (Amendment) Act 2016 (“the 2016 Act”) sets out the transitional arrangements that apply to learner drivers whose provisional licence entitlement to drive a category B vehicle commenced before the introduction of GDL.  The 2016 Act specifies a six-month transitional period and the arrangements that will apply to such learners.  Legislation necessarily has a defined date on which it becomes operational.  It is on commencement of the relevant GDL provisions within the 2016 Act that the transitional period will begin.  In practice, this transitional period will cover from 1 October 2026 to 31 March 2027.  At 1 October 2026, there will still be many provisional licence holders who had started to learn under the current system.  These provisional drivers will be at different stages – some will have just started to learn, some will have been actively learning for some months and some will be about to take a first or repeat practical test.  We need to take account of these types of existing provisional licence holders and allow them a reasonable amount of time to continue with that training and pass a practical test which, significantly, will not include driving at speeds above 45mph.   Should they pass a practical test before 31 March 2027, they will fall under the current testing arrangements and therefore be required to display the ‘amber’ R plate and remain restricted to a speed limit of 45mph for one year.The first opportunity, unless exempted from the Mandatory Minimum Learning Period, to take a practical test that will assess the training required under GDL, will be after the end of the transitional period, that is, 1 April 2027.  Critically, candidates that pass this test will have been trained and tested at speeds greater than 45mph and will be subject to GDL’s post-test arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department has any plans in place to allow a victim of financial coercive control to reset their credit score to what it was before the abusive relationship began.
answer_text: I fully recognise the devastating and long-term impact that financial coercive control can have on victims of domestic abuse. Economic abuse can leave individuals with coerced debt, damaged credit ratings and significant barriers to rebuilding their independence long after an abusive relationship has ended.Credit scoring, consumer credit regulation and the operation of Credit Reference Agencies are reserved matters and are the responsibility of the UK Government. I note that HM Treasury has recently committed to work with Credit Reference Agencies, lenders and the third sector to improve how coerced debt is reflected on victim-survivors’ credit files, with the aim of making it easier for them to access financial products in the future. I have also written to HM Treasury to raise this important issue.Financial abuse is recognised as a form of domestic abuse within the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021. Through the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy 2024–2031, I and my Department remain committed to strengthening responses to coercive and controlling behaviour, including financial abuse, and to supporting victims to recover safely and sustainably from the harm they have experienced.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any measures in place to financially assist a victim after leaving a domestic abuse relationship, where there has been financial coercive control.
answer_text: I fully recognise the significant difficulties that victims of domestic abuse can face when leaving abusive relationships, particularly in circumstances where financial or economic abuse has been a feature of that abuse. Financial control can severely limit a victim’s independence and ability to rebuild their life safely.The provision of direct financial assistance following financial coercive control is not a matter that falls within the remit of the Department of Justice.Financial support, including welfare benefits and emergency assistance is primarily provided by the Department for Communities. Individuals leaving abusive situations may be able to access this support, subject to eligibility and their personal circumstances. Staff in the Jobs and Benefits network can assist with claims, respond sensitively to vulnerable customers, including those affected by domestic abuse, and signpost to specialist services where appropriate.While my Department does not provide financial assistance directly, it plays an important role in guiding victims through the justice system, in supporting victims to engage with statutory organisations and services, and in improving the overall response to domestic abuse. This includes the implementation of the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021, the development of protections for victims, and the provision of funding for support services such as advocacy and victim support organisations.These services can assist victims in understanding their rights, navigating the criminal justice process and connecting with the appropriate agencies that can provide financial, housing and practical support.Victims may also access protective measures and seek to recover property through the courts. Victims may be eligible for compensation following a criminal trial or if civil proceedings are lodged before the court. The Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service operate an Exemption and Remission policy to support those who cannot afford to pay fees for court actions.Addressing domestic abuse requires a coordinated, cross-government approach. My Department continues to work with Executive colleagues and partner organisations to ensure that victims receive the protection and support they need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether someone who receives their provisional license before the 1 October 2026 and passes their driving test after the 1 October 2026, will be subject to the restrictions under the new Graduated Driver Licensing scheme.
answer_text: Transitional arrangements for learners whose provisional licence entitlement to drive a category B vehicle commenced before 1 October 2026 are set out in the Road Traffic (Amendment) Act 2016.  During the six-month transitional period set, in practice covering from 1 October 2026 to 31 March 2027, such provisional licence holders will be able to complete their training and take the driving test for which they have been prepared.  Should they pass a practical test before 31 March 2027, they will be required to display the current ‘amber’ R plate for one year and remain restricted to a speed limit of 45mph as is currently the case.  If those learners do not pass a practical test by the end of the transitional period, then from 1 April 2027 they will become subject to GDL requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department is planning any initiatives to mark the National Year of Reading 2026.
answer_text: I was pleased to speak at the Northern Ireland launch of the National Year of Reading 2026 and my Department will be supporting Libraries NI in its delivery of a programme of activities across the year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department is planning any initiatives to mark the National Year of Reading 2026.
answer_text: The National Year of Reading 2026 provides an opportunity for my department to reinforce the importance of strong reading cultures across all schools. I was pleased to speak at the Northern Ireland launch, highlighting the value of reading for pleasure and its role in raising standards.The initiative aligns closely with the Department’s TransformED programme and the Strong Foundations Literacy Framework, published in December 2025, which emphasises high-quality texts, regular reading routines, enjoyment of reading, and secure foundational skills.To support the National Year of Reading, the department has written to all schools to outline the opportunities and resources available through the UK-wide campaign led by the National Literacy Trust, and to encourage schools to consider how these can complement their literacy priorities.In addition, the department is providing a programme of literacy webinars throughout 2026, focusing on evidence-informed approaches to reading, including reading for pleasure. We will also continue to work with the National Literacy Trust to promote further opportunities for schools as the year progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any action his Department is taking to prevent lengthy delays in approvals for applications made to the Affordable Warmth Scheme.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for the Housing Executive as the administrator of the Affordable Warmth Scheme. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive who has advised me as follows:“The NIHE make every effort to minimise the time taken to process applications, however, it is only able to release approvals when sufficient budget is available and to the highest priority cases. Approvals are released in accordance with the monthly approval profile that is aligned to the budget available.  Due to funding constraints, approvals are prioritised in line with the matrix agreed with DfC, which prioritises those without an operational heating system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the average waiting time between approval of an Affordable Warmth Scheme application, and the granting of funding.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive who has advised as follows:“Applicant eligible - grant approval issued – 73 calendar daysApproval issued - to all documents returned/paid - 150 calendar daysApprovals may cover multiple energy efficiency measures, and these measures may not be completed concurrently.  Once the applicant receives the grant approval, they find their own contractor(s) and complete the work at their own pace. The grant has a validity period of 4 months however this is often extended at the request of the applicant.Instructions to issue final payment is dependent on all measures being completed and the applicant and / or their contractor(s) returning all necessary documentation.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of households in North Down that have availed of the Affordable Warmth Scheme, in each of the last two years.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought information from the Chief Executive, who has advised as follows:“The table below details the number of homes helped through the Affordable Warmth Scheme in the North Down Parliamentary Constituency in the last 2 financial years and Q1 -Q3 of 2025 -2026:North Down ConstituencyFinancial YearsApplication / Referrals(b) Received2023 – 20242024 - 20252025 – 2026 Q1 – Q38Approvals issued1068235Homes Helped (completions)1116259Table Supporting Notes 8Details for Q1, Q2 and Q3 of the current financial year (2025-26) have been provided only.  Information for Q4 (1 January – 31 March 2026) forms part of the quarterly and annual compendium publication of housing statistics that is scheduled for release in May 2026.”  These statistics can be found at:https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/topics/housing-statistics”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has any plans to put parent and child parking spaces on a legislative footing, to allow inappropriate use to be enforced.
answer_text: The management of the majority of publicly provided off-street carparks transferred from the Department to local Councils on 1 April 2015.  The provision of dedicated parking bays for specific purposes is therefore a matter for the local Council which operates the facility.The vast majority of remaining off-street parking places operated by the Department are “park and ride” or “park and share”, and there is no established need to provide parent and toddler parking bays in these carparks.The dimensions and markings used for on-street parking are prescribed in the Traffic Signs Regulations (TSR) (Northern Ireland) 1997. Within the TSR(NI) 1997 there is a range of sizes for both the width and length of parking bays. Traffic Signs Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1997.A degree of flexibility is available when providing on-street parking as the width and length of individual streets can vary, however, the Department must take account of the existing streetscape and other features present when providing on-street parking bays. The minimum sizes for parking bays that the Department currently applies align with those used in the UK.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans her Department has for the former rail bridge on the Green Road, Bangor, once its abandonment order has been secured.
answer_text: Once the Abandonment Order becomes operational, the Department intends to dispose of the small portion of land, currently within its ownership, in the immediate vicinity of the Bridge as the Superseded Road or Bridge is no longer required for road traffic purposes. This will be carried out in accordance with the procedures for the disposal of government owned land, at which point the new landowner will assume responsibility. Responsibility for the other sections of land in the immediate vicinity of the Bridge that are currently in private ownership will revert back to the original landowners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on efforts to secure a school crossing patrol at Rathmore Primary School, Bangor.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation of the School Crossing Patrol (SCP) Scheme. The EA has advised me that Rathmore Primary School, Bangor, meets the criteria for an SCP at two locations.  While an SCP has been secured for one of these locations, the EA has so far been unable to recruit for the second location, despite advertising the post eight times.The EA will continue to work with Rathmore Primary School to promote the vacancy.The recruitment process for the School Crossing Patrol (SCP) position at Grange Park Primary School, Bangor, has now closed. One applicant has been identified to move forward to the next phase of formal shortlisting and the arrangement of an interview.The EA is recruiting for the SCP vacancy at St Patrick's Primary School, Holywood. In the previous recruitment phase, one applicant was successful, completed preemployment checks and undertook training, but subsequently withdrew from the post. As a result, the vacancy will now be readvertised.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the rationale behind the lifting of the 45mph speed limit for newly qualified drivers in the Graduated Driver licensing scheme.
answer_text: The removal of the 45mph speed restriction for newly qualified drivers is established by the Road Traffic (Amendment) Act (NI) 2016.There is no evidence that speed restrictions on learners or newly qualified drivers improves road safety.  Between 2019 and 2023, the principal causation of fatal and serious injury collisions involving car drivers aged 17-23, who were responsible for the collision, was excessive speed for the conditions.Under the current system, learner and newly qualified drivers do not have the opportunity to gain the experience of driving at speeds greater that 45pmh until one year after passing their test.  To prepare novice drivers properly for the challenges of driving on their own, they need to experience higher driving speeds under supervision and be tested while driving safely at speeds higher than 45mph, assuming conditions allow.  This will better prepare them for driving alone at the same speed as other road users.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the introduction of residents' parking schemes.
answer_text: I have considered my officials’ recommendations in relation to residents’ parking schemes that will provide a streamlined approach for considering, assessing and prioritising requests for schemes going forward. On the 10th of March Imade a statement to the Assembly as to how future residents’ parking schemes will be assessed by my Department, with applications opening in April 2026.

answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation of the School Crossing Patrol (SCP) Scheme.
