question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps he is taking to strengthen coastal defences in North Down, against inclement weather and coastal erosion.
answer_text: Under the existing policy and legislative framework, my Department is not responsible for coastal erosion risk management and coastal defences in Northern Ireland.However, I agree that urgent action is needed to ensure areas, such as North Down, are resilient to the impacts of climate change. This is emphasised in the draft Climate Change Adaptation Programme that has been prepared by my Department and is currently being considered by Executive colleagues.  At present, Departments can only operate within the existing legislative framework and the deficiencies in the existing framework for managing coastal erosion were highlighted in the draft adaptation programme.The Coastal Forum, which I co-chair with the Minister for Infrastructure, has enabled good progress to be made on a range of coastal management issues. It is important that the Forum continues to operate until new policy and a legislative framework are in place that designates responsibility for coastal erosion risk management to a lead Department.  In the interim, through the PEACE PLUS programme, €9.6 M will be invested in coastal monitoring and adaptation planning.  This project being led by Ulster University will ensure that momentum is maintained and will provide opportunities for coastal communities to participate in climate change adaptation planning.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any urgent measures her Department is putting in place, to ensure that the damaged seawall in Donaghadee is repaired.
answer_text: Following a recent period of severe weather and tidal surges along the North Down coastline, a short section of wall under my Department’s ownership partially collapsed on Thursday 5th February. Officials closed an adjacent section of footway and nearby car parking in the interest of public safety until repairs could be assessed and undertaken. I am pleased to confirm that repair work commenced on 10 February and will take approximately one week to complete.While on site, officials will also inspect the remaining length of Department maintained sea wall and address any further areas of concern identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps the Coastal Forum is taking to strengthen coastal defenses, against inclement weather and coastal erosion.
answer_text: The Coastal Forum’s aim is to work in partnership to progress costal management issues within the existing policy and legislation framework.  Work to date has focused on gathering scientific data to create the baseline required to measure any future coastal changes Following the last meeting of the Coastal forum in November, the Costal Forum Working Group is currently in the process of making recommendations for a 2026-2032 work programme.  DfI’s current responsibilities in relation to costal management are limited to maintenance of 110 km of defences for coastal roads, 26 km of coastal sea defences, two tidal barriers and approximately 34 km of sea defences for the railway network.   Repairs are carried out as required.I have also been advised by Translink that the maintenance of sea defences around the railway network is carried out proactively on through annual inspection and repair programmes and supplemented by pre- and post-storm event inspections to maintain a resilient and high performing railway.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the timeline for the commencement of the new Local Development Plan, for each local council.
answer_text: All councils have commenced work on their new Local Development Plans (LDPs) to provide the spatial framework for development for their areas over a 15-year period. To date, nine out of the eleven councils have published development plan documents, six of which, have adopted Plan Strategies and are working towards their Local Policies Plan publication. Council timetables indicate that the remaining two councils are due to publish draft Plan Strategy documents this year. There is no legislative timeframe for councils to bring forward LDPs and no target has been set by my Department for the completion of plans by councils. Councils set and update their own timetables for the preparation of their plans, taking into consideration their own resourcing, evidence gathering processes and governance structures, and these are forwarded to my Department for agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 32555/22-27, to detail any assessments that have taken place, to determine whether a school in North Down, is or is not, included in the part-time 20mph sped limit scheme.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. We are acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, particularly around schools.My Department is currently taking forward a third tranche of part-time 20mph zones at 40 schools across the North which will bring the total delivered since 2020/21 to 273 schools https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/20mph-speed-limit-schools-tranche-3. This Tranche has predominantly focused on schools in rural areas where the national speed limit would normally apply.This initiative is a significant intervention that will help to ensure that children, parents and staff feel safer as they travel to and from school on a daily basis.My Department is also taking forward a review on a number of speed related matters, including 20mph speed limits which is currently out to consultation.  Further consideration on the use of permanent 20mph zones, including those at schools, will be taken on completion of the consultation. I would encourage you to contribute to this significant and important piece of work. Details can be found at Speed limit review launched by DfI | Department for Infrastructure.Concerns about driver behaviour, such as speeding, should be directed to the PSNI on their 101 number.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions he is taking to address the shortage of essential medicines. [R]
answer_text: There are over 14,000 medicines licensed in the UK, and the majority are in good supply and are available in sufficient quantities to meet patient needs. However, medicines are manufactured as part of a highly regulated, complex and global industry; therefore, medicine supply disruptions are not uncommon and can occur for a variety of reasons such as manufacturing issues or increases in demand. The Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) leads on the maintenance of medicine supply chains to the UK and works closely with the devolved administrations, including my Department, the UK medicines regulator and other operators within the supply chain. Through national and local medicine shortage management groups, my Department is updated on any anticipated upcoming supply disruptions, including essential medicines.If there is a medicine supply disruption, there are well established tools and processes that can be applied both locally and nationally to ensure patients can have access to the treatments they need. This includes providing healthcare professionals with advice and guidance on the actions they can take to ensure patients can access appropriate treatments. These communications include National Patient Safety Alerts, Medicine Supply Notifications and recommending healthcare professionals registering with the NHS Specialist Pharmacy Service (SPS) medicines supply tool. The SPS medicines supply tool provides up-to-date information on anticipated resupply dates, appropriate alternatives, clinical information and recommended actions to take based on the above advice.Medicines can also be placed on a list of medicines that cannot be exported from the UK or hoarded. This ensures all medicines within the UK can be made available to patients. Other tools and processes that can be utilised include the importation of medicines from other markets and Serious Shortage Protocols. All these actions can be considered and applied where appropriate to anticipated supply disruptions.While there are well established tools and processes that can be applied, I recognise the impact these supply disruptions can have on patients and on Health and Social Care providers in Northern Ireland. Consequently, I had previously written to Wes Streeting MP, Secretary of State for Health and Social Care, and Karin Smyth MP, Minister of Health (Secondary Care), highlighting my support for wider collaboration to improve long-term supply resilience across the UK.DHSC published a document in August 2025, entitled ‘Managing a robust and resilient supply of medicines’ which aims to reduce the frequency of shortages and minimise the impact on patients when they do occur. The document provides examples of collaboration between my Department, the UK Government and others to resolve some recent complex medicine supply disruptions. I welcome the aims of this publication and my Department is committed to working with our partners across the UK to find strategies to improve the security

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she has any plans to ban scrambler bikes in public places.
answer_text: Scramblers are designed primarily for off road use only. If they are to be used on a road then they must comply with the same rules as for all other motorbikes i.e. the correct driving license category for the size of the engine, insurance, MOT and must be taxed and registered. Anyone using a scrambler bike illegally on or off road may be subject to enforcement action by the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, given the financial losses faced by Northern Ireland’s seed potato growers due to virus-related downgrades, to outline any urgent steps his Department is taking to ensure that allotments are not becoming an unregulated source of disease pressure that would undermine one of our most important agricultural sectors.
answer_text: There has been a marked increase in the level of virus affecting potato crops in recent years resulting in the downgrading of classification of some seed crops increasing, and in some cases rejections. This is in common with the rest of the UK and the EU where the same trend has been seen. Warm summers, high aphid numbers and less effective aphicides have all contributed to this issue.Northern Ireland is recognised as a high-grade potato seed production area and my Department regulates the potato industry through a number of measures to reduce incursion or spread of potato diseases, viruses and pests. The marketing of seed potatoes is controlled through the Seed Potato Classification Scheme which involves growing crop inspection followed by tuber inspection and certification for marketing for Northern Ireland commercial seed growers to supply large scale potato producers and retailers of seed potatoes, including garden centres. My inspectors also undertake routine checks of retail outlets to ensure that seed potatoes meet requirements for retail. The regulatory system for seed potatoes is both robust and important, to maintain our high standards, reputation and export markets for the valuable Northern Ireland seed potato industry. I would emphasise that the protections and regulations in place are to protect the whole sector. Provided private gardeners and allotment holders buy seed annually from legitimate outlets, as advised on the DAERA website, there is very little risk to commercial crops in Northern Ireland from virus spread originating from private growers. Import controls apply to seed and ware potatoes entering Northern Ireland under the Windsor Framework and are currently under the direction and control of the Secretary of State.  Those controls mean that seed potatoes can only be imported to Northern Ireland under the NIPHL scheme to professional operators who grow and market seed potatoes, and cannot be supplied to private individuals or moved out of Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she has any plans to change or review the current age restrictions to drive a tractor on a public road.
answer_text: Part II of the Motor Vehicles (Driving Licences) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1996, outlines the regulations for the granting of driving licences here and in Regulation 6 it specifies the minimum ages for holding and obtaining licences, including those for driving a tractor on public roads. I have no plans to change or review the current age restrictions to drive a tractor on a public road.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the rationale of the passenger restrictions in the Graduated Driver licensing scheme.
answer_text: The Graduated Driver Licensing (GDL) passenger restrictions and protections are established by the Road Traffic (Amendment) Act (NI) 2016.  International research consistently shows that passenger restrictions are one of the most effective components of GDL in reducing crash risk among novice drivers. Limiting passengers, especially other teens, significantly lowers crash and injury rates. Various international studies report that restricting novice drivers to zero or one young passenger notably decreases crash risk (eg, Fell et al 2012, Masten et al, 2013; McCartt et al, 2010; Zhu et al, 2016). Overall, the global evidence base indicates that passenger restrictions are a critical protective factor within GDL frameworks, helping new drivers develop skills under safer, lower risk environments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any evidence base that shows the Graduated Driver Licensing scheme reduces road deaths.
answer_text: The Road Traffic (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 (“the 2016 Act”) established that the introduction of Graduated Driver Licensing (“GDL”) here is the settled will of the Assembly. The Act contains the framework and detailed measures on how GDL is to be implemented. Statistics continue to show an over representation of new drivers (those with less than two years’ experience since passing their test and starting to drive unaccompanied) are involved in fatal and serious road collisions. New drivers may be of any age, but the majority will be aged 17-23. The most recent GDL Monitoring Report (March 2025, published by my Department’s Analysis, Statistics and Research Branch / NISRA) included the following for the five years 2019-2023 (compared with a 2012-2016 baseline):•	although 17-23 year olds accounted for only 8% of all car driving licence holders they were deemed responsible for 22% of all fatal or serious (KSI) collisions, and 20% of all collisions, where a driver was deemed responsible.•	there were 208 car passengers aged 14-20 killed or seriously injured. The majority of these young passengers (154, or 74%) were injured while travelling with a driver aged 17-23.A key piece of the evidence that supports the introduction on GDL can be found in a report commissioned by my Department to assess the expected impact of the introduction of the proposed GDL system here on road casualties. The report, ‘The Potential Impact of Graduated Driver Licensing in Northern Ireland’ was produced by TRL in 2019/20 for DVA in 2019/20 and can be read in full at: https://www.trl.co.uk/uploads/trl/documents/PPR934---The-potential-impact-of-Graduated-Driver-Licensing-in-Northern-Ireland.pdf The report found that international evaluations showed that introducing GDL can reduce collisions.  However, effectiveness varies widely across jurisdictions ranging between 5% to 40%, depending on factors such as the baseline licensing system and the strength of GDL measures. TRL’s review found that GDL is effective for the age population it targets and is not limited to the youngest learner drivers (aged 16 in some jurisdictions). In jurisdictions where GDL is applied to older ‘young drivers’ or all new drivers, reductions in novice driver collisions are reported for all age groups.  These findings are consistent with the large body of work showing that all new drivers, regardless of age, are at increased crash risk until they gain necessary on-road experience.The report considered the greatest potential impact of introducing GDL in Northern Ireland to lie in the core learner and probationary components, particularly night-time and passenger restrictions. It concluded that GDL offers substantial potential to improve road safety in Northern Ireland and that strong probationary restrictions, supported by complementary measures, could deliver casualty reductions in line with international best practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the rationale for the removal of the Cadastral layer from the Spatial NI dataset.
answer_text: A decision was made in October 2025 by Land & Property Services (LPS), the lead public authority for implementing the INSPIRE Regulations here to retire the Spatial NI for INSPIRE platform for several reasons, including:Uncertainty following Brexit pending further direction from the Department for Environment Food and Rural Affairs (Defra) and the Westminster Government on future INSPIRE policy and infrastructure.Core software components were nearing supplier endoflife.Very low platform usage, meaning the data was rarely accessed, further reinforcing the need to pause any further investment until the future direction of INSPIRE is confirmed. This decision was communicated to all stakeholders on 8 December 2025.During this interim period, LPS will continue to supply the INSPIRE cadastral parcel dataset to any public body holding a valid INSPIRE licence. Requests should be sent to inspire.spatialni@finance-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether it is proposed that the full one-year 45mph restricted drive limit would still apply to those that pass their driving test, before the introduction of the Graduated Driver Licensing scheme in October 2026.
answer_text: When GDL is introduced on 1st October 2026, those drivers who have passed a category B test prior to that date will fall under the current (amber) R plate scheme, including being subject to a 45mph speed limit for 12 months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of individuals who are residing in long-stay inpatient psychiatric units, having been assessed as being ready for discharge into supported living accommodation, in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Regionally, psychiatric units are not identified as a long stay provision, but unfortunately some patients who are medically optimised are delayed discharges due to various factors.BHSCT - Learning Disability services in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, Muckamore Abbey Hospital have 7 delayed discharges with main obstacle being having properties and staff aligned for supported living care.Across acute inpatients there are fewer than 5 patients who are considered medically optimised for discharge and their discharge is delayed.NHSCT - The Northern Health and Social Care Trust has 5 (mental health) delayed discharge into supported living units.SHSCT - currently has 0 mental health inpatients whose discharge is delayed and who are assessed to require supported living accommodationWHSCT - 1 patient has been assessed as suitable for supported living, Within Adult Learning Disability in the Northern Sector there is 1 delayed discharge where the discharge date impacted due to bespoke adaptations required to his home.In the Southern Sector 6 service users are delayed discharge with 2 of those planned for discharge before 4th March 2026. SEHSCT – of the delayed discharges there are 4 people who are awaiting a placement in either supported living or a nursing home. Additionally, 3 people with a learning disability in inpatient psychiatric hospitals who have been assessed as being fit for discharge and also assessed as requiring supported living services.With regards to dementia inpatient beds, all 5 Trusts have advised there are no patients delayed awaiting supported living accommodation

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any evidence base that shows the Graduated Driver Licensing scheme has worked successfully in other justifications.
answer_text: The Road Traffic (Amendment) Act (NI) 2016 (“the 2016 Act”) established that the introduction of Graduated Driver Licensing (“GDL”) here is the settled will of the Assembly. The Act contains the framework and detailed measures on how GDL is to be implemented. A key piece of the evidence that supports the introduction on GDL can be found in a report commissioned by my Department to assess the expected impact of the introduction of the proposed GDL system here on road casualties. The report, ‘The Potential Impact of Graduated Driver Licensing in Northern Ireland’ was produced by TRL in 2019/20 for DVA in 2019/20 and can be read in full at: https://www.trl.co.uk/uploads/trl/documents/PPR934---The-potential-impact-of-Graduated-Driver-Licensing-in-Northern-Ireland.pdf The report found that international evaluations showed that introducing GDL can reduce collisions.  However, effectiveness varies widely across jurisdictions ranging between 5% to 40%, depending on factors such as the baseline licensing system and the strength of GDL measures. TRL’s review found that GDL is effective for the age population it targets and is not limited to the youngest learner drivers (aged 16 in some jurisdictions). In jurisdictions where GDL is applied to older ‘young drivers’ or all new drivers, reductions in novice driver collisions are reported for all age groups.  These findings are consistent with the large body of work showing that all new drivers, regardless of age, are at increased crash risk until they gain necessary on-road experience.The report considered the greatest potential impact of introducing GDL in the North to lie in the core learner and probationary components, particularly night-time and passenger restrictions. It concluded that GDL offers substantial potential to improve road safety in the North and that strong probationary restrictions, supported by complementary measures, could deliver casualty reductions in line with international best practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail a breakdown of the additional £30 million, allocated to roads via the December Monitoring, broken down by DFI Roads (i) Division; and (ii) Section Office.
answer_text: The table below provides a breakdown, by Roads Division, of the additional £30m allocated to roads maintenance, as a result of the December monitoring round. Budget and expenditure for road maintenance is recorded on a divisional basis rather than by section office.  East£mNorth£mSouth£mWest£mTotal£m5810730

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, regarding the proposed Graduated Driver Licensing scheme, to detail the rationale as to why the booking of a driving test is based on a six-month wait from the issue of the provisional licence, rather than once the learner driver has completed the required 14 modules of the training programme.
answer_text: The Minimum Mandatory Learning Period (MMLP) of 6 months between the provisional licence date of entitlement to drive a category B vehicle and the earliest date of taking the practical driving test is established by the Road Traffic (Amendment) Act (NI) 2016.  It is provided to give learner drivers a learning period in which they can take a steady, progressive, approach to completing the new Programme of Training.  Each learner will learn at their own pace, but the MMLP coupled with the Programme of Training provides a minimum timeframe that is considered  sufficient to facilitate the absorption of the skills and behaviours the training programme is designed to instil.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any evidence base used to support her answer to the Urgent Oral Question in the Assembly on 27 January 2026, that the Graduated Driver licensing scheme could potentially lower car insurance for newly qualified young drivers.
answer_text: Evidence published by the Association of British Insurers demonstrates a clear relationship between driver age, collision risk and the cost of claims, with younger drivers more likely to be involved in collisions and therefore facing higher average premiums.Additional evidence from international jurisdictions, such as New Zealand, Canada and several Australian states, indicates that Graduated Driver Licensing (GDL) models can lead to meaningful and sustained reductions in collisions involving this age group. This evidence is drawn from international research and evaluations reviewed by the Centre for Automotive Safety Research (University of Adelaide) and the Transport Research Laboratory (TRL), which analysed crash outcomes and licensing reforms across highperforming road safety jurisdictions. Should similar benefits be realised here, that reduced risk profile could potentially be reflected in lower insurance premiums for newly qualified young drivers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, as part of the restrictions introduced under the Graduated Driver Licensing scheme, whether engine size has been considered as a measure.
answer_text: In advance of the introduction to the Assembly of the Road Traffic (Amendment) Bill, a 2011 consultation exercise on Proposed Changes to the Learner and Restricted Driver Schemes and on Graduated Driver Licensing did consider putting restrictions on high powered vehicles.  There was not, however, any clear evidence that vehicle power or vehicle power to weight ratio restrictions for new drivers would lead to fewer young driver fatalities and casualties. The view was then taken that such a measure would not be taken forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, as part of the restrictions introduced under the Graduated Driver Licensing scheme, whether a requirement for a black box has been considered as a measure.
answer_text: The Department considered the use of black box technologies, separately from GDL, in 2013. There was, however, insufficient evidence to assess the impacts of telematics and its potential effectiveness in changing driver behaviour. The use of black box technologies was not, therefore, considered as a measure for inclusion in the Road Traffic (Amendment) Bill as introduced in the Assembly in May 2014.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the length of the road network, broken down by road classification, for each DfI Roads section office.
answer_text: Information on the Highway Network is published on the following Open Data NI page:https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/@transportni/highway-network.The data can be downloaded and filtered using the ADOPTION_STATUS_NAME column to show current adopted road sections and provides the digital length. This is a 'live' link and is updated each evening to include any new road sections.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in relation to the September 2024 Civil Service Administrative Officer recruitment drive, whether there are any plans to fill more vacancies,in the next six months.
answer_text: A further round of allocations from the September 2024 Civil Service Administrative Officer recruitment competition is currently being taken forward with the intention of placing the majority of successful candidates remaining on the merit list within the next six months.  This is subject to the preferences which candidates selected on their application forms (e.g. working pattern, location).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the rationale for the requirement of a six-month wait between the issue of the provisional driving license and a driving test, under the Graduated Driver Licensing scheme, rather than a specific number of lessons or hours of driving.
answer_text: The Mandatory Minimum Learning Period (MMLP) of 6 months between the provisional licence date of entitlement to drive a category B vehicle and the earliest date of taking the practical driving test is established by the Road Traffic (Amendment) Act (NI) 2016.  The MMLP period allows for differences in learning ability and pace and gives the learner time to take lessons and practice the skills and behaviours the training programme is designed to embed.  This ensures a steady, progressive, approach to completing the new Programme of Training and provides the opportunity to learn in a range of driving conditions including different weather and light conditions and across more than one Season.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in relation to the September 2024 Civil Service Administrative Officer recruitment drive, to detail the number of vacancies filled, in the North Down local council area.
answer_text: My Department records vacancies by city or town rather than council area. Bangor and Newtownards are part of the Ards and North Down Borough Council area. As at the end of January 2026, a total of 1 vacancy had been filled in Bangor, and 2 vacancies had been filled in Newtownards from the September 2024 Civil Service Administrative Officer recruitment competition.As at the end of January 2026, NICSHR was managing a caseload of 8 vacancies in Bangor and 6 vacancies in Newtownards which may be filled from the September 2024 Civil Service Administrative Officer recruitment competition. A further round of allocations from this recruitment competition is currently being taken forward.When a department identifies a vacant post that needs filled, the request is referred to NICSHR to initiate the process to fill the post. NICSHR liaises with all departments on an ongoing basis to seek to prioritise resourcing activity within allocated financial and staff resources. Therefore, details of the total number of vacant posts in each department is not held centrally by NICSHR or the wider Department of Finance. Each Civil Service department is responsible for managing its resources – both financial and staff. Each department determines its individual workforce needs and is responsible for ensuring that any additional staffing resources are affordable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to individuals residing in long-stay inpatient psychiatric units that have been assessed as being ready for discharge into supported living accommodation, to outline any barriers that prevent this transition.
answer_text: Following the Bamford Review of Mental Health and Learning Disability Services (2007), Northern Ireland moved from long stay psychiatric units to community-based provision, including supported living for those with ongoing psychiatric care and support needs. Regionally, psychiatric units are not identified as a long stay provision, but unfortunately some patients that are medically optimised are delayed discharges due to various factors.Both statutory and non-statutory services are experiencing high demand on services and more complex presentations, compounded by adverse experiences, substance misuse and lack of support networks.For patients assessed as requiring discharge to supported living placements; the main barriers are the lack of available placements; need for specialist or bespoke placements; and staff having the specific skill set to provide the balance of care and support individuals requires.An additional barrier is service providers refusing to accept an applicant based on forensic or substance misuse issues or the residency status of an individual. Supported living placements predominately have a tenancy for life contract, therefore an individual placed in supported living accommodation, has the right to remain there for life. The result is minimal flow through this sector with vacancies arising infrequently and demand being persistently high.Supported living placements represent a cross Departmental issue with Supporting People money and housing stock resting with The Northern Ireland Housing Executive under The Department of Communities. Placements are identified and supported in collaboration with HSC Trusts, NIHE and community and voluntary sector partners, including Housing Associations and care providers.My officials engage in interface and planning meetings with partner agencies and ensure that demand and occupancy are kept under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she plans to carry out a review of driving instructor regulations.
answer_text: The regulatory framework for driving instructors here is set out in the Motor Vehicles (Driving Instruction) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2010.  These, together with the Trainee Licence Regulations, provide the basis for the regulation of Approved Driving Instructors (ADIs).  I have no plans to review the Regulations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the Driving Instructors Association was consulted, before her announcement on 27 January 2026, regarding the introduction of a Graduated Driving Licensing scheme.
answer_text: During the development of the Graduated Driver Licensing (GDL) proposals, my Department was in regular contact with the Northern Ireland Approved Instructors Council (NIAIC) as an individual organisation and as part of a wider GDL Stakeholder Group.  Their input helped shape the GDL provisions within the Road Traffic (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.Officials continued to liaise with NIAIC on the 2017 consultation on the consequential changes to the practical driving test.  Since my announcement that GDL will be implemented from 1 October 2026, the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has arranged a meeting with NIAIC for 12 February 2026.  Monthly meetings have also been scheduled with NIAIC to the end of September 2026 to update them on the practicalities of GDL.  To further ensure broad and representative engagement across the approved instructor industry, regional information workshops will also be held in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) number of actionable road defects; and (ii) budget allocated, in each local council area, over the last five years.
answer_text: My Department collects and stores data in a manner which mirrors the boundaries of the district council areas. Road surface defects, including potholes, are managed through the Roads Maintenance Client Management System which records the total number of defects and the number outstanding for each local maintenance office area.The Department undertakes routine safety inspections that include the investigation of customer enquiries. Repair work is only carried out subject to a defect meeting our current intervention levels outlined in DEM 160/21 Road Maintenance Standards for Safety – Limited Service.  All surface defect data is published on the Open Data NI website from 2008 to date, and can be accessed at https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/@transportni/surface-defectsSurface defects are categorised using a range of options, which include:  pothole, rutted, depressed, cracked, crazed uneven, edge deterioration etc. A priority setting is based on location, severity and risk to the public.The data can be sorted to show if it has been instructed for repair and is completed by filtering the DEFECT_STATUS column to equal ‘Completed’. question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the accountability and governance arrangements in place for the Planning Appeals Commission, for which her Department is the sponsoring body.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any capital development plans for the South Eastern Regional College campus, Bangor.
answer_text: I am advised by South Eastern Regional College that it currently has no capital development plans for its campus in Bangor.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action she is taking to tackle the shortage of driving instructors.
answer_text: The Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) is responsible for the regulation of driving instructors, which includes assessing the suitability of applicants, checking tuition standards, and taking appropriate action when instructors fail to meet the required standards. The DVA do not employ driving instructors, nor do they have responsibility for managing, monitoring, or addressing workforce levels within the private driving instruction sector. The supply of Approved Driving Instructors is a matter for the independent industry and is shaped by market demand rather than by any statutory function of the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, following NI Reval2026, whether his Department has carried out any assessment on the viability of the hospitality sector, given the proposed increase in business rates liability in 2026.
answer_text: As set out in my statement to the Assembly on 2 February 2026, I have stopped Reval2026 in response to the concerns raised by businesses, including those in the hospitality sector, about the implications of proceeding with the draft valuations at this time. My full statement is available at:Finance Minister Oral Statement - Reval 2026 | Department of Finance

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any involvement that driving instructors had with the planning and development of the Graduated Driver Licensing scheme.
answer_text: During the development of the Graduated Driver Licensing (GDL) proposals, my Department was in regular contact with the Northern Ireland Approved Instructors Council (NIAIC) as an individual organisation and as part of a wider GDL Stakeholder Group.  Their input helped shape the GDL provisions within the Road Traffic (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.Officials continued to liaise with NIAIC on the 2017 consultation on the consequential changes to the practical driving test.  Since my announcement that GDL will be implemented from 1 October 2026, the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has arranged a meeting with NIAIC for 12 February 2026.  Monthly meetings have also been scheduled with NIAIC to the end of September 2026 to update them on the practicalities of GDL.  To further ensure broad and representative engagement across the approved instructor industry, regional information workshops will also be held in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail on what date did her Department first raise with the BBC that the announcement on Graduated Driver Licensing scheme could be made on the BBC’s day of road safety coverage.
To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any support he plans to give to our hospitality sector, due to rising business rates following NI Reval2026.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the legislative process required to introduce the Graduated Driver Licensing scheme by October.
answer_text: The Road Traffic (Amendment) Act (NI) 2016 (the 2016 Act) sets out the statutory framework for Graduated Driver Licensing (GDL) and includes details of the key elements of the scheme, including the six-month mandatory minimum learning period, removal of the 45mph speed limit, the requirement for a Programme of Training and Logbook and passenger restrictions.  To facilitate the introduction of the GDL measures in the 2016 Act, I will be bringing forward a suite of subordinate legislation. This will consist of a Commencement Order to commence the GDL provisions in the 2016 Act and three Regulations to make the necessary amendments to the relevant Driving Licence, Motorways Traffic and Specified Restrictions Regulations. In terms of Assembly procedure, the Driving Licence and Motorways Traffic amendment Regulations are subject to negative procedure and the Specified Restrictions amending Regulations are subject to the draft affirmative procedure. The Commencement Order is not subject to any Assembly procedure. The required SL1s and draft Regulations will shortly be sent to the Infrastructure Committee for scrutiny.These interdependent pieces of subordinate legislation must all be made with an operational date of 1st October 2026 if GDL is to be introduced on that date. To provide that certainty for the public, Approved Driving and Motorcycle Instructors, driving schools and importantly, for those who will be starting to learn how to drive, I am aiming to have the subordinate legislation in place before summer recess.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to 37730/22-27, to detail any engagement her Department has had with users of the Metro 3 corridor service on Holywood Road, regarding any changes to the service, following construction of the Tillysburn park and ride facility,
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised that Translink will re-assess the Metro service to coincide with the construction of the Park and Ride facility at Tillysburn Park, when that commences.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to 38248/22-27, to detail when she will address this matter.
answer_text: The necessary amendment to the Rehabilitation of Offenders (Exceptions) Order (Northern Ireland) 1979 will come into effect on 17th February 2026 and will create eligibility for self-employed / personal employees working in Regulated Activity to apply for an AccessNI Enhanced check.In preparation for this change, AccessNI will be issuing guidance to Registered Bodies and updating the relevant pages on the nidirect website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she plans to introduce a statutory code of practice for private parking operators, to ensure fairness, transparency, and protections for motorists.
answer_text: Whilst my Department is responsible for on-street parking enforcement, it has no responsibility for the regulation of private car parks. However, any organisation that operates parking on private land and requests keeper details from the DVLA must be approved by an Accredited Trade Association such as the Approved Operator Scheme (AOS) managed by the British Parking Association (BPA). Members of this scheme must adhere to the BPA AOS Code of Practice for the control and enforcement of parking on private land and unregulated public car parks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions he is taking to improve waiting times for gynaecological (i) consultant appointments; and (ii) surgery.
answer_text: There is currently significant work underway across the health and care system to reduce waiting times for both consultant appointments and surgery and develop pathways for gynaecology-specific conditions within each Trust across Northern Ireland.To support this, the Department has established a Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum, which includes representation from Trusts, the Public Health Agency, and General Practitioners.  This forum is overseeing ongoing service improvements and the development of gynaecology service pathways across primary, intermediate, secondary, and tertiary care levels which are clinically led.  It is also responsible for implementing the recommendations of the “Getting It Right First Time” (GIRFT) Gynaecology Report.Most referral pathways begin in primary care, and the gynaecology pathways being developed reflect this.  If a GP is unable to assess or treat a patient locally, the patient may be referred to the Gynaecology Enhanced Primary Care Service—if they meet the criteria—or to secondary care gynaecology services.Primary and Secondary Care teams are working collaboratively to ensure patients are seen in the right place, at the right time, by the right professional.  In addition to accepting referrals from primary care, a pilot initiative is underway to identify patients on secondary care waiting lists who could be managed within the Enhanced Primary Care Service.  Eligible patients will be assessed and treated by one of the 16 specialist GPs with enhanced skills in gynaecology.  This service currently manages referrals for pessary fitting, HRT management, initial assessment and treatment of heavy menstrual bleeding, and contraceptive management including coil fitting.  As of 30 September 2025, the Enhanced Primary Care Service has delivered 1,567 face-to-face appointments and 587 remote consultations at a cost in-year of £1.07m.  When fully operational, it is expected to manage approximately 6,072 patient contacts annually (including both face-to-face and remote appointments).Secondary Care services are also developing action plans to address waiting times and implement relevant GIRFT recommendations within each Trust.  Between April and December 2025, additional non-recurrent funding has been approved of £3.9m to provide waiting list initiatives—both in-house and via the independent sector to address red flag or other time-critical assessments and treatments.  As well as Trusts carrying out administrative validation exercises, to review and validate current outpatient waiting lists, clinicians are also carrying out enhanced clinical validation of outpatient lists.  Additional outpatient clinics are also in place to provide one stop clinics for colposcopy, hysteroscopy, smears and pessaries that cannot be provided in primary care.   From October 2025, this has resulted in a 52% reduction of gynaecology patients waiting for an outpatient appointment over 3 years and 58% of gynaecology inpatient and daycase patie

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the current legislation and regulations in relation to the parking of (i) occupied; and (ii) unoccupied caravans and motorhomes, on public roads overnight.
answer_text: Article 91 of The Roads (NI) Order 1993 (1993 No. 3160 (N.I. 15) provides that any person who without reasonable excuse encamps or maintains an encampment on a road including with a caravan shall be guilty of an offence. Regulation 27 of the Road Vehicles Lighting Regulations (NI) 2000 (SR 200 No.169), sets out requirements regarding the use of front and rear position lamps, rear registration plate lamps, side marker lamps and end-outline marker lamps which apply to vehicles parking on roads during the hours between sunset and sunrise.  This would also apply to caravans.Regulation 119 of the Motor Vehicles (Construction and Use) Regulations (NI) 1999 (SR 1999 No.454), concerns the unnecessary obstruction of a road by a motor vehicle or a trailer.Caravans attached to vehicles and/or displaying a Vehicle Registration Mark (VRM) and motorhomes are classed as motor vehicles and subject to any parking restrictions that may be in force. Where parking restrictions do not apply, they are subject to the same provisions as other motor vehicles and should not be parked so as to cause a danger or obstruction. Article 32 of the Road Traffic (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 refers. The PSNI are responsible for investigating offences under Article 32.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting time for gynaecological consultant appointments, in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting time for gynaecological surgery, in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The level of detail asked is not held and whilst validated inpatient data files may be broken down by specialty, Gynaecological Surgery is not identified as a specialty.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any additional resources he plans to allocate to gynaecology services to address waiting times.
answer_text: There is currently significant work underway across the health and care system to reduce waiting times for both consultant appointments and surgery and develop pathways for gynaecology-specific conditions within each Trust across Northern Ireland.To support this, the Department has established a Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum, which includes representation from Trusts, the Public Health Agency, and General Practitioners.  This forum is overseeing ongoing service improvements and the development of gynaecology service pathways across primary, intermediate, secondary, and tertiary care levels which are clinically led.  It is also responsible for implementing the recommendations of the “Getting It Right First Time” (GIRFT) Gynaecology Report.Most referral pathways begin in primary care, and the gynaecology pathways being developed reflect this.  If a GP is unable to assess or treat a patient locally, the patient may be referred to the Gynaecology Enhanced Primary Care Service—if they meet the criteria—or to secondary care gynaecology services.Primary and Secondary Care teams are working collaboratively to ensure patients are seen in the right place, at the right time, by the right professional.  In addition to accepting referrals from primary care, a pilot initiative is underway to identify patients on secondary care waiting lists who could be managed within the Enhanced Primary Care Service.  Eligible patients will be assessed and treated by one of the 16 specialist GPs with enhanced skills in gynaecology.  This service currently manages referrals for pessary fitting, HRT management, initial assessment and treatment of heavy menstrual bleeding, and contraceptive management including coil fitting.  As of 30 September 2025, the Enhanced Primary Care Service has delivered 1,567 face-to-face appointments and 587 remote consultations at a cost in-year of £1.07m.  When fully operational, it is expected to manage approximately 6,072 patient contacts annually (including both face-to-face and remote appointments).Secondary Care services are also developing action plans to address waiting times and implement relevant GIRFT recommendations within each Trust.  Between April and December 2025, additional non-recurrent funding has been approved of £3.9m to provide waiting list initiatives—both in-house and via the independent sector to address red flag or other time-critical assessments and treatments.  As well as Trusts carrying out administrative validation exercises, to review and validate current outpatient waiting lists, clinicians are also carrying out enhanced clinical validation of outpatient lists.  Additional outpatients are also in place to provide one stop clinics for colposcopy, hysteroscopy, smears and pessaries that cannot be provided in primary care.   From October 2025, this has resulted in a 52% reduction of gynaecology patients waiting for an outpatient appointment over 3 years and 58% of gynaecology inpatient and daycase patients wai

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether any assessment has been made as to whether the actions she is taking as Minister for the Economy are having a negative impact on jobs and business investment in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: As Minister for the Economy, I continue to drive forward a range of actions in line with my economic vision, focused on good jobs, improving productivity, promoting regional balance and decarbonising our economy.Our investment offering remains strong and Invest NI data show that the number of investments in 2024/25 exceeded that of 2023/24, while the latest figures from NISRA show that the number of private sector employee jobs here increased over the year to September 2025, and that our economic output is growing strongly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any action being taken to ensure that pupils learn about the Holocaust in schools.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Curriculum is designed to be flexible, enabling schools to select the most appropriate contexts and topics for their pupils. While teaching about The Holocaust is not a statutory requirement, there are currently opportunities for pupils to learn about it across the curriculum.At Key Stage 2, The Holocaust and related themes can be explored within ‘The World Around Us’ and ‘Personal Development and Mutual Understanding’ areas of learning.At Key Stage 3, pupils can study The Holocaust through History, where there is a statutory requirement to examine significant events of the 20th century, as well as through ‘Learning for Life and Work’ and ‘Environment and Society’ areas of learning.As part of the wider work on curriculum reform, I have established the Northern Ireland Curriculum Taskforce to design a new statutory curriculum framework. Subject-specific working groups for History, and Learning for Life and Work are considering how these areas will be developed within the new statutory curriculum, including how sensitive and important historical topics can be effectively supported. This work is progressing, and there will be public consultation in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any work being untaken to reduce litter on Northern Ireland Environment Agency-owned beaches, in North Down.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) do not own any beaches in the North Down area. However, Crawfordsburn Country Park, which takes in Helen’s Bay/Crawfordsburn beach, is managed by the NIEA, site staff regularly remove litter from beach areas of the site.  Crawfordsburn Country Park also has over fifty bins across the site which are serviced multiple times a day, and there is extensive litter signage in place.  Responsibility for the enforcement of littering offences rests with the Ards and North Down Borough Council. However, officials maintain communication with councils and other partner agencies where incidents of concern are identified. The Environmental Crime Unit (ECU) provides support to local councils through the operation of the Fly-tipping protocol. Under this protocol NIEA assists local councils by dealing with incidents of illegal waste dumping above 20m3 and any incidents involving hazardous waste that local councils do not have the facilities to manage.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether any discussions have taken place with Invest NI, as to whether the actions she is taking as Minister for the Economy are having a negative impact on jobs and business investment in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: As Minister for the Economy, I continue to drive forward a range of actions in line with my economic vision, focused on good jobs, improving productivity, promoting regional balance and decarbonising our economy. Invest NI continues to work closely with me and my Department to deliver this vision.Our investment offering remains strong and Invest NI data show that the number of investments in 2024/25 exceeded that of 2023/24, while the latest figures from NISRA show that the number of private sector employee jobs here increased over the year to September 2025, and that our economic output is growing strongly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any action being taken to ensure that pupils learn how to manage their private finances.
answer_text: Financial capability, including money management and saving, is a statutory and integral part of the Northern Ireland Curriculum. It is embedded across all key stages and delivered both through classroom teaching and through pupils’ everyday experiences inside and outside school. This provides pupils with opportunities to develop practical skills in planning, managing and using financial resources, as well as supporting their independence and confidence in managing their personal finances.Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) provides support and guidance to schools in this area and has worked closely with partner organisations to enhance the quality of financial education. CCEA has developed strong working relationships with Young Enterprise Northern Ireland (YENI) and the Money and Pensions Service (MaPS) to promote financial capability and co-create resources for classroom use.A notable example is the ‘Your Money Matters’ textbook and accompanying teacher guide, produced by YENI and co-funded by Martin Lewis OBE and MaPS. This resource has been made available to every post-primary school and is designed for pupils aged 14–16. It covers a comprehensive range of practical financial topics, including spending and saving, borrowing, debt, insurance, student finance and future planning, helping pupils develop the essential knowledge and skills needed to manage their private finances effectively.Through these combined actions—statutory curriculum provision, professional guidance, and high-quality learning resources—the Department continues to ensure that pupils are supported to develop the knowledge and skills required to manage their private finances effectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she plans to undertake an infrastructure cost review, similar to that undertaken in GB, to review how major infrastructure projects were specified and procured.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the inflationary pressures that continue to drive significant cost increases across the construction sector in both Britain and the South, with major schemes experiencing rising tender prices and significant annual uplifts. These pressures are widening funding gaps and can impact on project viability, timelines and delivery models, as seen across multiple strategic infrastructure schemes.In the North, the adoption of annual budgeting practices and legal challenges have hindered my department’s ability to deliver and sustain a pipeline of major infrastructure projects over the past five years. Consequently, this has resulted in a lack of substantive data regarding sector inflation trends.The Investment Strategy for Northern Ireland (ISNI) 2050 includes an Enabling Action Plan designed to address longstanding issues in infrastructure delivery.  The plan identifies key risks to infrastructure delivery and groups 12 enabling actions under three themes: People, Process and Policy.Major projects in the North are developed through formal business cases and contract documentation prepared in line with Public Procurement Policy, through Centres of Procurement Expertise to ensure best value for money.  As such there are no plans within the Department to undertake an infrastructure cost review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment on the cost of the delivery of infrastructure projects in Northern Ireland, over the last five years.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the inflationary pressures that continue to drive significant cost increases across the construction sector in both Britain and the South, with major schemes experiencing rising tender prices and significant annual uplifts. These pressures are widening funding gaps and can impact on project viability, timelines and delivery models, as seen across multiple strategic infrastructure schemes.In the North, the adoption of annual budgeting practices and legal challenges have hindered my department’s ability to deliver and sustain a pipeline of major infrastructure projects over the past five years. Consequently, this has resulted in a lack of substantive data regarding sector inflation trends.The Investment Strategy for Northern Ireland (ISNI) 2050 includes an Enabling Action Plan designed to address longstanding issues in infrastructure delivery.  The plan identifies key risks to infrastructure delivery and groups 12 enabling actions under three themes: People, Process and Policy.Major projects in the North are developed through formal business cases and contract documentation prepared in line with Public Procurement Policy, through Centres of Procurement Expertise to ensure best value for money.  As such there are no plans within the Department to undertake an infrastructure cost review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he plans to introduce mandatory electronic invoicing, in the public sector.
answer_text: At present there is approximately 65% electronic invoicing on the Civil Service’s current finance system, Account NI. My officials are actively exploring how to streamline and modernise invoicing processes for Departments and the Non Departmental Public Bodies who use the Civil Service Finance Shared Services. I have no plans to bring forward a policy to mandate electronic invoicing for the wider public sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients that have availed of the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme, since its commencement.
answer_text: From the launch of the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme on 2 June 2025 up to 20 January 2026, a total of 494 applications have been successfully prior approved under the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on her discussions with the legal sector on ending the strike action by barristers.
answer_text: Engagement with the Bar Council and Criminal Bar Association has been ongoing since before action began. I met with representatives of the CBA again on 5 November 2025 and 15 January 2026 when I proposed a means of navigating the current impasse which builds on work done to date. My proposed approach would both allow the concerns of the CBA to be addressed at pace and fulfil our requirements in terms of appropriately managing public money. Crucially, the proposed approach will also involve the Solicitors Criminal Bar Association, those have remained engaged and committed to the agreed process throughout the last year and who have ensured no impact on access to justice by continuing to deliver services to their clients. As the proposed approach is a matter of ongoing discussion, it would not be appropriate to comment further at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the (i) rationalisation; (ii) co-location; and (iii) better utilisation, of buildings across the wider public sector.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the management and strategic planning of the Civil Service Office Estate, which includes ensuring that the Office Estate meets the changing needs of the NICS and delivers on its energy efficiency targets. My Department published the first NICS Office Estate Strategy Progress Report 2022-2025 in May 2025, which demonstrates how rationalising and consolidating offices is ensuring better utilisation of the office estate. The progress report showed that since 2022 the department had divested of 16 buildings; reduced the Net Internal Area of the Office Estate by 12% and reduced the number of workstations by 20%.The second report outlining progress up to 31 March 2026 will be published prior to summer 2026.My Department remains happy to work with others on the better utilisation of buildings across the wider public sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether his Department has explored opportunities to pool, or integrate, transport for health, education and other services, including shared maintenance hubs, fleet management and scheduling, across the Departments.
answer_text: At present, there are collaborative contracts in place for Vehicle Spare Parts, Vehicle Batteries and Tyres, and Vehicle and Plant Hire Services which pool requirements across the Civil Service and wider public sector, including health and education. Officials within my Department are represented on the NICS Fleet Decarbonisation Working Group led by the Department for Infrastructure which seeks to rationalise fleets and share best practice among large fleet users.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has any plans to review arm’s-length bodies, and organisational landscape, across the public sector.
answer_text: In June 2020, the Executive agreed to a review of Arm’s Length Bodies (ALBs), in accordance with the commitments in New Decade, New Approach (NDNA), with the Executive subsequently agreeing in December 2021 that it would be for sponsoring departments to take forward action plans for the review of their ALBs, and it is for individual Ministers and departments to engage with and review their ALBs as part of their existing governance and accountability frameworks. My officials commenced a new one-off exercise in September 2025 to gather information on the status of these ALB reviews. This exercise will compile a central, validated list of ALBs that provides an assessment of the status of departmental ALB reviews, their conclusions, and Ministers priorities in relation to ALB reform, as well as views on the need for potential legislation to enable that reform. On completion of the review, I intend to bring the findings to the Executive for consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any steps being taken to explore opportunities for (i) mergers; (ii) simplification; or (iii)shared governance, across the Civil Service.
answer_text: The Department of Finance has responsibility for the delivery of shared services to all Departments as well as other public bodies. The development of those shared services is managed through engagement with Departments by shared-service providers in DoF, and through specific pieces of work such as the Integr8 programme.The Integr8 Programme will modernise and integrate Finance and HR services by centralising transactional processing and redesigning processes, structures, roles and data to ensure they are streamlined, adaptable and customer focused. The transformation will also involve the design and implementation of a single, cloud-based Finance and HR technology solution. This will improve efficiency, customer experience, and data insights that support decision-making. It is expected that new services will be deployed during 2027-28.Corporate Services Directors from across departments also meet regularly as a collective forum to consider opportunities for improved coordination, rationalisation and more efficient delivery of corporate functions. This includes exploring where services may benefit from greater alignment, simplification, or shared governance arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the digitalisation of Government services.
answer_text: My Department is currently undertaking work across several business areas on the digitisation of Government services.Land & Property Services (LPS)Land & Property Services is delivering a multi-year transformation programme to modernise systems and services, including launching Rates Online, digital access to draft valuations, moving all Land Registry registers online, providing a fully digital application process for solicitors, enabling online citizen access and paperless processing, and enhancing the Government Land and Property Register as a single digital record of central government land and property assets.NICSHRResourcing Division is embedding technology enabled recruitment, providing digital assessment booklets and a real-time competition dashboard. Pensions Division now offers an online portal for NI Civil Service Pension Scheme members, and work with HR Connect is delivering a new APEX/FACES case management dashboard. Employee Relations is automating sickness absence case management, Learning & Development is exploring AI for future training needs, and the Occupational Health Service is implementing a cloud-based case management system with enhanced analytics.NISRANISRA is adopting a digital-first approach by modernising surveys and the Census, piloting modular online surveys, achieving high online Census uptake, improved user-driven analysis tools, and the creation of new linked datasets to support better policymaking and services.DSFDoF provides a central NI Identity Assurance (NIDA) service to support digitised government services that enables citizens to access a wide range of NI Direct online services across multiple public bodies.Integr8 ProgrammeIntegr8 is a transformational programme to modernise and integrate NICS Finance and HR services by centralising transactional processing, redesigning processes and structures, introducing a single cloud-based system with greater automation and data insights, and rolling out new services from 2027–28.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting time for an ADHD assessment for (i) children; and (ii) adults, in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: There is currently no commissioned service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) for adults or children in Northern Ireland.  ADHD provision has evolved across Trusts in response to population demand and expertise within HSC teams.  There is some provision available across the Health and Social Care system, but there are inconsistencies and lengthy waiting times across Trusts. In response, the Department commissioned a needs assessment on ADHD in early February 2025, the findings of which will help inform options for a commissioned ADHD service across Trusts, subject to future budget availability.  The needs assessment, which covers both adults and children, will aim to determine the level of demand for a commissioned service, as well as recommendations for what such a service might look like, including potential implementation and staffing costs. The ADHD needs assessment report was received by the Department at the end of November 2025 and is currently being considered by officials, with a view to identifying next steps and arranging publication thereafter.  Data on the number of people, children and adults, waiting for an ADHD assessment or on average waiting times from referral to diagnostic outcome for an ADHD assessment is not held centrally by the Department. SEHSCT have provided the following information which has been provided through a manual process as validation of Encompass reporting has not been completed. SEHSCT Children’s ADHD Services Waiting list run date Number waiting Average waiting times (days) *Financial year end 2020/21467Not available Financial year end 2021/22611262 days Financial year end 2022/23740330 days At Oct 23 (pre Encompass)1061341 days Financial year end 2023/24N/a encompass launch N/a encompass launchFinancial year end 2024/251459665 days 31 Jan 20261902559 days Note: Average waiting time in days : time taken from referral until waiting list run date Adult ServicesThe Trust is not commissioned to assess for ADHD in Adults.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the consultation on the sale and supply of puppies and kittens, whether he has any plans to table legislative proposals to the Assembly within this year, based on the consultation outcomes.
answer_text: A consultation response and way forward document is currently being finalised for publication, and I will then be able to make a final decision on next steps. This legislation will be introduced as soon as possible thereafter, and I anticipate that this will be in the first half of this year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions he has taken since taking office to address the waiting time for assessment for ADHD for (i) children; and (ii) adults, in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: There is currently no commissioned service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) for adults or children in Northern Ireland.  ADHD provision has evolved across Trusts in response to population demand and expertise within HSC teams.  There is some provision available across the Health and Social Care system, but there are inconsistencies and lengthy waiting times across Trusts. In response, the Department commissioned a needs assessment on ADHD in early February 2025, the findings of which will help inform options for a commissioned ADHD service across Trusts, subject to future budget availability.  The needs assessment, which covers both adults and children, will aim to determine the level of demand for a commissioned service, as well as recommendations for what such a service might look like, including potential implementation and staffing costs.
