question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the funding she requested, compared with the proposed funding allocated, in the Minister of Finance's Proposed Draft Budget 2026-29/30.
answer_text: The proposed allocations for my Department would go some way towards addressing the level of investment required for our infrastructure and for maintaining service delivery. However, these would not allow the Department to deliver everything it wanted to and therefore would require some difficult decisions to be made in prioritising services and capital schemes for delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has any plans to review the regulation of the private tutoring sector.
answer_text: I recognise the concerns in respect of the current gaps pertaining to the lack of ability for self-employed people to undergo Access NI Enhanced Checks and I understand that the Minister of Justice intends to address this matter. The regulation of the private tutoring sector would not be a matter for my department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any efficiency and reform programmes his Department has for the Civil Service.
answer_text: The People Strategy 2025 – 2030 is my Department’s key programme in relation to the efficiency and reform of the Civil Service. The interventions within the Strategy will strengthen NICS capacity and capability through the Job Families & Professions Framework, strategic workforce planning, streamlined recruitment and HR operations, updated HR policies, enhanced line manager capability, and accelerated digital upskilling.In addition, my Department has been leading on a number of wider reform programmes and initiatives which will further enable and support civil servants in the work that they do in departments. These include:Integr8 Programme: will enable the transformation of how Finance and HR services are delivered across NICS, supported by a cloud-based technology solution which will enhance process automation.Departmental Efficiency plans: DoF continues to support all departments as they develop longer term financial sustainability plans, encouraging clear proposals for efficiencies and transformation over five years to reduce costs, improve services and ensure resources are directed to priority needs.Transformation Fund: in March 2025 I announced £129 million in funding, out of an available £235 million, for six major public service transformation projects across Health, Justice, Education and Infrastructure. A second call for proposals issued from the Transformation Board in June 2025, and I expect to bring recommendations to the Executive shortly.Draft Budget proposals: include a further £135 million for transformation over the budget period, supporting the continued drive to improve public services and strengthen long term financial sustainability.Climate and Sustainability Action Plan: was published in May 2025, setting out 38 actions to support efficiency and reform through a leaner estate, better energy use, reduced waste, and more sustainable, modern working practices.NICS Office Estate Strategy: has delivered significant progress since 2022, with 16 buildings divested, a 12% reduction in office space and a 20% reduction in workstations. Strategic Asset Management Unit: establishing a centralised, professional and resilient asset management system that streamlines reporting, reduces departmental burden and ensures consistent, evidence-based decision making across the public estate.Land & Property Services Transformation: delivering a multi-year transformation programme that modernises systems, streamlines processes and enhances customer experience, creating more efficient, consistent and digitally enabled land and property services across government.NISRA Modernisation: modernising statistical outputs and surveys by expanding the use of automated analytical processes and moving key surveys online, improving data quality and reducing costs across its statistical portfolio.Roll out of TEAMs and new digital ways of working tools such as Co-pilot: the Department has rolled out Microsoft 365 to departments and provides practical adv

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he plans to introduce a government efficiency framework, similar to HM Government.
answer_text: Each Executive Minister and their respective Permanent Secretary have a responsibility for ensuring efficiency in within their department. Ministers and Departments share a collective responsibility for ensuring efficiency across government. In addition, my Department continues to lead on work to support transformation and improve efficiency. While a different approach to that of the British Government, this includes implementation of the Executive-agreed Budget Sustainability Plan, which is intended to support long-term fiscal stability, efficient allocation of resources, and sustainable public service delivery. Through this, my Department has been providing support and guidance to all Departments as they seek to put in place five-year financial sustainability plans, including encouraging Departments to identify and set out clear proposals for efficiencies and transformation initiatives.In March 2025, I also announced the allocation of £129 million of transformation funding, out of an available £235 million, to support six transformation projects across a range of priority areas including Health, Justice, Education and Infrastructure. A second call for transformation proposals issued from the Transformation Board to Departments in June 2025, and I expect to bring recommendations on these to the Executive shortly.The draft Budget proposals that I have brought forward also include a commitment of a further £135 million to transformation over the budget period to support the continued drive towards transforming public services and long-term financial sustainability.More widely, the Programme for Government includes the establishment of a Delivery Unit within The Executive Office to support to the Executive in driving reform and improving public services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 37458/22-27, to detail the current estimate of the road maintenance backlog.
answer_text: Unfortunately, the budget that the Executive receives from London continues to fall substantially short of what is needed to run nor recover our services after over a decade of austerity, and as a result, my Department does not have funding for everything my Department wants or needs to do.The ‘Review of the Structural Maintenance Funding Requirements for the Department for Infrastructure (Roads)’ highlighted the need for increased spending on Capital Structural Maintenance. However the impact on the integrity and safety of the road network is as a result of the longer-term underfunding, rather than the impact of a single year. This report valued the backlog at £1.2 billion in 2018.Without commissioning another review, I cannot provide an estimate of the current backlog. However, with my Department’s new Road Maintenance Strategy, which is now open for public consultation, and my commitment to investing £1.3m in the provision of a full digital survey of the entire road network in the North, I am taking action to address the consequences of years of under investment. I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made to improve the quality of our road network and contribute to the delivery of the priorities set out in the agreed Programme for Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions she has had with HM Government, since it announced the UK was rejoining the Erasmus student scheme, regarding its implication for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Following the British Government’s announcement of its intention to rejoin the Erasmus+ scheme, my officials and I have engaged extensively with them to ensure that the interests and priorities of this jurisdiction are fully reflected in the new arrangements being established. On 17 November 2025, I met with Jacqui Smith, Minister of State for Skills, together with counterparts in the other Devolved Governments, to discuss the scheme in advance of the announcement on reassociation in December.  My officials continue to meet with their counterparts on a fortnightly basis as we work towards rejoining Erasmus+ in 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has any plans to give local councils powers to increase the current maximum fines for (i) dog fouling; and (ii) littering.
answer_text: In December 2022 my Department amended the Environmental Offences (Fixed Penalties) (Miscellaneous Provisions) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2012 to increase the maximum penalty capable of being specified by district councils for the offences of dog fouling or littering from £80 to £200. I have no plans to further increase these maximum fixed penalties.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail why the supply of grit ran out in parts of Northern Ireland, during the recent period of adverse weather.
answer_text: In preparation for the beginning of Winter Service, our salt reserves were filled to capacity over the summer months, with additional re-orders available throughout the winter as required. 55,000 tonnes of salt are held in salt barns across the North in advance of the winter season with an additional 20,000 ton strategic reserve held by the Irish Mining Company, Carrickfergus for use by the Department.Re-orders are set to 90% of full capacity to ensure stock levels are maintained. From 1st January onwards, salt stocks are maintained in a linear fashion against a minimum holding for each depot of 6 days’ supply for two actions per day.I can confirm that during the recent period of adverse weather all gritting actions were fully completed and the department did not run out of supply.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of (i) the grit stock levels; and (ii) its ability to cover designated roads.
answer_text: In preparation for the beginning of Winter Service, our salt reserves were filled to capacity over the summer months, with additional re-orders available throughout the winter as required. 55,000 tonnes of salt are held in salt barns across the North in advance of the winter season with an additional 20,000 ton strategic reserve held by the Irish Mining Company, Carrickfergus, for use by the Department.Re-orders are set to 90% of full capacity to ensure stock levels are maintained. From 1st January onwards, salt stocks are maintained in a linear fashion against a minimum holding for each depot of 6 days’ supply for two actions per day.My Department targets the limited resources available on the busier main through routes, as per the current winter service policy. The application of this policy means that 28% of the total road network, which carries around 80% of traffic, is salted, and provides action where it is most needed and most-effective.In addition to gritting this 28% Primary Salting Network, salt bins and grit piles are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis, on other routes adopted or maintained by my Department, but which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule. Where possible, once the condition of the Primary Salting Network is secured, secondary salting may be undertaken on other, more rural roads, particularly during prolonged periods of hazardous weather. These decisions are taken by experienced engineers who review all available data and Met office expert opinion. The full policy can be viewed on the Department’s website below:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/winter-service-rsppg-e022	It must, however, be emphasised that the service provided cannot eliminate the risks – even on roads that are gritted, ice free conditions cannot be guaranteed, and I would urge motorists to drive with caution, take the ever-changing weather conditions into account and leave extra time for their journey. During this recent cold spell, staff across all our roads divisions have been working tirelessly day and night in some very difficult conditions, to keep traffic moving safely. We owe a huge debt of gratitude to our gritter drivers and snow blowers, who are often the first traffic on many roads, leaving their own families behind to ensure we can travel safely. Their hard work and persistence ensured tangible results for our road users.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the proposed Tillysburn Park and Ride facility.
answer_text: The site of the proposed Tillysburn Park and Ride is located on the A2 Belfast to Bangor transport corridor, a key arterial route into Belfast and will encourage commuters to choose public transport or cycling for part of their journey.  The proposed scheme will also integrate with a proposed new cycle route linking Victoria Park and the Sydenham Greenway.  Pre-application discussions for this site commenced with Belfast City Council in August 2021.     Whilst I am supportive of expanding our Park and Ride schemes where needed, the member will be aware that my Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to under funding and austerity by the British Government.  As the capital budget for my Department in 2025/26 is less than required, available funding must be prioritised accordingly.  Whilst my officials continue to consider options for Park and Ride sites, it is likely that there will be limited development of Tillysburn Park and Ride in the current financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of decisions by the PSNI in recent cases where they have not released an image of those guilty of sexual assault, stating that it does not meet their threshold.
answer_text: The publication of images of those found guilty of sexual assault is entirely an operational matter for the Chief Constable.As the Member is aware, the Chief Constable is operationally independent from both me as Justice Minister and my Department and is accountable to the Policing Board.  You may, therefore, wish to raise this matter directly with the Chief Constable or the Policing Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for an update on the recruitment and retention of school crossing patrol staff; and (ii) to detail any any measures to address shortages.
answer_text: School Crossing Patrol (SCPs) staff are employed by the Education Authority’s transport division and are managed locally with the assistance of the school to which they are assigned.  Recruitment figures between 1 April and 14 December 2025 are detailed as follows. School crossing patrol recruitment figures 1/4/2025 – 14/12/2025County VacanciesFilledAntrim284Armagh51Londonderry103Down242Fermanagh40Tyrone80Total7910Note: Figures may include vacancies which were unfilled and re-advertised.SCP posts can be filled through a range of means including a public recruitment exercise, appointment of a reserve candidate or redeployment. The EA provides several options to support the school, including: Contacting the school to arrange for SCP opportunities to be publicised across the local community and on the school’s social media channels. Identifying if there are suitable and available relief staff or if there is a reserve candidate who could be employed as a SCP on a temporary basis. Assisting the school in identifying an existing school staff member, or an external individual who they believe is a suitable candidate, who could be employed by the Education Authority as a SCP on a temporary basis.Exploring the feasibility of a SCP from another location providing support for some or all of the school week.Where the school identifies someone to fulfil the role on a temporary basis the EA’s Locality Inspector will visit the school to train the new SCP.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the budget required to address the backlog in road resurfacing.
answer_text: There has been historic underfunding of the road network for a number of years, and I am keen to improve our infrastructure with the limited funding available, as it delivers real benefits for our citizens, our communities and the economy.The ‘Review of the Structural Maintenance Funding Requirements for the Department for Infrastructure (Roads)’ highlighted the need for increased spending on Capital Structural Maintenance, however the impact on the integrity and safety of the road network is as a result of the longer-term underfunding, rather than the impact of a single year. This report valued the backlog at £1.2 billion in 2018.On 2nd December, I was pleased to launch the new Road Maintenance Strategy which is now open for public consultation. The main objectives of the strategy are to improve the overall condition of the road network and enhance safety and quality, by delivering a targeted programme of intelligent maintenance investment and a sustainable maintenance regime. 	Through this new strategy, my Department will ensure that the road maintenance function is	organized to improve day to day effectiveness and enhance our ability to respond to incidents	and weather events. 	Along with this strategy my Department is investing £1.3m in the provision of a full digital	survey of the entire road network in the North. This will provide a much greater level of	understanding of road condition and allow my officials to take data-driven decisions and	deliver more focused maintenance interventions.	The value of deeper, more accurate information to support intelligent road maintenance is	widely recognised. A growing number of Local Authorities are already benefiting from this	type of roadscaping technology, however, the North is the first to collect such a vast national	data set to produce a national road condition baseline. 	However it is important to recognise that these measures on their own will not address the	consequences of years of under investment and I will continue to work with Executive	colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made to improve the quality of our road network	and contribute to the delivery of the priorities set out in the agreed Programme for Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for an update on the delivery of the 10x Economic vison; and (ii) to detail the measurable progress made since its launch.
answer_text: Since my predecessor, Conor Murphy, announced a new Economic Vision for the North in February 2024, my Department’s delivery focus has centred around the four key priorities of: Good Jobs, Productivity, Regional Balance, and Decarbonisation.Significant progress has been made against these priorities during the mandate so far. For example, we have:taken significant strides in the development of an Employment Rights Bill to majorly update our employment law framework and raise working standards;set out a clear roadmap to expand university provision in Ulster University’s Magee campus, honouring a long-standing commitment;established Local Economic Partnerships in all Local Government Districts to help put local economic decision-making in the hands of local people;put in place Sectoral Action Plans to stimulate some of our highest-growth and highest-productivity potential sectors; andput in place a new Skills Action Plan and Skills Fund to better the skills needs of our economy. At the outset of this year my Department published a year one progress report on the delivery of my Economic Vision: Delivering the Economic Vision - Year one progress report | Department for the Economy. Further information on my Department’s priorities this financial year is available in our published 2025-26 Business Plan: DfE Business Plan 2025-26 | Department for the Economy. My Department will publish further updates on delivery as the mandate progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the delivery of the Northern Ireland Road Safety Strategy to 2030; and (ii) whether current targets for reducing Killed or Seriously Injured casualties remain achievable.
answer_text: (i) The Road Safety Strategy itself is published and runs until 2030. The latest Road Safety Strategy to 2030 Annual Statistical Report, monitoring the progress of the strategy against agreed targets and key performance indicators, was published on the 21 August 2025.  The progress report for the 2024-25 Action Plan was published on the 30 October.  A new action plan is in the final stages of development.  The development of the Action Plan involves cooperation and discussion across several departments and organisations, including the emergency services. Producing a high-level strategic plan takes time and I will be pleased to announce its publication in due course.   (ii)The long-term goal is to eliminate deaths and serious injuries on our roads by 2050, and this is a challenging target, which will be helped by increasingly effective safety technology.  However, it is not accepted that any death or serious injury is inevitable, and the Strategy strives to achieve the maximum reduction in casualties by 2030.  In support of this I am prepared to take the necessary actions to address the behaviours that contribute most to road deaths, including drink and drug driving, excess speed and using a hand-held mobile phone while driving.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how her Department is incorporating new road safety technologies into infrastructure planning.
answer_text: On 2nd December, I was pleased to launch the new Road Maintenance Strategy which is now open for public consultation.The main objectives of the strategy are to improve the overall condition of the road network and enhance safety and quality, by delivering a targeted programme of intelligent maintenance investment and a sustainable maintenance regime. Through this new strategy, my Department will ensure that the road maintenance function is organised to improve day to day effectiveness and enhance our ability to respond to incidents and weather events.Along with this strategy my Department is investing £1.3m to deliver a complete digital survey of the entire road network in the North. This will provide a much greater level of understanding of road condition and allow my officials to take data-driven decisions and deliver more focused maintenance interventions saving money in the longer term.The value of deeper, more accurate information to support intelligent road maintenance is widely recognised. A growing number of Local Authorities are already benefiting from this type of roadscaping technology, however, the North is the first to collect such a vast national data set to produce a national road condition baseline. It’s a landmark initiative that, in just six months, will give my Department an unprecedented understanding of its road infrastructure to better target maintenance, reduce costs, and react quickly to future opportunities and innovations.The survey will be completed by the end of the 25/26 financial year and data will then be processed over a period of a couple of months and used to develop the future structural maintenance programme and inform future in-year monitoring rounds for 2026/27.My Department’s Intelligent Transport Systems (ITS) Strategy Vision 2025 and associated internal operational documents also consider the use of ITS equipment such as variable messaging signs; CCTV; Automatic Number Plate Recognition; Motorway Incident Detection and Automatic Signals; Mandatory Variable Speed Limits; Ramp Metering; Traffic Signal Control Systems; Traffic Counters; Vehicle Activated Signs; Enforcement Cameras; Emergency Telephones; Parking Information;  and Part time 20mph signage at schools.My Department also works in partnership with the PSNI Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership (NIRSP) regarding new technologies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps her Department is taking to improve safety on school routes, to encourage cycling and walking to schools.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. We are acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, particularly around schools.My Department is currently taking forward a third tranche of part-time 20mph zones at 40 schools across the North which will bring the total delivered since 2020/21 to 273 schools https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/20mph-speed-limit-schools-tranche-3.This initiative is a significant intervention that will help to ensure that children, parents and staff feel safer as they travel to and from school on a daily basis.My Department is also taking forward a review on a number of speed related matters, including 20mph speed limits, and I plan to undertake a public consultation exercise as part of this review in the new year.  Further consideration on the use of permanent 20mph zones will be taken on completion of the consultation.In addition to this, officials are currently updating design guidance for active travel, including the development of a toolkit of measures that can be implemented around schools to encourage walking, wheeling or cycling for the school run and routes to key destinations including schools have been prioritised for future pedestrian and cycling improvements as detailed within my Department’s draft Active Travel Delivery Plan.My Department’s road safety and promotion programmes such as the Cycling Proficiency Scheme (CPS), the Practical Child Pedestrian Safety Training (PCPST) and the Active School Travel Programme (AST) all play an important role in encouraging young people to walk, wheel and cycle by providing them with the necessary skills and confidence to feel safe when making active travel choices.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the (i) use; and (ii) roll-out, of (a) safety camera vans; and (b) fixed cameras.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information.  This is an operational matter for the PSNI and NIRSP.  You may find further information at the following link Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership | PSNI

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the budget for road safety education campaigns, for the last five years.
answer_text: The budget on Road Safety Education Campaigns reported on an accrual’s basis, consistent with annual accounts: 2025-26 - Current Budget £2,446k 2024-25 - Closing Budget £840k2023-24 - Closing Budget £491k2022-23 - Closing Budget £211k2021-22 - Closing Budget £1,476kThe 2025-26 budget includes £500k sponsorship transferred from the NI Road Safety Partnership in December monitoring.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any assessment her Department has made of average speed camera systems; and (ii) whether expansion of the use is planned.
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer given to AQW 37349/ 22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she has considered the introduction of fast-track arrangements in the planning system for strategic (i) housing; (ii) renewable energy; or (iii) economic investment, proposals.
answer_text: My Department continues to work closely with local government and other key stakeholders through the planning improvement agenda with the aim of enhancing the performance and long-term effectiveness of the planning system in the North.  The measures and initiatives therein aim to improve all aspects of the system, including development management processes, to help ensure that sustainable housing, renewable energy, and economic development proposals are facilitated as efficiently and effectively as possible. Recent improvements include new legislation to allow council planning authorities to introduce statutory validation checklists, with the aim of improving the quality of planning applications entering the system, supporting more efficient consultee responses, and thereby reducing the potential for delays in the process.The Planning Act (NI) 2011 introduced a hierarchy of development, categorising development as local, major, and major development deemed regionally significant within the planning process.  The aim of the hierarchy is to encourage a more proportionate and responsive approach to processing planning applications, with resources and decision-taking mechanisms tailored according to the scale and complexity of the proposed development.The Strategic Planning Policy Statement: Planning for Sustainable Development (Edition 2 2025) (SPPS) states that “Major developments have important social, economic and environmental implications for a council area. With potential to deliver important benefits for the local community, these applications are given appropriate priority to avoid undue delay and risk to investment decisions.”  In practice, the relevance of, and weight to be given to social, economic and environmental considerations is a matter of planning judgement and planning balance in any given case. There are no plans at this time to introduce systemic measures that would prioritise or fast-track planning applications for specific classes of development.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she intends to introduce proposals for reform of the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, to address inefficiencies.
answer_text: My focus, and that of my department, remains on the ongoing planning improvement agenda to drive forward measures and initiatives to make our regional planning system more effective and efficient, including the associated legislative initiatives. I remain committed to creating a culture of continuous improvement in the system and I have asked my officials to commence a process of scoping wider strategic matters, including the best way forward for the regional and strategic planning policy framework in the North. I recognise that there will be a need for a new Planning Bill on the far side of this work which will be subject to extensive policy development, consultation and Committee/Assembly scrutiny.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has made any assessment of the current planning fees adequately reflecting the cost of processing applications.
answer_text: The financial sustainability of the planning system remains a key priority for my Department and is embedded as a key theme of the planning improvement programme. My officials continue to work with local government to scope and review the financial resilience of the planning system with the aim of identifying opportunities to increase levels of income for statutory and non-statutory functions and services.  In October 2025, and a public consultation was launched to seek views on introducing charges for planning services that are not currently subject to fees, such as Non-Material Changes and the Discharge of Conditions. The consultation also seeks views on broader planning fee structures, exploring potential new fee categories, adjustments to existing charges, and the overall appropriateness of the planning fees charged in the current system.  My Department has also introduced three inflationary uplifts to planning fees, with a 12.3% increase in 2023, 4% increase in 2024 and 2.1% increase in 2025 with plans to introduce a further uplift of the planning fees in April 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans she has to address staffing shortages within council planning departments, that are contributing to planning delays.
answer_text: My Department recognises the importance of a well-resourced planning system in supporting sustainable development and economic growth. In response to ongoing staffing challenges across the system my Department, in partnership with Local Government and Belfast Metropolitan College, has developed a bespoke Trainee Planner Programme which is scheduled to commence in January 2026. This unique initiative, which is open to all local planning authorities, aims to address current staffing shortages and future-proof the profession by creating a structured pathway into planning as a career. Successful candidates will begin as trainees and participate in a structured three-year programme that combines in-house training with academic study. Four councils have signed up to the programme and it is hoped that others will join in future years. In addition, my officials are currently working on a number of external recruitment competitions seeking a range of professional and technical planning staff, to fill several vacancies within my Department’s Planning Group. Officials continue to collaborate with councils and engage with local universities and colleges to promote planning as a career of choice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the continued under-performance of several local councils in meeting statutory planning targets.
answer_text: My Department is working closely with local government and other key stakeholders to deliver a Planning Improvement Programme with the aim of improving the efficiency of the planning system in the North. This programme has included many key initiatives including new legislation to allow councils to introduce statutory validation checklists which will encourage better quality planning applications which in turn will reduce processing times. This follows new legislation to improve pre-application community consultation and pre-determination hearings, both areas that have a key focus around improvements and streamlining the planning system in the North.My officials have also conducted a detailed analysis of each council’s performance against the statutory indicators and have met with individual planning authorities to help identify the challenges, blockages and delays that they face in the operational aspects of the planning process.  This has informed opportunities for support, shared learning and best practice which should enable more consistent and effective processeses.We are currently seeing improvements in performance with the average annual processing time for major applications in 2024/25 the lowest since 2015/16.  I am, of course aware, that some councils are performing better than others and I appreciate that further progress needs to be made in this regard. With this in mind, my Department will continue to work closely with local councils and key stakeholders to deliver improvements which make a tangible difference to all citizens in the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps he is taking to strengthen enforcement against illegal dumping and waste crime.
answer_text: The Environmental Crime Unit (ECU) of NIEA continues to investigate and prosecute the most serious and persistent waste criminality in Northern Ireland.  The ECU focuses its resources on those cases which present the most significant risk to the environment and public health.  ECU utilises powers contained within the Waste and Contaminated Land (Northern Ireland) Order 1997 to pursue enforcement and where appropriate, criminal prosecution. Such prosecutions are often coupled with confiscation proceedings under the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002, aimed at depriving those convicted of the proceeds generated from their crimes whilst asserting the ‘Polluter Pays Principle’. The NIEA continues to work in close partnership with local councils to address fly tipping through a working protocol. Both the NIEA and District Councils have access to various provisions under the Waste and Contaminated Land (Northern Ireland) Order 1997, enabling them to undertake enforcement action where appropriate.NIEA implements a multi-agency approach, promoting coordinated and proactive engagement with a number of partner agencies. By working together with partner agencies, the NIEA takes a comprehensive approach in tackling illegal waste criminality through effective inter-agency collaboration.  My Department is seeking to increase the severity of penalties associated with waste offences which impact the water environment. The draft Fisheries and Water Environment Bill is the legislative vehicle for amendments to fines and penalties relating to offences for the water environment in the Waste and Contaminated Land (Northern Ireland) Order 1997. This supports action that has been committed to within the Lough Neagh Action plan and ensures that there is a consistent enforcement regime with appropriate powers to ensure that aquatic and ecosystem health is prioritised and supported.The public consultation on policies for inclusion in the draft Bill ended on 6th November and officials have incorporated analysis into the policy memorandum for consideration by the Executive.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any action being taken to secure more emergency housing, to ensure that families are not forced to relocate far from schools, and local support.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive has increased its portfolio of temporary accommodation year on year, with over 350 additional units secured across Northern Ireland since the beginning of the 2025/26 financial year.When providing temporary accommodation placements, the Housing Executive will always consider the individual circumstances of the applicant, including proximity to schools, family and support networks in as far as is practicable in the context of challenges in supply and demand for temporary accommodation.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any surveillance that is being undertaken by his Department outside the current bluetongue Temporary Control Zones.
answer_text: Following the detection of Bluetongue Virus (BTV) at a farm near Bangor, County Down, my department quickly implemented a 20km Temporary Control Zone (TCZ) around the holding.  This zone includes restrictions on movements of susceptible animals within and out of the area to mitigate the risk of onward spread of the virus. DAERA vets are now carrying out representative sampling and testing on large cattle herds in the TCZ to ascertain if there is further infection.Tracings have been completed of any animals which moved onto or off the affected premises near Bangor, in the risk window from 1 September 2025.  Any holdings identified as receiving animals from the affected farm will be subject to surveillance and testing if required. A similar approach is now being taken with respect to the TCZ established on 6 December 2025 around a premises near Greyabbey.An enhanced active surveillance testing protocol was also introduced at all Northern Ireland abattoirs in June 2024, and this is ongoing.  You will be aware that it is as a result of this rigorous surveillance programme that my Department operates that Bluetongue was originally detected in cattle from the premises outside Bangor.In addition, any reports to DAERA by livestock keepers or private veterinarians throughout Northern Ireland of the suspicion of epizootic diseases such as Bluetongue will be thoroughly investigated by my veterinary officials, with the relevant surveillance conducted including sampling and testing at the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute (AFBI) in order to detect or rule out disease. Further consideration will be given to next steps once the epidemiological picture becomes clearer and we will remain in close contact with affected farmers, and wider industry, on next steps.  Updates on this developing situation will be made available on the DAERA website at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/bluetongue-situation-northern-ireland
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any engagement he has had with the Department of Environment, Food and Rural Affairs on extended producer responsibility, including how this will be implemented in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My officials engage with the Department of Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, together with Scottish and Welsh Government counterparts, extensively on a continual basis, regarding Extended Producer Responsibility for packaging (pEPR).PackUK was appointed, by all four UK nations, in January 2025 as the scheme administrator responsible for the implementation and delivery of pEPR across the UK including Northern Ireland. As DAERA Minister, I represent Northern Ireland on the PackUK Four Nations Ministerial Board, alongside GB counterparts, overseeing the governance and delivery of pEPR.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any (i) progress towards landfill reduction; and (ii) measures planned to accelerate the transition to alternative waste treatment methods.
answer_text: NI Local Authority Collected Municipal Waste Management Statistics released on 11 December 2025 show a landfill rate of 14% for 2024/2025, compared to 18.4% for 2023/2024. This is the lowest rate ever recorded. The quantity of waste sent to landfill decreased by 22.8% from 183,748 tonnes in 2023/2024 to 141,836 tonnes in 2024/2025. My Department consulted on recycling reforms in 2024. The proposals in the Rethinking Our Resources Consultation included measures to assist in delivery against targets set in Northern Ireland’s legislation, including 65% of municipal waste to be prepared for re-use and recycled by 2035 and a reduction in municipal waste sent to landfill to 10% or less by 2035. Responses helped inform our draft Departmental Response, which contains recommendations aimed at driving down the volume of residual waste and improving both the quality and quantity of material being collected for recycling, to support local reprocessing .I am currently seeking approval from my Executive colleagues for publication of the Departmental Response.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps he is taking to ensure compliance with livestock movement restrictions in the bluetongue Temporary Control Zones.
answer_text: My Department is taking the necessary measures to ensure compliance with the livestock movement restrictions imposed by the declaration of the bluetongue temporary control zones (TCZ).		You will be aware that disease control measures including certain movement restrictions have been applied to those farms within the TCZ to mitigate against risk of onward disease spread. Any non-compliances of the necessary movement restrictions will be taken seriously and investigated by my Department’s Welfare and Enforcement branch for breaches of the legislation and possible enforcement action.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount spent on reimaging murals and memorials by the Housing Executive, in each constituency, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive Communities Funding cannot be reported by constituency. Please see detailed below the amount spent on reimaging murals and memorials in each Housing Executive Area, in each of the last five years.Year: 2025-26 (YTD)NIHE/Area OfficeAwarded amountApplicationsWest£  7,350.002North Down & Ards£  7,780.003South & East Belfast£  9,622.072Total£  24,752.077Year: 2024-25South Antrim£   19,789.20 3Mid & East Antrim£   11,851.00 3North Down & Ards£   17,870.00 6West Belfast & Shankill£      2,950.00 1Total£   52,460.20 13Year: 2023-24North Belfast£    3,820.00 1Mid & East Antrim£ 18,816.00 4West£    4,800.00 1South Antrim£    9,940.00 2West Belfast & Shankill£    3,631.00 1Total£ 41,007.00 10Year: 2022 -23North Down & Ards£   12,570.00 4South Antrim£   21,100.00 6Lisburn/Castlereagh£      1,300.00 1West£      6,404.00 2South & East Belfast£      6,395.00 2North Belfast£      3,625.00 1Total£   51,394.00 16Year: 2021-22West£    19,494.00 5Mid & East Antrim£       4,106.68 2South Antrim£       4,760.00 1South & East Belfast£    14,898.00 3North Belfast£       4,158.34 1Total£    47,417.02 12”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the delivery of the Regional Dementia Care Pathway.
answer_text: My Department acknowledges that Dementia is one of the biggest challenges to our health and social care system in Northern Ireland, with the region projected to have the largest increase in the number of people living with dementia across the UK by 2040. My Department established a Regional Dementia Project Board in May 2024 in order to bring a sharpened focus on improvement in demenita care and services across the region. The most recent meeting of the Board was held on the 24th November 2025. The Dementia Project Board will aim to focus on the roll out of the Regional Dementia Care Pathway. The Project Board has three key workstreams which focus on the improvement within dementia services in line with the Regional Dementia Care Pathway. The Education and Training Workstream is tasked with addressing training requirements for staff, families and carers. The development of dementia awareness training for all health and social care staff is due to be completed and launched by January 2026. The Dementia learning and development framework 2016 is also being reviewed and updated.The Service Users and Carers Workstream has been established in a bid to ensure that key decisions and actions in regard to the provision of dementia services is informed by the perspectives and experiences of persons with lived experience. Recruitment to the Dementia Service Users and Carers Involvement Group has been concluded and the initial meeting of this group is scheduled for January 2026.The Service Delivery Workstream is reviewing the current network of dementia services across the region to identify models of best practice alongside where improvements can be made. A task and finish group has been established within the acute setting and work is taking place to develop a regional Dementia Hospital Charter.   In the community, there is a task and finish group working towards gaining national accreditation for Memory Services across the 5 Health and Social Care Trusts. In undertaking this work, the aim is to harmonise services for the delivery of dementia care. This workstream will also consider system readiness for the introduction of any new Disease Modifying Treatments for early Alzheimer’s Disease.It is recognised that whilst some improvements to the current dementia pathway can be made through some service redesign within existing services, that other areas identified as requiring improvement will require a funding allocation. The current savings requirement of 614m for the health service make funding allocation extremely difficult however I remain committed to identifying additional funding for dementia where I can.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the timeframe for the introduction of the Building Safety Act.
answer_text: I am currently finalising work on the draft building safety policy and subject to Executive agreement, my department intends to introduce a Building Safety Bill early in the next mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to progress Reform of Adult Social Care in Northern Ireland, including the implementation of its outcomes.
answer_text: A number of reviews and inquiries have pointed to the need for change in how we deliver adult social care, and it is imperative that we take action to address these challenges if we want our social care system to be sustainable in future years. While measures are already underway to reform aspects of the social care system, including the specific pressures associated with winter, my Department is also focused on delivering long-term, sustainable reform. To support this, we are in the process of finalising a 10-Year Strategic Plan for Reforming Adult Social Care and Support, alongside an associated 3-Year Delivery Plan. These plans have been developed in partnership across organisations and across sectors and will set out a clear roadmap for longer term reform and improvement in adult social care services. Among other things, they will seek to make the shift towards greater preventative activity, promote and facilitate the uptake of self-directed support and make greater use of technology, alongside examining our current approach to the provision of homecare and the model of care within our care homes. The plans will also have a focus on workforce, on the effectiveness and efficiency of current processes and procedures and on the adequacy of current policy and legislative frameworks. We will continue to work with partner organisations, through the Social Care Collaborative Reform Board, to drive delivery in these areas.In addition, my Department has convened a series of cross-sectoral roundtable discussions on Homecare and Self-Directed Support (SDS). These discussions have focused on both short-term measures and longer-term solutions, including addressing unmet need in Homecare and ensuring services are person-centred, empowering people, families, and unpaid carers to make meaningful choices about their care. Several examples of effective practice have been identified across our HSC Trusts, and we will consider the potential for regional application of those as part of our reform activity.The work on adult social care reform is about building a system that works, not just for today, but for the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment she has made of the scope for reforming the planning system to support economic development, noting growing industry concern, regarding the impact of current planning constraints on infrastructure investment.
answer_text: The planning system plays an important role in supporting infrastructure investment across the North. My Department continues to work in partnership with local government, who are responsible for the determination of over 99% of planning applications, and other key stakeholders to advance the planning improvement agenda with the aim of enhancing the performance and long-term effectiveness of the planning system in the North.  The measures and initiatives therein aim to improve all aspects of the system, including the Local Development Plan and development management processes, to help ensure that sustainable economic development and infrastructure projects are facilitated as efficiently and effectively as possible. Examples of this work include the delivery of new legislative provisions to enable council planning authorities to introduce statutory validation checklists, with the aim of improving the quality of planning applications entering the system, supporting more efficient consultee responses, and thereby reducing the potential for delays in the planning application process. The Planning (Development Management) Regulations (NI) 2015 are currently under review to consider the classes and thresholds for local, major and regionally significant development. This follows new legislation introduced to improve the Pre-Application Community Consultation and streamline the Pre-Determination Hearing process. My officials continue to work with the 12 planning authorities to help identify challenges, blockages and delays in the operational aspects of the planning process, to help inform opportunities for support, shared learning and best practice, with the aim of improving performance.  Many of the system interventions and support measures to improve the system will take time to bed into culture, practice and procedure, and for the benefits and performance improvements to be realised. However, early indicators of positive change are encouraging.  It is worth noting there has been a significant improvement in the average processing time for major planning applications in 2024/25, which saw the lowest processing times since the transfer of planning powers in 2015. This is in addition to improving statutory consultee response times, as 77% were responded to on time, that is within 21 calendar days or within an agreed extended timeframe.Through the planning improvement agenda, I am committed to working closely with local government and other stakeholders within the shared responsibility for the two-tier planning system, to deliver improvements which support the economy and infrastructure investment as well as our local communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any discussions he has had with the Treasury regarding lowering the 20 per cent VAT rate applied to hot takeaway foods.
answer_text: I fully recognise the significant challenges facing our hospitality industry, particularly in relation to the impact of higher VAT rates here, relative to the South.I have raised this issue on several occasions with the previous and current Chief Secretary to the Treasury (CST), both at our Finance: Interministerial Standing Committee meetings and in writing ahead of the British Government’s Autumn Budget. It was also raised during the meeting the deputy First Minister and I had with the Chancellor on 12 August. I am also aware of proposals for a VAT reduction pilot for the North. My officials have raised this with their Treasury counterparts, as well as the Department for the Economy, to understand the practical, administrative and financial implications. Unfortunately, the British Government continues to show no willingness to substantively engage on this issue.Ultimately, VAT remains an Excepted Matter, and the power to change VAT rates rests solely with the British Government, and only they could bear the cost of any reduced rate. For my part, I will continue to make the case for a tax system which is fair, proportionate and progressive, and allows our businesses to compete on a level playing field in the all-Ireland economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for an update on the progress of the Mobile Action Plan for Northern Ireland; and (ii) to detail any recommendations produced by the sub-groups of the Barrier Busting Taskforce for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Mobile Action Plan (MAP) was published on 23 June 2022. The delivery vehicle for the actions identified within the MAP is the Barrier Busting Taskforce (BBT). The BBT is comprised of an Oversight Group and three delivery subgroups, namely, The Communication Subgroup, the Publicly Owned Assets Subgroup and the Planning Subgroup. The Oversight Group recently met on the 9 December 2025. Although the work of the BBT will come to an end in 2026, several departments and organisations will continue associated workstreams which will help ensure the fast and efficient roll out of mobile communication infrastructure. My officials will produce a final report, agreed by BBT members once all the MAP actions have been delivered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the review of permitted development rights for telecommunications, including the anticipated timescales for the completion of this work.
answer_text: My Department recognises the importance of an efficient and effective planning system, which continues to support sustainable development in the North and facilitates the development of telecommunications infrastructure in an effective manner whilst keeping the environmental impact to a minimum.My officials have a positive working relationship with mobile operators and remain committed to working constructively with the industry and other key stakeholders to consider relevant matters as appropriate through the auspices of the Barrier Busting Taskforce (BBT) and its Planning Subgroup. My officials have commenced early policy development into reviewing the permitted development rights (PDRs) for telecommunications. This will involve extensive stakeholder engagement at the appropriate time, including with the mobile industry, as these proposals develop.  												Any recommendations arising from the outworkings of the BBT and its subgroup will be brought to me and other key stakeholders in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any proposals his Department has made to the Treasury regarding ensuring that VAT in Northern Ireland remains competitive with that in the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer to AQW 36958/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of specialist arterial vascular consultants, in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Demand for vascular surgery continues to rise, driven by an ageing population, an increased prevalence of diabetes and rising obesity levels. Emergency demand pressures are also significant, with ruptured aneurysms, acute limb ischaemia, and major trauma injuries all requiring urgent surgical attention. The vascular surgery service is a regional specialty based in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust which is responsible for managing complex elective and emergency vascular disease across Northern Ireland. The Trust currently has eight consultants in post with a further one WTE expected to take up post in February 2026. There are also a small number of general surgery consultants in other Trusts who undertake varicose vein procedures. The revised Elective Care Framework, which was published on 24 May 2025, sets out a five-year roadmap to reduce waiting times, including how earmarked funding would be invested across a range of initiatives to support waiting list reduction and strengthen core capacity. This includes specific funding for vascular services and my Department are currently working with Belfast Trust to agree the expansion of vascular theatre, bed and diagnostic imaging capacity, My Department is also working with the Northern Ireland Medical & Dental Training Agency to increase the number of medical trainees across a range of specialties, including vascular surgery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any financial assistance he plans for farms affected by the bluetongue virus within the exclusion zone.
answer_text: There is no direct financial assistance planned at this time. Compensation is payable, however, in instances where my Department directs animals to be culled. My Department’s Veterinary Service is undertaking significant surveillance and testing, where necessary, aiming to stem any potential spread of disease for the benefit of local farmers and the wider agri-food industry.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant AQW 35025/22-27, to detail the evidential basis as to why this legislation will apply to motorcyclists, as well as other motor vehicles.
answer_text: Whilst it is my intention that this legislation would also apply to motorcycles to align with other parking contraventions across the North, my officials will give further consideration to this as the work progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the annual projected costs of her recently announced road network digital survey that will assess road conditions.
answer_text: On 2nd December, I was pleased to launch the new Road Maintenance Strategy which is now open for public consultation.The main objectives of the strategy are to improve the overall condition of the road network and enhance safety and quality, by delivering a targeted programme of intelligent maintenance investment and a sustainable maintenance regime. This means instead of filling one or two potholes and skipping over others, there will be more patch resurfacing and when this is done, the repairs will last longer because they will be better quality.  This means filling more potholes, where it is needed most.Through this new strategy, my Department will ensure that the road maintenance function is organised to improve day to day effectiveness and enhance our ability to respond to incidents and weather events.Along with this strategy my Department is investing £1.3m to deliver a complete digital survey of the entire road network in the North. This will provide a much greater level of understanding of road condition and allow my officials to take data-driven decisions and deliver more focused maintenance interventions saving money in the longer term.The value of deeper, more accurate information to support intelligent road maintenance is widely recognised. A growing number of Local Authorities are already benefiting from this type of roadscaping technology, however, the North is the first to collect such a vast data set to produce a jurisdiction wide road condition baseline. It’s a landmark initiative that, in just six months, will give my Department an unprecedented understanding of its road infrastructure to better target maintenance, reduce costs, and react quickly to future opportunities and innovations.The survey will be completed by the end of the 25/26 financial year and data will then be processed over a period of a couple of months and used to develop the future structural maintenance programme and inform future in-year monitoring rounds for 2026/27.However, it is important to recognize that these measures on their own will not address the consequences of years of under investment and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made to improve the quality of our road network and contribute to the delivery of the priorities set out in the agreed Programme for Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions his Department is taking to increase the number of specialist arterial vascular consultants.
answer_text: Demand for vascular surgery continues to rise, driven by an ageing population, an increased prevalence of diabetes and rising obesity levels. Emergency demand pressures are also significant, with ruptured aneurysms, acute limb ischaemia, and major trauma injuries all requiring urgent surgical attention. The vascular surgery service is a regional specialty based in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust which is responsible for managing complex elective and emergency vascular disease across Northern Ireland. The Trust currently has eight consultants in post with a further one WTE expected to take up post in February 2026. There are also a small number of general surgery consultants in other Trusts who undertake varicose vein procedures. The revised Elective Care Framework, which was published on 24 May 2025, sets out a five-year roadmap to reduce waiting times, including how earmarked funding would be invested across a range of initiatives to support waiting list reduction and strengthen core capacity. This includes specific funding for vascular services and my Department are currently working with Belfast Trust to agree the expansion of vascular theatre, bed and diagnostic imaging capacity, My Department is also working with the Northern Ireland Medical & Dental Training Agency to increase the number of medical trainees across a range of specialties, including vascular surgery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any plan she has to improve the employment rate for people with disabilities.
answer_text: Disability employment is the remit of the Minister for Communities who is currently consulting on a draft Disability and Work Strategy for the North. I am however committed to ensuring that everyone in society can access good jobs, no matter what their barriers. I am aware that the rate of employment for those who are classified as long-term sick or disabled is 40.6%, compared to an employment rate of 81.5% for those without a disability. There is clearly work to be done to address this issue.As such, my officials have worked closely with officials from the Department for Communities on the draft Disability and Work Strategy which proposes a range of commitments for my department centred around provision of specialist careers advice, and improved access to skills, education and training provision for disabled people and people with health conditions.  These proposals will enhance what we already do to help ensure equitable access.  In terms of Higher Education, the Department, through the Disabled Students' Allowance (DSA), provides support to students in higher education with disabilities, assisting them in obtaining the qualifications they need to pursue their chosen career path.  Work has also been progressing on a refreshed approach to Widening Participation with proposals co-designed with stakeholders.  A public consultation is now in the final stages of development.Disability support is available to support Apprenticeship NI and Higher Level Apprenticeship training while the Apprenticeship Inclusion Challenge Fund specifically promotes apprenticeship uptake from underrepresented groups, with two projects focusing on promoting apprenticeships for disabled people.Our range of skills provision is delivered through both Further and Higher Education institutions and includes specific programmes, funded through my Skills Fund, that support those with additional barriers including disability, such as Inclusive Futures and Step Up, one of the key strands of which is the Disability Access Route to Education and Employment.  In June 2025, following a comprehensive review, I outlined my proposals for a programme of reforms for school leavers with Special Educational Needs. Subject to appropriate investment, the changes will enhance the pathways and supports for school leavers who want to access the Department for the Economy’s education, skills and training provision.  These proposals have the potential to make a significant difference to school leavers with Special Educational Needs.  I intend to enhance flexible working rights, including improving access to flexible working from the first day of employment, in my forthcoming Employment Rights Bill. These positive measures should increase the opportunity for employees, including those with disabilities, to secure flexible working and the associated benefits of this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail her plans to tackle the rate of economic inactivity.
answer_text: The Department for Communities (DfC) leads on employability and reducing economic inactivity, but addressing this issue requires collaboration across multiple departments and agencies due to its complex causes, including health-related barriers. My department contributes significantly, particularly through commitments outlined in the draft Disability and Work Strategy, currently out for consultation. We have also worked closely with the Skills Council, its Equality, Diversity and Inclusion sub-committee (SEDI), Ulster University, and other stakeholders on initiatives such as the Epic Futures project, which seeks evidence-based solutions for fair and inclusive employment.Our department has enhanced pathways to education and training, making them more flexible and accessible through online learning, mentoring, and financial support where appropriate. Higher and Further Education Institutions, as well as other training providers, offer a range of opportunities with disability and childcare support available. Key programmes—such as Skills for Life and Work, Skill Up, Step Up, and All Age Apprenticeships—aim to broaden access, especially for those facing additional barriers.I also intend to strengthen flexible working rights in the forthcoming Employment Rights Bill, including access to flexible working from day one of employment. These measures are designed to support employees, particularly those with disabilities or caring responsibilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any strategies taken by (i) his Department; and (ii) the Health and Social Care Trusts, to treat peripheral arterial disease.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Clinical Guidelines are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here.  Where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. My Department endorsed NICE Clinical Guideline 147, on diagnosis and management of peripheral arterial disease in 2012 (updated 2020). It is the Department’s expectation that Trusts work to implement the guidance. Information and advice about the symptoms, causes and treatment of PAD is available on NI Direct at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/conditions/peripheral-arterial-disease-pad.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any plan she has to tackle our skills deficit
answer_text: In October, my department launched the Skills Action Plan, prioritising skills development to address current deficits and create opportunities across the North. The Plan builds on progress already made; the 2024 Employer Skills Survey reported skills-shortage vacancies have reduced from 13,700 to 7,500, with only 7% of employers now reporting skills related vacancies.Collaboration with Industry is at the heart of my Action Plan, with a focus on a sectoral approach to ensure the Economy has the skills and talent required to deliver my Economic Vision. This approach has worked to good effect through the Green Skills Delivery Group, with a wide range of business now engaged in the development of new Pathways and Curriculum. I recently launched the Apprenticeship Action Plan, which sets out the roadmap to improve the apprenticeship system by enhancing alignment with our economy’s needs.I have also prioritised a Skills Fund which supports over 20 projects aimed at providing both upskilling and reskilling solutions. These projects are making a significant impact by equipping individuals with the necessary skills to thrive in their respective fields and contribute to the overall growth of our economy.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Investment Strategy for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Work is ongoing to complete a draft Investment Strategy.Once finalised, the timeline for approval and publication will then be a matter for the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any (i) official procedure for a foster carer to quickly resolve a situation where their Universal Credit has been incorrectly assessed due to taking on an additional foster child; and (ii) accountability measures that exist should this occur due to departmental error.
answer_text: Fostering Allowance is disregarded in a claimant’s Universal Credit assessment. Universal Credit claimants receive a monthly statement of their assessment which includes signposting to tell Universal Credit if they think a mistake has been made. Claimants can raise their concerns via their online journal, by telephone, or by post.  Should a mistake occur in a claimant’s assessment calculation, my officials are committed to investigating and redressing errors promptly, ensuring accountability and learning is taken on board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures in place to allow advanced payments from the Strategic Planning and Performance Group to support patients who have to travel to other parts of the UK for specialised medical treatment, but do not have disposable income to pay and reclaim expenses.
answer_text: The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) aims to process and approve patient travel claims within 30 days for all patients approved to travel to access treatments outside Northern Ireland under the Extra Contractual Referral (ECR) process.  Patients may be approved under the ECR process to tertiary healthcare providers in England, Scotland and Wales and occasionally to Republic of Ireland.  Once claims are approved, they are forwarded to the Business Service Organisation who issue the payment to the patient.  Historically, BSO processed payments within an average of 10 days (2020-2025).  Since April 2025, the payment process has been streamlined and payments are now issued by BSO within three working days, with current compliance at 100%.Average SPPG Processing Time (Days) by Year and Postcode AreaThe table below provides the SPPG’s average processing time for approval of claims in the last five years broken down by Trust.  BHSCT and SET are currently not separated, as current recording process determines Trust by post code, however plans are in place to change this.   Year2020/212021/222022/232023/242024/25BHSCT & SET930392215NHSCT729402015SHSCT828402314WHSCT828382315   Patient Travel Support The Strategic Planning and Performance Group supports patients who must travel for specialist treatment outside of Northern Ireland, as outlined in the Patient Travel Policy which can be found Transfer for treatment by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group - DOH/HSCNI Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG).  The SPPG policy is to book the most cost effective travel arrangements and key element include:-Flights or ferry: this will normally be booked and paid for by the SPPG/Trust; Accommodation: Use hospital provider on- site accommodation is expected when available.  If unavailable the SPPG will reimburse the accommodation costs (room only rate) up to a maximum allowance, usually 3 star (at £125 per night).  The SPPG patient travel team will book and pay for accommodation directly upon request;Escorts:  The SPPG/Trust will fund one patient escort where this is justified on the basis of clinical need of the patient as demonstrated by the referring consultant.  Both parents are approved and funded to travel with paediatric patient.  Transport:  onward public transport costs are reimbursed on receipt.  Taxi costs may be reimbursed if more cost effective than the public transport rate or if deemed clinically necessary by the referring consultant.  Taxi and trains fare can also be booked and paid for by SPPG in exceptional financial hardship circumstances;Subsistence costs of £15 per day per adult.  Patients who are inpatients in hospital receiving treatment will not qualify for subsistence as meals are provided by the healthcare provider.    The Patient Travel Team are very mindful of the current cost of living crisis and aim to support patients as much as possible within the terms of policy arrangements.  Claims are processed in

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps being taken to ensure that fluctuations in a foster carer's income, due to taking on additional placements, do not trigger incorrect automated assessments, suspensions, or overpayment claims, from the Department for Communities.
answer_text: Foster Carers receive a Fostering Allowance for the care they provide; this is disregarded in the claimant’s Universal Credit assessment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement she has had with HM Government on the Planning and Infrastructure Bill.
answer_text: Following the introduction of the Planning and Infrastructure Bill into Westminster, Matthew Pennycook MP, Minister for Housing and Planning in the Ministry for Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG), contacted me and a number of Ministers from other Departments. Since March 2025, I have been involved in a series of correspondence with MHCLG in relation to amendments to the Bill as it progresses through the Westminster legislative process. There has also been direct engagement between my officials and officials from MHCLG.I am content that we have assurances from MHCLG that the provisions of the Bill do not adversely impact on devolved matters.
