question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any (i) guidance issued to (a) Health and Social Care Trusts; and (b) fostering service providers, on the treatment of foster care allowances in the assessment of Universal Credit; and (ii) steps being taken to ensure that potential foster carers are given correct and full information to make an informed decision on becoming a carer, particularly in relation to financial penalties within the benefits system.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any safeguards in place to ensure that foster carers who respond to urgent requests from Health and Social Care Trusts to take additional children are not financially penalised with regards to their (i) Universal Credit; or other benefits, claims.
answer_text: Foster Carers who are in receipt of Universal Credit are not financially penalised for providing care. The Foster Care Allowance they receive is disregarded from the assessment of their Universal Credit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of schools which currently have evacuation chairs for pupils, staff or visitors with mobility impairments, for use in the event of an emergency.
answer_text: The School Building Handbook does not set any requirements for evacuation chairs. The requirement for evacuation chairs is based on a fire risk assessment to determine if it is necessary as part of a building's fire evacuation plan.The Education Authority has advised that there are currently 80 schools which have evacuation chairs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given the cost of placements for children in residential care, to detail any steps in place to encourage and support foster carers to apply without being penalised by the Universal Credit system.
answer_text: The following response is provided on the basis that the question is in relation to encouraging more applications for  people to become a foster carer and to be supported in that role. Foster Carers are entitled to an allowance to cover the cost of caring for children requiring foster care in their home. Allowances are set at a regional level, and by age (banding). Within the current benefits system, the allowances received by foster carers in return for fostering are fully disregarded for the purposes of calculating entitlement to benefits. For the purposes of calculating income related benefits, children placed within a home on a fostering basis are not included in the benefit assessment. HSC Trust staff discuss financial circumstances with prospective foster carers as part of the enquiry to foster process and can also signpost prospective foster carers to seek independent advice and guidance on this topic from The Fostering Network.People applying to become foster carers are supported through the assessment process, this support continues if they are approved and go on to provide care for children and young people.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether he will review the use of town centre boundaries, as defined in extant area plans, including the Ards and Down Area Plan 2015, as a criterion for determining business eligibility for departmental grant schemes.
answer_text: Any changes to the town centre boundaries would need to be brought forward through the relevant Council. If, a Council revises their boundaries, the Department will consider reviewing its operational approach.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his Department's policy on the treatment of income from foster care allowance, in the assessment of Universal Credit claims.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any official escalation and redress procedure for a foster carer who has received incorrect advice from his Department, leading to a financial loss, or the unwarranted overpayment or removal of childcare support, or closure of their claim.
answer_text: My Department is committed to providing the highest standard of service across all areas. While I accept that mistakes can at times occur my officials are committed to investigating and redressing any errors in a timely manner. In taking account of a suitable remedy for redress, should that be applicable, consideration must be taken on an individual case basis where a claimant wishes to escalate their concerns. In addition to apologising and aiming to put things right, extra-statutory or ex-gratia payments, and decisions on overpayment recoverability, may be considered as part of the complaints process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any verifiable steps being taken to ensure that all Universal Credit staff consistently provide accurate, timely and complete information to potential foster parents, ensuring they understand that they will not be financially penalised for providing care.
answer_text: In addition to training, Universal Credit staff have access to the relevant guidance to ensure that they have the necessary information to inform potential foster parents that there is no financial penalty for those providing foster care. Staff are regularly reminded of the importance of providing timely and accurate information. Internal checks are also in place to help ensure the consistency of a quality service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any mandatory training provided to (i) Universal Credit frontline staff offering advice in community settings; and (ii) Online Work Coaches, on the treatment of fostering income, particularly regarding fostering allowances, and the application of Qualifying Care Relief for self-assessment purposes.
answer_text: Universal Credit staff are fully trained to provide a supportive quality service; however, frontline staff who work in community settings do not provide advice, rather they provide information and signposting. Work Coaches receive mandatory training on the treatment of ‘other’ income, which includes that fostering income is wholly disregarded by Universal Credit. Self-assessment is not a function within Universal Credit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) how fostering allowance is considered as part of the monthly Universal Credit assessment period, given that foster carers incur significant and often uneven expenses throughout the year, including costs for setting up a bedroom, purchasing bedding and equipment, and ensuring the foster child is fully included in family life through holidays, birthdays, and other events; (ii) any guidance in place to ensure (a) that this non-regular expenditure pattern is fully considered; and (b) that fostering allowance continues to be disregarded when calcula
answer_text: Universal Credit wholly disregards Fostering Allowance when calculating a claimant’s Universal Credit entitlement, and this is reflected in Universal Credit guidance. There is no financial penalty for those people providing care. Universal Credit does not assess the income or expenses of claimants in relation to the care they provide.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, given the £40 million investment in the Bangor Waterfront redevelopment through the Belfast Region City Deal, to detail any proactive steps being taken to prepare and build the capacity of local frontline community groups, particularly in sectors like heritage, arts, health, and leisure, to enable them to successfully tender for and deliver high-quality, high-impact social value outcomes as part of this development.
answer_text: Ards and North Down Borough Council, as Project Promoter, is responsible for the delivery and reporting of Social Value outcomes which will be achieved through appointed consultancy teams and contractors.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the location of the 130km of greenways in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: As of April 2025, data received from councils shows that approximately 130km (32%) of the Primary Network has been delivered. I will deposit a copy of the map showing the location of greenways which make up the 130km in the NI Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, given the £40 million investment in the Bangor Waterfront redevelopment through the Belfast Region City Deal, to detail (i) any social value plans, beyond traditional apprenticeships, that have been mandated for the Bangor element of the deal, to ensure measurable benefits are delivered to local communities.
answer_text: Ards and North Down Borough Council, as Project Promoter, is responsible for the delivery and reporting of Social Value outcomes which will be achieved through appointed consultancy teams and contractors.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any progress their Department has made in delivering on its commitment to end violence against women and girls, including how this has been reflected in actions undertaken during the 16 Days of Activism against Gender-Based Violence.
answer_text: As an Executive, we are determined to do all that we can to end violence against women and girls.We have said from the outset that this was an issue we are committed to tackling, and we have made significant progress since we launched the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework and Delivery Plan in September last year.We are driving meaningful change across communities and organisations, with visible impact in schools, sports clubs, and the community and voluntary sectors.We are getting money directly to organisations with the experience to make a difference. Through the Change Fund, over £3.2 million has been invested in community-based action to support prevention and cultural change.We have also invested an additional £2.2 million to build capacity in the community and voluntary sector, amplify key messages, and scale delivery.A few weeks ago, Junior Minister Reilly and the deputy First Minister attended the launch of the Fair Game Initiative which we are supporting through the Change Fund. This innovative programme, which is a collaboration with the Irish Football Association, Women’s Aid and the PSNI, uses sport to challenge harmful attitudes and create safe environments for young people both on and off the pitch. It was such a positive event and highlighted what can be done when we all work together to address these issues.The 16 days of activism against gender-based violence has also been an opportunity to reinforce our messaging and to engage with our partners. Last week, we issued an Executive statement reaffirming our determination to tackle all forms of gender-based violence, abuse, and harm.We have also supported events both here in Parliament Buildings and on our social media platforms including an event with Women’s Aid and the Mothers’ Union, to amplify our collective approach to dealing with this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the rationale as to why her Department has set a target of over 200 kilometers of active travel infrastructure in the first ten years, yet for years ten to twenty, her Department target is over 1000 kilometers.
answer_text: The draft Active Travel Delivery Plan, issued for public consultation in November 2024, set out plans for the development of over 200km of high-quality active travel infrastructure in urban areas outside Belfast over the next 10 years. Beyond that, later phases are expected to deliver over 1,000km of future routes.The 200km represents a reasonable initial assessment of what could be delivered through the Active Travel Delivery Plan based on future budgets that meet the requirements of Section 22 of the Climate Change Act by 2030 and continue at that level into the future. It does not include active travel network development included within the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan, or delivery of greenways that are part funded by my Department and typically delivered by local councils.The proposed priority routes in the draft Active Travel Delivery Plan are those that have been assessed as having the greatest potential within our larger towns and cities to deliver benefits in terms of encouraging more people to walk, wheel or cycle for many of their shorter everyday journeys. At this stage it is difficult to provide a more accurate estimate of the capacity for delivery, as capacity will be refined over time as schemes from the plan come through to delivery.The 1,000km referred to as future routes within the draft Active Travel Delivery Plan represents what comprehensive, connected future networks would look like.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan and its 2025 targets.
answer_text: Significant progress has been made on the majority of short term schemes in the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan. Street lighting has been provided on the urban section of Comber Greenway and sections of Forth Meadow Greenway and Lagan Gateway have been delivered in partnership with Belfast City Council. Work is also complete on the upgrade to existing facilities on Stranmillis Embankment and the schemes on Lagmore Avenue, West Belfast Greenway Phase 1a and Island Street are now in construction. Design is substantially complete on the Montgomery Road, Sydenham Greenway Phase 1 and Durham Street schemes, all of which are currently at varying stages of the statutory process related to legislating the proposed cycle routes before construction can commence. A further eight schemes are at early or design stage with stakeholder engagement complete for the Sailortown & Ulster University scheme and a Pre-Application Community Consultation has been undertaken for West Belfast Greenway Phase 1b. It is expected that the statutory consultation process will commence in early 2026 for the Ravenhill Road scheme. Future schemes will be prioritised with consideration to the emerging Eastern Transport Plan and other significant changes that have occurred in Belfast in recent years, not least the relocation of Ulster University and the increased numbers of students now living in the city centre.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the rationale as to why domestic customers cannot have their gas and electricity provider under the same bill, as customers can in GB.
answer_text: Customers in Britain can receive a single bill for both gas and electricity because in their market some suppliers choose to provide this. Some suppliers in the North do supply both gas and electricity to the same customer, however, to date have not chosen to invoice on the same bill.Customers could lobby their supplier to provide this service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any discussions he has had with the Chancellor of The Exchequer, since he came into post.
answer_text: I met with the Chancellor of The Exchequer in August of this year.I raised the need to progress negotiations on the Executive’s full Fiscal Framework including the need for additional fiscal devolution and Executive borrowing powers. Alongside the Deputy First Minister, I emphasised the need for the Autumn Budget to support growth and continued investment in, and transformation of, our public services. I also stressed that any tax and spend decisions taken in the Autumn Budget should fully consider the impact that they have on our people and local businesses.Additionally, I have held numerous bi-lateral and joint Devolved Government Finance Minister meetings with the current and former Chief Secretaries to the Treasury.  These bi-lateral engagements focused on the specific challenges facing the Executive and our priorities for upcoming spending reviews and fiscal events. Joint discussions, held alongside my fellow Devolved Government Finance Ministers, provided a valuable platform to collectively raise and address matters of shared interest with the Chief Secretary.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions she has had with the Utility Regulator, regarding the lack of competition in the local gas market.
answer_text: The local gas retail market has been fully open to supply competition for several years and a number of companies are active in supplying the business sector. Where the incumbent suppliers, SSE Airtricity and Firmus Energy, retain a dominant market share in the domestic sector, they are subject to a regulated tariff regime. As part of the tariff review process, which is carried out in liaison with the Department and the Consumer Council, the Utility Regulator scrutinises every element of the gas tariff to ensure it reflects the actual cost of purchasing and supplying gas to homes and small businesses. This ensures that gas consumers do not pay any more than necessary for their gas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) any Ministerial visits to North Down; and (ii) their purpose, since February 2024.
answer_text: I have not had the opportunity to date to undertake any Ministerial visits to North Down.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the new build school for Crawfordsburn Primary School.
answer_text: The contract for the construction of the new school building for Crawfordsburn Primary School was awarded to Bell Contracts Limited on 14 May 2025. The construction phase commenced in September 2025 and is progressing well. The build is programmed to run over 18 months, with the final phase of works being the demolition of the old school building and associated site works.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 35132/22-27, to detail why her Department does not hold, or has not sought, information in relation to this.
answer_text: Information on defendants in troubles related criminal cases is not held by the Department of Justice. While the Northern Ireland Courts & Tribunals Service does hold certain information, for example in relation to the use of non-jury Crown cases, this information is not solely related to troubles related cases nor sufficient to allow for the identification of types of defendants, as originally requested.As stated previously, this is an operational matter, and I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Director for Public Prosecutions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the timeframe for the full delivery of the Belfast Region City Deal, Bangor Waterfront project.
answer_text: In line with the latest programme submitted by Ards and North Down Borough Council, construction is expected to commence in 2027 following Full Business Case approval, with completion in Autumn 2030.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the new build school for Bangor Central Integrated Primary School.
answer_text: Procurement of a building contractor for the new build school is underway. The return date for tenders has been extended until the end of November 2025 due to clarification requests from tenderers. It is still anticipated that construction of the new school will commence on site in the first quarter of 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any planned capital works in schools, in North Down.
answer_text: There are currently eight capital projects being progressed in North Down.The status of these projects is detailed below.Major Capital Works ProgrammeBangor Central Integrated Primary School - Procurement of a building contractor for the new build school is underway and it is anticipated that construction of the new school will commence on site in the first quarter of 2026.Priory Integrated College - The final stage of design for the new build school has been completed.  Due to an increase in costs a Business Case Addendum (BCA) is being progressed by the Department’s economists. Following completion of the BCA this project will be put on hold until there is budget available to move to the construction stage. Crawfordsburn Primary School - The construction of the new build school commenced in September 2025.  The programme estimates an eighteen-month construction period. School Enhancement Programme St Columbanus’ College – The project proposal is to construct a new teaching block to accommodate Science, PE, Careers and Sixth Form facilities. The project is in the later stages of design. Glencraig Integrated Primary School – The project proposal is for extension and refurbishment to enhance the existing classrooms, provide ensuite cloakrooms, pupil toilets, resource and multi-purpose spaces.  A technical feasibility report to explore options to deliver the proposals has been completed and a Business Case to determine the preferred option is being considered by the Department. Killcooley Primary School - The project proposal is extensions and/or refurbishment of existing teaching accommodation, support and ancillary spaces and general deficiencies as identified through the site.  A technical feasibility report to explore options to deliver the proposals has been completed and a Business Case to determine the preferred option is being considered by the Department.Sullivan Upper School - The project proposal is an extension to provide new staffroom and classrooms with associated resource space and refurbishment of other areas within the school.  The project is in the early stage of design.A decision to release the School Enhancement Programme projects to the construction stage will be taken on a case-by-case basis, dependent on budget availability.Special Educational Needs Capital Investment Programme Clifton School – Following approval of a School Enhancement Programme project in November 2024 a decision was taken to extend the scope of this project and transfer the project to the Special Educational Needs Capital Investment Programme.  The proposal is to provide a double-storey extension that will include a sports hall and supporting facilities, an additional four classrooms, a multi-purpose room, enhanced school meals accommodation and improvements to the external area around the school.  The revised designs for the extended project were submitted to the Department in September 2025, and a revised Business Case is in progress. As I have previousl

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department is assessing options to expand park and ride capacity, in North Down.
answer_text: Enhancing connectivity is a key priority of the Department through public transport and active travel, including opportunities for advanced park and ride schemes.  As such, funding requirements for park and ride schemes throughout the north continues to be submitted on an annual basis. My Department has been operating in difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity and as the capital budget for the Department continues to be less than required, available funding, including that for Park and Ride, must be prioritised accordingly. At present we are progressing Park & Ride proposals at Comber and Newtownards.The preferred site in Comber is located along the A22 Belfast Road corridor immediately adjacent to the Comber Greenway with the potential to provide parking for 410 vehicles in the first phase with scope for further extension. Appraisal and design works are ongoing to enable the submission of a planning application.I can advise that my Department is also progressing the provision of the Newtownards Park and Ride facility at William Street.  Since the land was vested, the Department, with assistance from the Departmental Solicitor’s Office (DSO), has spent a considerable amount of time addressing complex legal title issues in conjunction with Ards and North Down Borough Council.I understand that these issues have largely been resolved with the matter having recently been referred to Land and Property Services (LPS) with a view to determining formal valuation and compensation. On receipt of the Valuer’s Report the Department will issue further instructions to DSO to address any remaining legal matters and to complete the purchase of the land.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of whether domestic cats are members of the family and should be afforded the same protections as dogs.
answer_text: Under the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, cats are classified as ‘protected animals’, which means their welfare needs are enshrined in law. All protected animals, regardless of species, are afforded the same legal protections. I am pleased to advise that Animal Welfare Pathway 2025-27 includes reforms which are aimed at improving traceability and welfare standards of both cats and dogs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any safeguards in place to ensure that the public can access free GP services in the event that GPs withdraw from their contracts.
answer_text: I have been clear that I, and my Department, remain fully committed to the principle that health should be free at the point of service.  This includes the service provided by GPs.  My priority has been, and continues to be, to ensure that patients can continue to access safe, high quality, sustainable GP services when they need to.  My Department is continuing to take measures, both in the short and longer term, to secure improved sustainability of General Practice provision and ensure better support for the delivery of these vitally important services.  Despite the pressures on General Practice, with the support of GP Federations and Health and Social Care Trusts in taking on GP contracts, my Department has been able to ensure that in recent years, only one contract hand-back has resulted in practice closure. In terms of short-term measures in response to emerging difficulties, the General Practice Improvement and Crisis Response Team is able to support practices which are at risk to develop and implement effective practice recovery plans to ensure that they can deliver their contractual obligations to their registered patients.Looking to the future of General Practice, I am committed to working with GPs on the future of primary care services and that we continue to engage constructively with General Practice.  I have written  to the Chair of Northern Ireland General Practitioners Committee seeking their agreement to an approach to the GMS contract for 2026/27 and wider issues in General Practice, with the aim of securing the long-term future of General Practice over the coming years and reflecting my ambitions for General Practice to be at the heart of the neighbourhood model of care outlined in my Department’s recently published Health and Social Care Reset Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has raised the issue of current firework regulations and enforcement on the grounds of animal welfare with the Minister of Justice.
answer_text: I am aware of concerns about the impact of fireworks on animal welfare. The Department of Justice (DoJ) has responsibility for licensing fireworks in Northern Ireland, and I understand that fireworks licences may include a condition to notify landowners and farmers prior to any displays.		I have agreed to attend a meeting with the All-Party Group on Animal Welfare to listen to members’ views on this matter in the New Year, and I am open to discussing the issue further with the DoJ thereafter.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action being taking by the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust to provide Changing Places facilities across its hospital estate.
answer_text: Since June 2022 amendments to building regulations requires that all new build hospitals and primary care buildings and those whole hospital or primary care buildings formed by a material change of use of existing facilities should provide at least one changing places facility.    The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust will continue to design new buildings and refurbishment projects to comply with the latest Building Regulation requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any work her Department is undertaking with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, through the NI Coastal Forum, to ensure actions, addressing coastal erosion with links to the Blue Carbon Action Plan 2025-30, are being addressed.
answer_text: Minister Muir and I co-Chaired a meeting of the Coastal Forum on 19 November which included updates on work being undertaken to demonstrate the potential role that blue carbon habitats can perform as nature-based solutions for coastal adaptation. The Coastal Forum has asked the Coastal Forum Working Group to produce a stocktake report of the Coastal Forum Work Programme 2021-2025 and make recommendations for a 2026-2031 Work Programme.This review will provide an opportunity to ensure that new policies and legislation that have been introduced since 2021, such as the Blue Carbon Action Plan, are included in the updated work programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 32114/22-27, for an update on the outcome of the consultation on the requirement for a GP to countersign a health declaration for prospective childcare workers.
answer_text: My officials have been analysing all 495 responses to the consultation exercise, and I will be taking decisions on proposed next steps in the coming weeks.   If it is considered that any urgent changes should be made to the Minimum Standards in advance of the completion of the wider review, these changes will be communicated through a Departmental Press Release, a Circular to HSC Trusts and a letter to all childcare providers.The full consultation response document will also be published on the Departmental website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any action he plans to take to protect cats from cruelty, exploitation and suffering.
answer_text: The welfare of cats is protected under The Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 (“the Act”). The Act makes unnecessary suffering an offence and states that a person in charge of an animal, including cats, must ensure the needs of the animal are met.		My Animal Welfare Pathway 2025-27 includes reforms to enhance protections for companion animals, including a ban on third-party pet sales and exploration of mandatory cat microchipping.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any work her Department is undertaking to address the planning implications of offshore renewable energy infrastructure, where cabling is coming onshore, in line with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs and the Department for the Economy.
answer_text: The Department for the Economy has established the Offshore Renewable Energy Action Plan (OREAP) Steering Group which brings together key delivery partners to develop and deliver the action plan for offshore renewable energy in the North of Ireland. My Departmental planning officials are involved in this group.I and my officials will continue to collaborate with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, the Department for the Economy and relevant stakeholders in relation to the matter of offshore renewable energy infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to outline the cost of the Official Report Interpretation and Translation Service in the last two years.
To ask the Minister of Justice to outline why she has no plans to widen support to personal litigants accessing pro bono assistance, beyond housing and family court matters.
answer_text: ANSWER – AQO 2800/22-27Pro bono assistance, legal advice at no cost, is primarily a decision for legal professionals, and would not be paid for by the Department. While my Department does fund free emergency advice at court for those facing homelessness, through the Housing Possession Court Duty Scheme, I am focusing resources on enhancing access to justice in other ways, through my Department’s Enabling Access to Justice Reform Programme.  This includes:  simplifying the process of accessing legal aid and ensuring scope and financial eligibility rules are balanced in a way which enables the most acute needs to be addressed for the widest number of citizens; ensuring the wider needs of citizens are addressed at the initial point of contact with the system with the introduction of a model of support for specific vulnerable groups beginning with victims of domestic abuse; introducing measures to prevent the system and legal aid being weaponised to perpetuate abuse; and embracing technological solutions and enhancing the use of alternative resolution mechanisms to reduce the impact of the adversarial court process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps her Department has (i) put in place; and (ii) plans to put in place, to promote greater inclusion of the benefits of nature-based solutions in the planning process, for example coastal adaptation and water quality improvements, to support blue-green infrastructure initiatives.
answer_text: I recognise that the two-tier planning system plays an important supporting role in the promotion and implementation of nature-based solutions, including taking into account the expertise of statutory consultees such as the Department of Agriculture, Environment & Rural Affairs (DAERA) in respect of impacts on the natural environment, habitats and biodiversity, and DfI Rivers in relation to flood risk, during the plan-making and decision-taking process. My Department’s Strategic Planning Policy Statement (SPPS) provides regional strategic planning policy for a range of topics including Furthering Sustainable Development, which is at the heart of the SPPS, as well as Mitigating and Adapting to Climate Change and it emphasises the importance of Ecosystem Services. The SPPS core planning principle ‘Preserving and Improving the Built and Natural Environment’ recognises that the planning system plays an important role in conserving, protecting and enhancing the environment whilst ensuring it remains responsive and adaptive to the everyday needs of society, as well as halting the loss of biodiversity. The SPPS also directs that when plan-making and decision-taking, planning authorities should contribute positively to health and well-being through, inter alia, facilitating the protection and provision of green infrastructure such as parks, green spaces and street trees and blue infrastructure such as ponds, streams and lakes. My Department’s Regional Development Strategy (RDS) 2035 also highlights the need to provide adequate provision for green and blue infrastructure in cities, towns and neighbourhoods, and new developments.Furthermore, my Department has an important oversight role to ensure that council’s emerging local development plans (LDPs) take account of the RDS 2035 and SPPS, including in relation to green and blue infrastructure. During the plan preparation stage, councils engage with stakeholders (such as the Coastal Forum Working Group), as part of evidence gathering to support the formulation of their local policies. Councils also consult with relevant statutory bodies, such as DfI Water Drainage & Policy Division and DfI Rivers to ensure the appropriate sustainable environmental solutions are considered in their Plan Strategies such as nature-based Sustainable Drainage Systems (SuDS). To date six of the eleven councils have an adopted Plan Strategy in place, and two councils have their draft Plan Strategy published, all of which have included nature-based solutions such as coastal and water quality improvements. My officials have worked with representatives of NI Water and the Special EU Programmes Body to launch the Water Enhancements through Sustainable Treatment (WEST) project, which is a cross-border initiative between NI Water and Uisce Éireann to improve water quality in Lough Erne, Lough Melvin, and Donegal Bay, which face toxic pollution issues from agriculture, forestry, urban runoff, and wastewater discharges. The WEST p

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action being taken between NI Water and her Department, to resolve a leaking pipe that for months has affected Park Drive, Bangor.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information.NI Water has advised that a field technician attended the site on 15 October 2025, to investigate the leak and confirmed that the run of water was caused by a private leak. Consequently, NI Water referred this matter to its Leakage Department for further review, and on the 21 October 2025, it was confirmed that there was a private leak coming from the properties at Castle Street. The local residents are in the process of trying to resolve the issue, however, it may take several weeks for it to be resolved.NI Water has also advised the residents to update NI Water once the leak has been capped.If you have any further queries on this matter, please contact NI Water on their number for Elected Representatives 0345 300 6461, or via waterline@niwater.com.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 35025/22-27 and AQW 33012/22-27, whether her Department has any plans to improve the provision of motorcycle parking.
answer_text: I currently have no plans to increase the provision of motorcycle-specific parking spaces across the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he plans to introduce compulsory microchipping for domestic cats.
answer_text: As set out in my Animal Welfare Pathway 2025-27, I have committed to gathering an evidence base about mandatory microchipping of cats and it is my intention to consult on the matter early in 2026.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she plans to (i) restart hot food for jurors; or (ii) allow facilities for jurors to get hot meals.
answer_text: The provision of jury meals is an administrative matter managed at operational level by the NI Courts and Tribunals Service (NICTS).Prior to the COVID-19 pandemic, jurors may have been provided with a sandwich lunch or hot meal for longer trials. During the pandemic, the NICTS implemented a range of measures designed to protect all court users and mitigate against the risk of disease. One of the measures implemented at that time was to cease providing jurors with a meal and to replace this with a meal allowance. Given the unpredictable nature of court business, previous arrangements often resulted in significant amounts of food having to be discarded. The current arrangements, by which jurors are provided with tea and coffee making facilities and claim a meal allowance when appropriate, reduces the amount of food waste.	Each year NICTS supports approximately 9,000 jurors and there is little evidence that the current arrangements, which are explained in advance, are not working for jurors or that there are significant issues. This is evidenced by the fact that, in the last 3 years, a total of 3 complaints have been received via the NICTS complaints process about the provision of food for jurors. Jury facilities will be considered as part of the implementation of the NICTS Estate Strategy. NICTS recently launched a survey on the quality of court buildings and facilities. This includes jury facilities and will help inform future plans. In the meantime, NICTS remains committed to the substantive work of delivering justice via high quality court and tribunal services. Senior officials being forced to spend time answering administrative queries about food provision detracts from this mission and does nothing to support the needs of victims and witnesses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action her Department is taking with Northern Ireland Electricity Networks to reinstate the public realm footpath in Main Street, Bangor, adjacent to the ASDA superstore.
answer_text: Utility companies are responsible for the permanent reinstatement of roads and footways to an agreed specification.  It is common practice for Utility companies to carry out interim reinstatements in public realm areas to keep the area safe for the travelling public. Under the Street Works (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 utility providers have up to 6 months to source materials and complete a permanent repair.My Department is aware that NIE is currently sourcing material to permanently complete the reinstatement on the Main Street in Bangor.I appreciate that the use of temporary bituminous surfacing within areas of public realm improvement can be visually unappealing, however, a permanent reinstatement using materials sympathetic to the original scheme will be completed in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the third sector organisations that receive annual funding from his Department, broken down by (i) the funding provided; and (ii) the reason for the funding.
answer_text: The annual funding provided to third sector organisations by my Department is as follows:Third Sector OrganisationAnnual Funding Amount NI Civil Service Sports Association Limited£102,000Charity for Civil Servants£82,000The Somme Association£47,000The reason for the funding provided to the above third sector organisations is as follows:NI Civil Service Sports AssociationThe purpose of this grant is to assist the delivery of the WELL Programme to NICS staff. The WELL Programme aims to improve the health and wellbeing of staff through the provision of support, education and information on a wide range of health and wellbeing issues. Funding is provided to support the administration relating to the delivery of activities.Charity for Civil ServantsThe purpose of this grant is to assist the Charity for Civil Servants to alleviate hardship among serving and former NICS staff, their families, and their dependants through the provision of financial assistance and other support and wellbeing services.  This funding is provided specifically to support the administration of activities and accommodation costs here.The Somme AssociationThe Somme Association has responsibility for the Ulster Memorial Tower on the site of the Battle of the Somme near Thiepval in France and Thiepval Wood. In 1928, the NI Government assumed financial responsibility for the future upkeep of the memorial and since 1997 an annual allocation of £47,000 is provided to fund salaries for the custodians of the tower and make a contribution towards the Director's salary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the third sector organisations that receive annual funding from his Department, broken down by (i) the funding provided; and (ii) the reason for the funding.
answer_text: I refer you to my response to AQW 23380/22-27 (5 March 2025) which detailed external organisations in receipt of financial support from this Department for 2024/25 which is the latest information available.This Department awards a total of £1.8m annually in the Core Grant Funding Scheme. Core Grant does not fund service delivery but is awarded to voluntary and community sector organisations which are, through the work of the organisation, contributing towards the outcomes envisaged by key departmental strategic and policy initiatives. The list of recipients each year is determined by a competitive application process, and there is no guarantee that a current recipient will receive future funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has considered a reduction of rates relief for a derelict building as part of the measures to address dereliction in the Dilapidation Bill.
answer_text: Derelict property is not encompassed within the rating system. For a property to be included within the rating system it has to be capable of beneficial occupation in line with valuation caselaw and the provisions of the Rates (Northern Ireland) Order 1977. Vacant property contained within the rating valuation list that may or may not be in a poor state of repair but is deemed capable of beneficial occupation is currently rated.On 18 November 2025 the Finance Minister laid out his planned policy direction for the rating system and noted his view that to tackle the high prevalence of vacant units work now needs to be undertaken to move the Non-Domestic Vacant Rate from 50% to 75% and then to 100%.I will have no powers under the Dilapidation Bill to consider a reduction of rates relief for any derelict building.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps he is taking to ensure that vets and private customers are able to receive supplies of veterinary medicines from suppliers based in Great Britain from 1 January 2026.
answer_text: In line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the supply of veterinary medicines to Northern Ireland falls under the Direction and Control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra).Nevertheless, I remain committed to do all I can to ensure the continued supply of veterinary medicines into Northern Ireland, including from Great Britain.I welcome the publication of the UK Government’s new Protecting Animal Health policy in June 2025 and the practical solutions which it provides to ensure the continued supply of veterinary medicines in Northern Ireland.  This policy supports the rollout of two new schemes, which are designed to provide a responsive, adaptable and flexible means of addressing supply issues that arise with veterinary medicines, including unforeseen issues. I believe that these new schemes offer sustainable solutions and represent a significant step forward in securing the future of our local veterinary medicines supply. Over the time ahead I will continue to engage with UK Government and the Veterinary Medicines Working Group, on the important detail, governance, and oversight arrangements to ensure that these schemes deliver the outcomes they are designed to achieve.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail what discussions he has held with HM Government regarding extending the grace periods for the supply of veterinary medicines to Northern Ireland.
answer_text: In line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the supply of veterinary medicines to Northern Ireland falls under the Direction and Control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra).		On 11 July 2024 I wrote to the Rt Hon Hilary Benn MP Secretary of State for Northern Ireland and Steve Reed OBE MP Secretary of State for Defra, copying in Nick Thomas-Symonds MP, Paymaster General and Minister for the Cabinet Office proposing the urgent need for an extension to the current grace period.I also sit on the UK Government’s Veterinary Medicines Working Group, jointly chaired by Baroness Hayman, which includes veterinary experts, industry representatives, and political stakeholders.  In this forum, I can continue to provide my expertise and advice to the UK Government on potential measures to prevent disruption to the future supply of veterinary medicines. I welcome the UK Government’s publication of the Protecting Animal Health policy in June 2025, and the practical solutions it offers to prevent restrictions on access to veterinary medicines in Northern Ireland.  This policy underpins the rollout of two new support schemes, designed to provide a responsive, adaptable, and flexible approach to addressing supply challenges with veterinary medicines, including unforeseen issues.I will continue to engage with the UK Government on the need to maintain the supply of veterinary medicines into Northern Ireland to protect animal health and welfare now and beyond the end of the grace period.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any steps his Department is taking to raise awareness to support earlier presentation among women at risk from ovarian cancer.
answer_text: The prevention and early detection of cancer is more important than ever both in terms of ensuring effective diagnosis, treatment and the best outcomes for patients. This is especially true for rare cancers, such as ovarian cancer, because the symptoms are often non-specific and only appear in advanced stages. The Public Health Agency (PHA) works closely with the Department of Health, local Health and Social Care Trusts, and other partners to support a range of activities to raise awareness of ovarian cancer symptoms and services, including: Ovarian Cancer Awareness Month - each March the PHA issue communications and social media messaging to increase awareness of ovarian cancer. The PHA uses social media channels to provide cancer awareness messaging, and information on the signs and symptoms of cancer. These posts provide information on the support available and potential steps people can make to help reduce their risk of developing cancer. The following public awareness campaigns - ‘Be Cancer Aware’, and ‘Spot Cancer Early’ campaigns ran across 500 community pharmacies in Northern Ireland as part of the Living Well service, to increase awareness of the signs and symptoms of cancer, including ovarian cancer. In addition, the Northern Ireland Cancer Network (NICaN) Gynaecology Clinical Reference Group are currently reviewing the primary care guidance for suspected gynaecological cancers, including the red flag referral criteria for ovarian cancer that will support the earlier diagnosis and treatment of this cancer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to improve access to neurology services.
answer_text: I recognise that current Neurology services are under considerable pressure. In the context of those pressures my Department’s Regional Review of Neurology report proposes a range of recommendations in four priority areas to deliver improvements in services.These include the development of person-centred care to ensure effective care coordination; a specific focus on the development and growth of the neurology workforce in the context of significant shortages; addressing shortfalls to ensure equity to timely, responsive services; and generating efficiencies through new and innovative ways of working. Implementation of the recommendations proposed in the report would increase capacity, reduce waiting lists and support timely and equitable access to appropriate care and support.My officials are currently reviewing responses to a recent public consultation on the report with a view to identifying next steps including the development of an implementation plan. I must be clear however that implementation of the report’s recommendations will require significant additional funding. While I will consider this in the context of competing priorities and future budget settlements, the availability of funding may impact the scale and pace of implementation.In the interim, Strategic Planning and Performance Group continue to actively consider ways to drive improvements in services using current resources through collaborative approaches and innovative ways of working.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the impact of the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Local Change Fund, in North Down.
answer_text: Momentum Funding provided by The Executive Office enabled Ards and North Down Borough Council to hold 11 conferences and events, as well as deliver a significant media campaign, to highlight the Local Change Fund and Ending Violence Against Women and Girls. 100% of partner organisations indicated that those events/workshops led to direct opportunities to mentor, support, and collaborate with potential applicant organisations for the Change Fund. In Ards and North Down, the Local Change Fund has been delivering since April 2025, across 11 community organisations with plans to reach over 3,000 participants by 31 March 2026, in youth groups, sports clubs, businesses, and schools. Early results are positive, with 95% of participants surveyed to date indicating an increased knowledge of VAWG, 93% indicating a better understanding of what a healthy relationship is, and 84% reporting increased confidence in challenging inappropriate behaviour.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work his Department is doing to develop condition-specific treatment pathways for neurology patients.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of condition-specific treatment pathways for neurology patients to ensure effective coordination of care. My Department recently concluded a public consultation on the report of the Regional Review of Neurology. In the context of work already undertaken, while also recognising the need for further development, the report proposes that condition-specific pathways should be finalised within the first two years of the implementation period.My officials are currently completing an analysis of responses to the consultation which will inform the development of a final implementation plan. I must be clear however that significant additional funding will be required to support implementation. While I will consider this in the context of competing priorities and future budget settlements, the availability of funding may impact the scale and pace of implementation.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the amount of funding; and (ii) any other support, that will be made available to Ards and North Down Borough Council, under the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls strategy.
answer_text: To date Ards and North Down Borough Council has received funding of £180,000 from the Local Change Fund. This includes allocations of £60,000 from the Momentum Fund 2024/25 and £120,000 from the Local Change Fund for 2025/26. TEO officials have provided advice and guidance to Councils in the delivery of actions to end violence against women and girls and have provided opportunities for learning and collaboration, such as the Showcase and Shared Learning Event held in June this year. All Council areas are also benefitting from support and activities provided through the £1.4m EVAWG Regional Change Fund. The eight organisations currently funded are detailed below. Women’s Aid Federation of NIRelate NINexusBarnardo’s White Ribbon NI NSPCCWomen’s Resource & Development Agency  Youth ActionThe amounts noted may increase as part of the £2.2m additional funding secured for EVAWG projects in 2025/26; the process for allocating this funding is underway.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to increase the number of neurology specialists, including nurses, neurologists and allied health professionals to meet demand for neurology services.
answer_text: In the context of pressures within neurology services, including those within the workforce, my Department’s Regional Review of Neurology report identifies the need for substantial growth in the Consultant, Nursing and Allied Health Professional (AHP) workforce. It is intended that this growth will create additional capacity to help reduce waiting lists and ensure timely access to care and support. In addition, the report recommends the development of a broader skill mix through extended roles such as Advanced Nurse Practitioners and Advanced Practice AHPs. This will ensure the most efficient use of resources and that people with a neurological condition are seen by the most appropriate person at the right time.My officials are currently reviewing responses to a recent public consultation on the report with a view to identifying next steps including the development of an implementation plan. I must be clear however that implementation of the report’s recommendations will require significant additional funding. While I will consider this in the context of competing priorities and future budget settlements, the availability of funding may impact the scale and pace of implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any action being taken by his Department to tackle fuel poverty.
answer_text: In order to tackle fuel poverty, my Department is developing a new Fuel Poverty Strategy for Northern Ireland, which I will bring to the Executive for approval in the very near future.My Department also continues to deliver the Affordable Warmth Scheme (AWS), which supports owner-occupiers and those in the private rented sector most at risk of fuel poverty with a range of heating and insulation measures to improve the thermal efficiency of their home. Officials are currently developing a new fund, which will replace AWS when it ends.My Department’s Discretionary Support Scheme is also available and can provide short term financial assistance to low-income families including interest free loans to help with fuel costs.The Northern Ireland Housing Executive also offers support through the Energy Advice Helpline which offers impartial advice to all households on reducing energy bills, accessing grants, and managing fuel costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any discussions his Department has had with dental training providers, in the last 12 months, to ensure that dentists receive cleft training as part of their course and ongoing development.
answer_text: There has been no direct communication in the past twelve months in relation to the specific issue of ensuring dentists receive cleft training as part of their course and ongoing development. However, in the last twelve months a number of conditions which impact CLAP patients have been included in broader educational events provided by the Northern Ireland Medical and Dental Agency; Hypodontia (the failure of teeth to develop) and assessment of carious first molar teeth. Cleft lip and palate patients are reviewed at various stages on their care pathway by a MDT which includes the input of a consultant orthodontist. This consultant orthodontist will have undertaken an additional two years of training after specialty training to deal with particularly complex cases requiring surgical treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, given the ongoing pressures on police resources, whether her Department has considered extending the powers for firework licensing and monitoring to local council environmental health officers, who already carry out regulatory and noise control functions.
answer_text: My Department already has in place a service level agreement with each Council area and has warranted Environmental Health Officers (EHOs), to carry out health and safety checks and/or inspections in relation to both the storage and use of explosives i.e. fireworks.The Department works closely with EHOs before any licences are issued to either store fireworks, for selling purposes, or for any publicly attended fireworks display and some private displays.To ensure compliance EHOs may also monitor any fireworks display and they also conduct interim inspections of firework retailers in the life of their certificate of registration, which is normally issued for five years.  Furthermore, EHOs have the powers to issue improvement or prohibition notices to fireworks retailers.EHOs will flag any concerns to my Department and my officials will take the appropriate action, which could be the refusal of a fireworks display licence or the revocation of a firework retailer’s certificate of registration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on Invest NI’s action to develop the Balloo South Industrial Estate, Bangor.
answer_text: Invest NI continues to actively market the available land at Balloo South Industrial Estate in Bangor to potential investors. 	The organisation continues to engage with local stakeholders including Ards and North Down Borough Council to consider how its property can best contribute to economic development in the area.  Invest NI will also continue to explore development opportunities that support sustainable growth and deliver long-term benefits for the local economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 30624/22-27, to detail any actions his Department is taking to speed up the supply of new social housing, in North Down.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive acts as a statutory consultee in the Local Development Plan process and has worked closely with Ards and North Down Council on the draft Plan Strategy published in October 2025. This strategy introduces an Affordable Housing Policy for urban and rural settlements, alongside a specific policy for affordable housing in the countryside. Collectively, these measures aim to increase the delivery of affordable homes where they are most needed through a sustainable, mixed-tenure approach. The publication of the NI Annual New Build Commissioning Guide enables the Housing Executive to set out local social housing requirements and direct Housing Association site searches to areas of under-provision. Current analysis highlights unmet need in Bangor, Donaghadee, and rural settlements such as Groomsport.To support delivery, an annual programme of Site Identification Studies is undertaken. In 2025, work began on a Site Investigation Study for Bangor, which will be shared with a nominated Housing Association upon completion. The South Place Shaping team continues to engage with Housing Associations to assess proposals for social new build schemes in line with the 5-year Social Housing Need & Demand Assessment, considering both location suitability and proposed housing mix.Under the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) for the North Down Parliamentary Constituency: Completions: 43 new social homes delivered to date in 2025/26 (see Table 1).Under Construction: No schemes currently on site. Programmed Starts: 81 social homes scheduled for 2025/26–2027/28 (see Table 2). The SHDP formulation process for 2026/27–2028/29 is ongoing, with several new proposals submitted during the 2025 Bidding Round. Some are expected to be programmed subject to Departmental approval, while others require further discussion to agree an appropriate housing mix.Table 1: New social housing Completions in the North Down Parliamentary Constituency in 2025/26Housing AssociationScheme NameLocation TypeNeed GroupStrategic GroupUnitsOnsite YearCompletionYearArkDonaghadee RoadBangorCompetitive Land Design & BuildActive ElderlyUrban82022/232025/26Ark12 Ballywalter RoadMillisleCompetitive Land Design & BuildGeneral NeedsRural92023/242025/26ClanmilSavoy Redevelopment (Blocks B & C)BangorNew BuildActive ElderlyUrban262021/222025/26Table 2: New social housing programmed to start in the North Down Parliamentary Constituency 2025/26 – 2027/28Housing AssociationScheme NameLocation TypeNeed GroupStrategic GroupUnitsOnsite YearCompletionYearChoiceWorcester AvenueBangorNew BuildGeneral NeedsUrban492026/272029/30ClanmilBangor Marina RegenerationBangorNew BuildGeneral NeedsUrban322026/272028/29”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how her Department's active travel funding was spent in the 2024-25 financial year
answer_text: Details of my department’s spend on active travel since the introduction of the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022 to financial year 2024/25 is available at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/details-spend-active-travel-introduction-climate-change-act-northern-ireland-2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department’s active travel team has engaged with Translink regarding its restrictions to bicycles on trains.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department’s active travel team engage regularly with Translink on issues related to improving combined active travel and public transport journeys. Whilst the issue of bikes on trains is an operational matter for Translink, officials are broadly in agreement with their approach, which mirrors policy in other jurisdictions such as The Netherlands  Our combined focus is on the provision of high-quality cycle parking at stations and the prioritisation of connections to public transport in active travel infrastructure delivery plans to maximise the combined active travel and public transport journey benefits.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her Department's plans to link the Newtownards to Bangor Greenway from the Green Road to Bangor City Centre.
answer_text: I am aware that the Bangor to Newtownards greenway is on programme to complete early next year, and my officials are positively engaged with the council to progress delivery of the Comber to Newtownards greenway. My Department has provided letters of offers to the council for approximately £5.2m in capital funding to support both projects.  I am committed to working with our council delivery partners to provide the highest quality active travel infrastructure to encourage more people to walk, wheel and cycle. The significant investment from the department is a demonstration of this commitment.Future active travel routes in Newtownards will be prioritised in line with the emerging Active Travel Delivery Plan. Officials are now updating the plan following responses to a public consultation carried out earlier this year and I hope to be in a position to publish the final plan in the coming months. While the Active Travel Delivery Plan focusses on shorter everyday journeys to key destinations, connections to the proposed greenways will also be considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how the end of the Comber to Newtownards Greenway will be linked to the Newtownards to Bangor Greenway.
answer_text: I am aware that the Bangor to Newtownards greenway is on programme to complete early next year, and my officials are positively engaged with the council to progress delivery of the Comber to Newtownards greenway. My Department has provided letters of offers to the council for approximately £5.2m in capital funding to support both projects.  I am committed to working with our council delivery partners to provide the highest quality active travel infrastructure to encourage more people to walk, wheel and cycle. The significant investment from the department is a demonstration of this commitment.Future active travel routes in Newtownards will be prioritised in line with the emerging Active Travel Delivery Plan. Officials are now updating the plan following responses to a public consultation carried out earlier this year and I hope to be in a position to publish the final plan in the coming months. While the Active Travel Delivery Plan focusses on shorter everyday journeys to key destinations, connections to the proposed greenways will also be considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any planned cycling infrastructure schemes, in North Down.
answer_text: My Department has been working closely with Ards and North Down Borough Council on the delivery of the Bangor to Newtownards Greenway which is due to complete early next year, and also on development of the proposed Newtownards to Comber Greenway.Future significant investment in active travel infrastructure by my Department will predominantly be prioritised in line with the emerging Active Travel Delivery Plan, which includes network maps for our larger towns and cities including Bangor, Donaghadee and Holywood in the North Down area. My officials are now updating the plan following responses to a public consultation carried out earlier this year and I hope to be in a position to publish the final plan in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her Department's plans to extend free public transport for those currently receiving half fare concession, due to a qualifying disability.
answer_text: I had intended to reconsider the extension of free travel as part of this year’s budget allocation process but due to a major upgrade of Translink’s ticketing systems, it was not possible to progress this option during the current financial year.However, the Department continues to work closely with Translink to explore how this policy could be implemented, and I am keen to progress this as soon as possible.I fully recognise the significant societal and economic benefits that free public transport for people with disabilities could bring.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of troubles related criminal cases (i) pending; or (ii) being prepared, that are against (a) members of proscribed terrorist organisations; and (b) veterans of the security forces.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for Director of Public Prosecution Service. The Department of Justice does not hold the data to respond to this AQW.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans his Department has for integrating multi-disciplinary dental and orthodontics work to support treatment pathways for patients with cleft palates.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, every baby born with a cleft lip and/or will be managed by the Joint Cleft Palate multi-disciplinary team clinic in the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children. This is one of 12 specialist cleft centres in the UK.  A consultant dental surgeon must be in attendance at each clinic regardless of the age of the patient or the type of cleft. Every cleft baby will be seen at the Joint Cleft Palate (JCPC) Multi-Disciplinary Team Clinic at various ages, with a view to successful discharge on reaching adulthood.  This includes a specialist paediatric dentist, specialist orthodontist and restorative dentists alongside other health professionals.I am acutely aware that access to General Dental Services is challenging across Northern Ireland for many people, including patients with a cleft lip and/or palate. I recognise that some patients, particularly those not currently registered with a dentist, are experiencing significant difficulty in obtaining appointments with a Health Service dentist. Given the significant challenges across the wider dental system there no current plans to open a specific workstream on dental services for cleft palate patients without additional evidence of need. In an effort to improve access for all patients however, in June this year, I announced an additional £7m investment aimed at supporting General Dental Services.  The investment is funding the continuation of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme, 30% enhancement on fees paid to dentists for Health Service fillings, extractions, and root canal treatments, and an allocation to support dental practitioners who continue to provide Health Service dental care.  In addition, a further £2.5million has been made available for General Dental Services to help offset the impact of increased National Insurance contributions on General Dental Services. These measures reflect my Department’s ongoing commitment to sustaining dental provision, despite significant financial pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current waiting times for patients for a specialist consultant appointment to access advanced osteoporosis treatments, including (i) zoledronic acid infusions; (ii) denosumab; (iii) teriparatide; (iv) romosozumab; and (v) abaloparatide, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The current waiting times for patients for a specialist consultant appointment to access advanced osteoporosis treatments, including the above listed drugs broken down by Health and Social Care Trust is unavailable.All five Health and Social Care Trusts were asked to provide input for this Assembly question  response. Only Belfast, South Eastern and Northern Trust provided a response.BHSCT confirmed the current waiting time for a routine appointment at the consultant Osteoporosis service is approximately 12 months. The waiting time for Zoledronic acid infusion is then an additional six months thereafter.All of the other therapies Denosumab, Teriparatide and Romosozumab are accessed via home care arrangements through nursing referral and are usually accessible once the patient has had the relevant blood work up and consent has been agreed.The initiation of home care delivered treatment is around one to two months once the prescription has been issued, this applies to Teriparatide and Romosozumab. For Denosumab a New Face to Face patient appointment has an approximate 4 months wait from referral.Initiation of Abaloparatide is paused at BHSCT pending the provision of home care services contract by Theramex.South Eastern Health & Social Care Trust (SET) have confirmed that the Trust does not have dedicated waiting lists for these specific Osteoporosis infusions.However Osteoporosis patients could be captured at SET within several specialities depending on the presentation including ,Rheumatology, Endocrinology, Orthopaedics and Gynaecology outpatients.The Northern Trust (NHSCT) confirmed that the Trust offers all infusions for Osteoporosis with the exception of  Abaloparatide. There are plans to bring this drug into Homecare for use in the coming weeks.The Current waiting time for new Fracture Liaison Service (FLS) appointments at NHSCT  are currently 86 weeks for urgent patients and 156 weeks for non -urgent/routine patients.My Department officials plan to meet the Chief Executive of the Royal Osteoporosis Society in December 2025 to better understand how to improve Fracture Liaison Services for patients in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department carries out regular monitoring and reporting on access to dental care for priority groups, particularly those with cleft palates.
answer_text: My Department does not currently monitor access to dental care for individuals with a specific medical condition, including those born with a cleft lip and/or palate.  However, the Cleft Registry and Audit NEtwork (CRANE) Database collects information about all children born with cleft lip and/or cleft palate in England, Wales and Northern Ireland. In Northern Ireland, every baby born with a cleft will be managed by the Joint Cleft Palate multi-disciplinary team clinic in the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children. This is one of 12 specialist cleft centres in the UK.  I am acutely aware that access to General Dental Services is challenging across Northern Ireland for many people, including patients with a cleft palate. I recognise that some patients, particularly those not currently registered with a dentist, are experiencing significant difficulty in obtaining appointments with a Health Service dentist. Under the current General Dental Services Regulations, dental practitioners are self-employed, independent contractors who choose the amount of time they spend delivering Health Service care. While I recognise that patients with cleft may be at increased risk of dental decay for a range of reasons, there are many other factors that patients and carers can do to reduce the risk of dental disease. Prevention of disease in early years plays a significant role in determining decay experience later in life. Ensuring regular toothbrushing and reducing the frequency of daily sugar intake are positive steps everyone can take.  Dental examinations are beneficial to instil positive attitudes towards dental visits and acclimatise patients to the dental surgery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any plans her Department has to improve access to legal guidance for personal litigants, particularly in cases where court documentation explicitly states that it does not constitute legal advice, and where individuals may be at risk of procedural disadvantage.
answer_text: The Department cannot offer legal advice or guidance to those involved in judicial proceedings. However, enhancing support for litigants in person is a key element of the Modernisation Programme I launched in 2021. Good progress has already been made with the establishment of a Litigant in Person Reference Group to help identify barriers to the participation of unrepresented parties. One of the key messages which has emerged is the need to improve understanding of how best to engage with the court system and reduce the fear of doing so.To that end, the Department has developed a number of work streams aimed at improving the experience of court users, particularly those who are not legally represented. Themis is a major business transformation and IT modernisation programme being taken forward by the NICTS Vision 2030 Portfolio.  The programme will improve the experiences of service users and the legal profession and will modernise administrative processes.  Themis will also enable NICTS to provide additional information on the administrative courts and tribunals processes to all service users, with a commitment to focus on plain English where possible.The Programme will be delivered incrementally, completing in summer 2029.  Although NICTS cannot provide legal advice to litigants, Themis is being developed utilising a user-centred approach with a wide range of user journeys informing change, with Litigants in Person personas and engagement with the Litigant in Person Reference Group integral to the development of our future administrative processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, regarding her proposed new legislation regarding inconsiderate pavement parking, whether the prohibition on parking wholly on the pavement will apply to motorcyclists.
answer_text: Following my recent announcement to introduce further measures to address inconsiderate pavement parking across the North, I have asked my officials to take forward work to develop the necessary secondary legislation to introduce these new measures. It is my aim to introduce these legislative changes within the current 2022-27 mandate.In line with other parking contraventions, it is also my intention for motorcycles to be subject to the restrictions being brought forward as part of this legislation, including parking wholly on a pavement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to improve urgent and emergency care services in the Ards and North Down area, particularly for older residents.
answer_text: My Department has taken a whole-system approach to planning for winter pressures across all Health and Social Care Trusts, including in the Ards and North Down area. The overarching Winter Preparedness Plan 2025–26, published on 16 October 2025, outlines a series of measures designed to protect services, improve patient flow, and manage pressures across the system.
