The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust has taken a number of steps to improve urgent and emergency care services for older residents in the Ards and North Down area.  A range of actions are already in place to support winter preparedness, improve patient flow, reduce avoidable admissions and enhance discharge processes.Firstly, the Trust has expanded its Hospital at Home service, increasing capacity for patients to receive hospital-level treatment in the comfort of their own homes. This approach is particularly beneficial for older patients, as it enables them to access the care they need without the disruption and risks associated with prolonged hospital stays.At the Ulster Hospital, additional investment has allowed the Frailty at the Front Door service to be enhanced. This service focuses on identifying and assessing older, frail patients as soon as they arrive at the Emergency Department (ED). By providing timely assessment and intervention, the service helps reduce time spent in the ED and, where appropriate, prevents unnecessary hospital admissions.The Early Review Team and Home Care Support teams have been expanded across the Trust, including the Ards and North Down area. The Early Review Team focuses on reviewing domiciliary care packages to ensure services are appropriately allocated. Where care packages are no longer required, resources can be redeployed to other patients, supporting timely discharge for those who need ongoing care at home. In parallel, the Care Home Team works closely with care homes to implement anticipatory care planning and provide support, helping to prevent unnecessary hospital admissions and maintain residents’ care in their familiar environment.In addition, the Trust has developed a range of pathways in partnership with the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service and Community Care teams. These pathways aim to avoid hospital admissions for older patients where it is clinically safe and appropriate to do so, ensuring that care is delivered in the most suitable setting.  To support these efforts, my Department has re-purposed £10 million to fund targeted actions across the care pathway, from pre-hospital care through acute care and into the community. These measures are designed to supplement existing bed capacity and provide alternative pathways for patients, which for older residents are often more appropriate than inpatient admission.NIAS continues to engage with all HSC trusts to optimise access to all care pathways for patients.  NIAS have developed a suite of pathways for South Eastern HSC Trust which allow for patients to be treated by the mos

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any programmes their Department deliver to encourage youth participation in civic life.
answer_text: A number of Executive Office Good Relations programmes fund projects which include content promoting youth participation, as well as delivering work which focuses on increasing young people’s self-efficacy, skills and confidence, directly improving the capacity of young people to participate in civic life.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her proposed new legislation regarding inconsiderate pavement parking will be introduced to the Assembly, before the end of the 2022-27 mandate.
answer_text: Following my recent announcement to introduce further measures to address inconsiderate pavement parking across the North, I have asked my officials to take forward work to develop the necessary secondary legislation to introduce these new measures. It is my aim to introduce these legislative changes within the current 2022-27 mandate.In line with other parking contraventions, it is also my intention for motorcycles to be subject to the restrictions being brought forward as part of this legislation, including parking wholly on a pavement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the number of applications to the Olympic Legacy Fund.
To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the progress of the Controlled Schools unit within the Education Authority.
answer_text: Work within the Education Authority (EA) to establish and implement the Controlled Schools Unit (CSU) is progressing well. The CSU team is currently engaging with Controlled Schools across all phases through a series of roundtable events, ensuring that the voices of school leaders help shape future priorities and actions. In addition, a ‘Contact Hub’ has been established and is being piloted across 100 Controlled Schools to strengthen and support. The CSU remains on track to become fully operational early in the new year, providing a focused and responsive service for the Controlled sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to improve screening and early intervention for osteoporosis.
answer_text: At present I have no immediate plans to address screening and early intervention for Osteoporosis. My Department officials are planning to work with Royal Osteoporosis Society and MLAs to understand the support required by Osteoporosis patients to help them to manage their health condition.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to improve access to post-fracture services for osteoporosis patients, including the Fracture Liaison Service.
answer_text: My Department officials are planning to work with Health and Social Care Trusts and the Royal Osteoporosis Society to better understand how to improve Fracture Liaison Services for patients in Northern Ireland.Patients aged 16+ in need of post-facture or Musculoskeletal (MSK) care can avail of treatment initially through self-referral to Physiotherapy in acute hospital settings across all of Health and Social Care Trusts in Northern Ireland.My Department has invested in the expansion and roll out of the long-term health reform programme Multidisciplinary Team (MDTs) in primary care. This investment will create further Physiotherapy availability across the region for patients to also self-refer to Physiotherapy within General Practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work being carried out to promote the importance of bone health among the public.
answer_text: The importance of eating a healthy diet and taking part in physical activity is recognized as important for good bone health. The Food Standards Agency promotes the Eatwell Guide which illustrates government recommendations on eating healthily and achieving a better diet. The guide recommends including some milk and dairy food (or dairy alternatives) in people's diet as they are good sources of protein and vitamins, and an important source of calcium, which helps to keep bones strong.Key areas of focus for the PHA to improve bone health and reduce fracture risk include:Reviewing and updating the  Vitamin D leaflet (https://www.publichealth.hscni.net/sites/default/files/2025-02/3%20VitaminD_leaflet%20FINAL.pdf) on an annual basis which highlights that ‘Vitamin D is an essential vitamin for everyone. Adults and older people who don’t have enough vitamin D can develop a condition where the bones become soft and there is a greater risk of broken bones (known as osteomalacia)’.Encouraging the population to participate in physical activity in line with four UK CMOs Physical Activity Guidelines (2019).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to improve access to NHS dental care for patients with cleft palates.
answer_text: I am acutely aware that access to General Dental Services is challenging across Northern Ireland for many people, including patients with a cleft palate. I recognise that some patients, particularly those not currently registered with a dentist, are experiencing significant difficulty in obtaining appointments with a Health Service dentist. Under the current General Dental Services Regulations, dental practitioners are self-employed, independent contractors who choose the amount of time they spend delivering Health Service care. In Northern Ireland, every baby born with a cleft will be managed by the Joint Cleft Palate multi-disciplinary team clinic in the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children. This is one of 12 specialist cleft centres in the UK. A consultant dental surgeon must be in attendance at each clinic regardless of the age of the patient or the type of cleft. Every cleft baby will be seen at the Joint Cleft Palate (JCPC) Multi-Disciplinary Team Clinic at various ages, with a view to successful discharge on reaching adulthood. This includes a specialist paediatric dentist, specialist orthodontist and restorative dentists alongside other health professionals.There are many lifestyle factors that patients with a repaired cleft palate can modify to reduce the risk of dental disease. Ensuring regular toothbrushing and reducing the frequency of daily sugar intake are positive steps everyone can take. Measures my Department are taking to improve access to dental care for all patients will gave a positive impact for those with cleft. An additional £7 million is being invested in General Dental Services in 2025/26. This investment will be used to fund the continuation of the Enhanced Child Dental Examination Scheme, a 30% enhancement on fees paid to dentists for Health Service fillings, extractions and root canal treatments, and a further £1.6 million allocation to support dental practitioners who continue to provide Health Service dental care to their patients.In parallel, the Dental Access Scheme (DAS) continues to provide access for unregistered patients with an urgent or pressing oral health need.While these additional investment interventions are necessary to stabilise General Dental Services in the short term, I am clear that, in common with other services, it will require sustained effort to ensure sustainability over the longer term.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps her Department is taking to strengthen the regulation of online takeaway platforms and their cyber security platforms, to protect local businesses from scammers manipulating their payment information.
answer_text: While my Department does not have a direct regulatory role in the operation of online takeaway platforms or their cybersecurity, we are committed to supporting local businesses to operate safely and securely online.My Department’s Trading Standards Service (TSS) tackles online and mass marketing scams, providing practical advice and support to any business affected. TSS works closely with partners such as the National Trading Standards Scams Team, the PSNI, and the ScamwiseNI Partnership to identify and support victims, raise awareness of emerging scams, and provide guidance to businesses and consumers. TSS also delivers educational talks, issues press releases and social media alerts, and collaborates with banks, charities, and law enforcement to support those affected.TSS is a member of the Department of Justice Business Crime Partnership which helps ensure the North is a safer and more competitive place to do business, with actions shaped by the needs of the business community.Businesses can contact TSS via Consumerline (Freephone 0300 123 62 62 or Consumerline@economy-ni.gov.uk). Businesses are also encouraged to report fraud to Action Fraud (0300 123 2040 or www.actionfraud.police.uk).My Department will continue to work with partners to ensure local businesses are aware of risks and equipped to protect themselves from online scams and cyber threats.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any opportunities for members of the public to access mental health first aid training, in order for them to be able to assist others before the arrival of medical professionals.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency (PHA) provide a range of mental health awareness and skills courses that are open to the public. These programmes focus on raising awareness and understanding of positive mental health, poor mental health, mental illnesses and providing individuals with the skills to identifying and respond to individuals experiencing emotional difficulties. A range of community, voluntary and statutory organisations are commissioned by the PHA to provide mental health awareness and skills courses which are delivered in person and online. Examples of courses include: Mindset Adult Mindset Adolescent Mood Matters Adult Mood Matters Young People Living Life to the Full Mental Health First Aid (MHFA) is a 12 hour evidence-based mental health skills programme which teaches participants how to recognise the symptoms of mental health problems, provide initial help and guide a person towards appropriate professional help. The course is for any person aged 18 and over and is delivered both in-person and online by accredited MHFA trainers.Information on how to access mental health awareness and skills courses can be found at: https://mhfa.pharesourcehub.co.uk/Home/TrainingProvidersIn addition to the above courses, the PHA commissions an online 6 session awareness course focussing on the use of cognitive behavioural therapy techniques. Stress control is a class, not group therapy, and is open to the public. Dates of upcoming programmes can be found at https://ni.stresscontrol.org/

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 33129/22-27, to detail why Tourism NI or Tourism Ireland do not have a role in the marketing of Northern Ireland as a cruise ship destination.
answer_text: One of the actions in my 10-Year Tourism Vision and Action plan is to increase the economic impact of visitors accessing the North through working in partnership with sea carriers and cruise operators. Tourism NI engages with and supports Visit Belfast, Belfast Harbour and Cruise Belfast in assisting their sales and visitor servicing in this area. In addition, my officials have regular engagement with the Cruise Lines International Association, the preeminent cruise trade association, on issues of mutual interest with the most recent meeting being on 13 November. By the end of 2025 163 cruise ships will have visited Belfast and Derry welcoming over 330,000 passengers and crew with an estimated economic impact in excess of £23.5m. The marketing of ports in the North as cruise ship destinations is also supported by the cruise industry bodies, Cruise Ireland and Cruise Britain. In 2024, Cruise Ireland and Cruise Britain signed a memorandum of understanding to work collaboratively to market both Ireland and Britain as a cruise destination. This collaborative effort was recognised in September 2025, with the award of ‘Destination of the Year’ presented to Ireland and Britain at the Seatrade Cruise Awards in Hamburg.Tourism NI’s marketing remit is to promote the North as a tourism destination to residents across Ireland. Tourism NI supports the delivery of visitor servicing operations at the Belfast Cruise Welcome Hub via an annual Service Level Agreement with Belfast City Council. Many of the tourism businesses that attend Tourism Ireland overseas platforms, where they promote Ireland as a holiday destination in international markets, serve cruise business and bring cruise visitors to visitor attractions in the North.One example is Excursions Ireland (a ground operator for cruise lines, specialising in shore excursions) who joined Tourism Ireland on its stand at World Travel Market in London this month. Excursions Ireland delivers cruise visitors to many local tourism businesses in the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any work her Department undertakes to place physical barriers and change design, to reduce risk in known suicide spots.
answer_text: As part of my department’s work in support of the Protect Life 2 Strategy, my officials continue to engage with stakeholders, including the Mental Health Champion, the Public Health Agency, local Councils and the Cross Departmental Coordinated Action Group, to explore options for progressing suicide prevention infrastructure. The Westlink, Belfast and Foyle Bridge, Derry have been identified as prevalent suicide spots where prevention and safety infrastructure could be installed. At the Westlink, my Department is working on a pilot to increase the height of the parapets on Clifton and Divis Street Bridges that has an estimated cost of £1.8m.  These parapets would be undertaken through the development of a green living wall instead of the normal metal railings that would be more pleasing to the eye, environmentally friendly and enhance the general appearance in the area.  I will be endeavouring to allocate funding in future years to try and get these schemes, that are so important for the local communities, pushed forward.  At Foyle Bridge, the proposed suicide deterrent measures include additional railings, however due to the complex nature of the structure, a detailed structural assessment must be undertaken to ensure that the bridge would be able to cope with the additional containment measures and increased wind resistance.  I have now allocated funding to take forward the structural assessment of the Foyle Bridge and Departmental officials are now engaging with our consultant to progress this as soon as practicable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has any plans to review the eligibility criteria for budgeting advances, paid through Universal Credit, to support people getting back into work.
answer_text: The eligibility criteria for Budgeting Advances available through Universal Credit includes support for claimants getting back into work, such as travelling expenses, work clothes, tools and childcare costs.Additional financial support may also be available through the Adviser Discretion Fund to benefit claimants to assist them in overcoming financial barriers to starting, increasing hours or returning to work.Further information on Budgeting Advances and the Adviser Discretion Fund can be found here: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/universal-credit-advance-payments#toc-2  and https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/adf

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will be engaging with the British and Irish Governments to review the mutual recognition of driving disqualifications, to ensure that there are no legal loopholes which could allow disqualified drivers from obtaining a valid licence in Northern Ireland and vice versa.
answer_text: I have instructed my officials to request that this international agreement, between the British and Irish Governments, is revisited.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 32301/22-27, whether her Department has any plans to offer wage subsidies and tax incentives for employers to take on skilled apprentices.
answer_text: As previously referenced in my response to AQW 32301/22-77, my Department continues to provide significant support for apprenticeship training and employer incentives, with the latter recently increased by 20% under the ApprenticeshipsNI 2025 programme which commenced delivery in September 2025. 		Tax relief falls outside the responsibilities of my Department, however employers in the North are not required to pay Class 1 National Insurance contributions for apprentices under 25 if specific conditions are met.	My Department’s Apprenticeship Action Plan establishes a comprehensive strategic framework to enhance the apprenticeship system. Notably, it introduces the Apprenticeship Navigators pilot initiative, designed to assist businesses, especially those engaging apprentices for the first time, in navigating the recruitment process and effectively supporting their initial apprentice.  The Action Plan further outlines commitments to increase awareness, simplify access to information, and identify additional opportunities to reduce participation barriers for both employers and apprentices.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when the Acute Mental Health Inpatient Accommodation 3.1 Business Case was (i) submitted; and (ii) approved.
answer_text: This Acute Mental Health Inpatient Accommodation 3.1 Business Case has not been finalised, the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust are considering the service model and the number of beds which they will be proposing for the new acute Mental Health Inpatient Unit.   The SEHSCT have been working with SPPG on the Outline Business case, the final BC has not been submitted for consideration. The Acute Mental Health Inpatient Accommodation 3.1 Business is on the priority list of projects for Capital Funding but no decisions have been made to allocate funding in 25/26 due to the scale of capital budget requirements, as noted in AQW 33517/22-27. The draft Budget Review outcome when received, will inform the Capital Investment Plan. This will set out the specific projects to be taken forward over the next ten years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has any plans to review the Explosives (Fireworks) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2002, to restrict types of fireworks and the conditions of use.
answer_text: I have no current plans the review the existing fireworks regulations.  The existing regulations provide a robust framework that seeks to ensure fireworks are sold and bought responsibly for use in both public and private settings.A fireworks licence includes conditions on use, including specifying the types of fireworks that can be used and the time and location of the display.  A fireworks licence will only be issued if it is deemed safe to do so and additional conditions can be added to any licence as necessary.Some fireworks are only suitable for professional displays and their use is restricted to professional display operators.  Member of the public are only permitted to purchase and use the fireworks specified on their licence, which are commonly referred to as ‘garden fireworks’.Northern Ireland has among the most robust firework regulations in these islands. I recognise that this is an area of concern for many members of the public, however, and have instructed my officials to examine penalties and enforcement concerning the illegal use or sale or fireworks in the pending review of sentencing issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 33846/22-27, to detail any plans she has to widen support to personal litigants accessing pro bono assistance, beyond housing and family court matters.
answer_text: I have no immediate plans to widen support to personal litigants accessing pro bono assistance.  However, my Department’s Enabling Access to Justice Reform Programme will consider additional ways to enhance access to justice and I will shortly consult upon reform opportunities for merits testing and financial eligibility to ensure that provision represents value for money, that resources are being used to best effect and to ensure that the needs of the most vulnerable are prioritised.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 33129/22-27, to detail the rationale as to why the marketing of Northern Ireland as a cruise ship destination is left to the individual port owners, rather than a collective strategy from Northern Ireland.
answer_text: One of the actions in my 10-Year Tourism Vision and Action plan is to increase the economic impact of visitors accessing the North through working in partnership with sea carriers and cruise operators. Tourism NI engages with and supports Visit Belfast, Belfast Harbour and Cruise Belfast in assisting their sales and visitor servicing in this area. In addition, my officials have regular engagement with the Cruise Lines International Association, the preeminent cruise trade association, on issues of mutual interest with the most recent meeting being on 13 November. By the end of 2025 163 cruise ships will have visited Belfast and Derry welcoming over 330,000 passengers and crew with an estimated economic impact in excess of £23.5m. The marketing of ports in the North as cruise ship destinations is also supported by the cruise industry bodies, Cruise Ireland and Cruise Britain. In 2024, Cruise Ireland and Cruise Britain signed a memorandum of understanding to work collaboratively to market both Ireland and Britain as a cruise destination. This collaborative effort was recognised in September 2025, with the award of ‘Destination of the Year’ presented to Ireland and Britain at the Seatrade Cruise Awards in Hamburg.Tourism NI’s marketing remit is to promote the North as a tourism destination to residents across Ireland. Tourism NI supports the delivery of visitor servicing operations at the Belfast Cruise Welcome Hub via an annual Service Level Agreement with Belfast City Council. Many of the tourism businesses that attend Tourism Ireland overseas platforms, where they promote Ireland as a holiday destination in international markets, serve cruise business and bring cruise visitors to visitor attractions in the North.One example is Excursions Ireland (a ground operator for cruise lines, specialising in shore excursions) who joined Tourism Ireland on its stand at World Travel Market in London this month. Excursions Ireland delivers cruise visitors to many local tourism businesses in the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any consideration she has given to removing parental rights for individuals found to be in breach of a contact order.
answer_text: Parental responsibility is defined under Article 6 of the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 so as to include parental rights in relation to a child. The threshold for removal of parental responsibility is high. It is a rare and extreme measure and will only occur when the Court considers it to be in the best interest of the child or children involved, which is its primary consideration. Where an individual is found to be in breach of a Contact Order, the Order may be varied or discharged by the Court. Where the breach continues, enforcement proceedings may be initiated where the Court will determine whether a penalty should be imposed in the form of a warning, fine or possible custodial sentence. The Court may consider making further Article 8 orders which may achieve the same or similar ends to a removal of parental responsibility: a Specific Issue Order, Prohibited Steps Order or Residence Order. Each case which comes before the Court is considered by a Judge with expertise in these matters and the current system affords the Court with an appropriate degree of independence when reaching its decision. I consider that the substantive law under which a judge adjudicates Article 8 orders relating to children in family proceedings strikes the correct balance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she plans to make it a legal requirement for sex offenders to undergo treatment programmes in prison, before being considered for early release.
answer_text: ANSWERThe provision of treatment programmes in prisons for those convicted of sexual offences forms part of the Prisoner Development Model, the framework used to deliver rehabilitation across prisons in NI.All sexual offenders have the opportunity to engage in assessment and treatment in relation to their offending behaviour.  However, readiness and willingness to engage are important factors in an individual’s potential to benefit from participation. It is also important to note that Need-Responsivity research has demonstrated that putting lower risk offenders into medium or high risk interventions can have a negative effect in terms of increasing, rather than reducing, risk.I am content that the existing arrangements enable and encourage sexual offenders to engage in treatment programmes to address their offending behaviour.No sex offenders are released early from prison in Northern Ireland.  Article 19 of the Criminal Justice Order (NI) 2008 allows for certain prisoners to be released from custody early under the Conditional Early Release Scheme (CER).  Those subject to the notification requirements of Part 2 of the Sexual Offences Act 2003 are statutorily excluded from being considered for early release under Article 19 (3)(c) of the Order.  To ensure that public confidence in the criminal justice system is not eroded a number of other offences deem an applicant to be presumed unsuitable for CER.  These include prisoners who have current and/or previous convictions for sexual offending (but who are not currently subject to Sexual Offences Act 2003 notification requirements).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she plans to introduce a database of domestic abuse offenders for use by the PSNI.
answer_text: I am aware that the Irish Cabinet have recently approved the establishment of a Domestic Violence register. However, the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) already has access to several national databases containing information on individuals with a history of offending or abusive behaviour. These include the Police National Computer (PNC), the Police National Database (PND), and the ViSOR, which records details of violent and sexual offenders. Collectively, these databases assist the police in identifying repeat or high-risk offenders and inform safeguarding decisions, including those made under the Domestic Violence and Abuse Disclosure Scheme (DVADS). Given these existing national databases, I do not consider there is a need to establish further provision at this time, but I will keep the situation under review with operational partners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of whether the Domestic Violence and Abuse Disclosure Scheme provides the same protections for the public as Clare’s law in England.
answer_text: I am satisfied that the Domestic Violence and Abuse Disclosure Scheme (DVADS) provides the same protections for the public as Clare’s Law in England.Both schemes operate under the same two key principles:the Right to Ask – any member of the public can request information form the police if they are concerned that their partner or ex-partner may have a history of domestic abuse.  You can also ask on behalf of a friend or relative, who you believe may be at risk; andthe Right to Know/Tell – Police can proactively disclose information to a potential victim if they believe that the person is at risk.The aim of both schemes is to enable individuals to make informed decision about their relationships and enhance public protection.The main difference is that the guidance associated with DVADS in Northern Ireland is policy-based whilst in England it has been set in statute, largely to ensure that there is consistent practice across the 43 police forces there. I am satisfied that the policy-based approach is appropriate for Northern Ireland and allows flexibility to update and refine the scheme in response to changing operational needs or emerging risks. However, both schemes function in the same way to safeguard potential victims of domestic abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she plans to give the Domestic Violence and Abuse Disclosure Scheme a statutory footing.
answer_text: The Domestic Violence and Abuse Disclosure Scheme (DVADS) in Northern Ireland has operated on a non-statutory basis since its introduction in 2018.  My Department published guidance which includes advice for PSNI on the practical application of the scheme.The Domestic Abuse Act 2021 placed a statutory requirement on the Secretary of State to issue guidance to police forces in England and Wales on the application of the scheme.  This was to address inconsistencies across 43 separate police forces. This is not necessary in Northern Ireland, where there is only one main police service delivering the scheme consistently across all areas.Maintaining the scheme on a policy basis also allows greater flexibility, enabling the Department and the PSNI to adapt quickly to changes in practice, emerging risks, and evolving forms of abuse. I am satisfied that the current arrangements provide robust and consistent protection for the public although my Department will continue to work with partners to raise public awareness of the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has plans to alter restrictions on media access to family court proceedings.
answer_text: I believe that greater transparency regarding decisions made in the family courts would help improve public understanding of the process and enhance confidence in the family justice system. However, this needs to be balanced with protecting the anonymity of the children and families involved.Following policy scoping work carried out by my Department to consider the potential to increase the transparency of family courts, I discussed this issue with the Lady Chief Justice, who indicated that she would consider a pilot. The Judicial Family Working Group subsequently carried out a targeted consultation on proposals for a media reporting pilot in the Family Division of the High Court. Further to that, I understand that a pilot will run from 10 November 2025 to June 2026.I welcome the pilot, as it will be an important step in informing consideration of how best to achieve greater openness and transparency of the family courts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the considerations taken into account when housing a child sex offender, following their release from prison.
answer_text: The management of individuals convicted of sexual offences who are living in the community is an operational matter for relevant agencies which are outlined below.  Critically, these agencies know where sex offenders are housed and work collaboratively to share information and manage any risks posed. PSNI deals with cases of offenders who are subject to notification requirements under the Sexual Offences Act 2003, commonly referred to as the ‘sex offenders register’. This requires offenders to notify police of certain personal details, including their address. PSNI also manages individuals who are subject to civil prevention orders, such as the Sexual Offences Prevention Order, to manage risk from sexual harm. Conditions can include only residing at an address that is approved by the police or prohibiting them from going to certain places such as to schools or playparks.PBNI manages individuals who are subject to statutory supervision by in the community (for example, offenders who are on licence). The suitability of addresses for such offenders living in the community is assessed on a case-by-case basis, taking into account all relevant factors.The Public Protection Arrangements Northern Ireland (PPANI) bring together agencies – including PSNI, PBNI and NI Housing Executive - to manage sexual and violent offenders who pose a risk of serious harm. These agencies take into account a range of factors when assessing suitability of accommodation for each relevant offender being managed under PPANI. These include: whether there are children or vulnerable adults living at or frequently visiting the address; whether the accommodation is in immediate proximity to the victim’s or child centred locations; and whether there are concerns about association with other offenders in the area. Guidance has been developed to ensure all agencies deploy a consistent approach to approving accommodation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she plans to introduce a timeframe on a period of absence, after which a parent would lose parental rights.
answer_text: Parental responsibility is defined under Article 6 of the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 so as to include parental rights in relation to a child. Parental responsibility is automatically conferred on the birth mother at the time of birth. However, the 1995 Order distinguishes between parental responsibility that is automatically conferred on the other parent, e.g. if he or she was married or in a civil partnership with the birth mother at the time of the birth, and where it can be acquired, e.g. upon registration as the child’s unmarried father. Parental responsibility which is automatically conferred can only be lost when an adoption order is made, whereas a court can terminate parental responsibility which has been acquired. The threshold for removal of parental responsibility is high. It is a rare and extreme measure and will only occur when the Court considers it to be in the best interest of the child or children involved, which is its primary consideration. Mechanisms exist which may enable parental responsibility that has been acquired to be removed from an absent unmarried parent where it is considered to be in the best interests of relevant children, and this may include applications where such a parent has been absent for an extended period. Each case would be treated on the facts presented. In the case of married parents, the Court may consider making an Article 8 Order which may achieve the same or similar ends to removal of parental responsibility: a Specific Issue Order, Prohibited Steps Order or Residence Order. Each case which comes before the Court is considered by a Judge with expertise in these matters who will weigh up the family life issues involved, with the children’s best interests as a paramount concern. The current system affords the Court with an appropriate degree of independence when reaching its decision. I consider that the substantive law under which a judge adjudicates issues relating to children in family proceedings strikes the correct balance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any measures being taken in prisons, to ensure that child sex offenders are separated and cannot share their criminal knowledge.
answer_text: The location of prisoners within establishments is carefully managed taking into account a range of factors including individual needs and risks, safety, security, and available accommodation.  It would not be possible to configure the prison population to separate prisoners solely based upon the nature of their offending and still satisfy other considerations.Prisons have routines, regimes and procedures in place to ensure that effective risk management arrangements are in place for all prisoners and that they receive appropriate supervision from prison officers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has plans to make it compulsory for sex offenders to meet with their victims, if requested by their victim.
answer_text: My Department is still exploring how we deliver restorative justice in relation to sexual offences, taking into consideration the recommendations in the Gillen Review.Due to the sensitive nature of such cases, we intend to collaborate with key advisors from the statutory, community and voluntary sectors and academia so that we can offer a process that is inclusive, effective and, most importantly, safe.                     It is important to note that any agreed process must be victim led and must follow the fundamental principle of restorative justice - 'informed consent' - meaning that no one, victim or offender, will be unduly pressured or coerced into participating in a restorative process. Therefore while victims may wish to communicate with those who harmed them, for example by letter, the offender retains the right to refuse to meet with them or to receive and/or respond to any communication.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans her Department has to improve road safety and visibility, including white line painting and reflective road stud provision, on the Portaferry Road between Newtownards and Portaferry.
answer_text: Road safety remains an extremely high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users.A previous review of the 40mph speed limit on Portaferry Road, Newtownards determined that the extension of the existing 40mph speed at this site would not provide a tangible benefit at this time. However, I have asked my officials to review this again should further development of the area adjacent to Teal Rocks be undertaken.My Department has also completed nearly 1km of resurfacing between Newtownards and Teal Rocks to help with surface quality. These works included a bus layby and hardstand which provided a safe facility for pedestrians while they wait on the bus service. I can advise that the widening of the footway from Teal Rocks towards Newtownards was recently completed to aid safer pedestrian movements along this section of the A20 Portaferry Road.Officials continue to introduce road safety measures along Portaferry Road and in an effort to further improve driver awareness, warning signage, verge marker posts, and white lines have been upgraded along the route. This includes enhanced signing and road markings on Finlays Road to make the presence of the junction more apparent to motorists who may not be familiar with the area.My Department receives information from the PSNI on collisions involving personal injury.  Officials are currently reviewing the latest Road Traffic Collision data for accident cluster sites. If the Portaferry Road is highlighted and a common causation factor can be identified, then effective road safety measures may be introduced, if appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the extent of the use of reflective road studs on roads; and (ii) whether there are plans to increase their use to improve road safety, particularly in rural roads.
answer_text: My Department’s policy https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/drd/edge-of-carriageway-markings-and-reflecting-road-studs-rsppg-e031.pdf  outlines the criteria for the provision of road studs on the network, including their use on rural roads and for improvements in road safety.  I will ask my officials to consider the use of reflective road studs on rural roads as a road safety measure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any requirements, in terms of visible clothing and lighting, that electric bicycle users must adhere to, to ride on a public road.
answer_text: The current Highway Code makes two specific references in relation to clothing (Rule 59) and lighting (Rule 60). The rules are generic to all cyclists and not specific to electric bicycle users.Rule 59 advises that the cyclist should wear the correct size of clothing and ensure that it is securely fastened. A cyclist should wear a cycle helmet which conforms to current regulations, wear light coloured, reflective or fluorescent clothing which helps other road users see them in daylight or poor light.Rule 60 advises that between sunset and sunrise a bicycle must have a white front light and red rear lights lit. It must also be fitted with a red rear reflector (and amber pedal reflectors, if manufactured after 24/1/96). White front reflectors and spoke reflectors will also help the cyclist to be seen. Flashing lights are permitted but it is recommended that cyclists who are riding in areas without street lighting use a steady front lamp.Further information on electric bikes can be found on the NI Direct website. https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/electric-bikes-electrically-assisted-pedal-cycles#toc-0

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any legal requirements for an owner of a private car park to ensure that accessible car park spaces are only used by blue badge holders.
answer_text: Whilst my Department is responsible for on-street parking enforcement across the North, it has no responsibility for the operation and management of private car parks nor the enforcement companies that operate within them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plan for dentistry and improving public access to NHS dentistry.
answer_text: In June this year I announced £7m investment for 2025/26 aimed at supporting dental practices while longer-term reform is developed. The investment will fund continuation of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme, a 30% enhancement on fees paid to dentists for Health Service fillings, extractions and root canal treatments, and a further £1.6m allocation to support dental practitioners who continue to provide Health Service dental care.  In addition, a further £2.5million has been made available for General Dental Services to help offset the impact of increased National Insurance contributions on General Dental Services. These measures reflect my Department’s ongoing commitment to sustaining dental provision, despite significant financial pressures. I acknowledge the challenges facing many patients, particularly those not registered with a dental practice, in accessing dental care when they need it. General Dental Practitioners (GDPs) are self-employed, independent contractors who are free to choose the amount of Health Service (HS) care they provide. However, my Department has prioritised improving access to dental care through targeted investments, including the Dental Access Scheme (DAS). This scheme allows unregistered patients with an urgent or pressing (but non-urgent) dental need to receive treatment. While DAS does not cover ongoing care, it ensures that those in greatest need are not left without dental support. Longer term, my Department’s Health and Social Care NI three-year plan includes a commitment to develop an action plan and programme of work for the reform of General Dental Services by April 2027. I am clear that in common with other services, General Dental Services will require sustained effort to ensure sustainability over the longer term.  That is why I commissioned a cost-of-service review which will help inform the future direction of General Dental Services. The ability to implement transformative change however is significantly constrained by the Department’s £6 million budget deficit, which limits the scope for transformative change.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the inspection process by her Department, following the reinstatement road works by a utility company.
answer_text: The Street Works (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 forms the legislative framework for street works by statutory undertakers and requires the undertaker to comply with the Specification for the Reinstatement of Openings in Roads.My Department applies the procedures for inspection of reinstatements and for dealing with reinstatement failures as set out in the Northern Ireland Road Authority and Utilities Committee (NIRAUC) Code of Practice for Inspections.  Where a utility company fails to carry out reinstatement to an acceptable standard, the utility company is duly notified to carry out remedial works to bring the reinstatement to the required standard.The number of inspections carried out on utility company reinstatement works on roads and footways, in North Down and Ards, over the last five years; and the times further work was required following the inspection are included in the table below:LocationNumber of InspectionsNumber of time further work requiredNorth Down & Ards3,182909Permanent reinstatements are normally carried out using the same materials and in some cases it may be possible to reuse some or all modules. Interim reinstatements may be carried out using different materials prior to permanent reinstatement. If interim reinstatement is used, permanent reinstatement must be completed within six months unless agreed with the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the options available to her Department when a utility company fails to complete road works in a timely manner.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of (i) inspections carried out on utility company reinstatement works on roads and footways, in North Down, over the last five years; and (ii) times further work was required following the inspection.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the length of time a utility company is permitted to put a temporary surface, instead of a like for like replacement, in a public realm area.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to increase access to regular lidocaine infusions as a treatment for chronic pain across the Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: In the absence of sufficient, high quality evidence to support the long-term efficacy, safety and cost effectiveness of Intravenous (IV) Lidocaine infusion for the management of Chronic Pain in non-cancer patients I am not planning to increase treatments across Health & Social Care Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how his Department promotes co-ownership for potential new homeowners.
answer_text: Co-Ownership is a Registered Housing Association and Industrial & Provident Society, funded by a combination of private finance and significant funding from my department which is provided in the main through a Financial Transaction Capital (FTC) loan.  As such it is an independent body, governed by its own Board of Directors and responsible for its own promotional activity in relation to its scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any additional support being put in place to ensure adequate staffing levels on Translink services.
answer_text: My Department provides funding to Translink to provide public transport services and facilities. As such, Translink is responsible for ensuring it has adequate staffing levels to deliver these services within its allocated budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has any plans to introduce a scheme similar to the Chancery Bar Litigant in Person Support Scheme in England and Wales.
answer_text: The Chancery Bar Litigant Support Scheme (CLIPS) is run by the Chancery Bar Association in England and Wales and aims to provide legal assistance to litigants in person appearing in the Judges’ application list.As the scheme involves Barristers volunteering to give their time to provide legal advice it would not be in the gift of the Department to replicate a similar scheme in Northern Ireland. Any such scheme would need to be initiated by the profession.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the current arrangements for personal litigants accessing pro bono assistance; and (ii) any plans she has to improve access to this.
answer_text: Pro Bono advice for personal litigants is offered by a number of organisations.  My Department through the Legal Services Agency funds the Housing Possession Court Duty Scheme administered by Housing Rights.  The scheme allows for two part-time solicitors to attend courts when housing repossession hearings are listed and they offer free legal advice to those who may not have previously accessed legal services. The scheme has the twin aim of increasing access to justice and preventing homelessness. Within the coming months my Department intends to consult on additional ways to enhance access to justice for citizens and delivering value for money.  I recognise that navigating the system is challenging for those with limited legal knowledge or expertise and my Department continues to consider other ways to assist citizens who represent themselves, where possible, including addressing barriers to active participation and the development of potential interventions, most recently within the family courts.  My Department has developed a number of tools to assist separating parents navigate decision making for their children and prepare for court which I expect to launch in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he plans to develop a new woodland strategy.
answer_text: The forestry strategy, Northern Ireland Forestry – A Strategy for Sustainability & Growth, guides the delivery of forestry policy in Northern Ireland. The strategy sets out the long-term objective to increase woodland cover to 12% of Northern Ireland’s land area by 2050. This aim aligns with recommendations of the Climate Change Committee regarding the level of afforestation needed to contribute to net zero 2050 targets.I have recently announced the commissioning of a Northern Ireland Tree Planting Action Plan to be developed in co-design with stakeholders. The Plan will highlight key actions needed to deliver the strategy of 12% forest cover by 2050, necessary to realise forestry’s contribution to net zero targets within the Climate Action Plan.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the percentage of woodland cover, in each of the last five years.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

answer_text: My Department’s road maintenance budget allocated to road divisions, comprising Capital and Resource, is set out in the table below for the period 2020-21 to 2024-25:  YearYearYearYearYearRoads DivisionStructural Maintenance20-2121-2222-2323-2424-25  £mEastResource2.62.72.83.75.6Capital10.414.014.914.119.8Total Structural Maintenance13.016.717.717.825.4NorthResource4.13.63.55.05.7Capital20.326.426.322.725.7Total Structural Maintenance24.430.029.827.731.4SouthResource5.15.25.17.17.4Capital24.023.529.231.632.7Total Structural Maintenance29.128.734.338.740.1WestResource6.44.54.96.99.8Capital27.524.840.629.128.9Total Structural Maintenance33.929.345.536.038.7Figures for the financial year 2024-25 are provisional and subject to audit finalisation.Capital Structural Maintenance includes resurfacing and reconstruction, asphalt patching, surface dressing, capital investment in both drainage infrastructure and in the structural stability of embankments. question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the percentage of woodland cover in North Down, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department publishes and updates the Northern Ireland woodland register approximately every 10 years. The last update for the register was in 2022 and indicated that the percentage of woodland cover in the North Down Assembly Area was 8.63%.Since then, the Department has grant aided new woodland in the subsequent years through the Small Woodland Grant Scheme. The following areas 0.47ha and 2.42ha were planted in 23/24 and 24/25 respectively, which increases percentage woodland cover in the North Down Assembly Area to 8.66%.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any plans he has to increase woodland cover.
answer_text: Increasing woodland cover is one of my Department’s priorities. The current forestry strategy, Northern Ireland Forestry – A Strategy for Sustainability & Growth, sets out the long-term objective to increase woodland cover, currently 8.6%, to 12% of the Northern Ireland land area by 2050. This aim aligns with recommendations of the Climate Change Committee regarding the level of afforestation needed to contribute to net zero 2050 targets.At the recent Woodland Trust launch event of its report, State of the UK’s Woods and Trees: Northern Ireland, I announced the commissioning of a Northern Ireland Tree Planting Action Plan. The Tree Planting Action Plan which will be co-designed in collaboration with stakeholders over the coming months.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she has any plans to increase the current fixed penalty fine for speeding offences on public roads.
answer_text: Officials are considering options for a review of penalty fines for speeding.  Once I have received briefing and advice from them I will consider the next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail her plans for future local enterprise support, from April 2026.
answer_text: My Department does not have a role in the administration of Go Succeed, which is funded through the Shared Prosperity Fund. It was anticipated that this funding would be transferred to the Executive following the British Government’s Spending Review in June. In anticipation of that announcement my Department was ready to establish a co-design group.  However, the British Government has instead created a role for the NIO. I have written to the Secretary of State seeking clarity on the future of this vital programme. Disappointingly, that clarity has not yet been provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she plans to set up a task and time-bound co-design group, to make improvements to Go Succeed.
answer_text: My Department has no role in the administration of Go Succeed, which is funded through the Shared Prosperity Fund. It was expected that this funding would be transferred to the Executive following the British Government's Spending Review announcement in June.		In anticipation of that announcement my Department was ready to establish a co-design group. However, the British Government has instead created a role for the NIO. I have written to the Secretary of State to ask for clarity on the future of this vital programme. Disappointingly that clarity has not yet been provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people in North Down, who have received a vehicle under the Motability scheme, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: My Department does not hold the information requested. 		Eligibility for Motability is chiefly determined by entitlement to the enhanced rate of the mobility component for PIP or the higher rate mobility component for DLA. Claimants may opt to exchange all or part of their benefit in return for a motor vehicle through the Motability scheme.		Motability is an independent charity responsible for the administration of the Motability contract hire scheme. The Department has no role in the policy or administration of the scheme and simply facilitates payment of the mobility component of the disability benefit for eligible claimants who wish to avail of the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, with regard to the £90 million of capital funding, currently ring-fenced to the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme, to detail the amount of funding from (i) the Executive; and (ii) the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: The ring-fenced budget currently allocated to my Department for the A5 Western Transport Corridor is £165m.  Of this, £90.4m is from the Executive as Flagship funding, and £74.6m is from the Shared Island Funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the marketing of North Down, undertaken by Tourism Ireland, over the last five years.
answer_text: Tourism Ireland has promoted North Down internationally by engaging with travel and, lifestyle media for many years. Recent examples include:Supporting the filming of a television series presented by Fred Sirieix, which aired on ITV1 and ITVX in spring 2025 and featured a journey through County Down, including local tourism experiences.Facilitating a feature on the North’s seafood trails, including North Down, in the September 2025 edition of BBC Countryfile magazine.Arranging for Italian travel writers to visit Bangor to explore its connections to St Columbanus and its significance in European Christian heritage.Organising a media fact-finding visit in June by journalists from Germany, France, and the Netherlands, which included Bangor Castle Walled Garden.Collaborating with Rory Best in June 2025 on a campaign promoting golf here, including Holywood Golf Club, as part of a partnership with The Telegraph, and other media outlets such as the Financial Times and BBC Breakfast.In addition, Tourism Ireland has implemented targeted digital marketing campaigns, participated in leading international travel exhibitions, and worked in partnership with tour operators to feature North Down in bespoke travel itineraries. These efforts have amplified North Down’s visibility among audiences in Europe, North America, and other key markets over the past five years. Tourism Ireland has also facilitated press coverage in travel publications and organised online influencer collaborations, alongside highlighting North Down to 7+ million followers on social media. These initiatives complement the ongoing media, television, and partnership activities and form part of Tourism Ireland’s wider programme to promote the North to international audiences.Most recently, Tourism Ireland’s CEO attended and addressed an Ards and North Down tourism event with local tourism industry operators on 14 October 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on providing the necessary legislation to allow local councils to set their own parking charge tariffs in off-street car parks.
answer_text: It is the Department’s intention to bring forward legislation using powers in the Local Government Act 2014 (“the 2014 Act”) that would disapply the provisions of the 2000 Order for Councils, fully divesting all councils from the legislative requirements of the Off-Street Parking Order (NI) 2000. The use of these powers is subject to the draft affirmative resolution procedure and therefore legislation must be laid before, and approved by, a resolution of the Assembly. However, before my Department proceeds, all councils need to have their own administrative orders fully drafted and in a state of readiness for implementation to ensure continuity of governance arrangements for off street car parking once the 2000 Order ceases to apply. My officials have had ongoing engagement with a number of councils in relation to the development of their off-street parking orders and have provided all councils with a comprehensive template for an order, as well as drafting guidance and advice.   As my Department is keen to bring about a resolution to this issue as soon as practicable, my officials have recently written to all councils seeking an update on the development of their administrative orders. It is hoped this will identify a mutually agreed timeline for the completion of all of the necessary preparatory work and the making of the required legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current waiting time for endometriosis-related surgery in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has any plans to install charge points for mobility scooters in town and city centres, to assist those with mobility issues.
answer_text: The Department does not have any plans to install charge points for mobility scooters in town and city centres. Mobility scooters come with rechargeable batteries that last between 8 and 25 miles and can have a typical recharge time of 6-16 hours so are more suited for home charging.
