question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on how the Cancer Strategy for Northern Ireland 2022-2032 is being implemented in relation to ovarian cancer, particularly regarding earlier diagnosis, improved access to genomic testing and the expansion of specialist gynaecological oncology services.
answer_text: The Cancer Strategy was published in March 2022, with a recurrent investment of £10.6m in 2024/25 to allow for implementation. I recognise that this falls short of £34.2m outlined in the Cancer Strategy Funding Plan, however, this reflects the financial challenges and competing pressures across the Health and Social Care system. Despite these financial challenges, good progress has been made on a range of projects, impacting on ovarian cancer and gynaecological cancer care. Earlier diagnosis Action 6. Of the cancer strategy is to deliver regular, effective, targeted evidence-based ‘Be Cancer Aware’ campaigns. The Public Health Agency (PHA) continues to promote the Be Cancer Aware campaign messaging to raise awareness of cancer signs and symptoms and encourage the public to present to their GP.  The campaign signposts to the ‘Be Cancer Aware’ website which includes information on ovarian cancer. https://www.becancerawareni.info/signs-and-symptoms/ovarian-cancerThe PHA has also undertaken a sustained programme of proactive messaging to raise awareness of the signs and symptoms of ovarian cancer and direct people to information, including press releases, features, social media messaging, infographics and video.To support early detection and improve vigilance among primary care health professionals, the Northern Ireland Cancer Network (NICaN) delivers monthly primary care cancer and palliative care education sessions. These sessions target GPs, nurses, pharmacists, and allied health professionals, and are delivered in partnership with the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust and Macmillan GP facilitators. A dedicated session on ovarian cancer was held in November 2022. All sessions are recorded and accessible via the NICaN website. Currently, the NICaN Gynaecology Clinical Reference Group are reviewing the primary care guidance for suspected gynaecological cancers, including the red flag referral criteria for ovarian cancer. Once regionally agreed, the updated guidance will be disseminated to all GPs and Trusts, and supported by a new GP/primary care education and awareness session with a focus on ovarian cancer.Action 15 of the strategy commits to developing new pathways and diagnostic services to improve diagnosis. Two Rapid Diagnosis Centres (RDCs) have been set up in Whiteabbey and South Tyrone hospitals to facilitate the Vague Symptom Pathway allowing GPs to refer patients who do not meet usual red flag criteria to be assessed.A Regional Health and Social Care (HSC) Gynaecology Services Forum has been established to oversee the implementation of service improvement recommendations from the “Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) Gynaecology Report for Northern Ireland," (January 2024).  These include streamlining diagnostic pathways and expanding the use of skill mix, utilising nursing and allied health professionals for diagnostic procedures such as colposcopy and hysteroscopy.  This should improve timescales in diagnosis and timely access to trea

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide a breakdown of funding provided via the Household Waste Recycling Collaborative Change Programme, in the last five years, broken down by local council.
answer_text: In the last five years, funding totalling £4,640,320.00 has been awarded to five Councils via my department’s Household Waste Recycling Collaborative Change Programme, broken down as below:Antrim & Newtownabbey Borough Council 	£2,610,255Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council £240,000Belfast City Council £768,000Mid & East Antrim Borough Council 	£995,065Mid Ulster District Council £27,000Grants of up to 50% are still available to support eligible projects, and I would encourage all Councils to submit applications to help further develop the local circular economy.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the awareness campaigns for the symptoms of ovarian cancer.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency (PHA) works closely with the Department of Health, local Health and Social Care Trusts, and other partners to support a range of activities to raise awareness of ovarian cancer symptoms, including:Ovarian Cancer Awareness Month - each March the PHA issue a press release and social media messaging to increase awareness of ovarian cancer (https://www.publichealth.hscni.net/news/ovarian-cancer-awareness-month-be-aware-signs-and-symptoms-0). Social media - the PHA posts regularly through social media channels to promote cancer awareness messaging, providing information on the signs and symptoms of cancer, including ovarian cancer, and healthy changes people can make to help reduce their risk of developing cancer in the future. ‘Be Cancer Aware’ Website - all PHA cancer awareness messaging signposts to its ‘Be Cancer Aware’ website. The website provides information about the signs and symptoms of a range of cancers, including ovarian cancer, and signposts to recommended sources of support or further information (https://www.becancerawareni.info/signs-and-symptoms/ovarian-cancer).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether (i) updated guidance will be issued to GPs on diagnosing ovarian cancer; and (ii) faster pathways will be introduced, given that late diagnosis leads to expensive treatments and poor outcomes.
answer_text: (i) The NICaN Gynaecology Clinical Reference Group is reviewing the NICaN primary care guidance for suspected gynaecological cancers, including red flag referral criteria for ovarian cancer. Once regionally agreed, the updated guidance will be disseminated to all GPs and Trusts and published on the NICaN website.(ii) A Regional Health and Social Care (HSC) Gynaecology Services Forum has been established to oversee the implementation of service improvement recommendations from the “Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) Gynaecology Report for Northern Ireland," (January 2024).  These include streamlining diagnostic pathways and expanding the use of skill mix, utilising nursing and allied health professionals for diagnostic procedures such as colposcopy and hysteroscopy.  This should improve timescales in diagnosis for gynaecological cancers including ovarian cancer. The recently published Elective Care Framework Implementation and Funding Plan sets out proposals for how we will systematically tackle the backlog of patients waiting longer than Ministerial standards and how we will transform services to allow us to meet the population’s demands in the future.   Examples within the plan that may support the faster  diagnosis of ovarian cancer include investment of £3.5 m in Gynaecology services where demand for outpatients, day case and inpatient red flag and time critical gynaecology services is significantly above capacity available. There is a need to expand gynaecology capacity to manage red flag and time critical patients and expand capacity in medical, nursing and allied health profession patient pathways. This investment will allow for 4,100 assessments and 900 treatments

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) what specific steps have been taken to improve early detection of ovarian cancer; and (i) what evidence there is of progress in reducing the time from first presentation to diagnosis.
answer_text: The Department recognises the critical importance of early detection and timely diagnosis in improving outcomes for women affected by ovarian cancer. Regarding awareness to help improve early detection of ovarian cancer, the Public Health Agency (PHA) continues to promote the Be Cancer Aware campaign messaging to raise awareness of cancer signs and symptoms and encourage the public to present to their GP.  The campaign signposts to the ‘Be Cancer Aware’ website which includes information on ovarian cancer. https://www.becancerawareni.info/signs-and-symptoms/ovarian-cancer The PHA has also undertaken a sustained programme of proactive messaging to raise awareness of the signs and symptoms of ovarian cancer and direct people to information, including press releases, features, social media messaging, infographics and video.To support early detection and improve awareness of red flag symptoms among primary care health professionals, the Northern Ireland Cancer Network (NICaN) delivers monthly primary care cancer and palliative care education sessions. These sessions target GPs, nurses, pharmacists, and allied health professionals, and are delivered in partnership with the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust and Macmillan GP facilitators. A dedicated session on ovarian cancer was held in November 2022. All sessions are recorded and accessible via the NICaN website. Currently, the NICaN Gynaecology Clinical Reference Group are reviewing the primary care guidance for suspected gynaecological cancers, including the red flag referral criteria for ovarian cancer. Once regionally agreed, the updated guidance will be disseminated to all General Practitioners and Health and Social Care Trusts, published on the NICaN website, and supported by a new GP/primary care education and awareness session with a focus on ovarian cancer.(ii) Hospital waiting times statistics presents information on waiting times for cancer services and reports on the performance of the five Health and Social Care Trusts against the waiting time targets which state that:At least 98% of patients diagnosed with cancer should receive their first definitive treatment within 31 days of a decision to treat.At least 95% of patients urgently referred by a GP with a suspected cancer should begin their first definitive treatment within 62 days.Data for ovarian cancer is not routinely published due to the relatively small number of patients involved and are therefore grouped with gynaecological cancers.  Latest published data (1st April 2025 to 30 June 202%) reports that 90.1% patients diagnosed with gynaecological cancer begin treatment within 31 days this compares with 85% in the same quarter period in 2023, which shows an improvement in time from diagnosis to treatment. However, in April to June 2025, 15.9% of were treated within 62 days of referral, down from 23.9% for the same period in 2023, indicating significant issues in time from referral to diagnosis. There is widespread recognition of t

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the progress of TransformEd NI.
answer_text: The TransformED Reform Programme is driving a bold, ambitious and system-wide transformation of education.At the heart of TransformED is the need to equip and empower our educators. This is demonstrated by the engagement of 161 post-primary schools and over 7,000 teachers benefiting from the Online CPD Academy. In addition, the £31 million Teacher Professional Learning Fund is enabling schools to deliver high-quality, evidence-based development tailored to their needs, while the new Initial Teacher Education (ITE) Bursary Scheme is addressing critical subject shortages.Research-informed practice is being embedded through initiatives such as the Science of Learning newsletters, teacher-edited publications, and over 50 practitioner-led conferences.  Much needed curriculum reform is advancing through the establishment of the Curriculum Taskforce and subject working groups.  My ambition is to have a new framework developed for consultation in the Spring.  Assessment and qualifications reform is underway, with pilots of Computer Adaptive Assessment in 522 schools and a system-level sample assessment in literacy and numeracy. A public consultation on GCSE, AS, and A-level reform aims to reduce exam burden and enhance meaningful learning.Legislative progress is being made on inspection reform and raising the participation age to 18.   The RAISE programme continues to be rolled out across Northern Ireland, with innovative projects such as Reading with AI.As we move through the academic year, our strategic focus is on deepening, accelerating, and scaling implementation. Key priorities include supporting schools in accessing professional learning, advancing curriculum reform, progressing qualifications and legislative changes, and expanding evidence-based initiatives to close the attainment gap and ensure every child can succeed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the marketing of ports, other than Belfast, as a cruise ship destination.
answer_text: The marketing of all ports in the North as cruise ship destinations are the responsibility of the ports themselves, with support from relevant cruise industry bodies. The ports which currently welcome cruise ships are Belfast, Foyle, Bangor and Warrenpoint. These marketing activities are delivered through sales missions, promotional familiarisation visits to attract cruise ship calls and the provision of excellent visitor servicing to maximise the visitor experience for passengers arriving.In Belfast this is provided by Cruise Belfast, a partnership between Visit Belfast and Belfast Harbour, which delivers a collaborative sales, marketing and visitor servicing partnership to promote Belfast Harbour as a Cruise ship destination. Foyle Port delivers the marketing of its four docks - Lisahally, Fort George & Magilligan in the North and Greencastle in County Donegal. Foyle Port is supported in visitor servicing by Visit Derry. Both Bangor and Warrenpoint Ports are also supported in delivering visitor servicing to cruise ship passengers by their relevant local authority councils, Ards and North Down and Newry and Mourne. In 2024, Cruise Ireland and Cruise Britain signed a memorandum of understanding to work collaboratively to market both the island of Ireland and Britain as a cruise destination. This collaborative effort was recognised in September 2025, with the award of ‘Destination of the Year’ presented to Ireland and Britain at the Seatrade Cruise Awards in Hamburg.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how she plans to adequately resource NI Water.
answer_text: I have provided NI Water with over £500m of public money in 2025-2026 to enable it to deliver essential water and sewerage services for all people across the North.At opening budget, NI Water is receiving 86% of what it identified it could deliver this year, and when the additional funding that I secured in June monitoring is taken into consideration, this increases to almost 90% of the ask, which is a significant level of investment in our water and wastewater infrastructure. NI Water has received 23% of the opening resource and over 40% of the general capital budgets allocated to my department.  Since my predecessor John O’Dowd and I came into office we have announced the unlocking of wastewater capacity for some 5300 properties. This is an example of what can be achieved when we work in partnership to find solutions.I plan to continue that focus on unlocking capacity and my officials are continuing to work collaboratively with colleagues in DAERA and DfC to contribute to the aims and objectives of the Executive’s Housing Supply Strategy.I will also continue with the three-pronged approach which includes working with Executive colleagues to try and increase wastewater investment, responding to the public consultation on developer contributions, and introducing the Water, Flooding and Sustainable Drainage Bill.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she plans to review the intervention levels for road defects.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.  In response, my Department has had to reduce its routine road maintenance activities to a limited service, which prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety and protecting the transport network. Consequently, only the highest priority defects across the network are currently being repaired.The level of repairs that can be carried out in any financial year depends on the funding available to my Department. Until such times as sufficient funding is made available, it will not be possible to change the current level of service. The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she is giving any consideration to increasing the frequency of road gully cleaning, given the ongoing flooding issues on local roads.
answer_text: My Department places high importance on the need to adequately maintain the road network through day-to-day essential maintenance activities such as gully emptying, to maintain the structural integrity of the road network. However, allocations of funding to maintain our roads have been less than required for many years and as a result, my Department has only been able to deliver a limited service across a range of functions including gully emptying.My Department’s current Limited-Service Policy aims to inspect and clean, where necessary, all gullies once annually.Further ad-hoc cleaning may also be undertaken to address any specific localised issues as required, within available budgets, and in advance of and in response to severe weather events. However, in recognition of the importance of this area of work, I have allocated further funding to deliver additional works in the form of cleaning and maintenance of drainage lines and manholes.In preparation for Storm Amy, for example, my officials completed proactive culvert screen maintenance work. This preventative work helped minimise disruption and contributed to protecting hundreds of homes from flooding.The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made so my Department can deliver important aspects of road maintenance, such as gully cleaning, ensuring public safety and an efficient transport network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action she is taking to unlock building developments, currently held up with waste and sewerage connection issues.
answer_text: To help unlock developments and encourage economic growth for all parts of the North, I will continue to develop the three-pronged approach that is already showing results.  Firstly, I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment for NI Water and this year my Department will provide NI Water with over £500m of public money to enable it to deliver essential water and sewerage services for all people across the North.  When the additional funding that I secured in June monitoring is taken into consideration, NI Water is now receiving 90% of what it identified it could deliver this year, representing a significant level of investment in our water and wastewater infrastructure.  Secondly, my Water, Sustainable Drainage and Flood Management Bill, which is progressing through its Assembly Stages, will make provision for future regulations on sustainable drainage systems (SuDS), which will help to store stormwater so that it releases slower into the wastewater network and thirdly, my Department has consulted on proposals for developer contributions to help fund wastewater infrastructure improvements.  That consultation has now closed and a consultation response paper will issue in due course.My Department has also secured £15m over the next four years from the Executive’s Transformation Project to take forward an Urban Drainage Transformation Project.  The aim of this project is to retrofit a range of nature-based drainage solutions into the urban environment to manage rainwater on the surface and slow its flow into our rivers and sewers.  This innovative pilot project will help transform the way in which rainwater is managed and improve the effectiveness and efficiency of the existing infrastructure.I will continue to do all that I can to enable the release of wastewater capacity constraints to support future housing and wider economic development. Since my predecessor John O’Dowd and I came into office we have enabled the unlocking of wastewater capacity for over 5,000 properties across the North. This is already above the 4,300 that NI Water had originally planned to connect, if fully funded, by the end of this Price Control period in 2028, and it is only 2025.Additionally, I have consistently said that innovative thinking and working in partnership hold the key to unlocking our wastewater constraints. The recent announcement connecting 400 properties in Newry shows what can be achieved when we challenge traditional thinking and focus on smarter, more sustainable solutions - this lies at the heart of the three-pronged approach.Furthermore, I recognise the key role that my Department plays in the delivery of housing and, therefore, a Housing Supply Cross-Departmental group has been established involving officials from my Department, the Department for Communities, NI Water and the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, to help unlock wastewater capacity to enable housing connections.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are requirements for her Department to provide parking for motorcycles at on-street and off-street parking areas.
answer_text: There are no specific requirements for my Department to provide parking for motorcycles either on-street or in Departmental off-street parking areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting time for the transfer of patient notes from GB to NI once the submission of an application form to Business Services Organisation is made.
answer_text: When a patient registers with a GP practice in Northern Ireland (NI) and the patient has been identified as having relocated to NI from Great Britain (GB), the Business Services Organisation (BSO) coordinates the transfer of patients’ GP medical charts.  Records are transferred from England and Wales to BSO by courier and from Scotland via a postal delivery service. When records are received into BSO they are forwarded to the new GP practice on the next available weekly delivery via BSO courier. BSO does not currently monitor the time taken to transfer records from GB health authorities as they have no control over the timeliness in which medical records are issued to them. However, BSO routinely follows up with GB health authorities on any medical records that are outstanding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 31898/22-27, to detail the criteria by which the allocations to local council areas have been made.
answer_text: In distributing the resources available for roads maintenance, allocations relating to the areas covered by my Department’s four Roads Divisions are made on the basis of need, using a range of weighted indicators tailored to each maintenance activity (i.e. resurfacing, surface dressing etc.).  These take into consideration criteria such as road length, structural condition, traffic and population (for footway resurfacing).Divisions also use similar indicators when apportioning across council areas to ensure, as far as possible, an equitable distribution based on need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 31898 22-27, for her assessment as to whether the allocation to Ards and North Down Borough Council, over the last ten years, has been fair and equitable.
answer_text: The distribution of funding allocations for road maintenance is made on the basis of need which aims to ensure, as far as practicable, an equitable distribution of funds across the whole of the North. I can assure you that the Ards and North Down Borough Council area receives a fair and proportionate share of the funding that is made available to my Department for road maintenance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the road gully maintenance schedule in North Down
answer_text: My Department’s current Limited-Service Policy aims to inspect and clean, where necessary, all gullies once annually. In Ards and North Down this is currently on target.  Further ad-hoc cleaning may also be undertaken to address any specific localised issues as required, within available budgets.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action her Department is taking to tackle persistent flooding on the Bangor Road between Groomsport and Bangor.
answer_text: The recent flooding at Bangor Road, Groomsport has been attributed to heavy and sustained rainfall potentially overwhelming the drainage system.Road drainage systems are only designed to cope with normal average annual daily rainfall. They cannot, nor are they intended to, cope with the sudden, short duration, high intensity rain events that have become more common in recent times. My officials have carried out cleaning and jetting work to ensure the road drainage system is working to full capacity, however, with increased development, more water has been placed into the same system, resulting in overcapacity issues at times of sustained and heavy rainfall.We will, however, continue to monitor and clean the system to ensure it is operating to its full design capacity, as well as investigate any measures which can be implemented to reduce the impact of future weather events.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 31338/22-27, whether this work will also apply to GP patient records in order to speed up the transfer of patient records when that patient moves from GB to NI.
answer_text: The Department is aware of the challenges associated with the transfer of GP patient records when individuals move from Great Britain to Northern Ireland. While the work referenced in AQW 31338/22-27 primarily focuses on improving the efficiency of patient record transfers within hospital settings, efforts are also underway to enhance the interoperability of systems used in primary care.The Department continues to work with relevant stakeholders, including the Business Services Organisation and Health and Social Care Trusts, to improve the processes and infrastructure supporting GP record transfers. This work includes; Exploring opportunities to streamline registration procedures for patients relocating from Great Britain, and ensuring that GP practices are equipped to manage both electronic and manual record transfers. Giving due consideration to electronic solutions, such as extending GP2GP functionality across UK regions, or alternatively implementing an interim solution like Egres to facilitate the transfer of unstructured patient medical files — as is currently being explored between Northern Ireland and Scotland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline how the details of individuals given driving bans in the Republic of Ireland, are passed to authorities in Northern Ireland and vice versa, to ensure that the ban applies in both jurisdictions.
answer_text: On 30 October 2015, the British and Irish Governments entered into an agreement on the mutual recognition of driving disqualifications. This agreement came into force on 1 August 2017 and is given effect by Part 3 of the Crime (International Co-operation) Act 2003.Where a person is disqualified by a court in the south for an offence specified in the agreement, and that person is either normally resident in the north or holds a licence issued in the north, the Road Safety Authority (RSA) will send details of the disqualification to the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA). Provided the details fully meet the requirements of the relevant legislation, the DVA will administratively apply the disqualification in the north.A similar arrangement is in place where a person is disqualified in the north and is either normally resident in the south or holds a licence issued by the Irish licensing authority.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail when the next review of the Child Maintenance Service is due.
answer_text: The UK Government is currently conducting a review of the child maintenance calculation to make sure it is fit for purpose.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline (i) when the last review of the Child Maintenance Service in Northern Ireland took place; and (ii) any changes that were introduced.
answer_text: (i) A consultation on proposed Child Maintenance Service (CMS) reforms was published by the previous Government on 8 May 2024, titled ‘Child Maintenance: Improving the collection and transfer of payments’. This consultation, which extended to Northern Ireland, proposed removing Direct Pay so that all CMS cases would be managed in one service, allowing the CMS to tackle non-compliance faster and provide better support to victims and survivors of domestic abuse.  Responses to the consultation, including those from Northern Ireland, were evaluated and the Government published its response on 23 June 2025 setting out plans to reform the CMS, replacing the existing Direct Pay service and moving to a service where the CMS collects and transfers all payments. Introducing a single service type where the CMS monitors and transfers all payments will represent a significant improvement for many users of the service, removing many of the issues that currently exist, including hidden non-compliance in Direct Pay, and delays caused by the change to service type process. It will create opportunities for greater use of automation and streamlining processes and enable enforcement action to be taken as soon as payments break down. 	(II) The UK Government’s intention is to implement these changes as soon as parliamentary time allows. My Department will maintain parity of substance and timing between the respective jurisdictions on these forthcoming policy and service changes within the CMS.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of recommendations from the report on the Review of the Implementation of the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 that have been carried out to date; and (ii) when this work will be completed.
answer_text: The report on the Review of the Implementation of the Planning Act (NI) 2011 made numerous recommendations/actions covering key elements of the planning system.  Good progress has already been made, including the implementation of a number of planning fee inflationary uplifts; amendments to subordinate legislation enabling councils to bring forward planning application validation checklists; revised procedures for pre-application community consultation and removal of the mandatory requirement to hold a pre-determination hearing under the Planning Development Management Regulations 2015. Other work has included: a review of the internal administration of the ‘call-in’ process and enhanced use of digital technology in the planning system aided by introduction of the Regional Planning IT System (Planning Portal). The review of the classes and thresholds for development under the Planning Development Management Regulations 2015 is also at an advanced stage and a public consultation was launched at the beginning of October with regards to proposals to introduce new planning fees for non-material change, and discharge of conditions applications.I will continue to review progress with my officials.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the roll-out of the Deposit Return Scheme.
answer_text: UK Deposit Management Organisation (DMO), an independent, not for profit, private body, were appointed as the DMO responsible for the administration and day-to-day running of the Deposit Return Scheme (DRS) in England and Northern Ireland in May 2025. They were designated as the DMO in Scotland in June 2025.UK DMO are also responsible for designing and delivering the infrastructure required to enable the scheme. They are currently implementing their operational plan to support the rollout of DRS across the three nations by October 2027. My officials, alongside Defra and Scottish Government counterparts, will continue to work closely with UK DMO and stakeholders as the scheme progresses.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the total cost of staff away days within their Department, in each of last five years.
answer_text: Please see the table below which details the total cost of staff away days within The Executive Office, in each of last five financial years.   2020/212021/222022/232023/242024/25£0£641 £6,700 £4,402 £3,566

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will review (i) legislation; and (ii) sentencing guidelines, regarding (a) animal welfare; and (b) dog fighting cases, with a view of identifying and closing any legal loopholes or ambiguities that have allowed perpetrators to evade justice or continue these activities.
answer_text: I am satisfied that the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 provides a comprehensive range of protections for animal welfare, including specific offences in relation to animal fighting. 		I have no role in determining or reviewing sentencing guidelines, as that is a matter for the judiciary, which operates independently of Government.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of whether current legislation on dog fighting is sufficient.
answer_text: Dog fighting is illegal in Northern Ireland under The Welfare of Animals (Northern Ireland) Act 2011. This legislation sets out a comprehensive range of offences in relation to animal fighting including organising, attending, participating in, or promoting animal fights, as well as possessing related items, training animals, or providing premises for fights. The law also prohibits distributing, publishing or possessing images or videos of animal fights, except in licensed broadcasts. I consider the 2011 Act provides the Police Service of Northern Ireland with the necessary tools to address any instances of dog fighting and associated activities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he plans to review the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011.
answer_text: The Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 (“the 2011 Act”) provides comprehensive powers for local councils, the PSNI, and my Department to fulfil their respective animal welfare enforcement responsibilities. It enables proactive intervention to prevent unnecessary suffering and sets out substantial fines and penalties for animal welfare offences. The 2011 Act is on a par with animal welfare legislation throughout the United Kingdom, therefore, I currently have no plans to review it. Rather, I am using the powers contained within the 2011 Act to bring forward the majority of the legislative reforms published in my Animal Welfare Pathway.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has plans to introduce legislation to provide mandatory minimum prison sentences for those found guilty of (i) participating in; (ii) facilitating; and (iii) spectating, dog fighting.
answer_text: The Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 already provides courts with the ability to hand down custodial sentences to any person convicted of offences related to dog fighting, including participation, facilitation, and spectating. The maximum sentence available is 5 years imprisonment. It remains that sentencing guidelines and the application of sentences themselves are a matter for the judiciary.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has considered mandatory lifetime disqualification orders from (i) keeping; (ii) owning; (iii) dealing in; and (iv) transporting, any animal for anyone convicted of participating in dog fighting.
answer_text: The penalties available under The Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 include disqualification orders for those convicted of participating in animal fighting. A court may order that a convicted person be disqualified from owning, keeping, participating in the keeping of, or transporting an animal for any period it deems appropriate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the occupational support available to serving PSNI officers, to improve (i) wellbeing; and (ii) staff retention.
answer_text: The provision of occupational support available to serving PSNI officers is delivered through the PSNI’s Occupational Health and Wellbeing Service (OHWS).  This provides multidisciplinary, clinical, occupational health and wellbeing services delivered by permanently employed staff and contractors within the following disciplines: medical Services; nursing Services; Musculoskeletal Services; Mental Health Services; and Physical Health & Wellbeing Services. The PSNI’s OHWS is a matter for the Chief Constable who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  The Department funds the Police Rehabilitation and Retraining Trust (PRRT) to provide police officers and former police officers, and their families with clinical and support services. This is funded from the grant aid awarded to PRRT.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the schools where the part-time 20mph speed limit has been installed, in the North Down constituency.
answer_text: There are a total of 15 part time 20mph schools in North Down and Ards Council area, with a further two being installed under Tranche 3 of the Part Time 20mph roll out.The following schools from Tranches 1 & 2 fall within the North Down Constituency Area:1.	Ballyvester Primary School, Donaghadee2.	Clandeboye Primary School, Bangor3.	Donaghadee Primary School, Donaghadee4.	Millisle Primary School, Millisle5.	Priory Integrated College, Bangor6.	Rathmore Primary School, Bangor7.	St. Malachy's Primary School, BangorAlthough none of the Tranche 3 sites fall within North Down area, St. Mary's Primary School, Portaferry and Glastry College, Ballyhalbert will benefit from part time 20mph in the Ards area.This initiative is a significant intervention that will help to ensure that children, parents and staff feel safer as they travel to and from school on a daily basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail whether he has taken any action to provide clear communication on responsibility for patient transport for elderly and vulnerable patients attending hospital appointments, given that GP practices are now refusing to book transport whilst hospitals are still informing patients to contact their GP.
To ask the Minister of Finance whether he plans to bring forward any proposals to the Executive on Air Passenger Duty.
answer_text: ANSWERI am currently considering the recommendations of the independent Fiscal Commission regarding the broad range of tax powers that could be devolved to the Executive, as part of my department’s work on a full Fiscal Framework.  I will engage with the Executive on this in due course. I have no separate plans at this point to bring specific proposals to the Executive in relation to either the Executive’s rate-setting powers in respect of direct long haul Air Passenger Duty (APD), nor short haul APD, where the responsibility still rests with the British Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail her Department's (i) campaigns; and (ii) initiatives to help raise awareness of scams, targeting elderly and vulnerable people in society.
answer_text: Scams targeting older people are particularly cruel, often preying on someone’s loneliness, vulnerability, fear, or kindness to gain access to their personal information and ultimately their money.  The Department of Justice (DoJ) helps to address harm and vulnerability caused by fraud and supports partnership working to tackle the threat of scams through its membership of the ScamwiseNI Partnership, which is chaired by the PSNI.  The Partnership was established in 2016 and now has over 45 partners who work collaboratively to provide valuable information and crucial advice to keep the public informed, aware and vigilant. The ScamwiseNI Partnership has an effective online presence Scamwiseni | nidirect and the Department of Justice uses its own social media channels (X, Bluesky, YouTube & Instagram) to highlight current scams and the steps people can take to protect themselves.  In addition, in partnership with the Northern Ireland Policing Board, the Department provides annual operational funding of £4.25m to Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs), across each of the 11 council areas, who lead on the Department’s response to community safety issues at a local level. These projects include “Older Adults Cyber Safety Events”, “Older Persons Home Security Scheme” and “The Good Morning Scheme”, which is a confidential telephone service for people over 60, aimed at connecting older people with carers within their community and to help people live safely and independently in their homes.Mid & East Antrim PCSP provides an “Older People Support Programme” and many PCSPs have a dedicated Community Safety Warden Scheme, which provide sessions on crime preventions and raising awareness of a wide range of scams.  I was delighted to be invited to open Mid & East Antrim’s Challenging the Scamdemic: A Community Scam Awareness Conference back in March 2025.In Quarter 1 of this year Ards & North Down PCSP delivered 4 Scams & Crime Prevention Talks to 83 participants, all senior citizens. They regularly promote scam awareness through Facebook posts as well as their information stands. They also deliver scam awareness information at the Positive Ageing Roadshow each year and have provided a small grant for a scam awareness project for older people in 2025-26.  The Department provides a 50% funding contribution towards the Crimestoppers Northern Ireland Regional Manager salary, which is match funded by the PSNI. The Regional Manager has a key role in raising awareness and encouraging reporting of intelligence by promoting, developing and maintaining awareness of Crimestoppers throughout Northern Ireland with all partners, stakeholders and the general public.Additionally, Crimestoppers works with all PCSPs to develop and implement localised campaigns, create awareness and provide social media resources to all partners.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide an update on any financial support available to sports clubs to provide them with defibrillators, CPR education, and concussion awareness training, in the North Down constituency.
answer_text: In July 2016 my Department and Sport NI launched the Defibrillators for Sport initiative, with the aim to increase the number of automated external defibrillators (AEDs) in community settings by providing devices to sports clubs and community groups. A total of 1,200 defibrillators, at a cost of £850,000 were provided with successful applicants receiving free defibrillation training on safe use and maintenance. In addition, the scheme also provided CPR training to over 1,000 people.At present, neither my Department nor Sport NI currently has any financial support available to sports clubs specifically for the provision of defibrillators, CPR education or concussion awareness training.However, the Chief Executive of Sport NI has advised me as follows:-“Sport NI’s Creating Opportunities programme is currently open to applications. Under Creating Opportunities applications should focus on people from at least one of the following priority groups:		Women & girls.		People with disabilities.		Children & young people – especially those aged between 11-18 years.		Older people aged over 50.		Ethnically diverse communities.		LGBTQ+ community.		People living in areas of greatest need (based on NISRA postcode, quartile1).		People living in rural areas (based on NISRA postcode).“Therefore, if a club has a participation project that will enable participation through new or additional activity, of which equipment or training may form a part, then this could be eligible. All applications are assessed against the programme criteria on a case-by-case basis. Details on Creating Opportunities are available at https://www.sportni.net/funding/creating-opportunities/ “Creating Opportunities could not be used solely for the purchase of a defibrillator or solely for CPR training or solely for concussion awareness training. Any equipment or training must form part of a wider project focusing on one of the priority groups listed above and must be linked to a new or additional participation project for the club.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps being taken to promote fair and uniform access to disabled play facilities.
answer_text: The provision of play facilities in Northern Ireland is the responsibility of district councils.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the steps her Department is taking to restore the 303a bus service from Bangor to Donaghadee, given that this service has been cancelled on numerous occasions due to driver shortages.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink have been actively recruiting and training new driving staff to ensure reliability of bus services in the North Down area. New drivers and internal transfers to Newtownards Depot will ensure that service reliability on the 303a route will be delivered. A further cohort of drivers will commence on 6th October. The recruitment and training of new driving staff will ensure reliability of our service and retain passenger confidence, and I can confirm that the 303a service will improve this month following introduction of these staff to Newtownards depot.Translink will continue to monitor this service to ensure service punctuality and reliability going forward. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline why diabetic eye screening tests have been moved from community locations in North Down to the Ulster Hospital.
answer_text: The regional Diabetic Eye Screening service is provided by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT).  Clinics takes place at multiple locations in each Trust in an effort to optimise patient access. Unfortunately, the clinic location in the North Down area was based in a non-Health and Social Care facility.  Due to ongoing costs, the clinics could not be maintained at this location and was relocated to the Ulster Hospital. The Public Health Agency (PHA) is working with the provider and the South-Eastern Health and Social Care Trust to secure a fixed Trust location within the Bangor and Newtownards area for that patient cohort.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the steps being taken by his Department to outlaw third party sales of dogs and puppies.
answer_text: A public consultation was held on proposed new rules for sellers and suppliers of puppies and kittens, which included a prohibition on third-party sales, from 2 June to 25 August 2025.  My officials are currently analysing the submissions received and a formal consultation response will be published in due course.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients from Northern Ireland that have had CAR T-cell therapy, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Below are the number of patients from NI who have been approved for CAR-T therapy in the last five years.  To note, after approval, some of these patients may not have been treated for medical reasons.  Release of information in respect of a very small volume of patients creates the potential for patients to be identified and would breach HSC confidentiality responsibilities.  The extensive media coverage of CAR-T in recent months has the potential to increase the risk of breach as the wider public may have an increased interest in the therapy. To protect patient confidentiality the number of patients approved for CAR-T have therefore been grouped as follows:Calendar YearNumber of Approvals2020/2021/2022282023/202441The larger volumes in the latter years’ category are consistent with the publication of additional NICE recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the current funding arrangements for CAR T-cell therapy.
answer_text: SPPG Managed Entry of New Medicines process operates to provide timely access to medicines in line with DoH Circular HSC (SQSD) 12 -22 NICE Technology Appraisals – Process for Endorsement, Implementation, Monitoring and Assurance in NI.  In the absence of a published NICE determination, SPPG has the discretion to apply decisions made by other nationally recognised appraisal bodies such as the Scottish Medicines Consortium (SMC) or the All Wales Specialist Medicines Group (AWSMG).On an annual basis the DoH provides funding to SPPG for the costs of growth in the use of existing NICE approved medicines and to meet the costs of new drug therapies approved in-year. If CAR-T treatments are approved by NICE then patients in NI will be granted access to them on an equitable basis with other nations through the arrangements in place for referral to trusted centres in the wider NHS.SPPG holds funds centrally to commission services which are too specialist to be sustained locally at present and are delivered through providers in GB, including CAR-T, and are responsible for payment of invoices relating to treatment of NI patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any support her Department provides for employers to allow skilled apprentices with the appropriate level of contact time for their course requirements.
answer_text: Under the ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher Level Apprenticeships programmes. my Department funds the Directed Training (also known as ‘off the job’) element of an apprenticeship, which apprentices are required to undertake to gain the skills and knowledge needed for their chosen career. While the level of Directed Training is ultimately determined by industry need, and Training Providers will work with employers to ensure flexibility of approach, programme policy requires apprentices to be employed for a minimum of 21 hours per week to ensure sufficient contact time for course delivery. Businesses are also eligible for an employer incentive payment upon the apprentice’s successful achievement of a Level 2 or Level 3 apprenticeship framework, ranging from £670 to £2,008 depending on the occupational area. This incentive is designed to assist employers with the broader costs of supporting and mentoring apprentices. Departmental funding towards Directed Training has grown in line with employer demand and is now approximately £48 million per year across the ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher Level Apprenticeship programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount been spent on CAR T-cell therapy, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: CAR-T medications are purchased through a Commercial Access Agreement, with the price of the medications being covered by a Patient Access Scheme.  These are confidential pricing agreements agreed between pharmaceutical companies and the Department of Health in England (with input from NICE).It is not therefore possible to release the spend on CAR-T drugs without breaching this confidentiality agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any plans his Department has to improve the experience of citizens with dementia in accessing public services in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department works collaboratively with a wide range of organisations across the statutory, independent and voluntary and community sectors to improve the experience of citizens with dementia in accessing public services. The Regional Dementia Care Pathway is a comprehensive framework designed to support individuals throughout their dementia journey from early diagnosis to end-of-life care. The pathway promotes person-centred care tailored to individual needs. It includes provision for early onset dementia and those with learning disabilities. The Regional Dementia Project Board was established in May 2024 with extensive stakeholder engagement and seeks to improve efficiency and effectiveness within dementia service delivery. The Project Board is in the process of extending the membership to people living with dementia and their carers and this provides a platform for service user and carer expertise to contribute to improving service delivery and access to public services. There is also an education and training workstream that is developing mandatory dementia awareness training for all health and social care staff. This will enable the health and social care workforce to have a better understanding of dementia.All HSC trusts have Dementia Companions within the acute hospital setting who have undertaken work to promote more dementia friendly environments as well as providing one to one support to those with a dementia diagnosis to navigate the acute setting. In addition, Dementia Navigators have been appointed in each Trust to undertake work in the community setting to provide support and guidance to people with a dementia diagnosis and their families, including support to access appropriate pubic services to meet their assessed need.The Department supports the development and use of digital apps such as Apps4dementia which offer trusted apps for self-care, daily activity support, and condition management. These resources support people to live independently for as long as possible alongside accessing public services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans to use artificial intelligence to make efficiencies and improve outcomes in the health service.
answer_text: Artificial Intelligence (AI), and Generative AI in particular, presents significant opportunities to enhance both the productivity of the existing Health and Social Care (HSC) workforce and the quality of care delivered to service users.  Potential benefits include freeing up staff capacity through improved communication tools and the development of personalised treatment plans.The Department of Health’s Digital Strategy for Health and Social Care underpins this transformation, aiming to modernise service delivery through digital innovation. The development of a single electronic care record for every citizen, improving visibility and coordination of care across the system.AI is already being applied in several clinical and operational areas with upcoming plans include testing AI algorithms to support referral and decision-making processes, scheduling and acquisition, reporting and triage, and the generation of alerts for results acknowledgement. However, these capabilities must be carefully balanced against potential risks particularly in relation to clinical efficacy and information governance. It is essential that any AI tools deployed are trustworthy and uphold the privacy and rights of patients and service users.The successful adoption of advanced technologies such as AI and Generative AI depends on several key enablers:High-quality, codified data that accurately reflects service users’ needs and historical treatments, which is essential for training AI models.Robust clinical safety and ethical governance frameworks to oversee the safe and responsible introduction of AI tools.Specialist skills in cloud engineering and cybersecurity, recognising that the computational demands of AI are often best met through cloud-based infrastructure.The HSC system is actively exploring how emerging AI capabilities can be harnessed to improve efficiency across a range of activities. Several proof-of-value projects are currently underway, with strategic oversight provided by the DHCNI AI Steering Group.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how she is preparing the economy to make the most of the opportunities of Artificial Intelligence.
answer_text: My department is taking forward a range of activity to ensure we are well positioned to make the most of the opportunities of Artificial Intelligence, aligned with my economic priorities.  Through Invest NI, my department is investing £16.3 million in the Artificial Intelligence Collaboration Centre (AICC), led by Ulster University in partnership with Queen’s University Belfast. The Centre is promoting AI awareness and responsible adoption among businesses and provides hands-on support and skills development for SMEs, helping them to harness the potential of AI to improve their processes. Building on the work of the AICC, City Deal-funded projects such as Momentum One Zero in Belfast and the Cognitive Analytics & Digital Robotics Innovation Centre (CADRIC) in Derry are driving digital innovation and AI adoption to the next stage. We are also developing the skills pipeline, working closely with universities, colleges, and businesses to ensure people can gain the skills needed for new and evolving roles. This includes delivery of the Digital Skills Action Plan, the Software and Cyber Sectoral Action Plan, the funding of 390 postgraduate scholarships in AI-related courses through the AICC and a range of skills initiatives such as Skill Up, Assured Skills Academies, and Higher Level Apprenticeships supporting both new entrants and the existing workforce to take advantage of AI-driven opportunities. In light of the publication of two important AI-themed reports in recent weeks, including The Matrix foresight study on AI and the Future of Work, I am at the early stages of establishing a working group to take forward its recommendations and to identify how we can collaborate with industry, academia and wider society to manage, and capture fully the opportunities from, the transition to AI technologies. I am working closely with the Executive’s Chief Science and Technology Adviser in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans to make efficiencies and cut waste in the health service.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the total cost of staff away days within her Department, in each of last five years.
answer_text: The total spend on staff development days within the department, in each of the last five years is shown in the table below.  Please note that this excludes training.Year 2020/212021/222022/232023/242024/25TotalSpend£0.00£0.00£265.00£710.70£3,401.47£4,377.17

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance detail any plans he has to make efficiencies and cut waste in the civil service.
answer_text: All Ministers and departments share responsibility for delivering value for money. My Department is working closely with all departments as they prepare their 5 Year Plans. As part of this process, departments are being encouraged to identify and set out clear proposals for efficiencies and transformation initiatives that can be taken forward over the five-year period, with the aim of driving down costs, improving service delivery, and ensuring that resources are targeted where they are most needed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the organisations that have received funding from the Arts Council of Northern Ireland in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Arts Council.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive.  Due to the volume of detail required to provide an answer to your question, I have arranged for this to be placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any discussions that have taken place with GP practices in relation to some practices requiring private appointments and fees to sign the health declarations for those wishing to be registered as a childcare provider.
answer_text: My Department has recently carried out a public consultation to seek the views of a range of stakeholders on the requirement for prospective childcare workers to provider a GP-countersigned health declaration. The outcome of the consultation exercise will be published in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any discussions with the Department of Health regarding concerns that some GP practices now require private appointments and fees to sign the health declarations for those wishing to be registered as childcare providers.
answer_text: The Department of Health (DoH) has statutory responsibility for the regulation and inspection of registered childcare providers.The requirement for GPs to sign health declarations is embedded within the DoH Minimum Standards for Childminding and Daycare.    In March 2025, DoH launched a consultation to seek the views of stakeholders specifically in relation to two issues, which included the requirement for the GP-signed health assessments. The consultation closed in June 2025, and it is my understanding that responses are currently being considered by DoH. My officials engage regularly with their DoH counterparts, including through the cross-departmental Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Task and Finish Group, to discuss cross-cutting issues affecting the childcare sector. Concerns raised by stakeholders regarding GP-signed health assessments during my Department’s engagement as part of the development of the ELC Strategy have been highlighted to DoH.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are plans to install a sensory room at the Emergency Department waiting room at the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children to assist parents and children while they wait to be seen.
answer_text: Belfast Trust have advised that there are currently no plans to install a sensory room in ED at the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children due to the footprint of the department and the very limited clinical space.  However, there are currently plans in progress to increase the clinical space and the Trust have had an opportunity to review all requirements of the department.  They are currently engaging with a charity to procure a mobile sensory room to locate in the vicinity of ED and are hopeful that this will be available in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there is a requirement for highway maintenance vehicles to cover loose stones and other loose items when in transit, in order to protect other drivers.
answer_text: Drivers and operators are legally required to ensure that the load their vehicle is carrying is secure at all times, so that no danger or nuisance is likely to be caused to any person or property due to any part of the load falling off or being blown from the vehicle.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any (i) discussions she has had with the taxi industry to increase taxi availability in towns and cities; and (ii) action she has taken as a result.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the regulation of the taxi industry, in relation to taxi driver, operator and vehicle licencing. The recruitment of new and retention of existing drivers is the responsibility of the taxi industry.  My department has two main functions that currently impact on the availability of taxi drivers: setting maximum fares and awarding taxi drivers’ licences.My department increased the maximum fare for taxis in December 2023 and since the introduction of the test for taxi drivers, the Driver Vehicle Agency has made a number of changes to the theory test including lowering the overall pass mark for the theory test and providing revision guides.In working with the taxi industry, to address the range of issues it currently faces, I also announced earlier this year a phased review of taxi policy and legislation, commencing with a review of Class C Taxi Licensing. As part of this review, I have met with the taxi industry to better understand their concerns.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the timeline on (i) the Departmental response to the Rethinking Our Resources consultation; and (ii) the provision of guidance to local councils on the future of waste collection.
answer_text: The Departmental Response will require Executive approval, so I am unable to provide a definitive timeline.My officials engage with local councils regularly to provide support and guidance on matters relating to resources and waste collection and will continue to do so.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the timeline for the provision a 24-7 thrombectomy service.
answer_text: The current thrombectomy service operates from 8am-5pm. It is estimated that just over £5 million recurrent funding is required to expand to a 24/7 service.   Following approval of the final budget for 2025/26, my Department was left with a funding gap of over £600 million.  In this context, and while I remain committed to the establishment of 24/7 thrombectomy service, I am currently unable to allocate the required additional funding. Consequently, at this point, I am unable to commit to a timeline for a 24/7 service.In the interim I am committed to ensuring that as many people as possible benefit from the service within the current hours of operation. To this end, it should be noted that the thrombectomy rate in Northern Ireland in 2024/25 was 5.2%, higher than the regional rates in England, Scotland and Wales.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given the rise of Bangor and other ports being used as cruise ship destinations, whether her Department will invest in ports outside of Belfast.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for ports policy and the legislative framework within which ports operate in the north. There are four trust ports in the north–Belfast, Warrenpoint, Foyle, and Coleraine. Public Trust Ports are autonomous, self-financing statutory bodies whose constitutions are set out in legislation. They operate on a commercial basis with their profits re-invested to improve their facilities. While I appoint the Chair and members of the Trust Ports at Belfast, Foyle and Warrenpoint, I have no remit or input into their daily operational business activities. Bangor Port is owned and managed by Ards and North Down Borough Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any discussions he has had with the Chancellor, or His Majesty's Government regarding the reduction of VAT for our tourism and hospitality sector.
answer_text: ANSWERI have consistently engaged with the British Government on the challenges facing our tourism and hospitality sectors, particularly in relation to the higher VAT rates faced by businesses here relative to the South. VAT remains an Excepted Matter, and the power to change the rate of VAT rests with the British Government.I specifically raised this issue with the previous Chief Secretary to the Treasury (CST) Darren Jones MP, in writing, on 10 April and in person during our Finance Interministerial Standing Committee (F:ISC) meeting on 24 April. It was also raised at my meeting with the Chancellor on 12 August. I have called for a reduction in VAT to be considered to enable our hospitality businesses to compete on a level playing field in the all-Ireland economy. Unfortunately, the British Government continue to show no willingness to address this issue. In May of this year, my department also published the Cost of Doing Business research report. This report sets out the evidence concerning VAT disparity across the island and the impact on the hospitality sector locally. I will continue to call for change on this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the (i) number; and (ii) value, of successful applications to the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme, in the North Down constituency.
answer_text: As of 10 September 2025, a total of 1,165 children in the North Down constituency were registered for the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS), and £855,051 was claimed by childcare providers through the scheme to reduce childcare costs for working parents.AQW 31146/22-27, which was published in the weekly answers booklet and deposited in the Assembly Library for week ending 19 September 2025 provided a breakdown across all constituencies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions her Department has had in relation to EU regulations preventing the use of red diesel to power private leisure boats, which is impacting our maritime tourism industry.
answer_text: This falls outside the remit of my Department. As such. my Department has not had any discussions in relation to EU regulations preventing the use of red diesel to power private leisure boats.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions he is taking to prevent young people from starting the use of vapes and other nicotine products.
answer_text: In 2022, the Department of Health introduced The Nicotine Inhaling Products (Age of Sale and Proxy Purchasing) Regulations 2015 which banned the sale of nicotine vapes to under 18s. This legislation also made it illegal to buy such products on behalf of a child (proxy sale).The Public Health Agency (PHA) works closely with partners to deliver a range of initiatives to raise awareness of vaping, pilot new services, support enforcement and carry out research to better understand vaping behaviours in young people. A wide range of online and printed smoking and vaping resources were developed by the PHA, tailored for different groups, including children, young people, parents, carers and educators.Funded by the PHA, Cancer Focus NI (CFNI) delivered its ‘Smokebusters’ programme to over 11,000 P6 and P7 pupils across 171 schools in 2024/25, to educate children on the harms of smoking and vaping.The PHA co-ordinates and supports tobacco control enforcement activities in partnership with local Government, which includes test purchasing events for sales of nicotine inhaling products as well as tobacco to under 18s.The Tobacco and Vapes Bill, currently progressing through Parliament, will ban the sale of non-nicotine vapes and other nicotine products to under 18s. The Bill will also ban vapes and nicotine products from being deliberately branded, promoted and advertised to children to stop the next generation from becoming addicted to nicotine.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail regeneration funding in North Down, in the last five years.
answer_text: Since taking office, I have taken significant steps to expand early learning and childcare provision and address costs for working families.  From September 2025, an additional 2,400 funded pre-school places in 105 settings have transitioned to full-time pre-school provision of 22.5 hours per week, including a free school meal for eligible children.   This means that 50% of pre-school places are now full-time, with further work planned to transition a further 100 settings to full-time from September 2026. Significant additional investment has also been provided to a number of early years services to extend provision supporting children facing disadvantage, such as Sure Start, the Pathway Fund, Toybox, including a number of cross-departmental programmes to support children with additional needs/disabilities. These programmes target children and families facing multiple disadvantage; those who stand to benefit most from quality early years provision.  Additional funding has also been allocated to enhance sustainability and capacity within the early learning and childcare sector.One of the most significant achievements has been the development and implementation of the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS). Launched in September 2024, the NICSS introduced a 15% subsidy for working parents eligible for Tax Free Childcare (TFC), initially with children not yet at primary school. From September 2025, the scheme expanded to include children up to age 11 (aged 16 for children with a disability). When combined with TFC, eligible families receive up to 32% savings on their childcare costs.  Since its introduction, it is estimated that the NICSS has saved eligible families over £15m and over £30m when combined with TFC savings.These measures represent real progress and good value with the funding awarded by the Executive to date.  I remain committed to setting out what more is possible in the context of the full Early Learning and Childcare Strategy which I will bring forward for Executive consideration and public consultation in the coming weeks.  However, implementation of the Strategy will be subject to Executive approval and agreement to release the additional funding required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any support and funding his Department plans to provide to local councils to meet the requirements of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: The Climate Change Act (NI) 2022 sets legally binding targets for emissions reductions and requires all departments to contribute to carbon budgets and climate action plans. My department leads on climate policy and is consulting on a draft Climate Action Plan (2023–2027), which includes cross-departmental and local government responsibilities. DAERA offers a range of grants and funding schemes for environmental and climate-related projects, including for local councils. Future funding and support will be subject to departmental budgetary allocations. Councils are key delivery partners for adaptation, waste, land use, and community engagement and my department will continue to work with councils and offer support, for the emissions sectors which fall under my departments remit. This will be through funding schemes such the Environment Fund, Woodland Grants and Household Waste Recycling Collaborative Change Programme to assist them to meet the requirements of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the steps he has taken since taking office in relation to (i) the expansion of childcare provision; and (ii) addressing childcare costs for working families.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail planned future regeneration projects in North Down.
answer_text: The development at Queen’s Parade will of course have a significant contribution to the regeneration of Bangor and I was pleased cut the sod on Thursday 2nd October with a range of stakeholders. My Department has also issued a Contract for Funding for up to £500k to Ards and North Down Borough Council for a Borough Wide Shop Front project in Bangor, Newtownards, Donaghadee, Comber and Holywood.  As budgets have not been set beyond 25/26, further projects are not yet approved but my officials are working with Council colleagues to develop and prioritise regeneration projects for future delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will consider reviewing Universal Credit payment arrangements, to ensure fairness for claimants who receive backdated pay awards from their employer.
answer_text: My Department does not have any current plans to review the treatment of backdated pay awards in Universal Credit.  Universal Credit is designed to take account of a claimant’s earnings in the assessment period in which His Majesty’s Revenue and Customs informs the Department that they have been paid. Where a claimant receives backdated pay in a single assessment period, Universal Credit will take account of the backpay in that assessment period. The rules on the treatment of backdated pay awards apply equally throughout the United Kingdom to all Universal Credit claimants who receive earned income.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any discussions she has held with Translink, in relation to the introduction of permanent late-night bus and train services in towns and cities.
answer_text: I am aware of the potential benefits late night public transport services provide for the nighttime economy and those working and socialising in the city.  I have been working with my Executive colleagues and a range of other stakeholders, including Translink, to support the extension of late-night public transport services and subject to the necessary funding being in place, I would hope that these services could commence later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the redevelopment of Bangor Bus and Rail Station.
answer_text: Translink are developing a business case which is expected to be submitted to my Department by Spring 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether DfI Roads plan to improve the usability of her Department's tracking system for reporting faults, to (i) update users when the status changes; and (ii) provide users with clear updates and timescales as to what action her Department will take.
answer_text: My department receives approximately 90,000 road faults and 60,000 lighting faults annually.  The system for reporting such faults was first introduced by the Department in 2015, and is now available for members of the public to report instances directly using this online system or via the NI Direct contact centre.When a fault is reported, the customer receives an automated acknowledgement email which provides an indication of the priority of the fault.  The customer is also provided with a webpage link and reference number to facilitate the tracking of the fault.Customers can also use this link to identify the status of the fault, any results of investigation and where possible an indication of the timescale for repair.Due to the high volume of fault reporting, it is not possible to carry out adhoc investigations on all faults reported in this way.  Lower risk issues may therefore be dealt with during the planned routine inspections carried out by my officials on a cyclical basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline what the Interim Regional Planning Commission has achieved to date.
