answer_text: The interim Regional Planning Commission (iRPC) was established in an advisory role to reflect key stakeholder interests across the community, business and environmental sectors as part of the Planning Improvement Programme (PIP) in response to the recommendations set out in the NIAO and PAC reports on the planning system in the North.  During its first year the Commission considered the challenges and opportunities in the planning system here, including planning fees, ongoing work to improve statutory consultees’ performance, progress of the joint PIP work plan and Councillors’ post-election training. Over the past 12 months, an agreed work programme was implemented through focused stakeholder engagement sessions to gather evidence from a range of sectors, with the aim of identifying effective practices and offering potential recommendations for consideration to improve the planning system in the North. Stakeholders included the NI Chamber of Commerce, SOLACE, NILGA, local planning authorities, the Planning Appeals Commission, NI Housing Executive, NI Federation of Housing Associations, Rural Community Network, Renewable NI, Ulster Farmers’ Union, Royal Society of Ulster Architects and the RTPI. The minutes of the iRPC meetings are available to view on the Departments website and can be accessed here https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/interim-regional-planning-commission-minutes-meetings.  The work programme was completed by the iRPC early summer 2025, and its report is currently being finalised.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Bangor Waterfront project as part of the Belfast Region City Deal.
answer_text: Two design teams have been appointed by Ards and North Down Borough Council for development of projects at Pickie Fun Park and Ballyholme Yacht Club, with further design team appointments to follow in due course. My officials are also working with Council colleagues on a Social Value Action Plan for the Bangor Waterfront programme and will continue to work collaboratively with Council to progress the development

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the total cost of staff away days within her Department, in each of last five years.
answer_text: The total cost of staff away days within the department, in each of the last five years, is shown in the table below.		 2020/212021/222022/232023/242024/25TotalDepartmental Spend£0.00£2,242£21,460£44,894£32,944£101,540

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline (i) the current legislation regarding the use of e-scooters on public roads and footways; and (ii) whether she has any plans to change this legislation.
answer_text: It is currently illegal to use e-scooters on public roads and public spaces here and I have noplans to change that position. Anyone using an e-scooter illegally may be subject toenforcement action by the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the implementation of the 37 actions identified in the Lough Neagh Action Plan.
answer_text: Of the 37 actions within the Plan, 14 have been delivered, 22 are actively progressing, and one is pending, contingent on another action being taken forward first.  I remain committed to driving forward the delivery of the remaining actions at pace.  Progress to date reflects coordinated efforts across key themes including scientific research and innovation, enhanced monitoring and enforcement, education and stakeholder engagement, regulatory reform, and strategic communications. Whilst important strides have been made, it is essential to recognise that the recovery of Lough Neagh will require sustained, long-term action.  Given the depth and complexity of the problem, substantial recovery will take many years, if not decades.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the reduction in the use of rental properties for office accommodation in the public sector.
answer_text: ANSWERThe NICS Office Estate Strategy is baselined using data for the year ending 31 March 2022 gathered as part of the Office Estate Review. The Strategy and first progress report, published in May 2025, are available on the Department’s website.Of sixteen buildings removed from the portfolio since 2022, four were leased office buildings: Park House (Belfast), Design Centre (Belfast), Carlisle House (Derry) and The Klondyke Building (Belfast) as well a file storage facility at Dargan Crescent (Belfast.)The Strategy plans for a further four leased properties to be exited by 2028.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has any plans in relation to literary development in children.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount spent on staff away days within his Department, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department’s expenditure on staff away days in each of the last five years is set out in the table below:2020/212021/222022/232023/242024/25Department*£124£0£1,620£3,277£2,694Strategic Planning and Performance Group*£661£398£2,616*From April 2022 the Health and Social Care Board was closed and brought into the Department of Health as the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG).Staff away-days are part of business planning and staff development processes which enable engagement with staff about strategic priorities and targets and the development of branch business plans. Approval is required in advance for each activity and costs are monitored closely against the budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department is considering any new regulations regarding ownership of local football clubs, following the introduction of the Football Governance Act in England.
answer_text: At present I have no plans to bring forward any legislation similar to that of the Football Governance Act 2025. However, I do welcome the ongoing work by the Northern Ireland Football League (NIFL) to develop standards for club ownership in Northern Ireland and I have asked my officials to continue to engage with NIFL and other stakeholders on this matter.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any discussions they have had with the Westminster government to (i) improve; and (ii) reduce, the cost of transport from Northern Ireland to Great Britain.
answer_text: Department for the EconomyASSEMBLY QUESTIONS: PART INPUT REQUESTAQ No: AQW 31469/22-27For Part Input Answer on: Thursday 25 September 2025Lead / Requesting Dept: TEOTabled By:  Peter Martin MLAConstituency: North DownPolitical Party:	Democratic Unionist PartyQUESTION IN FULL: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any discussions they have had with the Westminster government to (i) improve; and (ii) reduce, the cost of transport from Northern Ireland to Great Britain.	Note from TEO as both DfE and DfI declined transfer DfI and DfE Colleagues,Further to previous correspondence regarding this AQW (email requests for it to be transferred to DfE / DfI), TEO will now answer this question. However, we require part input from both DfE and DfI to enable us to do so, as we have no policy responsibility for this matter.DfE helpfully previously provided part input to AQW 22197 which touched upon this matter (the price of transport links between Northern Ireland and Great Britain) with its content comprising a significant part of the answer, as TEO did not have the information or responsibility for this issue.  We would be grateful if part inputs could cover: (DfE) in that they look after flight routes and have a couple of NI-GB PSO routes and would deal with aviation policy.Could you both also detail any engagement which may have taken place between your Minister and their UKG counterparts.When returning this please send to me (Sarah Hamilton) and TEO Assembly Section. It would be helpful if you could get this to us by COP Thursday 25 September.Part Input Requested from DfE:(DfE) in respect of flight routes, NI-GB PSO routes and aviation policy.Detail any engagement which may have taken place between your Minister and their UKG counterparts.Part Input supplied by DfE:(DfE) in respect of flight routes, NI-GB PSO routes and aviation policy.civil aviation is a reserved matter for the British Department of Transport (DfT).  The Department for the Economy (DfE) has no role in the setting of flight ticket prices but has repeatedly made it clear to British Government officials that Air Passenger Duty (APD) tax is an unfair tax on flight tickets which needs to be addressed and removed at national level, given its particularly detrimental impact on the North's passengers due to our physical remoteness to Britain, and the lack of an alternative means of travel such as road or rail options.Derry City and Strabane District Council manage the Public Service Obligation (PSO) contract for the City of Derry Airport for its route to London.  DfE and DfT jointly fund the PSO and provide this funding to the council who manage the contract with Loganair. Passenger fares are commercial matters for CoDA management and airline operators.  DfE are developing an aviation policy for the north.  Department officials continue to engage with stakeholders across the aviation sector, local government, and tourism and investment partners.  The public consultation do

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 19691/22-27, for an update on actions taken by her Department to address persistent flooding affecting Balloo Avenue, Bangor.
answer_text: Road drainage systems are only designed to cope with normal average annual daily rainfall. They cannot, nor are they intended to, cope with the sudden, short duration, high intensity rain events that have become more common in recent times. Flooding at Balloo Avenue has been an ongoing and historic problem for the past 25 years with this area flooding during ‘extreme’ weather events.My Department has carried out cleaning and jetting work to ensure our portion of the system is working to full capacity, however, years of development has meant that more and more water has been put into the same system.Unfortunately, the problem cannot be fixed by simply carrying out some drainage work locally, with responsibility for large sections of the watercourse falling to riparian ownership. We will, however, continue to monitor and clean this system to ensure it is operating to its full capacity, as well as investigate any measures we can implement to reduce the impact of future weather events.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has taken any steps to map the locations of publicly-accessible defibrillators.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) leads on the implementation of the Community Resuscitation Strategy and is funded to provide a supporting role in advising organisations, businesses, and individuals regarding siting of defibrillators and best practice for defibrillators. NIAS has partnered with the British Heart Foundation to develop The Circuit (https://www.thecircuit.uk), a national online defibrillator network that connects every ambulance service in the UK. NIAS can readily provide information on Automated External Defibrillators (AEDs) registered on The Circuit to 999 callers who require access. Currently, there are 4,014 community defibrillators registered.All public access defibrillators (PADs) should be registered on The Circuit, and each must have an assigned guardian. Both NIAS and The Circuit recommend registering multiple guardians to reduce the risk of missed emails or oversight.The responsibility for maintenance, upkeep and checks of PADs then rests with the registered guardians of the defibrillator. Regular, automatic emails are sent to each guardian from The Circuit, regarding checks to be carried out.Public Access Defibrillators (PADs) should only be accessed under instruction from NIAS call handlers. This ensures that information on The Circuit remains correct. When a caller retrieves a PAD, it is no longer available on The Circuit, until it has been returned and made ‘emergency ready’ on The Circuit by the guardian. The location of all PADs is stored on The Circuit and available to the NIAS Community Resuscitation Team to aid decisions on new device placement.  A map detailing the location of nearby PADs can be found at https://www.defibfinder.uk or by contacting the NIAS Community Resuscitation Team at resus.admin@nias.hscni.net or via telephone at 028 9040 5215.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline his rationale not to review and extend the scope of the High Hedges Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 to cover all vegetation that blocks sunlight.
answer_text: I have no plans to review and extend the scope of the High Hedges Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 to cover all vegetation that blocks sunlight as there is no evidence of any significant issues with the current legislation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the actions her Department is taking to improve motorbike safety.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer for everyone. My officials are currently reviewing a number of potential interventions that may help make our roads safer for motorcyclists. These include targeted road user awareness campaigns, and investigations into the possible use of PRIME markings.  I can also advise that my officials recently met with PSNI Road Policing Strategic Unit to discuss this ongoing work.   When options are fully explored and provided to me I will then be able to consider those that would have the greatest positive impact for motorcyclists.In addition, there are a number of documents already used by my Department to ensure that the issues experienced by motorcyclists are known and considered when developing new roads infrastructure. This guidance is publicly available at;https://www.northernireland.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/motorcycle-infrastructure-design-guidance-dem-92-23.pdf .

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given it is Gynaecological Cancer Awareness Month, whether his Department will update the messaging used in cervical screening information to make clear it does not detect ovarian or any other gynaecological cancer, similar to what has been done in England.
answer_text: The current public information on cervical screening does not reference other forms of cancer. The Public Health Agency’s (PHA) public facing information and resources for all screening programmes are kept under regular review and are updated as necessary. The PHA will consider any recent changes made to the information in England and if it is appropriate will incorporate similar messaging locally at the next review of their patient leaflets for the Northern Ireland Cervical Screening Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline how her Department is planning to tackle the cost of doing business.
answer_text: In my previous role as Finance Minister, I commissioned the Ulster University Economic Policy Centre to examine the cost of doing business in the north. That research was published in May and highlights the range of cost challenges that businesses have faced in recent years. These challenges have worsened due to the decisions of the British Government, including an increase in employers’ National Insurance Contributions from April. Recent decisions by the US Administration in relation to trade and tariffs have also created additional costs, uncertainty and supply chain disruption cross tradeable sectors of the economy.  The Executive does not have the financial capability to mitigate bad decisions taken in London or Washington that are detrimental to businesses in the north. Ahead of its upcoming Budget on 26 November, my colleague the Minister of Finance continues to urge the Chief Secretary to the Treasury to reduce the cost of doing business in the north – and I will continue to support him and advocate for local business at every opportunity. In terms of what I can do within my remit, my department, alongside its delivery partners, provides a range of supports for businesses to help them become more competitive, through energy efficiency, innovation, skills and support for exploring new markets, for example.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the digitalisation of patients’ medical records.
answer_text: The digitisation of patient records through the encompass programme, which uses the Epic electronic patient record system is now live in all Health and Social Care Trusts after a phased rollout completed in May 2025.  This introduces a single digital care record for every patient and service user and aims to improve patient care, safety, and efficiency by providing staff with access to vital information and allowing patients to view and manage their own health data via the My Care patient portal.Stabilisation and optimisation activities are ongoing with additional elements delivered including Northern Ireland Specialist Transport and Retrieval (NISTAR) in July 2025.  Further areas including Mortality & Morbidity Pathways, Healthcare in Prisons, Child Health System Replacement and Children’s Social Care are under development and will be implemented over the coming year along with further enhancements to the My Care portal.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she has any plans to reform MOT testing.
answer_text: A Consultation on the future of MOT testing here launched earlier this year and closed on 16th April 2025. The consultation primarily sought views on potentially changing the frequency of MOT testing for private cars, motorcycles and light goods vehicles after their initial test at 4 years and 3 years respectively.The consultation presented a number of options for consideration. There was a focus on road safety, economic and environmental impact, increased insurance premiums and the impact on our local automotive industry. All of these issues will be factored into future deliberations on potential changes to the vehicle testing regime. My officials are currently preparing a detailed paper for my consideration on the future of MOT testing here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans he has to review the Local Government Act (Northern Ireland) 2014.
answer_text: I have tasked my officials with preparing a call for evidence on the current local government legislative framework which includes the Local Government Act (Northern Ireland) 2014. The results from this exercise will inform any future decision on the need to amend local government legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any plans in place to allow business rates holidays for businesses that can demonstrate loss of revenue following disruption due to roads and utility or regeneration works.
answer_text: The rating and valuation system already contains an appropriate mechanism to consider the impact of such works on businesses affected. Where a ratepayer feels that disruption has been significant and protracted an application may be made to the District Valuer for the valuation to be reviewed. Any decision will be determined by established case law and a long-standing statutory framework that supports the stability of a tax base used to fund central and local government services. The degree to which public works may affect the Net Annual Value (NAV) of premises in a particular locality is based on the duration of the works and severity of impact on the businesses affected. As an annual tax the system is not, however, designed to respond immediately to temporary events that affect selected properties.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any reforms to the planning system she aims to bring forward.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any discussions he has had with the NI Environment Agency (NIEA) to address anti-social behaviour on NIEA-owned beaches at Helen’s Bay and Crawfordsburn.
answer_text: Antisocial behaviour remains a widespread issue across the council borough and further afield.  Shortly after assuming my role, I can confirm that in February 2024 I met with officials responsible for the management of Crawfordsburn Country Park to discuss matters relating to ongoing antisocial behaviour.  During the meeting, my officials outlined issues within the park in particular:- While site management staff regularly maintain an enhanced presence throughout the spring and summer period when antisocial matters are most prevalent, NIEA does not have any enforcement remit in relation to addressing matters around antisocial behaviour.  Powers of enforcement are held by both Ards and North Down Borough Council and the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI).  During periods of antisocial behaviour onsite, my officials work in close contact with the PSNI in the management of these issues; and  - the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) response to a consultation around applicable enforcement legislation from the Department of Justice and Department for Communities. In its response to the consultation, NIEA outlined the need for commencement of currently outstanding articles within the Criminal Justice Order, which provided a potential opportunity for introduction of measures including issuing of fixed penalty notices in lieu of prosecution (as is currently required) along with powers of seizure of alcohol.Since this meeting my officials have attended multi-agency meetings with a range of relevant stakeholders, including both the local council and PSNI.  These meetings provided a forum for discussion and review of operational matters; however, the enforcement remit remains with both Ards and North Down Borough Council and the PSNI.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he is planning any legislative changes to ensure the distribution of surplus food from businesses to help alleviate food poverty.
answer_text: Whilst, due to a constrained Assembly Mandate, there are currently no plans to make legislative changes, I would like to highlight several areas work that my department are leading on to address these issues.  My department is currently leading several measures to address food waste through the Northern Ireland Food Strategy Framework.  This includes delivering a two-year cross departmental action plan which sets out 39 actions and a commitment to build purposeful partnerships across government and with key stakeholders to deliver real change and promote a positive future for our food system.	There are a number of actions within the plan that my Department will lead on that focus on addressing food inequalities. This will include working with key stakeholders to build a culture of redirecting surplus food to those who will benefit, helping to alleviate poverty, and developing a cross departmental approach to applying the “Right to Food” guiding principle as set out in the NI Food Strategy Framework.Alongside this my Department’s Waste Prevention Programme “Stopping Waste in its Tracks” focusses on food waste and redistribution with key actions to deliver behaviour change campaigns to reduce consumer food waste and to work with partners that support food redistribution networks to prevent food waste.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) her definition of what a good job entails; and (ii) what a employee should expect from an employer.
answer_text: In June 2024, my Department set out that it would use the Carnegie Framework as the basis for assessing Good Jobs.  This Framework identifies seven dimensions of job quality and my Department is working to progress all of these areas with a particular focus on improving job security, guaranteed hours and low wages. The latest results from our Good Jobs measure shows that 67% of jobs in the North met the ‘good jobs’ criteria. Research shows that improving job quality has benefits for employers and employees, helping to drive productivity. Indeed, the most successful organisations are those in which employers and employees work collaboratively and where employees are valued and respected.  My Good Jobs Employment Bill will help to reinforce the importance of positive working relationships between employers and employees. As a minimum, all employees should expect their employer to follow Labour Relations Agency codes of practice and to ensure adherence with employment legislation. The introduction of a Good Jobs Charter will help employers and employees to understand how to create better and more good jobs. It will follow the Carnegie framework and will identify those important aspects of work that best contribute towards the creation of a good job. These include issues such as job security, employee voice and representation, work-life balance and pay and benefits.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the outstanding provisions of the Reservoirs Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 will be implemented.
answer_text: The majority of the outstanding provisions of the Reservoirs Act (NI) 2015 (the Act) require further subordinate legislation to be implemented.  The Executive agreed to the progressing of five pieces of intertwined secondary legislation which would further implement the majority of the provisions of the Act in October 2024 and the Department tabled the legislation with the Committee for Infrastructure in November 2024. However, notwithstanding several Departmental briefings to the Committee for Infrastructure in the intervening period, the Committee scrutiny process remains ongoing at present and therefore a public safety risk remains until such times as the secondary legislation comes into force.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any plans she has to bring legislative changes in the Sentencing Bill to toughen fines and sentences for those convicted of the assault of retail workers.
answer_text: My proposed Sentencing Bill will legislate for a new offence of assaulting a person who is providing services to the public, performing a public duty or providing a public service.  This would include attacks on retail workers.This new offence will carry a higher maximum penalty on conviction than the sentences currently available for similar less serious assault offences.  Additionally, if a more serious assault offence is charged, I will make the fact that the victim is such a worker a statutory aggravating factor, meaning the courts will be required to reflect this specifically in the sentences imposed.I intend to introduce this Sentencing Bill before the end of 2025, subject to Executive approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail why it is not a requirement for bus operators to roll-out wheelchair seat belts on all buses.
answer_text: Bus operators must comply with the current legislative requirements around the construction and use of their vehicles.  These requirements, which can be found in the Public Service Vehicle Accessibility Regulations (NI) 2003 (SR 2003 No. 37) set out the minimum permissible number of wheelchair spaces and associated restraint systems (belts) which must be fitted to buses and coaches. Generally, all buses and coaches must have at least one wheelchair space and restraint system, although there are a small number of exemptions to the requirement for specific vehicle types, for example buses which are more than 20 years old and buses used for community transport. There is no restriction on operators wishing to install additional wheelchair spaces and belts in their own vehicles.  I will be writing to Translink in relation to providing additional wheelchair spaces on buses, whenever they are purchasing new fleet.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when her Department will carry out works to link the Newtownards to Bangor Greenway from the Green Road to Bangor City Centre.
answer_text: My department works closely with councils in the delivery of greenways and I was pleased be able to contribute significant funding to Ards and North Down Borough Council to support the Newtownards to Bangor greenway project.My department has developed a draft Active Travel Delivery Plan, which outlines proposals for future active travel networks within our towns and cities including Bangor.  Officials are currently reviewing responses to the public consultation, which closed in February 2025, and I hope to publish the final plan later this year. My department is also currently considering potential future improvements to pedestrian and cycling infrastructure between the proposed end of the greenways and existing active travel infrastructure on Balloo Road.  Design development is at an early stage and engagement with stakeholders and the public will commence once plans are suitably developed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action that has been taken to help reduce the costs of gluten free food products to assist those who have coeliac disease.
answer_text: The HSC provides a number of products on prescription with no charge which help support patients who need to maintain a gluten free diet.  The range of staple gluten-free products which are available on prescription includes bread/rolls, breakfast cereals, crackers and crispbreads, flour, flour mixes, oats, pasta, pizza bases.   Items which are not consistent with healthy eating advice such as cakes and biscuits are not recommended for prescribing.  Charitable organisations like Coeliac UK support patients with dietary advice and signposting to suitable recipes etc.Unfortunately, the Department of Health has no influence on the prices charged by manufacturers and retailers for gluten free products.  On a regular basis, charitable organisations and the media do highlight the increased costs of such products. (eg BBC Breakfast News 8.20am 4 September 2025) Manufacturers, however, have stressed the additional cost of producing small batches of a product separately from normal production processes, the additional cleaning of equipment and/or machinery, expensive ingredients and extensive testing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the rationale as to why she is yet to make a decision on actions to address inconsiderate pavement parking, despite having the findings report since December 2023.
answer_text: Following the public consultation on options to tackle inconsiderate pavement parking and the publication of the subsequent findings report, my officials have developed potential next steps for my consideration, and I plan to issue a formal response once I have determined the best way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline her plans for the high street.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for recommendation 8 of the High Street Task Force Report: ‘Fair Work on Our High Streets’. Delivering on this recommendation aligns with my economic priority on Good Jobs. Work is ongoing within my Department to progress this recommendation and this includes the development of a new Employment Rights Bill, promotion of greater adoption of the Real Living Wage, and collaboration with the Labour Relations Agency to create a Good Employment Charter.  This work will have a positive impact across High Streets in the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the uptake of the Back in Business scheme, in each local Council area.
answer_text: Land and Property Services publishes figures on the Back in Business rate support scheme from April 2024, which can be viewed via the link below. The figures include the number of applications and awards broken down by District Council, with the latest figures at 30th June 2025. Figures will be updated in early October to reflect the position at 30th September 2025.Portal | Back in Business rate support scheme applications and awards from April 2024

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any plans he has to review the non-domestic vacant property rate.
answer_text: ANSWERThis issue is currently being reviewed in the context of the 2025/26 Strategic Rating Reviews. The associated Terms of Reference for that process, published on 2 June 2025, refers to the issue of the non-domestic vacant property rate. Significant work has been undertaken in recent months on these reviews including background research, tax base analysis, and extensive stakeholder interviews.  The reviews are expected to be completed by early October 2025.  Following the completion of this work, I will be considering the various options in advance of briefing my Executive colleagues on next steps with a view to making any changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following discussion with the Rainbow Project as part of the co-design process for the new Gender Identify Service, whether the Rainbow Project asked for gender affirming care to be imbedded in the new service.
answer_text: Engagement with interested individuals and groups was held from 2020 onwards.  The specific requests of each individual/group were not considered in isolation.  A collective decision was taken based on clinical expertise and service user engagement to follow the Cass Review (2024) recommendations: “to adopt a holistic, multi-disciplinary integrated approach to assessing and responding to an individual’s needs in view of the range of co-presentations that may typically present in this patient cohort, and the range of complexities relating to gender identity development. The primary intervention for children and young people who are assessed as suitable for the service is biopsychosocial and psychological support and intervention; the main objective is to alleviate distress associated with gender incongruence and promote the individual’s global functioning and wellbeing.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the schools in North Down that will be included in the next roll-out of the part-time 20mph speed limit zoning.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer for everyone. I am acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads.The introduction of part-time 20mph zones at schools across the North is a significant intervention that has helped to ensure that children, parents and staff feel safer as they travel to and from school on a daily basis.The review of this introduction recommended the provision of a third tranche of part-time 20 mph speed limits at schools. I am delighted that an additional 40 schools will benefit from this key road safety measure bringing the total delivered since 2020/21 to 273 schools.In this third tranche, there is a preference on rural schools that are located on roads where the national speed limit applies as per the following policy:DEM 194/25 - Part-time 20mph Speed Limits at Schools - Tranche 3. The schools included in Tranche 3 are listed at the link below. 20mph speed limit at schools - Tranche 3I am very much aware that there are also schools in urban and residential areas, including within North Down, that would benefit from a reduction in the speed limit. I have therefore asked officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits, particularly near schools in predominantly residential areas and places such as town centres, where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling. I will continue to consider any steps my Department and our road safety partners can take to help reduce the number of people killed and injured in road traffic collisions. However, there are actions we can all take that will help. I would urge all drivers to take care on the roads, slow down, ignore mobile phones and never drive under the influence of alcohol or drugs. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the new Gender Identity Service will be gender affirming, in the therapeutic counselling service it offers to children.
answer_text: It is important to note that the Gender Identity Service in Northern Ireland is not new, it was established in 2014.  The service is not and will not be gender affirming in the therapeutic counselling service it offers to children in terms of accepting at face value a service user’s self assessment of their gender.The service which is currently being developed will follow the Cass Review (2024) recommendations: “to adopt a holistic, multi-disciplinary integrated approach to assessing and responding to an individual’s needs in view of the range of co-presentations that may typically present in this patient cohort, and the range of complexities relating to gender identity development. The primary intervention for children and young people who are assessed as suitable for the Service is biopsychosocial and psychological support and intervention; the main objective is to alleviate distress associated with gender incongruence and promote the individual’s global functioning and wellbeing”.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the new build for Bangor Central Integrated Primary School.
answer_text: A revised planning application to take account of the inclusion of Enhanced Learning Support provision at the school was approved by Ards and North Down Borough Council on 23 May 2025, as a non-material change to the existing planning permission.The invitation to tender stage to procure a contractor was recently published with a tender return date of 13 October 2025.  A business case addendum was submitted to the Department on 8 September for consideration and this is currently being reviewed.It is anticipated that construction of the new school build will commence in the first quarter of 2026, however this will be dependent on the relevant approvals being obtained.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail an update on the review into the regulation of (i) property management companies, (ii) service charges, and (iii) other aspects relating to shared property management.
answer_text: My Department conducted a detailed scoping report relating to the issue of shared property management including service charges. Following the conclusion of that report, I am engaging with officials to determine what further progress can be made on the regulation of shared property management.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on progress to reduce the waiting time for a MOT test.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) conducted over 1.16 million vehicle tests from 1 April 2024 to 31 March 2025, which is the highest number ever recorded in a single year.  This was achieved through a range of operational measures, including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, the use of overtime to cover staff absences, the provision of vehicle test appointments on Sundays and bank holidays when testing is not normally available, and an increase in the number of vehicles tested by each examiner. Regrettably, an average of around 900 vehicle test appointments are missed each week due to customers failing to attend, placing additional pressure on this service.The DVA has recently concluded a recruitment competition to appoint up to 42 new vehicle examiners.  The first cohort of 12 successful candidates has commenced training and is expected to be deployed to test centres upon completion of their training in early October. The appointment of these additional vehicle examiners will have a positive impact on waiting times.To further increase vehicle testing capacity, the DVA is investing in new test centres at Hydebank and Mallusk.  These new facilities will provide the capacity to test over 200,000 additional vehicles per year and are expected to open for vehicle testing in 2026. In March 2025, I took the decision to extend the use of TECs for five- and seven-year-old cars within specified date ranges for a further year to help manage MOT waiting times until the new testing facilities at Hydebank and Mallusk open for vehicle testing. The use of TECs has helped the DVA reduce the average waiting time for an MOT test from approximately 100 days at the end of May 2024 to around 32 days now.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the location of social housing units which are (i) planned; and (ii) currently under construction in North Down.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“In the North Down Area:There are currently 8 social homes under construction. The detail of these units is provided in Table 1 below.There are 81 social homes programmed to start as part of the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) 2025/26 – 2027/28. The detail of these schemes is provided in Table 2 below.Programmed schemes can be lost or slip to future programme years for a variety of reasons e.g. relating to delays in acquiring sites and/or failure to secure Planning Approval. Additional schemes can also be added to the SHDP ‘in-year’ through Competitive Land, Design & Build procurement route and/or the purchase of Existing Satisfactory / Off-the-Shelf properties.Table 1: Social housing units under construction in North Down.Housing AssociationLocal Government District Scheme NameLocation TypeNeed GroupStrategic GroupUnitsOnsite YearCompletionYearArkArds & North DownDonaghadee RoadBangorCompetitive Land Design & BuildActive ElderlyUrban82022/232025/26Table 2: Social housing units programmed to start 2025/26 – 2027/28 in North Down.Housing AssociationLocal Government District Scheme NameLocation TypeNeed GroupStrategic GroupUnitsOnsite YearCompletionYearChoiceArds & North DownWorcester AvenueBangorNew BuildGeneral NeedsUrban492026/272029/30ClanmilArds & North DownBangor Marina RegenerationBangorNew BuildGeneral NeedsUrban322026/272028/29”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the introduction of minimum unit pricing for alcohol.
answer_text: As I have made clear, I believe that Minimum Unit Pricing for Alcohol could be a key mechanism to reduce alcohol-related harm in Northern Ireland and to address the health inequalities that exist in this area.However, such a policy requires the support of the NI Executive and I continue to await formal confirmation of the position of all my Executive colleagues. Should Executive support not be forthcoming, I will be required to make a statement to that effect to the Assembly under the Licensing & Registrations of Clubs (Amendment) Act (NI), legislation to the Assembly by 6 April 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she has plans to introduce further exceptions for MOT testing.
answer_text: Temporary Exemption Certificates (TECs) were introduced by the previous Minister from 1 June 2024, due to the exceptional circumstances surrounding the delay in opening the Hydebank test centre and the high demand for vehicle testing services.  In March 2025, I took the decision to extend the use of TECs for five- and seven-year-old cars within specified date ranges for a further year to help manage MOT waiting times until new testing facilities at Hydebank and Mallusk open for vehicle testing.  The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) expects to award a new vehicle testing equipment contract later this year and for new test centres at Hydebank and Mallusk to open for vehicle testing in 2026.  In the meantime, I will keep the use of TECs under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the UK Shared Prosperity Fund.
answer_text: The UK Government’s Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF) allocated £45.5 million to Northern Ireland in 2025–26. This funding supported 18 projects across Northern Ireland aimed at: 	Tackling economic inactivity;	Helping people move into work; and	Supporting local businesses to start, grow and thrive.		SPF will end at the end of March 2026 and the UK Government has announced details of a new Local Growth Fund to support regional economic development from 2026/27 to 2028/29. Northern Ireland will receive Local Growth Fund support at the same cash level as its 2025/26 SPF allocation. 		The fund will be delivered in partnership between the Northern Ireland Office (NIO) and the Northern Ireland Executive, though the exact structure of this partnership is still being developed.		The Department of Finance continues to engage with the UK Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government and the NIO on this fund and a trilateral Ministerial meeting was held on 9th September to discuss further.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department will provide direction and guidance to local councils on the future of waste collection.
answer_text: My officials engage with local councils regularly to provide support and guidance on matters relating to resources and waste.The Departmental Response to the “Rethinking Our Resources” consultation, which will require Executive approval, is aimed at driving improvements in waste and recycling in Northern Ireland and promoting a more circular economy, whilst meeting our environmental targets.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the implementation of the Road Traffic (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.
answer_text: The Road Traffic (Amendment) Act 2016 provides the statutory framework for a revised drink driving regime, as well as for the introduction of Graduated Driver Licensing (GDL). Significant policy development activity and subordinate legislation would be required to implement the new drink driving arrangements fully. In keeping with one of the strategic interventions for my Department in the Road Safety Strategy Action Plan 2024/25, my officials have carried out an initial review of the drink drive legislative framework to explore options to introduce a lower legal alcohol limit applicable to all drivers. Work continues on this complex area, and I will consider all options before deciding on the best way forward.Implementation of the GDL scheme is also a major project entailing significant amendment of current driver training, testing and post testing arrangements. It requires further subordinate legislation as well as significant operational planning activity and development of the online digital solution required to support delivery of the Programme of Training and Logbook. Once operational planning and the work my officials are doing on the legislative changes has been sufficiently progressed, I will be able to provide an anticipated date for the introduction of GDL.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how his Department measures the effectiveness and outcomes of a GP practice.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Contact Assurance Framework is a comprehensive governance assurance process.  NICAF measures the effectiveness and outcomes of GP Practices primarily by requiring practices to evidence/provide assurances on the delivery of General Medical Services through the General Medical Service Contract. Outcomes are measured through a structured process of contract management, evidence submission and data analysis The Strategic Planning and Performance Group use this process to determine assurance levels (using a Green, Amber, Red assurance scale) for the entirety of the GMS contract. The scale allows for a consistent measure of effectiveness, where Green Substantial Assurance and no further action, Amber - Reasonable Assurance to Limited Assurance, Red - No Assurance – where targeted support and intervention is required allowing areas of concern to be addressed.    Practices requiring support and intervention will undergo a Contract and Governance Visit where improvement plans will be developed to help support continuous improvement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how his Department measures and allocates funding to GP practices.
answer_text: My Department allocates funding to GP practices using the Statement of Financial Entitlements (SFE) which provides the legal framework for how GPs are to be funded.  The major component of a GP practice income is their Global Sum.  Measurement of this Global sum is based on CarrHill Formula which bases the calculation on each individual practice patient list size.  This list size is then adjusted by each practice’s weighting to include practice location, deprivation, patient age, gender, and health needs and the patient list registration details / turnover. The other elements of Global sum include allocations for practice nurses and temporary patients. Each of the above elements are revised on a quarterly basis and allocations adjusted in line with this.  Allocations are made on a monthly basis.From 1st April 2025 funding previously paid via Quality and Outcomes and some specific Enhanced Services has been rebased and allocated to practices as part of Core funding.  The calculation of this funding was based on the income earned by each individual practice during the 2023/24 financial year and uplifted by DDRB approved rate.  Other non core Income can be accessed by Practice from the provision of Enhanced Services.  These are additional optional services that a practice may wish to contract for.    Practices contract for those services they wish to provide for their patients and claim reimbursement from SPPG on a monthly basis.  Calculation of amounts due is therefore demand led and based on activity levels across each individual practice.From 1st April 2025 £5m annual additional funding has been made recurrently available to practices to assist with GP Indemnity costs.  This funding has been allocated on a capitation basis and a one-off payment made to each individual practice.  Other types of funding allocated to GP Practices will include payments for Premises, Locum cover for sickness, maternity and paternity.  Funding for ICT is also provided for GP practices.  This funding is allocated directly to BSO who provide IT support services and equipment directly to practices.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the rationale why Northern Ireland motorists are required to produce a physical certificate of motor insurance when taxing their car; and (ii) whether she has any plans to review this, as this requirement is not in place in other parts of the UK.
answer_text: Current regulations here only allow for a legible printed copy of the electronically communicated insurance certificate to be produced at the Post Office counters when applying for vehicle excise duty (VED), not electronic copies. This was at the specific request of Post Office management in discussions with the Department for Transport (DfT) during policy development. In Britain, the introduction of the Continuous Insurance Enforcement Scheme (CIE) in 2011 enabled DfT to make legislative changes including the complete removal of the vehicle insurance check at the time of taxing a vehicle, even at the Post Office.I have directed my officials to engage with their counterparts in DfT to explore the introduction of CIE in the North, particularly in relation to its funding model. I will consider their findings when they become available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the clauses of the Crime and Policing Bill that will cover Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I understand that the Crime and Policing Bill will shortly commence its Committee Stage in the House of Lords. The current draft of the Bill, which is subject to further change as it progresses through the rest of itsParliamentary stages, can be found athttps://bills.parliament.uk/bills/3938The extent of the Bill can be found at current clause 200. Several matters in the Bill concern transferred matters which engage the legislative consent process.On 23 June this year, the Assembly agreed to provide legislative consent relating to several transferred provisions in the introductory version of the Bill.  The associated Legislative Consent Memorandum was laid on 16 May and this details the clauses for which legislative consent was granted. It can be found on the Northern Ireland Assembly’s website at https://www.niassembly.gov.uk/assembly-business/legislation/legislative-consent/legislative-consent-memorandums/crime-and-policing-bill/I laid a second Memorandum in the Assembly on 2 September 2025 indicating my intention to bring forward a second legislative consent motion in due course, detailing the devolved areas for which I am likely to seek further legislative consent for Westminster to legislate on (subject to Executive approval). This memorandum can also be viewed at the link above.Clause numbers are likely to change as the Bill progresses through Parliament. It is also possible that new clauses and/or consequential amendments may be added which might apply to Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether the forthcoming Sentencing Bill will propose changes to bail condition guidelines for those charged with causing death by careless driving.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 14 - AQO 2369/22-27		  ANSWER With your permission Mr Speaker I will answer Questions 11 and 14 together.    The Sentencing Bill will deliver on a number of the recommendations arising from my Department’s review of sentencing policy which commenced in 2016, the findings of which were subject to public consultation in 2019.    This will include: a statement of the principles and purposes of sentencing;  provision to allow further use of community community-based sanctions in sentencing;  statutory starting points for life sentence tariff calculation;  expansion and simplification of the current unduly lenient sentence referral provisions;  creation of a new offence of assaulting those providing a service to the public or performing a public duty;  creation of a statutory aggravator for vulnerable victims; and increasing maximum penalties for offences causing death by driving. The Bill will also include measures to encourage the disclosure of the location of victims’ remains, to be known as “Charlotte’s Law”, and provision for a statutory aggravator for hate crimes. I received Executive approval to the proposed content of my Sentencing Bill on the 19 December 2024.     It is intended that the Bill will be introduced to the Assembly before the end of 2025. In relation to cases of death by dangerous driving, the Bill will ensure that disqualification from driving is served after release from custody.    The grant or refusal of bail when a person is awaiting trial should always be based on the facts of the case.   Any decision to refuse bail should only be taken where this can be justified under domestic legislation and in compliance with the European Convention on Human Rights.    Any decisions on bail or remand into custody in individual cases are, therefore, matters for the independent courts and judiciary and are not matters that involve me or my Department.    There are no current plans to include new guidance on the granting of bail in the forthcoming Sentencing Bill; however, it is a matter which I intend to keep under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will consider a funding arrangement with Translink to ensure there are public transport connections from North Down to the Ulster Hospital.
answer_text: The provision of public transport services and funding for Translink is a matter for the Department for Infrastructure. Where my Department does have a statutory responsibility, however, is under Article 10 of the Health and Personal Social Services (Northern Ireland) Order 1972, where transport may be provided for patients with a medical need as identified by a clinician. As a general principle, patients are expected to make their own way to healthcare facilities, either using their own transport, or that of friends and relatives, or by using schemes that exist to help people who have transport difficulties. Where this is not possible, my Department can make arrangements for transport to be provided, as outlined in my Department’s Transport Strategy for Health and Social Care Services in Northern Ireland. These services are primarily delivered by the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service through their Patient Care Service; however, there are a range of options open to Trusts, including the use of volunteer car drivers, private ambulance services and the use of taxis. For those needing support, but without an identified medical need, I am aware of a range of services provided by other Departments, such as the Dial-a-Lift scheme, the Rural Community Transport Scheme, or services provided by Community Transport Associations. Additionally, those on low incomes who depend on Universal Credit may be eligible to reclaim hospital transport costs through the Hospital Travel Costs scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the outstanding provisions of the Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 will be implemented.
answer_text: The Mental Capacity Act (NI) 2016 (MCA) was brought forward by the Department of Health and the Department of Justice and has been implemented in a phased approach, with provisions relating to deprivation of liberty, research and money and valuables commenced to date. There are currently no indicative timings for full commencement of the MCA.  However, taking into account current resourcing constraints, my Department has been working collaboratively with Department of Justice officials with a view to developing an updated implementation plan to better understand the impact and implications of full MCA implementation, as well as understand any benefits or risks involved with how that may be approached. This will inform Departmental decision making.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any steps her Department has taken to ensure the budget for road maintenance is distributed in a fair and equitable way throughout Northern Ireland.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on plans to redevelop Donaghadee Health Centre.
answer_text: I had the pleasure of recently visiting Donaghadee Health Centre and engaging with staff. The practice recently received an extension to accommodate the multi-disciplinary teams roll out.  Further redevelopment of Donaghadee Health Centre does feature as part of the Ards and Bangor Primary Community Care and Treatment Centres project which is listed on the South Eastern Trust’s 10-year capital plan as a priority.  This is dependent on future funding allocations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has carried out a review of road safety on the West Circular Road, Bangor, following the recent fatal road traffic collision.
answer_text: I wish to extend my sincere condolences to the family of the young girl whose life was tragically lost at the signal-controlled junction of West Circular Road and Clandeboye Road on 8 July 2025.Road safety is an extremely high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users particularly vulnerable road user groups, including pedestrians.I understand that the PSNI are leading an investigation into this incident. Once the findings are shared with the Department, my officials will carefully consider if any action can be taken through the introduction of engineering measures to enhance road safety.Officials are also currently in the process of reviewing the latest Road Traffic Collision data from the PSNI for accident cluster sites, including the A2 West Circular Road, to identify if there are any common causation factors which would benefit from the introduction of effective road safety measures. In the interim, officials will review this junction in relation to pedestrian safety to ensure that that the existing engineering measures are appropriate and comply with the Department’s current policy guidelines.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she has engaged with Translink, regarding concerns about cancellation of services linked to recruitment and retention of bus drivers.
answer_text: As part of the Public Service Agreement, my officials have regular meetings with Translink to monitor service performance. Recruitment and retention are key issues to help maintain service reliability and Translink has a number of plans in place to manage this and ensure performance targets are achieved.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the installation of a controlled pedestrian crossing on East Circular Road, Bangor, at the junction of Ballymaconnell Road.
answer_text: A new controlled pedestrian crossing on the A2 East Circular Road, Bangor, has been programmed for this financial year and will be completed as soon as resources are available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 27844/22-27, whether Northern Ireland is continuing to mutually recognise licenses, following the removal of BE testing in GB in 2021, allowing drivers from Great Britain to tow heavier trailers and caravans into Northern Ireland without the drivers undertaking the BE test.
answer_text: Licences and licence categories, in the north and Britain are mutually recognised.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail why a large number of trees have been felled by the Forest Service at (i) Cairn Wood; and (ii) around Ballysallagh Reservoir.
answer_text: My Department’s Forest Service and Ards and North Down Borough Council have removed a number of trees in Cairn Wood which had experienced wind damage, as a result of Storm Éowyn earlier this year.  These trees were removed in line with public safety responsibilities, in particular those associated with the management of recreational use of the forest.  In addition, roadside trees have also been cleared along the B170 Ballysallagh Road, due to public safety concerns, following a planned tree inspection programme by Forest Service officials.  Storm Éowyn caused catastrophic damage in the area to the eastern side of the Ballysallagh Reservoir. Forest Service is currently clearing the affected area, which is expected to be completed by late summer 2025.  Replanting will take place following harvesting, in line with best practice and UK Forestry Standards
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail whether she plans to introduce legislation to toughen sentencing for those who attack police officers and emergency service workers.
answer_text: My proposed Sentencing Bill will legislate for a new offence of assaulting a person who is providing services to the public, performing a public duty or providing a public service.  This would include attacks on police officers and emergency service workers.This new offence will carry a higher maximum penalty on conviction than the sentences currently available for similar less serious assault offences.  Additionally, if a more serious assault offence is charged, I will make the fact that the victim is such a worker a statutory aggravating factor, meaning the courts will be required to reflect this specifically in the sentences imposed.I intend to introduce this Sentencing Bill before the end of 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she has any plans to review legislation regarding (i) parental leave; and (ii) pay for new parents.
answer_text: On 28th April 2025 I announced to the Assembly my intention to introduce an employment rights Bill within the remaining mandate. Policy commitments therein include enhanced rights to paternity leave and pay and flexible working and new rights to both carer’s leave and neonatal care leave and pay. Additionally, my department continually keeps the operation of statutory leave and pay entitlements for employed parents, and legislative developments in other jurisdictions, under review.  I am aware the Department of Work and Pensions and the Department of Business and Trade (DBT) have jointly launched a review of the parental leave and pay system in Britain. This review is expected to run for an 18-month period. My officials meet regularly with their counterparts in the DBT to discuss policy proposals. This will include the aforementioned review however, any changes arising from the review in Britain would be a consideration for the next mandate due to the limited time remaining in the present.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether local departmental employees can carry out further cuts of grass verges at junctions to ensure road safety.
answer_text: My Department maintains approximately 45,000km of grass verges on the road network and our approach aims to maintain the right balance between road safety and the control of grass and noxious weeds, while placing an emphasis on environmental protection enhancement.In December 2022 and to strengthen our approach, my Department issued a new policy on verge management across the road network to protect the environment. The policy remains in operation and increases the Department’s focus on protecting wildlife and promoting biodiversity when managing roadside verges and supports the actions needed to comply with the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022.In compliance with the policy, a single swathe is cut along verges on the strategic road network, urban roads, on heavier trafficked rural roads and at sight lines and junctions twice per year for road safety purposes. On lighter trafficked roads the cutting frequency is reduced to once per year and is scheduled towards the end of the growing season. Additional areas of grass, outside of that routinely programmed, may be cut to maintain visibility splays at junctions and around bends where forward visibility is required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 20847/22-27, for an update on the introduction of residents' parking schemes in North Down.
answer_text: I am currently considering my officials’ recommendations in relation to the development of an application and assessment process for residents’ parking schemes, and it is my intention to make an announcement as to how future schemes will be assessed by my Department in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the steps being taken to publicise the availability of prostate-specific antigen testing for men via their GP; and (ii) whether he will consider a national public information campaign to promote greater awareness of this.

To ask the Minister for Communities to outline (i) the legislation that addresses the ending of an introductory tenancy in a Housing Executive property; and (ii) the criteria where a tenant may be found to be in breach of their tenancy.
answer_text: Articles 9 to 12 of the Housing (Northern Ireland) Order 2003 set out the procedure bringing an introductory tenancy to an end. The legislation provides that, where the landlord has applied to the court for an order for possession of an introductory tenancy, the court is required to make the order provided that the landlord has served a notice of proceedings on the tenant which complies with the statutory requirements. In practice, a landlord would apply to the court for an order for possession of an introductory tenancy where the tenant’s behaviour is such that a court could be expected to make an order for possession of a secure tenancy on:Ground 1 (rent arrears), Ground 2 (conduct causing or likely to cause nuisance or annoyance, or conviction of an offence involving use of the house for illegal or immoral purposes, or conviction of an indictable offence committed in, or in the locality of, the house),Ground 3 (acts of waste or neglect causing the condition of the house to deteriorate) or Ground 5 (tenancy granted on the basis of a false statement).The requirement for the court to make an order for possession of an introductory tenancy is subject to a test of reasonableness and any Human Rights defence raised by the tenant.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are any plans to introduce the ability for the public to set up a lasting power of attorney for health and care decisions, similar to that available in England.
answer_text: The Mental Capacity Act (NI) 2016 (MCA), a piece of legislation brought forward by the Department of Health and the Department of Justice (DoJ), provides a framework for people who lack capacity to make a decision for themselves and for those who now have capacity but wish to make preparations for a time in the future when they may lack capacity. It includes provisions related to lasting powers of attorney.The MCA has been implemented in a phased approach, with provisions relating to deprivation of liberty, research and money and valuables commenced to date. Further work is needed to understand the resourcing implications for full commencement and so there are currently no indicative timings for full commencement of the MCA. Any decision regarding provisions related to lasting powers of attorney would need to be considered in collaboration with DoJ.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people who have opted out of organ donation since the opt-in legislation was introduced.

To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to review the criteria for community and third sector organisations to avail of the Core Grant Funding Scheme, to account for varying levels of capacity and specialisation in the sector.
answer_text: The Core Grant Scheme was reviewed by my officials and redesigned in partnership with the Community and Voluntary Sector in the months leading up to the launch of the 2025/26 Scheme in November 2024. Feedback and learning captured from the implementation of the new Scheme will be considered and where appropriate amendments will be made in advance of the launch of the 26/27 Scheme later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to increase the funding available to community and third sector organisations through the Core Grant Funding Scheme.
answer_text: Despite the ongoing budget challenges facing my Department, I remain committed to supporting the community and voluntary sector, and where the wider health allocation allows me to increase the scheme budget in future years I will do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of potholes currently awaiting repair in the North Down constituency.
answer_text: My Department collects and stores data in a manner which mirrors the boundaries of the district council areas. Road surface defects, including potholes, are managed through the Roads Maintenance Client Management System which records the total number of defects and the number outstanding for each local maintenance office area.This data is regularly published and available for public view through the OpenDataNI portal at https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/dataset/surface-defects and can be filtered by defect type and geographical area.In addition, official statistics on the condition of the road network are also now published through the NI Road Network and Condition Statistics. The 2023-24 publication can be found at Northern Ireland Road Network and Condition Statistics 2023-24 | Department for Infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of pothole repairs carried out in the 2024-25 financial year in the North Down constituency; and (ii) the proportion of those pothole repairs carried out compared to the number currently awaiting repair.
answer_text: My Department collects and stores data in a manner which mirrors the boundaries of the district council areas. Road surface defects, including potholes, are managed through the Roads Maintenance Client Management System which records the total number of defects and the number outstanding for each local maintenance office area.This data is regularly published and available for public view through the OpenDataNI portal at https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/dataset/surface-defects and can be filtered by defect type and geographical area.In addition, official statistics on the condition of the road network are also now published through the Northern Ireland Road Network and Condition Statistics. The 2023-24 publication can be found at Northern Ireland Road Network and Condition Statistics 2023-24 | Department for Infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the powers available to the police to deal with abuse of the elderly; and (ii) whether he has any plans to improve these powers through the introduction of adult safeguarding legislation.
answer_text: In relation to part (i) of the question, the Department of Justice has confirmed that the following powers are available to police:Police PowersPolice have general powers available to them for investigating criminal offences under legislation such as the Police & Criminal Evidence (NI) Order 1989.Domestic Violence and AbuseThe Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (NI) 2021 provides protection to all victims of domestic abuse, including older people who may experience harm within a domestic setting. This includes physical, emotional and financial abuse. The legislation recognises that domestic abuse can take many forms and affect individuals of any age.Fraud/Scams on the ElderlyThe Department of Justice (DoJ) helps to address harm and vulnerability caused by fraud and supports partnership working to tackle the threat of scams through its membership of the ScamwiseNI Partnership, which is chaired by the PSNI.  The Partnership was established eight years ago and now has over 45 partners who work collaboratively to provide valuable information and crucial advice to keep the public informed, aware and vigilant. The ScamWise Partnership has an effective online presence which is managed by staff from the Office of the Commissioner for Older People (COPNI) and the Department of Justice uses its own social media channels (Twitter & Instagram) to highlight current scams and the steps people can take to protect themselves.  ScamwiseNI launched a six-month long campaign from October 2023 to April 2024 which featured radio and newspaper advertisements, along with posters in public transport spaces, including bus shelters and inside buses.  Its aim was to help empower older people and those who are not regularly online to stay safe against scammers.In addition, in partnership with the Northern Ireland Policing Board, the Department provides annual operational funding of £4.25m to Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs), across each of the 11 council areas, who lead on the Department’s response to community safety issues at a local level. These projects include “Older Adults Cyber Safety Events”, “Older Persons Home Security Scheme” and “The Good Morning Scheme”, which is a confidential telephone service for people over 60, aimed at connecting older people with carers within their community and to help people live safely and independently in their homes.In relation to part (ii) of the question, the Adult Protection Bill creates new powers to allow Health and Social Care Trusts to enhance adult protection and safeguarding and work in partnership with police and other agencies to enforce the new powers as well as new offences introduced in the Bill. Part 1 (clauses 1 to 29) of the Bill provides for the identification and protection of an adult at risk, including provision for social workers to exercise certain powers conferred by the Bill or by court orders under the Bill.  For example, the Bill confers upon social services the power to see

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the progress of the Queen's Parade redevelopment scheme.

To ask the Minister of Finance to outline what steps his Department is taking to ensure that the Northern Ireland Civil Service's workplace discrimination procedures are in compliance with the UK Supreme Court ruling on For Women Scotland Ltd v The Scottish Ministers.
answer_text: ANSWER I refer the member to the response provided for AQW 26443/22-27.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline their assessment of the potential security implications that the escalating situation between Israel and Iran has for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The situation is the Middle East is becoming increasingly volatile and is escalating at an alarming rate.We urge dialogue and de-escalation and all parties to step back from further attacks that will only add to the regions suffering and to international instability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure pursuant to AQW 27844/22-27, to outline her assessment of the impact that the different requirements in BE testing between Great Britain and Northern Ireland has on visitors and tourists to Northern Ireland using caravans.
answer_text: Licences and licence categories, in the north and Britain are mutually recognised.  Therefore, there is no impact on British licence holders with a BE entitlement, who wish to tow a caravan here, as they will be doing so on their existing entitlement.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the recruitment process to appoint a Commissioner for the Ulster Scots and Ulster British tradition.
answer_text: We are committed to making the relevant appointments and establishing the Commissioner for the Ulster Scots and the Ulster British tradition, along with the Irish Language Commissioner, and the Office of Identity and Cultural Expression, as soon as possible.All three recruitment competitions are now at an advanced stage. Interviews are currently underway to enable appointments to be made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what steps he is taking to expand bowel cancer screening for people outside the 60-74 age demographic.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail what steps his Department is taking to recruit more radiologists across all Health and Social Care Trusts.
