answer_text: Demand for medical imaging has grown at a faster rate than capacity within Health and Social Care (HSC), despite significant investment over the last ten years to grow the imaging workforce in Northern Ireland. The commissioned radiology medical specialty training places have increased from a baseline of 37 in 2015/16 to 56 places in 2025/26 and the current radiology training programme is now at capacity.My Department has brought forward plans for the establishment of a multi-professional Northern Ireland Imaging Academy to address HSC imaging workforce deficiencies. The Imaging Academy will provide a continuous supply of locally trained consultant radiologists and advanced radiographer practitioners to address the increasing demand for imaging investigations and reduce reliance on costly outsourcing. A business case for the Academy to be established on the Mater Hospital site is being developed by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust. However, the development and any acceleration of capacity building remains subject to business case approval and the identification of additional recurrent funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline any discussions she has had with the Police Federation for Northern Ireland regarding recruiting additional police officers to meet required levels.
answer_text: The Police Service of Northern Ireland is under the direction and control of the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. Recruitment is an operational matter for the Chief Constable with whom I have regular meetings to discuss all the challenges facing the PSNI including police officer numbers.On 21 May 2025 the Permanent Secretary attended the Police Federation of Northern Ireland Annual General Meeting on my behalf, at which he assured the members present that I will continue to work to ensure that we have a properly resourced police service. I have also, on each occasion when I have met with the Chair of the Federation, taken the opportunity to update him as to developments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has any plans to open bowel cancer screening for patients at a lower threshold of sensitivity than 120 micrograms of haemoglobin per gram of faeces.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail what steps his Department is taking to recruit more clinical oncologists across all Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: Since 2019/20, a key priority of my Department is to address ongoing resilience issues with regard to oncology and haematology services stabilisation and the delivery of Systemic Anti-Cancer Therapy (SACT).  By 2024/25 my Department invested a total of £10.8m across all five health and social care Trusts for the Oncology & Haematology Stabilisation Plans.   This has funded 167.76 whole time equivalent (WTE) additional staff across a range of health professionals in oncology services including 9.75 WTE oncology consultant posts. These will improve consultant capacity and supportive skills mix. An additional £2.5m recurrent funding has been identified in the 2025/26 Elective Care Framework Implementation and Funding Plan to help complete stabilisation plans.My department is also conducting a review into radiotherapy services which will include a review of current workforce and future needs.There are no current plans to increase the local clinical oncology, medical specialty training programme in Northern Ireland, as several training positions remain unfilled.  I acknowledged it is important we fund positions in our local service to retain our oncology consultants.   My department continues to work with all health and social care trusts to inform regional workforce planning requirements including Registrar training places and associated allocation.  Trusts continue to do all they can to fill posts with ongoing recruitment strategies.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline how the Northern Ireland Civil Contingencies Framework takes account of cyber-attack preparedness, in-line with technological advancements such as Artificial Intelligence.
answer_text: In line with the Lead Government Department model outlined in the NI Civil Contingencies Framework, the Department of Finance has primary responsibility for preparing for and leading the response to NI public sector cyber incidents here.  The Northern Ireland Civil Contingencies Framework is the overarching document that outlines the prepare, response and recovery phases of an emergency and their associated structures.  The Framework is complemented by dedicated plans to address individual risks, and these are owned and exercised by the specific Lead Government Departments. These are regularly reviewed by Departments to ensure they reflect changes within the risk environments – such as the development of Artificial Intelligence. We have had a number of discussions with officials on this issue and continue to constantly review our civil contingency measures to ensure they are fit for purpose including for new and emerging threats.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the maintenance regime for tactile cones at pedestrian crossings.
answer_text: A six-monthly maintenance inspection of all traffic signal equipment is undertaken by an external contractor, on behalf of the Department.  These inspections include tactile cones.In addition to the six-monthly inspections, repairs are undertaken to tactile cones reported by members of the public via the NI Direct website.  The response time for tactile cone repair reported in this way, is usually between 4 and 8 hours, but will be dependent on the day and time the report is received.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether he is aware of any issues around the supply of Sandostatin octreotide cancer drug in certain dosages; and (ii) to outline how cancer patients can avail of this drug in the appropriate dosage that they require.
answer_text: Medicines are manufactured as part of a highly regulated, complex and global industry; therefore, medicine shortages are not uncommon and can occur for a variety of reasons. My Department works closely with the Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC), who leads on the maintenance of medicine supply chains to the UK, to put in place well-established tools and processes should a disruption arise, ensuring patients can continue to access appropriate treatments.My Department understands there were short-term supply disruptions affecting Sandostatin® (octreotide), however, the marketing authorisation holder has confirmed all strengths and preparations are now in-stock.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether providing tactile cones at pedestrian crossings, for those individuals who are blind or visually impaired, is a legal requirement.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users.Whilst there is no legal requirement, I can advise that my Department provides tactile signals at all new controlled crossing facilities in accordance with guidance outlined in the Traffic Signs Manual, Chapter 6, Traffic Control; and the Department for Transport’s ‘Inclusive Mobility Guide to Best Practice on Access to Pedestrian and Transport Infrastructure’My Department manages the provision of these tactile signals through its Policy and Procedure Guide RsPPG E005 ‘Controlled crossings: Assessment procedures’ Controlled crossings: assessment procedures RsPPG E005 | Department for Infrastructure

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the legal requirements for the provision of changing places facilities.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the local Building Regulations which apply when relevant building work is undertaken or when specified changes of use occur. Regulation 91 of Part R ‘Access to and use of buildings’ of the Building Regulations requires that “reasonable provision shall be made for people to have access to … and to use a building and its facilities”. This regulation is supported by the Department’s Technical Booklet R (TBR).In June 2022, amendments to TBR introduced a new expectation of at least one Changing Places Toilet (CPT) in certain buildings (defined by building type, size or people capacity) when they are to be newly built or created by a change of use. In scope buildings include: assembly, entertainment and recreation buildings with a capacity of 350 people or more; shopping centres/malls, or retail parks with a gross floor area of 30000m2 or more; retail premises with a gross floor area of 2500m2 or more; leisure and sport buildings with a gross floor area of 5000m2 or more; hospitals and primary care centres; cemetery and crematorium buildings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to explain why the traffic light sequencing at the Gransha Road Roundabout, Bangor, has been allowed to be altered twice in six months, leading to severe traffic congestion on the East Circular Road.
answer_text: Officials are aware of the two incidents which led to traffic congestion at Gransha Road Roundabout, Bangor caused by a recurring technical fault in the traffic signal system.Whilst the fault has been repaired, officials are also undertaking a thorough investigation of the system to ensure that there are no subsequent occurrences.  As part of this process, any necessary adjustments to the traffic light sequencing will be implemented to improve traffic flow and minimise disruption.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the capacity of each custody suite in Northern Ireland; and (ii) the average occupancy rate over the last six months.
answer_text: My Department neither collates nor holds the information requested in relation to custody suites in Northern Ireland.  The Member may wish to contact the PSNI directly for this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice in order ease pressure on the PSNI, whether the Department will undertake a review into PSNI responsibilities, with a view to making them more in line with other police forces in the rest of the UK.
answer_text: There are no plans at present for my Department to undertake a review into PSNI responsibilities, though we are always open to suggestions from the PSNI as to where any perceived gaps or differences in responsibilities and powers may exist that may require change. My departments current priority is continuing efforts to ensure that the PSNI is properly equipped and resourced to carry out its core functions effectively.  My Department continues to work closely with the PSNI on a range of measures aimed at easing the significant pressures currently faced by the service.  These include the Workforce Recovery Business Case and the PSNI-led Right Care Right Person initiative.In addition, my officials and I await the outcome of the Police Remuneration Review Body’s (PRRB) deliberations on Police Officer pay in Northern Ireland in order to maintain broad alignment with police forces in England and Wales.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail what powers the PSNI have to disperse large crowds engaged in anti-social behaviour.
answer_text: The PSNI have an extensive array of policing powers and obligations that can be used to deal with the dispersal of large crowns engaged in anti-social behaviour. The powers set out below specifically outline offences that may be committed relating to public processions and public order:Taking Part in an Un-notified Procession – Section 6(7) Public Processions (NI) Act 1998Advance Notice of Protest Meeting Related to Public Processions –Sec 7 Public Processions (NI) Order 1998Breaking Up A Public Procession – Sec 14(1) Public Processions (NI) Order 1998Disorderly Behaviour – Article 18 (1) Public order (NI) Order 1987Riotous Behaviour – Article 18 (3) Public Order (NI) Order 1987Provocative Conduct – Article 19 (1) Public order (NI) Order 1987Riot – Common LawAffray – Common LawUse of Words/Display of Material – Article 9 (1) Public Order (NI) Order 1987Obstructive Sitting – Article 20 (1) Public Order (NI) Order 1987Aggravated TrespassProtection of Person and Property Act (Northern Ireland) 1969Possession of Petrol Bombs – Section 2-4 Protection of the Person and Property Act (NI) 1969. Power to Require Removal of Disguises – Article 23a Public Order (NI) Order 1987. Power to Stop and Search in anticipation of violence –Article 23b Public Order (NI) Order 1987. Possession of Offensive Weapon – Article 22 (1) Public Order (NI) Order 1987. Confiscation of Alcohol (Young Persons) Act 1997. Public Processions Act (NI) 1998, Sec 13 (1) & (2) Consuming Intoxicating Liquor and Sec 13(3)&(4) Possession of Intoxicating Liquor. Common Assault – Section 42 Offences Against the Person Act 1861 AOABH – Section 47 Offences Against the Person Act 1861 Wounding or Causing GBH – Section 20 Offences Against the Person Act 1861 Wounding or Causing Grevious Bodily Harm With Intent –Section 18 Offences Against The Person Act 1861. Threats to Kill – Section 16 Offences Against The Person Act 1861 It is the amalgamation of these various powers, and obligations, that enable police to disperse crowds in public order scenarios.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) which parts of the Anti-Social Behaviour, Crime and Policing Act 2014 applies to Northern Ireland; and (ii) whether it has been fully implemented.
answer_text: (i) The parts of the Anti-Social Behaviour, Crime and Policing Act 2014 that apply to Northern Ireland are as set out in Section 184: Extent of the Act and are outlined below in part (ii).(ii) A breakdown of each section applying to Northern Ireland and their implementation is given below:Part 8 FirearmsS111 Increased penalty for improper importation of firearms etcThis section of the Anti-social Behaviour, Crime and Policing Act 2014was commenced on 14 July 2014.  As this is a reserved matter, operational implementation is a matter for the UK government.Part 9 Protection from Sexual OffencesS113 Sexual harm prevention orders and sexual risk orders, etcAnd Schedule 5Section 113 of the Anti-social Behaviour, Crime and Policing Act 2014: ‘Sexual harm prevention orders and sexual risk orders, etc’ extends to Northern Ireland.  It introduces Schedule 5 to the Sexual Offences Act 2003. However, only paragraph 6 of Schedule 5 applies to Northern Ireland. Section 113 was brought into force on 8 March 2015.S.115 Use of premises for child sex offencesAnd Schedule 6Section 115 of the Anti-social Behaviour, Crime and Policing Act 2014: ‘Use of premises for child sex offences’ extends to Northern Ireland.  The provision introduces Schedule 6 to the Sexual Offences Act 2003. However, only paragraphs 2(1), 2(2), 2(3) and 2(5) and 11(3) apply to Northern Ireland. Section 115 was brought into force on 8 March 2015.Part 11 Policing etc.S131 Abolition of Police Negotiating Board for the United KingdomS132 Establishment of Police Remuneration Review BodyAnd Schedule 7S133(5) Consultation about regulations: England and WalesS134 Consultation about regulations: Northern IrelandSince 13 March 2014, the Department of Justice has implemented Sections 131 to 133 and Schedule 7 of the Anti-social Behaviour, Crime and Policing Act 2014 to establish the Police Remuneration Review Body, which annually considers, for members of or below the rank of chief superintendent, changes to police officer pay; allowances for police officers across the United Kingdom having sought evidence from key stakeholders including the Northern Ireland Police Staff Associations, the Policing Board, the Chief Constable and the Department of Justice.In accordance with section 134 of this Act, my Department continues to invite the views of the Police Advisory Board for England and Wales [PABEW] in relation to draft regulations in respect of police pension arrangements, and likewise the PABEW continues to consult with the Northern Ireland Policing Board on these matters.S146 Retention of personal samples that are or may be disclosableSection 146 (2) of the Anti-Social Behaviour, Crime and Policing Act 2014 applies to Northern Ireland and was commenced on 13 May 2014. However, as this provision relates to the Terrorism Act 2000 and terrorism is considered an excepted matter as per Schedule 2, paragraph 17, of the Northern Ireland Act 1998, this would be a matter for the Home Secretary.S147 Powers to se

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) which parts of the Police Reform and Social Responsibility Act 2011 applies to Northern Ireland; and (ii) whether it has been fully implemented.
answer_text: The parts of the Police Reform and Social Responsibility Act 2011 that apply to Northern Ireland are set out in Part 5, Section 156: Extent of the Act. A breakdown of each section applying to Northern Ireland and their implementation is outlined below:Part 1, Chapter 6: Police and Crime Commissioners: Elections and VacanciesS58 Power to make provisions about elections etcCommenced on 15/09/2011.  There are no Police and Crime Commissioners in Northern Ireland.S58 (power to make provision about elections) extends to Northern Ireland. This will allow modifications made under the power to have the same extent as the forms or other provisions they modify (in the same way as amendments made by the Act itself). Part 4 Miscellaneous S151 Temporary control of drugsS152 Advisory Council on the Misuse of DrugsS154 Orders and regulationsS156, S157 and S158 (extent, commencement and short title)  extend to Northern Ireland and were commenced on 15 September 2011"Section 151 of the Police Reform and Social Responsibility Act 2011 extends to Northern Ireland. Section 151 gives effect to Schedule 17. Section 151 and Schedule 17 were commenced on 15 November 2011. Schedule 17 makes provision to allow the Secretary of State to make temporary class drug orders under the Misuse of Drugs Act 1971, to apply across the UK. The policy and legal frameworks relating to the misuse of drugs, including the Misuse of Drugs Act 1971 are reserved to the UK Government, in accordance with Schedule 3 to the Northern Ireland Act 1998. Section 152 of the Police Reform and Social Responsibility Act 2011 extends to Northern Ireland and was commenced on 15 November 2011.Section 152 makes minor amendments to Schedule 1 to the Misuse of Drugs Act 1971, with respect to the constitution of the Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs (“ACMD”). ACMD makes recommendations to the UK government on the control of dangerous or otherwise harmful drugs, including classification and scheduling under the Misuse of Drugs Act 1971. The policy and legal frameworks relating to the misuse of drugs, including the Misuse of Drugs Act 1971 are reserved to the UK Government, in accordance with Schedule 3 to the Northern Ireland Act 1998. Section 154 of the Police Reform and Social Responsibility Act 2011 extends to Northern Ireland (so far as relating to an order under section 58) and was commenced on 15 September 2011.Section 154 sets out the powers of the Secretary of State under the Act to make an order or regulations, and which of those are subject to an affirmative resolution of each House of Parliament.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide an update on the progress of the new Controlled Schools Unit, including the associated timescales.

To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of the Supreme Court judgment in the case For Women Scotland Ltd v The Scottish Ministers on 16 April 2025, for his assessment of whether only biological women should be participating in female sporting codes.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline what immediate out-patient mental health services are available for in-patients on discharge from services in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: In-patient mental health services currently in place, across the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust are detailed as follows:LocationNumber of BedsLagan Valley Hospital21Ulster Hospital24Downe Hospital25Downshire Site6 PICULagan Valley Hospital6 Older People BedsTotal82All Trusts are experiencing significant pressures as a consequence of increased demands upon services, including increased number of new referrals, increased complexity and acuity of people presenting to services and increased workforce pressures as a result of increased vacancy levels. There are currently 393 commissioned inpatient mental health beds for the region. My officials keep demand and occupancy under review, however at this time there is no plan to increase inpatient bed capacity, and the budgetary position does not allow for additional funding to be allocated, other than those services already commissioned at this current time.The South Eastern Trust provide the following outpatient follow up services for patients being discharged from mental health inpatient wards:1. Home Treatment Team support. This team will review those patients transitioning from wards to continue their acute care at home within twenty-four hours.2. For patients already known to Community Mental Health and Addictions Services, they are reviewed by their keyworker within three days.3. For patients open to an Outpatient Consultant, support is available from the Mental Health Outpatient Clinic.4. Recovery College & Wellness Recovery Network are available from point of discharge 5. Referral and support through Mindwise can be made at point of discharge, for those who consent to a referral being made.8. Outpatient support services may also be provided by GP Multi-Disciplinary Team Practitioners and community and voluntary services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has any plans to remove the requirement for drivers towing a trailer to receive a category B+E Test endorsement, in line with changes made to legislation in Great Britain where this requirement was abolished.
answer_text: Legislation introduced in Britain on 16 December 2021 removed testing requirements for towing a trailer (BE entitlement), meaning that all drivers who passed a category B (car) driving test from 1 January 1997 are now entitled to tow trailers up to 3,500kg Maximum Authorized Mass (MAM).  This change was made in an effort to address the HGV driver shortage situation in England, Scotland and Wales.At that time testing was not seen as a significant reason for HGV driver shortages here. This position has not changed.In EU Member States BE testing is still a requirement, as it is here. Road safety continues to be of paramount importance and therefore I have no plans to introduce changes similar to those in Britain.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the extent of the puppy smuggling trade in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on progress of securing of a veterinary medicines agreement with the EU.
answer_text: Following the coming into force of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, veterinary medicines are under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs. As such, this does not sit within my ministerial responsibilities. I am disappointed that veterinary medicines are not in scope of the forthcoming EU-UK SPS Agreement, but I will continue to engage with the UK Government and EU to urge a resolution to ensure the continued supply of veterinary medicines on a long term basis.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail his plans to regulate cat breeding.
answer_text: My Animal Welfare Pathway details the ten key reforms I will be focusing on during the remainder of the mandate. 		This includes an expert-led review of current dog breeding laws, to include assessing the need for regulation of canine fertility services.		We have also commenced a consultation which will seek views on the introduction of a version of Lucy’s Law in Northern Ireland to regulate the third-party sale of both kittens and puppies. 		The aim of this policy is to enable purchasers to feel confident that they are buying from a reputable breeder who has given the kitten or puppy the best start in life, and who ensures the welfare needs of their animals are met.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what his assessment is of the current average wait time for a diagnosis of coeliac disease.
answer_text: My Department does not specifically record the waiting time for coeliac diagnosis. The endoscopy procedure used to diagnosis coeliac is used to diagnosis a number of conditions. The HSC has implemented a number of gastroenterology pathways that ensure patients are assessed, diagnosed and treated as quickly as possible within available resources.  These pathways also help to identify coeliac disease as opposed to other gastrointestinal issues that may have similar symptoms. In addition to these pathways healthcare professionals undergo regular professional development training to raise awareness of this condition.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures being taken to optimise the use of no-biopsy protocols in the diagnosis of coeliac disease.
To ask the Minister of Health what assessment had been made of the cost to the health and social care service of undiagnosed coeliac disease and the resultant health complications.
answer_text: Coeliac disease is a lifelong autoimmune condition triggered by ingesting gluten. It can cause gastrointestinal upset, diarrhoea, constipation, vomiting, stomach cramps, mouth ulcers, fatigue and anaemia.  It is more likely to occur in families but is also associated with diabetes 1 and congenital conditions such as Turner and Downs syndrome.Symptoms are alleviated by adhering to a gluten free diet.  However, continued ingestion of gluten can lead to complications including malnutrition, lactose intolerance and complications in pregnancy. In rare circumstances it can also lead to bowel cancer.It is not possible to assess the number of people currently living with undiagnosed coeliac disease.  NICE estimates that around 1% of the population in the UK may have coeliac disease and organisations such as Coeliac UK suggest that up to a third of cases are undiagnosed.  However, prevalence can vary considerably across populations and the information available on undiagnosed patients does not identify if this includes those undergoing investigations but not yet diagnosed.Standards in the management of conditions are routinely informed by my Department’s endorsement of NICE Clinical Guidelines.  Once endorsed, guidance is issued to the HSC for adoption and integration into practice as the extant standards for condition management.  The guidance relevant to coeliac disease is set out in NICE NG20: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management. As the number of people who have not been diagnosed with coeliac disease is unknown, there has been no formal financial assessment measuring the cost to the Health and Social Care (HSC) of undiagnosed people. As with any undiagnosed condition there is potential for associated costs due to repeat primary care appointments, increased consultations, investigations, and treatment of avoidable complications.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she is planning to introduce legislation to give the PSNI additional powers to tackle anti-social behaviour.
answer_text: As set out in AQW 26439/22-27 my Department led a multi-agency, cross-departmental review of Anti-Social Behaviour (ASB) legislation and recently published a report following consultation on potential changes to said legislation. The consultation sought views on potential change to Anti-Social Behaviour Orders (ASBOs) and drinking in public. With respect to the ASBO, and in consideration of the views expressed via the consultation and subsequently, it is clear that further policy development work is required. Going forward, the Department will consider the issues raised through this exercise to inform final legislative proposals in this area. It should be noted that any amendment will not be brought forward until the next Assembly mandate (2027-2032) and will be subject to Executive and Assembly approval. As also set out in the recently published report, there is strong support for updating the legislative framework to tackle drinking in public and it is proposed to continue working with our partners in the Department for Communities and PSNI to finalise proposals in this area, with a view to bringing forward legislation in the next assembly mandate. This will again be subject to Executive and Assembly approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether an assessment has been made on (i) how many people are currently living with undiagnosed coeliac disease; and (ii) the cost implication of this to the health and social care system.
answer_text: Coeliac disease is a lifelong autoimmune condition triggered by ingesting gluten. It can cause gastrointestinal upset, diarrhoea, constipation, vomiting, stomach cramps, mouth ulcers, fatigue and anaemia.  It is more likely to occur in families but is also associated with diabetes 1 and congenital conditions such as Turner and Downs syndrome.Symptoms are alleviated by adhering to a gluten free diet.  However, continued ingestion of gluten can lead to complications including malnutrition, lactose intolerance and complications in pregnancy. In rare circumstances it can also lead to bowel cancer.It is not possible to assess the number of people currently living with undiagnosed coeliac disease.  NICE estimates that around 1% of the population in the UK may have coeliac disease and organisations such as Coeliac UK suggest that up to a third of cases are undiagnosed.  However, prevalence can vary considerably across populations and the information available on undiagnosed patients does not identify if this includes those undergoing investigations but not yet diagnosed.Standards in the management of conditions are routinely informed by my Department’s endorsement of NICE Clinical Guidelines.  Once endorsed, guidance is issued to the HSC for adoption and integration into practice as the extant standards for condition management.  The guidance relevant to coeliac disease is set out in NICE NG20: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management. As the number of people who have not been diagnosed with coeliac disease is unknown, there has been no formal financial assessment measuring the cost to the Health and Social Care (HSC) of undiagnosed people. As with any undiagnosed condition there is potential for associated costs due to repeat primary care appointments, increased consultations, investigations, and treatment of avoidable complications.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department is taking to reduce the time taken for a diagnosis for coeliac disease.
answer_text: Patients referred to secondary care for Coeliac Disease are cared for by specialist dietitians and gastroenterologists in multidisciplinary teams who manage a wide range of conditions.  Diagnosis is through a blood test at primary care level followed, in some cases, by an endoscopic procedure to obtain a biopsy of the patient’s intestine for testing.  Much work has already been done under service transformation to fully utilise the endoscopic capacity across the HSC.  The potential to further streamline the process is being explored whereby a diagnosis can be made routinely without a biopsy. Some years ago, the Trusts piloted dietetic led coeliac review clinics as an alternative to consultant outpatient clinics.  This service model proved effective in prioritising coeliac patients within gastroenterology.  Where it was possible to have specialist clinics, patients benefited greatly from high-quality cost-effective care and more timely reviews.  Consultant capacity was released for other gastroenterology patients as well.  Unfortunately, however, some Trusts were unable to recruit dietitians and recurrent funding support was not available in each area.  Although this service is available in some Trusts, any future development of dietetic led clinics across the HSC would require a dedicated resource funded recurrently which is not possible at present.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what action he is taking to encourage more people to become foster parents
answer_text: My Department has in place a central HSC Adoption and Foster Care Service which carries out ongoing activities to attract new foster carers and to find foster homes for particular children.  For example, there are 12 event or information sessions planned for June 2025, online and in person across Northern Ireland. In addition, reflective of the significant pressures across all our public services, there is a wider programme of reform underway to address a range of challenges across children’s social care.  Recognising the central role of foster parents as members of the Health and Social Care community, this Reform Programme includes a dedicated workstream focused on foster care to help address the current challenges with recruitment and retention of foster parents. This will include, among other things, consideration of the rates of allowances payable to foster parents and the support available to help them in the vital work that they do.  The draft fostering regulations, which will streamline the assessments of prospective foster carers and provide assurances about the quality of foster care services provided by independent agencies through the introduction of registration with RQIA, are nearing completion. While it is the general intention to make the regulations in the coming weeks, with the lead in period for registration with RQIA ending in late 2025, there are a number of elements which the Department must balance to determine the most appropriate date for making and bringing them into operation. Work is also continuing on the development of draft Minimum Standards for Fostering Services in Northern Ireland, to accompany and complement the regulations.  These will apply to all fostering standards, both statutory and non-statutory, improving consistency across Northern Ireland, and will be used by RQIA in their assessments of the quality of foster care services.  The draft standards are structured around the Northern Ireland Framework for Integrated Therapeutic Care, which will be rolled out for all settings for looked after children, and which has at its core collaborative care planning where the voice of all care givers, including foster carers, is encouraged and valued.  The draft Standards will be subject to a full public consultation with implementation planned for later this year.  It is anticipated that, collectively, the new regulations, the standards, and other improvements progressed by the fostering workstream of the Reform Programme, will go some way to addressing the current challenges in recruitment and retention of foster parents.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the foster care allowances will be increased to enable foster parents to provide more care.
answer_text: The allowances paid to foster carers in Northern Ireland (NI) are for costs associated with day to day care; and are outlined within the annual published Fostering Model Scheme. The Department reviews the allowances payable to foster carers on an annual basis in respect of the decision for an annual uplift to apply to these allowances. The annual review considers rates of inflation, the rates of allowances payable in other UK jurisdictions, and what is affordable within the financial envelope available to the Department. The current published allowance rates are 2024-25 rates, and these include the annual uplift increase of 3% which was applied to foster care allowance rates for the 2024-25 financial year. The current allowances rates are published on the Department of Health website and are available to view at www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/foster-care-allowances Consideration of an annual uplift to apply to allowance rates payable for 2025-26 is currently under review, and foster carers will be notified of the outcome as soon as possible. Payment of any uplift to rates for 2025-26 will be backdated to April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of children within the South Eastern Health and Social care Trust that require a foster care placement.
answer_text: From the most recent confirmed Statutory Functions data provided by South Eastern Trust as of 30 September 2024, there were a total of 581 children in foster care placements in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust - this includes kinship and non-kinship placements, and placements with Independent Fostering Providers. A further 39 children are in other arrangements and await an appropriate foster placement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline what discussions he has had with the Coastal Forum regarding consideration of, and potential funding for, the delivery of an outer breakwater for Donaghadee Harbour.
answer_text: Ards and North Down Borough Council is responsible for Donaghadee Harbour and therefore it is for the council to consider funding and delivery.		I recognise that the Coastal Forum can provide a valuable opportunity for departments and councils to engage in discussions on matters relating to coastal change. I will therefore be making arrangements with the Minister for Infrastructure to re-convene a Coastal Forum meeting as soon as diaries allow, thereby providing an opportunity to discuss this and other matters.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the cost of the work for the ramp at Marino train station in North Down.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:The Marino works include: Construction of a new access path to the Down platform at Marino Halt complying with DDA legislation. Segregation of the main walking route for railway passengers accessing/egressing the Down platform and private property. Delineation of land boundaries and usage of same between NIR and a private dwelling & driveway, therefore improving safety for both parties. Improved lighting for passengers accessing the Down platform and that area of the platform. Stabilisation of ground backing onto the Down platform by constructing retaining walls.  Rectification of land ownership boundaries. DfI provided funding to Translink of circa £540k, to carryout surveys, outline & detailed designs, obtain the relevant statutory approval, construction and oversight of the project works.  For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the TransformED strategy will make systematic synthetic phonics the sole mandatory methodology for teaching children to read.
answer_text: The TransformED strategy emphasises the importance of phonics instruction but does not mandate systematic synthetic phonics as the sole method for teaching children to read.  TransformED sets out plans to develop a new Literacy Strategy, which will consider all relevant evidence and research.  The Strategy will be informed by best practice both locally and internationally and will aim to provide teachers with clear guidelines and appropriate resources and training to deliver effective literacy teaching.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) how CPR training is being monitored across post-primary schools; and (ii) whether additional support is planned to encourage pupils to be able to learn this life saving skill.
answer_text: My Department has partnered with the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS), Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) and the Education Authority to create the Community of Lifesavers Education Programme. The programme provides training for teachers to deliver CPR and AED awareness to support schools in their delivery of content in Learning for Life and Work at Key Stage 3. An oversight group comprised of the key partners meets quarterly and continues to develop and monitor the training across schools in Northern Ireland, to ensure that these life-saving skills are made available to all our children and young people.The recently established Save a Life for Northern Ireland (SALNI) partnership brings together organisations dedicated to improving out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OHCA) outcomes. The partnership aims to increase survival rates by promoting CPR education, raising public awareness, and supporting community readiness through coordinated action. An Official from my Department attended the inaugural meeting of this partnership, and we plan to continue to work with key stakeholders in this regard.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any policy changes she is considering in light of the Supreme Court ruling in the case For Women Scotland Ltd v The Scottish Ministers Supreme Court delivered on the 16 April 2025.
answer_text: The Equality Commission is currently considering any potential implications here due to the British Supreme Court ruling and will issue guidance in due course.This issue must be approached with compassion, understanding and dignity for all.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any measures her Department has put in place to deal with vandalism targeting statues and historic monuments in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I recognise the effect that attacks on symbolic structures such as statues and historic monuments can have, including the distress and the disruption caused by the resulting damage.My Department, along with NI Policing Board, provides joint funding of £4.25m to Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs). PCSPs are local statutory stand-alone partnerships located in each council area, made up of elected, independent, and designated organisation members. The designated organisation members include PSNI, NIFRS, YJA, EA, Health Trusts, NIHE, and PBNI.  PCSPs address local community safety concerns, including crime and ASB as well as confidence in policing.  Although there are no projects being delivered which are specific to statues and historic monuments, PCSPs act swiftly to address local issues as and when they arise.My Department also chairs a multi-agency cross Departmental Community Safety Network to ensure a collaborative response to multi-faceted community safety issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any discussions he has had with Health and Social Care Trusts to ensure that appropriate mental health support and counselling services are provided for health and social care workers.
answer_text: Building on, consolidating and promoting staff health and wellbeing is one of the key themes of the Health and Social Care Workforce Strategy 2026: Delivering for our People, which was published by my Department in May 2018. My Department works collaboratively with HSC Trusts on an ongoing basis to support them in their delivery of effective processes and resources for improving staff health, wellbeing and safety at work.Through this work I am aware that all HSC Trusts currently provide a range of services and resources to support the mental health of staff.  These include the provision of telephone support helplines and counselling provided through Employee Assistance Programmes and    on-line resources to support healthy bodies and minds.  Where specific psychological support is required for staff, this is also available through referral to HSC Trust Occupational Health Services.  To further support this provision, I launched ‘Strengthening our Core: A Regional Framework for HSC Staff Health and Wellbeing in the Workplace’ on 11 September 2024 which is a valuable resource that assists HSC Trusts in their planning and implementation of staff health and wellbeing services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the work being carried out by her Department to assist the PSNI with enforcement of anti-social behaviour.
answer_text: It is very rare for community safety issues to be resolvable via one agency or body alone and anti-social behaviour (ASB) is no different. Addressing ASB issues requires collective partnership working across local and central Government to identify the causes and put in place joined–up, long term solutions.My Department, together with the Northern Ireland Policing Board, funds Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs) who play a key role in the Department’s operational response to local concerns regarding ASB. PCSPs liaise with the PSNI, local communities and other key partners, including local statutory bodies and civil society, to develop appropriate actions to tackle, and reduce the impact of, ASB. Actions currently being delivered by PCSPs include provision of community safety warden services; diversionary and community engagement projects; detached youth programmes; delivery of educational programmes; awareness raising; projects with Nighttime Economy stakeholders; and small grant schemes. To ensure PSNI and other relevant authorities have appropriate and proportionate powers to tackle ASB in circumstances where an enforcement approach is necessary, my Department led a multi-agency, cross-departmental review of ASB legislation. A report outlining a summary of responses received to a public consultation on potential legislative change and a proposed way forward was recently published on the Departments website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department plans to introduce a Household Support Fund, similar to that in England.
answer_text: My department’s Discretionary Support Scheme is the Northern Ireland equivalent of the Household Support Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of employees in her Department’s active travel team.
answer_text: There are currently 32 members of staff in my department’s active travel team.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 25384/22-27, whether her Department plans to fund an extension of the Newtownards to Bangor greenway to link up with Bangor city centre and the North Down Coastal Path.
answer_text: The Newtownards to Bangor Greenway is currently under construction and, through engagement with Ards and North Down Borough Council, officials are considering future active travel improvements between the Greenway and Bangor city centre. My Department has developed a draft Active Travel Delivery Plan which includes network maps for our larger towns and cities. The draft network plan for Bangor identifies routes between the Newtownards to Bangor Greenway that are currently in construction, Bangor City Centre, and connections to the North Down Coastal Path. Public consultation on the draft plan closed on 28 February 2025. My officials are now reviewing the responses received with a view to presenting a final plan to me for consideration in the autumn. This includes review of the currently identified prioritised routes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the timeframe for Bangor Central Integrated Primary School’s new build.
answer_text: A revised planning application to take account of the inclusion of Enhanced Learning Support provision at the school, was submitted on 21 February 2025 and is currently being considered by Ards and North Down Borough Council.It is anticipated that construction of the new school build will commence in the first quarter of 2026, however this will be dependent on the relevant approvals being obtained.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQO 1884/22-27, for his assessment of whether separate changing and toilet facilities should be the outcome of the review of policy.
answer_text: My Department is progressing a region-wide review of the existing policy position in respect of this issue. On receipt of the review of the broader current position, I will reflect as to what options or modifications are available to me. In coming to any future decision, I have advised my Department that I would expect any findings to learn from best practice elsewhere and also informed by relevant independent input. Through this work I hope to implement a new, region wide agreed position that treats trans people with respect, but which also respects the important right of women to access safe spaces.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 25773/22-27, to detail any action being taken by his Department to prevent overpayments.
answer_text: Civil Service Pensions (CSP) monitor the payment of all pension benefits and have checks in place to help identify and prevent potential overpayments. Management checks include 100% of pension awards calculated being subject to a reperformance check. CSP has controls in place to ensure that an individual no longer eligible to receive a pension payment will not receive one. These controls include: A data sharing agreement with the General Register Office (GRO) for the provision of Death Certificates on a weekly basis when the death certificate has an occupation entered as civil servant. Participation in the biennial National Fraud Initiative (NFI) data matching exercise. NFI mortality screening is carried out in the intervening years.Various attestation exercises are regularly undertaken.The Department of Finance Superannuation and Other Allowances Pension Scheme Statements are also subject to the annual audit process by the NI Audit Office.DoF Internal Audit also perform periodic audits as part of their work schedule.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQO 1884/22-27, whether he will be ensuring that Health and Social Care Trusts provide single-sex facilities following the Supreme Court ruling on For Women Scotland Ltd v The Scottish Ministers on 16 April 2025.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on weed spraying of footpaths in North Down.
answer_text: The Department has no statutory obligation to remove weeds, other than in relation to the control of noxious weeds, however there are policies and procedures in place in relation to weed control, aimed at ensuring the safety of road users and preventing the deterioration of the road pavement. The Department will also treat invasive species such as Japanese Knotweed found growing within areas for which it has responsibility.I can confirm that the weed control programme in the Ards and North Down area commenced 29th April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 25773/22-27, whether an independent review was carried out into the £1.2million of overpayments written off in the financial year ending March 2023.
answer_text: An independent review was carried out by NICS Internal Audit Services. The review included the control, governance and risk management arrangements associated with the Civil Service Pension (CSP) overpayments identified and subsequently written off in 2023. The recommendations have been accepted and are currently being addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given there is no bus service from Bangor to the Ulster Hospital at weekends, to detail any engagement he has had with Translink to ensure that there are public transport connections to the Ulster Hospital.
answer_text: The South Eastern Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust is mindful that some members of the public from North Down and the Ards Peninsula use public transport to access services at the Ulster Hospital, Dundonald. As such, the Trust has requested that Translink provides regular updates on the number of passenger journeys to the Ulster Hospital from Bangor, along with any other customer feedback on hospital access issues which are highlighted to their drivers. The last data provided by Translink was in September 2024 which recorded an average of 13.72 passengers per day, over a 105-day reference period, taking the bus from Bangor and disembarking at the bus stop outside the Ulster Hospital on the Upper Newtownards Road.  This average daily figure has remained relatively unchanged over the last 18 months. Neither Translink nor the Trust are aware of any adverse customer feedback to Translink, or through the Trust complaints department regarding public transport issues to or from Bangor.   The Trust organises and facilitates bi-annual multi-agency meetings at Dundonald, Lagan Valley and Ards to foster a more joined up approach to transport in and around South Eastern hospital sites. The meetings are attended by representatives from Translink, South Eastern Trust, Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI), Department for Infrastructure, council representatives and local elected representatives.  At a recent meeting, the Trust enquired of Translink if it would consider enhancing daily bus services including those between Bangor and the Ulster Hospital with the aim of attracting more uptake by patients and families as well as staff.  It could also reduce the over-reliance on single occupancy vehicles. Translink has yet to respond to the Trust’s request, however, any decision to commission and fund additional bus services rests exclusively with Translink and the Department for Infrastructure. South Eastern HSC Trust would welcome input from Members of the Legislative Assembly (MLAs) such as yourself or a representative from your office to join the Dundonald and/or the Ards group where topics like this can be discussed.  If this is something you would like to be part of, please email Travel.Planning@setrust.hscni.net and Trust staff will contact your office to provide you with more information.Please be reassured that the South Eastern HSC Trust is actively monitoring public transport issues on behalf of members of the public who avail of its services.  I also trust it demonstrates the Health and Social Care Service is keen to work with all stakeholders to enhance local public transport provision and to support regional net zero ambitions.

answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for securing pre-school provision in local areas and ensuring that sufficient provision is available to meet demand in all areas. question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any action his Department is taking to promote safe swimming and water-based activities in natural waters.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the monitoring and assessment of water quality at designated Bathing waters under The Quality of Bathing Water Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2008. During the bathing season (1 June – 15 September) weekly monitoring is carried out at 26 identified bathing water and 7 candidate bathing water sites.  Throughout the bathing season, monitoring results are displayed at all bathing sites and promoted through social media, allowing the public to make informed decisions regarding swimming and water-based activities. Current and historic findings for all bathing water sites are also provided on the DAERA and NI Direct websites.  I am also keen to emphasise the importance of water safety on our beaches and the need to be proactive in this area. To this end, my Department has agreed to support Water Safety NI in order to assist with amplifying their message.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any action his Department is taking to raise awareness of the dangers of swimming in undesignated bathing areas.
answer_text: Under the Quality of Bathing Water Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2008, my Department is responsible for monitoring and assessment of water quality at designated bathing areas and for raising awareness of the subsequent results at those sites. However, I am keen to emphasise the importance of water safety, regardless of the nature of the bathing area.  To this end, my Department has agreed to support Water Safety NI in order to assist with amplifying their safety messages.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of Education Authority funded pre-school places in North Down for the 2025-26 academic year.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for securing pre-school provision in local areas and ensuring that sufficient provision is available to meet demand in all areas.  The EA has advised that a total of 858 funded pre-school education places were available in the North Down parliamentary constituency area at stage one of the pre-school admissions process for the 2025-26 academic year.  A breakdown of the places available in each setting is set out in Table 1 below. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the Day Care Foods Scheme which provides free milk to children aged under five, whether the cut-off point is a child’s birthday or the end of the school year.
answer_text: Section 23 of The Healthy Start Scheme and Day Care Foods Scheme Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2006 states that children who have not yet reached the age of five, and who are being cared for in prescribed nursery settings, are entitled to 189ml of milk (one third of a pint of milk), free of charge.  Children who have not yet reached the age of one, may receive either milk, or infant formula made up to the same amount. This entitlement ceases when a child reaches the age of five.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice when police officers, who are members of the Police Pension Scheme 2006, will receive their Remedial Service Statements which are required as part of the McCloud remedy.
answer_text: While the Department of Justice has responsibility for overarching policy and legislative changes to the police pensions regulations, the management and administration of the police pension scheme is the responsibility of the Northern Ireland Policing Board, in its role as police pension scheme manager. I am committed to respecting the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board. You may therefore wish to direct your question to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether there is any provision of milk with free school meals.
answer_text: The Education Authority provides milk free of charge to all primary school pupils who take a school meal. Pupils attending a special school are able to access milk at lunchtime free of charge.  Milk is available to purchase by all pupils attending a post-primary school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of overpayments issued in the Civil Service Pension Scheme in each of the last ten years; and (ii) the total cost of these payments.
answer_text: ANSWER	The number of overpayments issued in the Civil Service Pension Scheme in each of the last ten (financial) years and the total cost is provided in the table below:Financial Year EndingNumber of OverpaymentsValue of Overpayments Raised (net)31 March 2016464£337,07931 March 2017208£221,62631 March 2018258£349,13031 March 2019704£949,54731 March 2020340£424,28631 March 2021305£340,15131 March 2022236£259,09131 March 2023413£1,566,07931 March 2024368£971,89131 March 2025304£444,831** Subject to audit

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any action the Education Authority is taking to ensure that the postal address listed on a school and pre-school application is the actual place of residence of the pupil.
answer_text: As the admissions authority for any school is that school’s Board of Governors, it is the duty of Governors to verify application information.  My Department has published guidance, Circular 2013/14 (revised October 2020), to advise Boards of Governors of the duty to verify information contained within applications for admission, including in relation to applicants who qualify for admission by virtue of their address, and to provide guidance on when and how to fulfil this duty.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Education Authority will review the provision of funded pre-school places at Groomsport Playgroup following its oversubscription.
answer_text: The EA has advised that at this stage in the pre-school admissions process, 97% of target aged children whose parents applied for a funded pre-school place have already received an offer of a place in a setting of their preference.The pre-school admissions process has two stages. Parents will be advised of Stage Two application outcomes on 6th June. The EA has advised that there are currently 2,316 funded places still available to accommodate the 620 children who have not yet received the offer of a funded place in a setting of their parents’ preference.  There are currently 63 places still available to be filled within a five mile radius of Groomsport Playgroup, some of which are located within a two mile radius.  I would encourage parents to remain with the admissions process to the end and provide further preferences when invited to do so in order to maximise the chances of their child being offered a place in a setting of their preference.  Should additional funded places be required in local areas, the EA will adjust allocations to settings as required.  It is important for the overall stability of the PSEP that all funded pre-school settings are supported and treated equitably in the allocation of places.  In a situation where there is overprovision, an increased PAN for one will result in a reduction for another.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions his Department is undertaking to promote obesity prevention.
answer_text: The new obesity strategic framework, “Healthy Futures” (https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/healthy-futures-obesity-strategic-framework-northern-ireland) was published on 26th November 2025. The framework provides an umbrella for actions to prevent and address overweight and obesity, from education and prevention through to weight management and treatment services. Previous obesity strategies focused solely on prevention. The vision statement for the new strategic framework, is ‘to work collectively to create the wider social and environmental conditions in Northern Ireland that enable and support people to improve their diet and participate in more physical activity, and that reduces the risk of related harm for those living with overweight and obesity’The Public Health Agency (PHA) developed the online campaign ‘Choose to Live Better’ to promote obesity prevention by providing public information on healthy eating and physical activity. The PHA work with safefood and the Food Standards Agency, along with a range of stakeholders across Ireland to develop and support the all-Island ‘Building a Healthier Food Environment’ five-year public health campaign to protect children’s health. Both campaigns can be viewed online at https://www.choosetolivebetter.com/ and https://www.safefood.net/healthy-eating/food-environment/understanding-food-environment respectively.PHA also partner with over 500 participating community pharmacies across Northern Ireland to support the Living Well Service. The ‘Move More Feel Better’ campaign was rolled during August-September 2025 and highlighted the role of pharmacy teams in providing advice and support to help people get more active. PHA regularly utilises its organic social media platforms to promote evidence-based obesity prevention, nutrition, and physical activity messages along with PHA commissioned services. The aim is to engage the public and encourage healthier choices. This strategy further compliments my announcement in May of this year, regarding the establishment of a Regional Obesity Management Service for Northern Ireland. My Department acknowledges that full implementation of this service will require consideration of financial and service requirements, including clinical capacity, specialist support and the development of a coordinated and sustainable service model and care pathways. The first phase of the ROMS, scheduled to commence in early 2026, will focus on the development of a community-based service, where adult patients will have access to lifestyle support to ensure maximum effectiveness, as well as obesity management medication, if clinically appropriate. Further phases will be developed, subject to funding.I have instructed my officials within my Department to develop an implementation plan, which outlines a timescale for rollout that provides equitable, sustainable, and affordable access to this technology and which is targeted to optimise outcomes for those with the highest clinical

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the proposed Regional Obesity Management Service, including when it is likely to be launched.
answer_text: On 21 May 2025, I announced my plans to establish a Regional Obesity Management Service (ROMS). Work is currently being taken forward by my Department to assess patient numbers and to develop a service specification which will set out access criteria. Implementation will be taken forward in a phased basis and in a carefully managed way to ensure that it operates effectively. In its first phase, which is expected to commence early 2026, the focus will be on the provision of medical injections for weight loss, with associated lifestyle support to ensure maximum effectiveness. As work progresses, there may be further phases to this service development to include other interventions, such as bariatric (weight loss) surgery, subject to funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients diagnosed with cystinosis, broken down Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Cystinosis is a genetic condition in which amino acid called cystine builds up and causes damage to your cells.The condition is rare and has a lifelong impact for those affected. Cystinosis occurs in about 1 in 100,000 to 200,000 live births. For Northern Ireland, the incidence could therefore be estimated as one new case every 5 to 10 years.The Department does not routinely release information in relation to a prevalent population for a condition where the number is so small as to potentially identify individuals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Obesity Strategic Framework, including when it is anticipated to be published.
answer_text: Since the close of the consultation on the Obesity Strategic Framework, my Department has been working through the detail of the responses and looking at the wider research and evidence base. In addition, my Department has been engaging with colleagues across the UK and Ireland to consider emerging developments for those living with overweight and obesity.Work on finalising the consultation response report is almost complete. A meeting of the Obesity Project Board was organised in February 2025 to review the report and make recommendations. Once agreed, the consultation report will be published, and we will work to finalise the Healthy Futures strategic framework, with an aim to publish this in 2025, subject to agreement and the availability of resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the support provided across each Health and Social Care Trust for patients with cystinosis.
answer_text: Cystinosis is a genetic condition in which amino acid called cystine builds up and causes damage to your cells. This is a rare disease, which is diagnosed in early childhood, usually by the age of two. The condition is rare and has a lifelong impact for those affected. Cystinosis occurs in about 1 in 100,000 to 200,000 live births. The Paediatric Nephrology team in the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children provide treatment and co-ordinate care for children diagnosed with cystinosis in their complex renal clinic where they are seen every 8 to 12 weeks.  Alongside medical and nursing care, the children also receive clinical input from dietetics, social work team and clinical psychology.  Each child is seen by genetics at diagnosis and as needed thereafter.  Children are also seen by endocrinology and ophthalmology at separate appointments.  The service also refers, as necessary, to other specialties depending on need including surgery, orthopaedics/spinal, gastroenterology and respiratory.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail why Albany Road, Bangor, is not gritted despite being a bus route and having a primary school on the road.
answer_text: The salted network is 7,000km long, covering 28% of the road network and facilitating approximately 80% of traffic. This network has been developed in line with my Department’s Winter Service Policy RSPPG E022.  Regrettably, Albany Road, Bangor does not meet the qualifying criteria for inclusion in the schedule of salted routes. Clause 2.1 outlines the criteria for inclusion of roads on the salting schedule. Clause 2.4.1 of the policy outlines the criteria for determining a route related to buses. It explains that each bus should be counted as the equivalent of a number of private cars equal to the full seating capacity of the bus (i.e) a 40 seat bus is counted as 40 vehicles. Details of all salting routes can be viewed on my Department’s website via the following link; DFI Roads Salted Network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when Albany Road, Bangor, will be resurfaced.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.The table provides the number of carriageway defects and the number of pothole repairs that had been reported by the public on Albany Road, in each of the last five years. It is important to note that reported defects have the potential to be the same defect reported by different members of the public and/or, the defect not meeting the department’s intervention criteria for repair.YearNumber of carriageway defects reported by the public to Albany Road, Bangor (2021-24 inc)Number of potholes repairs on Albany Road Bangor (2021-24 inc)2020/21312021/22282022/23262023/2415102024/25374Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council.My officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on progress on the Newtownards to Bangor greenway.
answer_text: The Newtownards to Bangor Greenway forms a section of the Newtownards to Bangor loop which is an identified secondary route in my Department’s Strategic Plan for Greenways. Ards and North Down Borough Council is leading delivery and my Department has provided approximately £2.8m capital funding to support the project. I was pleased to attend a recent ceremonial event to mark commencement of construction, and I look forward to seeing the greenway completed in due course. I am committed to working with our council delivery partners to provide the highest quality active travel infrastructure to encourage more people to walk, wheel and cycle. The significant investment from the department is a demonstration of this commitment.

answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.The table provides the number of carriageway defects and the number of pothole repairs that had been reported by the public on Albany Road, in each of the last five years. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of road defects reported on Albany Road, Bangor, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.The table provides the number of carriageway defects and the number of pothole repairs that had been reported by the public on Albany Road, in each of the last five years.
