question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether Health and Social Care Trusts’ policies provide for separate changing facilities for men and women for both staff and patients.
answer_text: As Minister, I want to support all staff, transgender and others. I want to work with colleagues across the system to reach an agreed regional position that treats everyone with respect.I have recently requested a region-wide review of the existing policy position. In addition, I have requested that the development or introduction of any further Trust level guidance be suspended until this focused review is completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action her Department plans to take to improve (i) pedestrian safety; and (ii) access to Bangor Bus and Rail Station from Main Street, across the Dufferin Avenue junction.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users including pedestrians.I can advise there is an uncontrolled crossing (pedestrian refuge island) at the Dufferin Avenue junction which aids pedestrians crossing the road from the Bus and Rail Station on Main Street.  There is also a controlled crossing (traffic signals) in place on Main Street and Abbey Street to facilitate safe passage of pedestrians in this area.These crossing provisions are considered to be suitable for the location in question, when reviewed against the PSNI injury collision data available to my department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when she will outline her next steps to addressing inconsiderate pavement parking.
answer_text: My Department sought the public’s views on options to tackle inconsiderate pavement parking and a Findings Report was published on 6 December 2023. Officials are currently developing the next steps for my consideration and a formal response will issue when I have determined the best way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the new build for Bangor Central Integrated Primary School.
answer_text: The Integrated Consultant Team have completed the revised technical design (RIBA Stage 4) to take account of the inclusion of Enhanced Learning Support provision at the school and a revised planning application was submitted on 21 February 2025.It is anticipated that construction will commence in the last quarter of 2025, however this will be dependent on the relevant approvals being obtained.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the redevelopment of Bangor Bus and Rail Station.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.A proposal to commence a project for Bangor Station Refurbishment, has been submitted to my Department and is under consideration by officials.  Translink also plan, subject to budget availability, to progress with proposals for a secure cycle parking facility at Bangor Station in 2025/26.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department plans to regulate the use of public footpaths in town centers by commercial premises as extra floor space and the use of A-Board advertisements, to ensure that streets are safe and accessible for those who are blind or have visual impairments.
answer_text: My Department has a duty to ensure that footways remain free from obstruction. Whilst it is recognised that businesses place A-boards on footways for advertising, they can form obstructions and may inconvenience pedestrians, particularly disabled people and people with prams. Public safety and convenience on the footway is the primary consideration of my Department.My officials liaised with councils, the Inclusive Mobility and Transport Advisory Committee (IMTAC) and disability groups to produce Pavement Café licencing guidance for DfI staff in 2024.  The guidance document deals primarily with maintaining a minimum acceptable footway width, sightlines to junctions/crossings and the siting of furniture and other equipment associated with the operation of the pavement café.In situations where we cannot secure the co-operation of shop frontagers and traders, then action may be taken to try and remove these obstructions under Article 87 of the Roads (NI) Order 1993. These powers may be used when supported by available evidence to justify their application within the operational constraint of the limited resources available to the Department.Councils are responsible for the licensing of pavement cafes under the Licensing of Pavement Cafes Act (NI) 2014, but my Department takes an active role as a consultee to the process. My Department places pedestrians at the top of the road user hierarchy and the provision of a pavement café must never be allowed to discourage the use of the footways or prejudice safety.  However, there is also a recognition that pavement cafes can bring vitality and economic benefit to our towns and cities, particularly in the current climate, and an appropriate balance must be struck with each case being considered on its merits. Although my Department is considered best placed to advise Councils on access issues, Councils must have due regard to all current legislation, including disability discrimination legislation, when deliberating on pavement café licence applications.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the recent announcement by the Secretary of State for Work and Pensions in relation to changes to the benefits system, including the Personal Independence Payment.
To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has any plans to regulate residential property service operators to ensure they meet their obligations and residents get value for money.
answer_text: Scoping work by the Civil Law Reform team in my Department commenced in January to review the regulation of property management companies, service charges and other aspects relating to shared property management. The scoping review has two main strands – stakeholder engagement and a review of the law in comparative jurisdictions. Officials aim to report to me at Easter with the findings of their scoping project and suggested next steps. I will then take time to consider how to progress this matter, taking into account the shortened mandate and its attendant constraints, especially on resources and capacity to legislate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has any plans to include supernumerary pupils into the approved enrolment number for schools.
answer_text: Children with a statement of special educational needs (SEN) are considered as supernumerary for the duration of their time at school.  I have recently approved work to be taken forward on proposals to change this practice and these proposals should be published in the coming weeks.Children admitted on appeal and children admitted by Direction of the Exceptional Circumstances Body (ECB) are considered as supernumerary for the year of their admission only and I have no current plans to change this practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has considered the use of (i) police community support officers; (ii) reserve officers; and (iii) High Court enforcement officers to support and alleviate pressure on the PSNI.
answer_text: The utilisation of policing resources is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  As I am committed to respecting the operational independence of both organisations, you may wish to direct your question to the Chief Constable or the Policing Board.  However, I remain committed to working with the Chief Constable, the Minister of Finance and my Executive colleagues to ensure we have a Service that is funded appropriately.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 24130/22-27, for his assessment of whether Health and Social Care Trusts, by not providing single-sex safe spaces for female staff, are in direct contravention of (i) the Workplace (Health, Safety and Welfare) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1993; and (ii) the Sex Discrimination (Northern Ireland) Order 1976.
answer_text: All six Health and Social Care Trusts have advised that they provide single-sex safe spaces for female staff.  Whilst compliance with legislation is a matter for legal consideration on a case by case basis, all six Trusts have advised they are aware of their duty as an employer to provide safe spaces for all staff in line with current employment legislation including (i) the Workplace (Health, Safety and Welfare) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1993; and (ii) the Sex Discrimination (Northern Ireland) Order 1976.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the review of loading bays in Bangor city centre; and (ii) whether any loading bays can be converted back to parking bays.
answer_text: My Department provides loading bays to help prevent the blocking of traffic lanes by delivery vehicles and maintain the free flow of traffic in busier urban areas.Officials have completed a review of loading bays in Bangor City centre and have observed these bays in active use by local businesses in the area. Their continued use means that it would not be considered appropriate to initiate removal at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given the decision not to award funding from the Core Grant Funding Scheme 2025-26 to Positive Life NI, (i) to detail any steps his Department is taking to support HIV prevention, treatment and awareness; and (ii) for his assessment of whether Northern Ireland is likely to achieve its commitment to ending HIV transmission by 2030.
answer_text: The high standard of applications for core grant has reinforced the valuable contribution that voluntary and community sector organisations make towards health and social care outcomes. The selection of the 25 highest scoring applications, from 259, to progress to stage 2 of the assessment process was based solely on agreed scoring criteria, rather than a departmental determination of the value of the applicants. The Department of Health’s Sexual Health Action Plan includes a strategic objective to strive to eliminate HIV transmission and to support those living with HIV to live well. As part of the Plan, a number of activities are underway in HIV surveillance, such as analysis of late and incident HIV diagnosis and increasing the timeliness of availability of HIV surveillance data. Actions are also underway relating to public communication activity focused on HIV awareness, testing and stigma. Efforts have also been made to introduce the Fast Track Cities initiative to Northern Ireland and a steering group has been established to support identification of priority actions to support this. Positive Life continues to be contracted by Public Health Agency Health Improvement to the annual value of £171,203 to deliver a range of services and health promotion initiatives that contribute towards tackling HIV transmission in NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department will reconsider its decision not to allow Policing and Community Safety Partnerships to use its funding to provide speed indication devices for road safety improvement.
answer_text: The Department of Justice and Northern Ireland Policing Board jointly fund Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs), via a Joint Committee, to identify and help address local community safety issues in each council area. PCSPs are legislatively required to address anti-social behaviour, community safety issues and confidence in policing. They do this by engaging with local communities to identify and help address community safety issues, which can include road safety.I am aware that PCSPs have previously purchased speed indicator devices (SIDs) to help address the issue of road safety in their local area and that there are currently 159 mobile and fixed SIDs located throughout Northern Ireland that have been procured / are being maintained by PCSPs. The Department for Infrastructure (DfI) is policy lead for road safety and, while it does not fund SIDs, it has provided a memorandum on the DfI website which details the use of these - Speed Indicator Devices (SIDs) DEM 191/23 | Department for Infrastructure. It should be noted that there are many other PCSP road safety initiatives which also help address road safety issues. These include cycle safety events; road safety awareness and education activities; public meetings with a road safety focus; road safe roadshows; kids courts and “Beesafe” as well as the use of SIDs. The DfI memorandum contains details of research which indicates that SIDs are only effective at reducing speeds for short periods of time, 2-3 weeks, after which drivers realise there is no associated enforcement, and they revert to their previous behaviour. It is therefore important that other road safety initiatives are employed to ensure the best outcome is achieved in addressing road safety issues in local areas.Given the number of initiatives in place across Northern Ireland to help address road safety, and the need to balance this with tackling other important community safety issues in local areas, a decision was taken by Joint Committee that, while PCSP funding can still be utilised to maintain existing SIDs, no further SIDs should be procured. Due to competing priorities, there are no plans to review this decision.It should be noted that other bodies and organisations have also procured SIDs for their local areas. This includes the Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership as well as local businesses and community organisations, which have all provided funding for SIDs in the past.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the consultation that each Health and Social Care Trust took with staff before allowing transgender or non-binary staff to use toilets, showers and changing rooms that are not the sex assigned to them at birth.
answer_text: (i) Pre-consultation engagement with regards the regional Gender Identity and Expression Employment Policy was undertaken across the HSCN I region during the period 10 January - March 2016. A number of round table discussions were held with the community and voluntary organisations. Trade Union colleagues were also invited. The regional LGBTQ+ staff forum was also engaged with.In 2017, public consultation took place and the Business Services Organisation posted an announcement on their website and each HSCNI Trust did same. This was also communicated via regional and local consultation lists.Stakeholder consultation was undertaken in accordance with Trust Section 75 duties to consult. An Equality Screening document was also completed and shared as part of the consultation. The outcome of the consultation was considered and then adopted within each HSC organisation as part of their policy processes.(ii) I can confirm that this week I have now requested the Department of Health in Northern Ireland conduct a region-wide review of the existing policy position. In addition, I have requested that the development or introduction of any further Trust level guidance be completely suspended until this focused review is completed. On receipt of the review of the broader current position, I will reflect as to what options or modifications are available to me. In coming to any future decision, I have already informed the Department that I would expect any findings to learn from best practice elsewhere, informed also by relevant independent input. Through this work I hope to implement a new, region wide agreed position that treats trans people with respect, but which also respects the important right of women to access safe spaces.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action her Department is taking to address parking and traffic flow concerns on Main Street, Crawfordsburn.
answer_text: My officials have undertaken traffic observations within Crawfordsburn village and have determined that any traffic delays at this location are in line with expected increases to journey times at peak periods and are not considered to be excessive. There are extensive existing double yellow line waiting restrictions to facilitate traffic progression throughout the village.  Crawfordsburn has numerous residential properties on the Main Street and any extension to these waiting restrictions has to be considered alongside the limited parking opportunities for residents of Main Street.Concerns about vehicles which are parked inappropriately, or cause an obstruction or danger to any other road user should be reported to the to the PSNI who are the enforcement authority using their non-emergency number 101.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any disciplinary action has been taken against any staff in a Health and Social Care Trust in the last five years for expressing opposition to allowing transgender or non-binary staff the use of toilets, showers and changing rooms that are not the sex assigned to them at birth.
answer_text: All six of the Health and Social Care Trusts have advised no disciplinary action has been taken against any staff member in the last five years for expressing opposition to allowing transgender or non-binary staff the use of toilets, showers and changing rooms that are not the sex assigned to them at birth.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any guidance each Health and Social Care Trust has issued in relation to the use of public toilets by transgender and non-binary people on Health and Social Care Trust sites.
answer_text: (i) Pre-consultation engagement with regards the regional Gender Identity and Expression Employment Policy was undertaken across the HSCN I region during the period 10 January - March 2016. A number of round table discussions were held with the community and voluntary organisations. Trade Union colleagues were also invited. The regional LGBTQ+ staff forum was also engaged with.In 2017, public consultation took place and the Business Services Organisation posted an announcement on their website and each HSCNI Trust did same. This was also communicated via regional and local consultation lists.Stakeholder consultation was undertaken in accordance with Trust Section 75 duties to consult. An Equality Screening document was also completed and shared as part of the consultation. The outcome of the consultation was considered and then adopted within each HSC organisation as part of their policy processes.(ii) I can confirm that this week I have now requested the Department of Health in Northern Ireland conduct a region-wide review of the existing policy position. In addition, I have requested that the development or introduction of any further Trust level guidance be completely suspended until this focused review is completed. On receipt of the review of the broader current position, I will reflect as to what options or modifications are available to me. In coming to any future decision, I have already informed the Department that I would expect any findings to learn from best practice elsewhere, informed also by relevant independent input. Through this work I hope to implement a new, region wide agreed position that treats trans people with respect, but which also respects the important right of women to access safe spaces.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any guidance his Department has provided Health and Social Care Trusts in relation to the use of toilets, showers and changing rooms by transgender and non-binary staff.
answer_text: I very much understand the argument for single sex spaces in certain circumstances, however, I also want Health and Social Care to be a welcoming and supportive place for everyone who works there, regardless of class, creed, race, or identity. I understand gender identity policies are under review at a number of HSC Trusts, which are important processes, and I will be seeking more information on that and what else may need to be done to provide greater clarity and protection.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the implementation of the recommendations made in the High Street Task Force report.
answer_text: On 10 October we wrote to Ministerial colleagues at the Departments for Communities, Economy, Finance and Infrastructure asking them to consider the recommendations of the High Street Task Force report and determine the way forward, bringing matters to the Executive for consideration as necessary.Responses have been received from all four departments, outlining how they would take forward recommendations that fell within their responsibilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the total cost of Northern Ireland Civil Service away days in each of last five years.
answer_text: The Department of Finance does not hold this information for other departments. The table below details the total amount spent in each of the last five years for away days which have involved activities such as business planning, workshops, staff conferences, training, Continuing Professional Development or Continuing Professional Education.These costs also include an employer supported volunteering package from Business in the Community (BITC) which provides the Department of Finance with a programme of five corporate team volunteering events for staff to make a difference with local charities / in the local community as well as access to a large number of other volunteering opportunities throughout the year.Financial YearAmount spent on away days (£)2019/2011,019.852020/212,500.002021/224,200.002022/238,842.872023/2411,454.51Total38,017.23

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the Housing Executive has any plans to reform its tenancy succession policy to allow two successions within the same family.
answer_text: The one succession limit is a statutory provision contained with the 1983 and 2003 Housing (NI) Orders. However, it is possible for two successions to occur within the same family where the second succession is a policy succession, and the applicant meets the criteria as set out under Rules 74 and 75 of the Housing Selection Scheme.Any change to the Housing (NI) Orders would require legislative change and I have no plans to make any changes relating to successions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any action being taken to reduce long-term sick absence in the Northern Ireland Civil Service.
answer_text: ANSWERI recognise the Civil Service, like other sectors, has been experiencing an upward trend in sickness absence levels. The NICS People Plan 2024-25 developed by NICSHR (which sits within my department) in conjunction with all departments, detailed the NICS commitment to provide the tools and interventions required to support the health and wellbeing of all staff and assist line managers to effectively manage sickness absence.One of the key outworkings of this is the NICS Health and Wellbeing Strategy and Action Plan, launched in January 2025, which will enable us to develop targeted interventions at both a NICS-wide and departmental level. In all departments line managers are responsible for managing sickness absence in line with the relevant HR policies and procedures. NICSHR also has a key role, supporting line managers and ensuring cases are progressed in a timely and supportive manner. To help line managers to support their teams before during and after a period of sickness NICSHR have taken forward a number of measures recently which will lead to improved outcomes for both the individual and the organisation, including: • The development of a Sickness Absence Toolkit to build line manager skills and knowledge when managing sickness • Updated, mandatory sickness absence training for all new line managers • The development of a new Line Manager Brochure to assist line managers in a range of functions, including the management of staff health and wellbeing. Simultaneously, NICSHR are commencing work to better understand the underlying reasons for sickness absence. This includes a closer analysis of trends evidenced from NISRA reports. NICSHR has also recently developed Management Information that includes the availability of live sickness data, allowing NICSHR to react more quickly to growing trends and ensure relevant business areas are provided with timely HR support and intervention. In addition, in October 2024 NICSHR established the NICS Mental Health Forum as a mechanism for departments to identify and share good practice and develop collaborative approaches to support staff. Work is also underway to strengthen and expand the services provided by the NICS Occupational Health Service to support a more proactive approach to sickness absence management by providing early intervention, holistic care, and tailored support to employees. Officials have also taken the lead in establishing a public sector Occupational Health (OH) Forum, to develop a system-wide collaborative approach to the transformation of OH services across the public sector here, to deliver value and outcomes that would be difficult or impossible to achieve individually. The NICS will continue to work jointly with colleagues, Trade Unions and other partners to support people to stay in work and return to work at the earliest opportunity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he plans to ban or reform leasehold flats, as the UK Government has announced its intention to do in England and Wales.
answer_text: ANSWERI have noted that the British Government has produced a White Paper on commonhold. My officials will consider that document along with other material in their ongoing scoping exercise into shared property, but we must be careful not to import solutions here to problems that have arisen in another jurisdiction. I understand that the leasehold system for flats in England and Wales has thrown up problems which do not exist to the same or to any extent here. For example, leases here typically tend to be for longer terms, often 999 years, whereas in England and Wales, many problems have arisen due to the much shorter nature of leases, often 125 years. Also in England, the ownership of common areas and structures of apartment buildings tends to remain with the landlord, with individual flat owners having no ownership rights. This is not the case here because common areas of such buildings are owned by the flat owners through the management company structure. In addition, our system of land law is markedly different from that in England and Wales. Broader changes to it would be a very large task given the nature of our system. It is not a current priority for my Department and would require a fully funded team with expertise to take forward.It is interesting to note that the voluntary commonhold scheme introduced in England and Wales in 2004 had a very low uptake. Likewise, the Northern Ireland Law Commission Report on Apartments of May 2013 noted that the overwhelming majority of respondents were not in favour of the introduction of a system akin to commonhold in this jurisdiction.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department is taking to support people with a musculoskeletal condition back into work, including whether they plan to use chiropractors to help people return to work.
answer_text: My Department funds the Condition Management Programme (CMP) which is a voluntary short-term wrap-around, multi-disciplinary healthcare provision that exists in Northern Ireland which provides the support to enable people with health conditions, including musculoskeletal conditions, progress towards, move into and remain in employment. The programme is delivered on behalf of my Department by the five Health and Social Care Trusts in Northern Ireland and facilitated by Health Care Professionals, such as Physiotherapists, Occupational Therapists and Mental Health Nurses. The CMP aims to improve self-efficacy in relation to participants’ skills and abilities, give strategies and supports to help them in managing their health condition(s) and to support the participant to remain in, return to or progress towards work.Chiropractic is not delivered by the CMP Health Care Professionals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of (i) the impact of Storm Éowyn on forests owned or managed by Forest Service; and (ii) their management of trees under their remit.
answer_text: Many of my Department’s forests have suffered significant damage from Storm Éowyn and the impacts are widespread across the Forest Service estate. The assessment of the full extent of damage is ongoing, however I can advise that the following forests have been particularly affected; Drum Manor, Gosford, Tardree, Ballysallagh, Mount Stewart, Castlewellan, Grange Park, Favour Royal, Mullaghafad, Lack and Kesh. Plans for required interventions following the storm are well advanced. Priority clearances associated with public roads, utility infrastructure, residential property and business accesses are now completed.  The focus is now on harvesting and re-establishing the storm affected areas including substitution of previously planned timber sales with priority harvesting due to the storm. I am advised that clearance of the wind damaged areas is expected to continue over the months and forthcoming year.   Forest Service manages forests in line with its duties under the Forestry Act (NI) 2010, and the UK Forestry Standards, which govern sustainable forestry management.  This management contributes to protecting the environment and climate change mitigation as well as providing other ecosystem services, including producing timber and promoting public access for health, wellbeing and recreational benefits.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has any plans to introduce a new levy on out of town shopping centres.
answer_text: The argument that out-of-town retailers “pay less” business rates was specific to a particular point in time, following a cancelled rates revaluation in 2009 due to the economic downturn. The assertion is no longer true when describing our rating system in 2025, following revaluation exercises in 2015, 2020 and 2023. With a fresh revaluation underway for 2026 our rating valuations here are updated more frequently than any other part of these islands.  Out of town retailers, and in-town retailers, pay in line with those frequently assessed rental values. Large retailers, many of which are located out of town, are a core part of the 25% of non-domestic ratepayers who do not receive any form of rate support. That is not to say that those retailers shouldn’t be paying rates at that level. I therefore have no plans to introduce a new levy on out of town shopping centres.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration being given to expanding the list of allied health professionals to include chiropractors.
answer_text: There are no plans to expand the list of allied health professionals to include chiropractors.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department's progress on providing the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service with a fire and rescue framework.
answer_text: Following publication of the Programme for Government in March 2025 work has started on the development of a fire and rescue framework, with a view to publication by the end of this calendar year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the last time the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service was inspected by His Majesty’s Inspectorate; and (ii) when the next inspection is due.
answer_text: (i) A Department of Health commissioned Inspection of NIFRS was undertaken by His Majesty’s Fire Service Inspectorate (Scotland) between June 2022 and January 2023, with the report published in September 2023.(ii) No date for a further inspection has been set.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the numbers reported on his Department’s sealing document SD1 in relation to the Seeds Act (Northern Ireland) 1965 that have the destination recorded as Great Britain in each of the last five years, broken down by (i) classification; and (ii) tonnage.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of whether the current law is adequate to tackle attacks on emergency services' personnel and equipment.
answer_text: Existing specific assault offences, which are designed to protect emergency services’ personnel, include assault on police, assault on fire and rescue personnel and assault on ambulance workers.  In addition, and particularly for more serious attacks, the range of offences designed to protect all members of the public apply. These include assault occasioning actual bodily harm, grievous bodily harm, manslaughter, attempted murder and murder.  Attacks on equipment can be dealt with under the offence of criminal damage. In light of the apparent increase in levels of unwarranted and unacceptable aggression being displayed across the board towards those whose work brings them into contact with the public, I intend to introduce a new offence of assaulting a person engaged in providing services to the public, performing a public duty or providing a public service within my Sentencing Bill. The offence will come with a higher maximum penalty in the magistrates’ courts than that available for the existing equivalent offences, and, for more serious assaults, the fact that the victim is such a person will become a statutory aggravating factor. Executive agreement to commence drafting the Sentencing Bill has been granted and I aim to introduce the Bill by the end of this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to improve musculoskeletal health, including how it will include chiropractors.
answer_text: Chiropractic services are not commissioned in Northern Ireland. Currently there are no plans to do so. The budgetary pressures facing the Department means priority is given to current commissioned services. Improving musculoskeletal health is a priority for my department. My predecessor commissioned my Chief Allied Health Professions Officer to work with the Advisory Committee of Allied Health Professionals to develop a rehabilitation framework for Northern Ireland and I have approved the focus of this framework. The framework will guide the strategic development of rehabilitation services across HSCNI. Musculoskeletal health is a key consideration of the framework. My department has worked collaboratively with key stakeholders including Versus Arthritis, the Community Rehabilitation Alliance and advanced practice musculoskeletal clinicians in the production of a draft framework. To ensure real co-production in the development of this framework, two events are planned for April 2025, where my officials will be engaging with service users, their families and carers as well as those delivering or responsible for the planning or commissioning of services, on the draft ensuring the framework is cognisant of lived experience and can deliver for those in need. It is expected that the rehabilitation framework will be launched in early autumn 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in light of funding provided by his Department to Stonewall for services and advice, to outline any advice received from Stonewall that has been implemented by his Department.
answer_text: The Civil Service here is an equal opportunities employer, committed to building a diverse and inclusive workforce that reflects the society we serve. Stonewall is one of several organisations the Civil Service engages with to support our wide-ranging work on equality, diversity and inclusion. Annual Civil Service membership of the Stonewall Diversity Champions Programme is £2,575 and provides access to training and resources to support the creation of LGBTQ+ inclusive working environments.  When developing Civil Service inclusion interventions, a wide range of information from multiple sources is considered including learning from training; statistics; benchmarking with other organisations; best practice research; and the lived experience of the Civil Service LGBTQ+ Staff Network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any support available to assist those with disabilities to access sport.
answer_text: ‘Active Living’, the Sport and Physical Activity Strategy for Northern Ireland, was launched by my Department in March 2022.  A key theme of the strategy is promoting participation, inclusion and community engagement in sport and physical activity with a particular focus on currently under-represented groups, which includes people with a disability.My Department, through our Arm’s Length Body Sport NI, provide Exchequer funding to Disability Sport NI to increase sport and physical activity opportunities for people in Northern Ireland with a disability. Since 2020 Exchequer funding of £2.19m has been awarded to Disability Sport NI.In addition, my Department participates in the Active Living: No Limits (ALNL) Forum, which is an initiative aimed at improving the health and well-being of people with a disability in Northern Ireland through participation in sport and physical activity. Last year the ALNL Forum launched their website, https://activelivingnolimits.co.uk/ which provides a platform to empower disabled people to explore opportunities that will allow them to become more active in a way that suits them best.  It also aims to provide a central hub where health professionals, district councils, national governing bodies of sport, as well as local clubs and groups can promote their regular inclusive and disability specific sport and active recreation opportunities.	As this issue is also an operational matter for Sport NI, I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has provided me with a response.Due to the volume of information to be provided I have arranged for the detail to be placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are any plans to introduce similar legislation to the Party Wall etc. Act 1996.
answer_text: I do not have any plans currently to introduce legislation similar to the Party Wall etc. Act 1996.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any financial support his Department is providing for poultry farmers who have been affected by the recent avian influenza outbreak.
answer_text: Compensation is payable for birds culled at the Department’s direction for disease control purposes. There is no financial support for consequential losses. While I acknowledge that the impact on the farm is much wider than this however, I have to act within the current legal and budgetary framework. I am therefore grateful to industry for the ongoing efforts to protect flocks from avian influenza and would reiterate the need for all bird keepers to practice the highest levels of biosecurity.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any discussions she has had with the taxi industry to increase taxi availability in towns and cities.
answer_text: My department is responsible for the regulation of the taxi industry in relation to taxi driver, operator and vehicle licencing.  The recruitment of new and retention of existing drivers is the responsibility of the taxi industry.I am aware though of the current issues facing the taxi industry and I have agreed to meet with some of their representatives on 28th May 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the roll-out of the My Care patient portal.
answer_text: Some 95,000 Health and Social Care Northern Ireland service users have signed up with the My Care patient portal, following its initial soft launch in alignment with the Go-Live in South Eastern Trust in November 2023, followed by Belfast and Northern Trusts in 2024. The number is expected to increase upon completion of the last two encompass Go-Lives in May 2025.My Care allows service users to access information such as appointments, letters, supporting information, laboratory & radiology results and care progress updates. It is free to use, and it is optional. Service users can continue interacting with healthcare services via traditional methods. Functionality will continue to be expanded across the lifetime of encompass.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the progress on the 14 high level actions identified in the Northern Ireland Rare Diseases Action Plan.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Rare Diseases Action Plan was published in March 2022, and it includes actions to be taken forward over a 5-year period. The Action Plan is being progressed by the Northern Ireland Rare Diseases Implementation Group (NIRDIG), chaired by Professor Ian Young, Chief Scientific Adviser for NI. Good progress is being made, and all the actions are at various stages of implementation, however full implementation within the 5-year timeframe is subject to available resource. The Action Plan and the Progress Report covering 2023-24 can be viewed at the following link:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-rare-diseases-action-plan-202223-and-reports

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any support her Department makes available for tackling dereliction in town centres.
answer_text: Support for tackling dereliction in town centres does not fall under the remit of my Department. Financial support  is however available from Councils e.g. Belfast City Council’s ‘Vacant to Vibrant’ grant scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions she has had with the tourism sector on raising public awareness abroad of the need for an electronic travel authorisation when entering Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My officials and colleagues from Tourism Ireland and Tourism NI have worked in collaboration with industry representatives to engage with the British Home Office to offer advice on the development of its Electronic Travel Authorisation (ETA) communications strategy, particular to Ireland travel. This close engagement has included several industry and stakeholder workshops and webinars to provide a comprehensive overview of the ETA Scheme. The webinars, aimed at stakeholders on Ireland, covered who the ETA applies to, when it comes into effect and how the Home Office is communicating this change internationally to highlight how ETA will affect visitors to Ireland. The most recent webinars were held on 11 and 18 February and were attended by over 400 representatives from tourism businesses, trade partners and tour/cruise/coach operators who welcome visitors from overseas to the North, whether they arrive direct or arrive in the South and cross the land border.  Tourism Ireland signposts ETA requirements on its suite of international websites and has shared comprehensive information about ETA with all key travel trade contacts in overseas markets, to ensure they are fully aware of the change and how to navigate it.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken by his Department to roll-out targeted lung cancer screening to tackle health inequalities linked to deprivation.
answer_text: The former Minister of Health, Robin Swann, agreed with the UK National Screening Committee’s recommendation to introduce a targeted lung cancer screening programme in asymptomatic people, with a history of smoking, aged between 55-74 years old. We are currently scoping out what the requirements of this new programme for Northern Ireland will be.  This will be a very significant service development encompassing new screening services and a range of additional diagnostic and treatment services. Its progress is dependent on budget and on workforce and will require input and collaboration from multiple partners.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any measures her Department has taken to inform the public of the transition from analogue to digital landlines.
answer_text: Telecommunications policy is a reserved matter, with the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT) holding responsibility. My officials maintain regular contact with DSIT on all telecommunications matters. The Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN) is the traditional copper-based analogue landline, which is being upgraded to deliver voice services over an internet connection via Voice over Internet Protocol (VoIP) technology. This is occasionally referred to as ‘digital phone’ or ‘digital voice’.  Although the migration to VoIP is an industry led initiative, DSIT, along with Ofcom, the communications regulator, is working with industry to ensure all sectors of the economy are prepared.  Ofcom has issued guidance on how to prepare for the migration, and has rules to protect customers during power cuts, and to require phone companies to provide access to emergency calls at all times.  More information is available at: www.ofcom.org.uk/phones-and-broadband/landline-phones/future-of-landline-calls/  Although my department is not responsible for the migration, my officials attend a DSIT led PSTN working group to ensure that all local issues are given consideration. At a recent meeting of this working group, officials were informed that a national public communications/ information exercise is being developed by both the service providers and the British Government which is due to be delivered later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to introduce mandatory suicide prevention training for frontline health and social care professionals.
answer_text: It is not my intention to make suicide prevention training mandatory for all public facing staff in all government bodies, but rather, to widely promote and encourage staff to undertake such training. Given the large number of people affected by suicide each year in Northern Ireland, caution is required about confronting staff with this information in the workplace, where they may not feel in a position to respond to the immediate emotional and psychological impact. The making of such sensitive training mandatory could be counter productive and may risk retraumatising some staff.There is an extensive programme of relevant training being delivered across the public sector. The PHA commission a broad portfolio of Mental Health and Wellbeing and Suicide Prevention training programmes across Northern Ireland, in line with the Protect Life 2 Strategy. Sessions aim to improve help seeking behaviour for individuals, help them support others in need and raise awareness of where help and support can be accessed.In Health and Social Care Trusts, the Towards Zero Suicide programme Patient Safety Collaborative, involves all five Trusts along with prisons and aims to research and adopt best practice in suicide prevention work across mental health services and the prison population. More than 10,000 HSC staff have been trained in online level 1 suicide prevention training, more than 700 practitioners have been trained in using evidence based safety planning interventions. There is also a significant programme of suicide prevention training being undertaken across government bodies.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any discussions they have had with the Home Office in relation to the introduction of the UK electronic travel authorisation scheme.
answer_text: The Economy Minister has led on engaging with Home Office Minister for Migration and Citizenship to find a solution to protect our local tourism sector.  Engagement with the Home Office is ongoing and we will continue to raise this matter during inter-governmental meetings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any discussions she has had with the PSNI in relation to the new enforcement of electronic travel authorisation requirements.
answer_text: I meet the Chief Constable on a regular basis to discuss a wide range of matters.  I can confirm that we have not had any discussions regarding the enforcement of electronic travel authorisation requirements.You may wish to contact the PSNI directly for further information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has given any consideration to providing a funding scheme for repair and maintenance of community-owned halls.
answer_text: I have advised officials of my intention to develop a scheme to provide funding for halls and facilities which deliver benefits to the wider community.I hope to make an announcement on this new programme in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail who the Director of Public Prosecutions is accountable to.
answer_text: The Public Prosecution Service does not fall within my scope of responsibility. The Public Prosecution Service (PPS) was established in June 2005 by the Justice (Northern Ireland) Act 2002.   Since the devolution of policing and justice in 2010, PPS has been a non-ministerial department with the role of Director of Public Prosecutions being independent. The Director is appointed by the Attorney General for NI; however, the Attorney’s role is one of consultation with the Director and not superintendence.  Funding of the PPS is direct from the Department of Finance, as with Executive departments. The Director sits on the Criminal Justice Board, as PPS is a key partner in the justice system. Given the independence of the Director of Public Prosecutions you may  wish to direct your question directly to him.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on discussions with NIE in connection to goodwill payments for customers affected by extended periods without electricity following Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: Discussions are ongoing and any proposals will be brought to the Executive for decision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the progress of the working group established following Storm Éowyn, consisting of the Department, Utility Regulator and NIE Networks; and (ii) any representations made on behalf of people who have suffered financial loss during Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: Discussions are ongoing and the Executive will be asked to make a decision when the options are finalised.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions she has held with holiday insurance companies in relation to the impact of the introduction of the UK electronic travel authorisation scheme.
answer_text: My Department and Tourism NI officials have been working alongside industry representatives and key stakeholders regarding the potential impact on the North’s tourism sector from the introduction of the British Government’s Electronic Travel Authorisation (ETA) Scheme. This has included close engagement and discussions with representative bodies including the Northern Ireland Tourism Alliance (NITA).  Insurance was an issue highlighted through these discussions requiring further investigation. In representing the wider sector, NITA has led on these enquiries particularly to ascertain the validity of any travel insurance if someone crosses the border from the South into the North without having an ETA and is involved in an accident.  NITA has raised this with insurance companies and has been advised that the status of an ETA would not be checked and not having one would not invalidate the insurance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions she has held with coach tour operators in relation to the impact of the introduction of the UK electronic travel authorisation scheme.
answer_text: The views and concerns of coach tour operators in relation to the impact of the British Government’s introduction of its Electronic Travel Authorisation (ETA) Scheme are well known. Close engagement with this sector has been ongoing through discussions which my predecessor and officials have had with representative bodies including the Northern Ireland Tourism Alliance (NITA) and the Inbound Tourism Operators Association (ITOA). These bodies include Bus and Coach NI and independent coach tour operators as members. I will also be meeting industry representatives on this matter in the coming weeks. I share the concerns of the tour operators. With 67% of our overseas holiday makers arriving via the South, many of which on bus and coach tours, seamless all-island travel is key to the success of our tourism sector. I am advised that the introduction of the ETA will result in the North of Ireland being excluded from the itinerary of many all-island tour operators with many citing concerns over future visitor numbers.  Tourism has grown substantially in recent years and there is considerable potential for further growth. The introduction of the ETA Scheme, however, threatens that progress.  That is why, on behalf of the industry, I will continue to lobby the British Government for a solution.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail his plans to support the high street and reduce costs for local independent traders.
answer_text: 70% of non-domestic ratepayers occupying property here get some form of rate support. 29,000 businesses from a taxbase of 75,000 receive a reduction of between 20% and 50% through Small Business Rate Relief (SBRR) scheme alone. The Back in Business scheme was also reinstated last year providing new businesses occupying long-term vacant retail units a 50% reduction in rates in the critical first two years of trading. A Strategic Review Cycle of rates support measures has now been commenced. Preparation is being made for reviews of SBRR and Non-Domestic Vacant Rating exclusions at the start of 2025/26. The process will build upon the evidence provided to me through the Cost of Doing Business research being undertaken now by Ulster University Economic Policy Centre. Ultimately though the rating system is a taxation regime, codified in statute. Changes to that system requires new legislation, an evidence base of need, and public consultation. The issues facing the High Street meanwhile remain multi-faceted, cutting across all Executive portfolios, and therefore it will be critical to look beyond the rating system to address these issues, including where wider intervention is to be deployed quickly to address specific and localised issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the funding provided by his Department to support boxing in the North Down constituency in each of the last five years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Sport NI.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Financial YearGrantee OrganisationTypeFundProgrammeAmount2020/21Bangor Abbey Boxing AcademyRevenueExchequerSports Hardship Fund£3,0002020/21North Down Boxing and Fitness ClubRevenueExchequerSports Hardship Fund£3,0002022/23North Down Boxing ClubRevenueLotteryActivate (Crowdfunder)£5,0002024/25North Down Boxing and Fitness ClubRevenueLotteryCreating Opportunities (Crowdfunder)£5,000”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact Sport NI directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.I can also advise that all funding information is available on the Government Funding Database. This website is publicly accessible and holds records of applications and funding to voluntary and community sector organisations including funding disbursed to the sports sector.  I have included the link for future convenience - https://govfunding.nics.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement her Department has had with the car insurance industry to reduce the cost of insurance for young drivers.
answer_text: I am aware that the cost of motor insurance is a concern for all our road users, particularly young drivers.My Department has no direct legislative influence on the prices set by insurance companies which are regulated by the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA).A Motor Insurance Taskforce was formed last year by the British Government and is investigating the causes of high premiums and how government can address them. Recognising the disproportionate impact high car insurance has on certain groups, including young drivers, the Taskforce will have an additional focus on those groups. The Taskforce is supported by motor insurance industry experts.My Department provides input to the Taskforce. The initial meeting of the Taskforce took place in November 2024. The then Minister of Finance, Dr Caoimhe Archibald MLA attended this taskforce on behalf of the North. Officials will continue to engage with the Taskforce in the coming months.

answer_text: The Department for Communities contributes grant funding to support community-based posts in the 2024-25 year in the North Down constituency through the Neighbourhood Renewal, Areas at Risk and Small Pockets of Deprivation programmes as per the table below: Funding StreamProject NameOrganisationNumber of Posts Supported24/25 Total Grant Funding Award Areas at RiskBeechfield AARArds Development Bureau & Community Network1£10,438.22Neighbourhood RenewalCommunity SafetyBangor NI Alternatives1£37,765.67Neighbourhood RenewalSTEP ProjectKilcooley Women’s Centre2£65,055.97Neighbourhood RenewalCommunity SupportKilcooley Community Forum2£50,968.47Small Pockets of Deprivation SPOD ProjectNorth Down YMCA2£58,300.90Small Pockets of Deprivation SPOD ProjectRathgill Community Association2£76,500.35In addition, my Department’s Voluntary and Community Division provides funding to three community organisations in the North Down Assembly constituency. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the review of pay and conditions for further education lecturers.
answer_text: The review of pay and conditions for further education lecturers was a condition of the agreement of the 2023/24 pay settlement between Trade Unions and Further Education Colleges as the employers.  This agreement was effected in April 2024.  Work is ongoing to ensure that effective collaborative working arrangements are in place across the six Further Education Colleges in order to progress this issue as a priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why Samaritans Northern Ireland have not secured funding from the Core Grant Funding Scheme 2025-26.
answer_text: When I announced my plan to redesign the Core Grant Scheme in partnership with the voluntary and community sector in July 2024, I made it clear that my intention was to address the fundamental unfairness in the previous Scheme which funded the same organisations for more than 20 years. I wanted the scheme to be open and transparent, and for as many organisations as possible to be able to bid for funding. Naturally, introducing competition meant that there would be winners and losers. The standard of applications was high, and the progression of the 25 highest scoring applications to stage 2 of the assessment process was based on agreed scoring criteria, rather than a departmental determination of the value of any of the 259 applicants. Those who have missed out this time will have a further opportunity later this year to bid for funding in 2026/27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are any plans to provide accessible pedestrian access from Sydenham train station to the George Best Belfast City Airport.
answer_text: My Department is committed to doing all that it can to ensure all public services are inclusive and accessible, especially for those with disabilities and young families. My officials have recently undertaken a study on the feasibility of providing DDA compliant ramps to facilitate improved access to/from Sydenham Halt. Translink is currently undertaking a feasibility study into improvement of connectivity between Belfast City Airport and the Railway. They are working closely with Belfast City Airport, my Departments Active Travel Team and Eastern Roads Division on this study, which is due to be completed in Spring 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the provision and location of hearing tests in North Down.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are plans to provide accessible pedestrian access to the Belfast bound platform at Helen’s Bay train station.
answer_text: I am fully committed to providing inclusive and accessible Public Transport and facilities for all customers. Unfortunately, because of the nature of legacy issues with railway infrastructure within the North it is not always possible to provide step free access to both platforms. The constraints of the site at Helens Bay, and problems caused by third party ownership of the surrounding land, mean that the Belfast bound platform at the station can only be accessed by stairs.  Translink has introduced a three-year programme to improve accessibility for users providing information on the website and journey planner to allow people to plan accessible journeys taking into account their needs.
