question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current figures for pandemic-induced backlogs in gynaecology care; and (ii) any resources being allocated to deal with these backlogs.
answer_text: Outpatient Waiting TimesThe number of patients waiting for a first consultant-led outpatient appointment under the Gynaecology specialty, by Health and Social Care Trust, is published on a quarterly basis in the ‘Northern Ireland Outpatient Waiting Time Statistics’ bulletin’s associated Excel file. The most recent issue of this bulletin has a reporting date of 30 September 2024 and is available to view at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-waiting-time-statistics-outpatient-waiting-times-september-2024 Inpatient Waiting TimesThe number of patients waiting for admission for inpatient or day case treatment under the Gynaecology, Gynaecological Oncology, and Gynaecology - Urology specialties, by Health and Social Care Trust, is published on a quarterly basis in the ‘Northern Ireland Inpatient And Day Case Waiting Time Statistics’ bulletin’s associated Excel file. The most recent issue of this bulletin has a reporting date of 30 September 2024 and is available to view at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-waiting-time-statistics-inpatient-and-day-case-waiting-times-september-2024These figures demonstrate the number of people waiting for outpatient assessment and also those awaiting treatments for gynae conditions.With regards to resources to deal with backlogs, £76m has been approved non-recurrently for waiting list initiatives (both independent sector and in-house) for 2024/25 to help reduce waiting times for patients requiring red flag and other time critical assessments and treatments across a range of specialties, including gynaecology.If the funding bid for £135M had been approved, then £1.4Million would have been assigned to address outpatient assessment for 4200 patients waiting the longest time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment on the progress that his Department has made towards meeting the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence guidelines for baseline access to endometriosis care.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Guidelines are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. While all NICE guidance is considered to be best practice in Northern Ireland, my Department has published circulars on four specific types of NICE guidance including: •	NICE Technology Appraisals (TA) •	NICE Clinical Guidelines (CG)•	NICE Interventional Procedures Guidance (IPG) •	NICE Public Health Guidance (PH). Each of these circulars sets out the roles and responsibilities for each HSC organisation in regard to the process for endorsement, implementation, monitoring and assurance.My Department established a regional Gynaecology Forum, following publication of the Getting It Right First Time Report, which provided a review of gynaecology services in Northern Ireland, including that suffering endometriosis.  This report details 20 recommendations to improve gynaecology services. My Department has established a forum with Trust, GP and PHA representation to oversee ongoing service improvement in this speciality and ensure that this improvement is implemented at a local and regional level.One of the regional actions was in respect to establishing a Task and Finish Group to develop and implement an approved pathway for endometriosis.  This group has recently been established and work is underway to commence this recommendation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures being taken by his Department to promote awareness of prostate specific antigen testing.
answer_text: There have been no specific measures to promote awareness of prostate specific antigen (PSA) testing. However, the Public Health Agency (PHA) regularly raises awareness of cancer symptoms and signs on non-paid marketing channels such as social media campaigns. The ‘Living Well,’ campaign programme will launch in over 500 NI Community Pharmacies in April/May 2025 with a focus on general awareness around the signs and symptoms of cancer. Members of the public who have concerns about signs and symptoms will be signposted to the ‘Be Cancer Aware,’ website for more information. This includes information on prostate cancer and personal testimonials. Anyone with symptoms which may be related to the prostate gland should contact their GP, who will arrange relevant tests, as appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps being taken to incorporate improved protections into employment law for women living with endometriosis.
answer_text: I would encourage all employers to offer enhanced flexible and supportive working arrangements for their staff living with endometriosis. Good employers have flexible supportive working policies in place such as homeworking, flexible hours, or compassionate leave. The Equality Commission for Northern Ireland publishes helpful guidance on the reasonable adjustment duty for employers. The Labour Relations Agency is also preparing draft guidance for employers regarding a range of fertility issues including conditions such as endometriosis. I intend to strengthen the rights to request flexible working through my planned Employment Rights Bill including increasing the number of permissible statutory flexible working requests within a rolling 12-month period and making the right available from the first day of employment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to improve the current waiting times of nine years and five months for an endometriosis diagnosis in Northern Ireland, with a view to reducing this to an average of less than one year by 2030.
answer_text: Following the recent publication of the Getting It Right First Time Report, which provided a review of gynaecology services in Northern Ireland and the 20 recommendations to improve gynaecology services, my Department has established a forum with Trust, GP and PHA representation to oversee ongoing service improvement in this speciality and ensure that this improvement is implemented at a local and regional level.Trusts have been asked to establish their own action plan to take forward recommendations and improvements which are relevant to their Trust. This should help to address the long waiting lists in each Trust area.One of the regional actions was in respect to establishing a Task and Finish Group to develop and implement an approved pathway for endometriosis.  This group has recently been established and work is underway to commence this recommendation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of routine abdominal aortic aneurysm screenings that have been carried out on men aged 65 and over in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency (PHA) has responsibility for commissioning, coordinating and quality assuring population screening programmes in Northern Ireland, which includes screening for Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm (AAA). The Northern Ireland AAA Screening Programme offers AAA screening to all men who are registered with a GP in the year they turn 65 (1 April to 31 March). Men over the age of 65, who have not previously been scanned as part of the programme or been told they have an aneurysm can self-refer to the programme. The PHA have provided information in the table below, which sets out the number of routine screenings for AAA that have taken place in each of the last five years.NUMBER OF MEN SCREENEDCOHORT19/2020/2121/2222/2323/24BELFAST1,2931,4411,6211,9081,760NORTHERN1,5742,1682,0002,1342,327SOUTH EASTERN1,3861,8371,5951,7361,700SOUTHERN1,3601,5921,5111,5931,690WESTERN1,0991,4781,3531,4121,452NORTHERN IRELAND6,7128,5168,0808,783P8,929PFigures for 19/20 and 20/21 have been taken from the Director of Public Health Core Tables - Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm screening uptake rates. Uptake is the proportion of men offered screening who are tested.  As part of the HSC response to the COVID-19 pandemic, all NI AAA Screening Programme invitations to routine screening and surveillance screening appointments were temporarily paused from Monday 23rd March 2020. Initial AAA screening did not resume until Monday 1st March 2021 meaning initial screening invitations were paused for 50 weeks. The number is slightly lower because over 600 appointments had to be cancelled in March 2020. These men were offered a second appointment when the programme restarted in March 2021. 1900 men that had DNA’d (did not attend) their first appointment and had not been offered their second timed appointment. These men were sent a second open invitation letter when the programme restarted. This is the number of men within the 20/21 cohort (men that turned 65 between 1st April 2020 to 31st March 2021) that have had an initial screening test. Initial AAA screening was paused for 50 weeks in 2020/21 due to the COVID-19 pandemic; this cohort were subsequently invited during 2020/21, 2021/22 and 2022/23 This is the number of men within the 21/22 cohort (men that turned 65 between 1st April 2021 to 31st March 2022) that have had an initial screening test. Initial AAA screening was paused for 50 weeks in 2020/21 due to the COVID-19 pandemic; the 21/22 cohort were subsequently invited during, 2021/22 and 2022/23Figures for 21/22 to 23/24 were downloaded from the AAA SMaRT system 18/02/2025 and so will vary slightly from those shared previously as this is a live database and men continue to be able to be screened outside of their screening year. HSC Trust is allocated based on the postcode of the GP Practice at which the man is registered.P Data for 22/23 and 23/24 is provisional and publication has been delayed due to the pause to the programme and the focus on the recovery of the progra

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail all Communities in Transition projects funded in North Down.
answer_text: Communities in Transition (CIT) operates across eight designated areas. The North Down CIT area covers Kilcooley and Rathgill. £1,757,961 has been allocated to projects in the North Down CIT area covering the period up to 31 March 2025. The following projects are being delivered for the period April 2024 – March 2025: Find Help NI - Health & Wellbeing Programme£88,529.70NI Alternatives Ltd – Community Safety and Policing£101,826.00NI Alternatives Ltd – Youth Programme£99,597.50In addition to projects specific to North Down CIT area, the area has received support from two of CIT’s regional projects: Ex-Prisoner Support and Advocacy; and Regional Restorative Practice.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, given that Communities in Transition had previously identified North Down (Rathgill and Kilcooley) as one of the eight areas of focus for targeted support, to detail why no areas in North Down were included in Phase Three of the Developing Women in the Community Programme on behalf of the Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime.
answer_text: Both ‘Communities in Transition’ (run by TEO) and ‘Developing Women in the Community’ (run by DfC) are projects that are part of a bigger cross-Executive initiative – the Executive Programme on Paramilitarism & Organised Crime (EPPOC). The Programme supports a mix of projects – some are place-based while others apply across Northern Ireland. Some are focused on particular demographics, for instance young people, women or those in the criminal justice system. All projects are designed to test ‘what works’ and, in line with a review of the Programme in 2020, should be evidence-based and data-driven. EPPOC adopts a portfolio investment approach, meaning that it uses a combination of different types of projects in different areas to achieve shared, measurable outcomes and to aim to ensure the best possible spread of investment. This means that projects, and investment, should evolve to take account of learning. Investment is meant to provide additionality rather than duplicate or backfill mainstream services or core funding. Programme investment is limited, meaning that it is not possible to cover every area or issue.Communities in Transition (CIT) has been in operation since 2018. CIT projects deliver in eight areas where there has traditionally been a high prevalence of paramilitary harm. These areas were identified and agreed in Phase 1 of the Programme (2016-2020). Data shows that these areas continue to experience paramilitary harm. CIT supports a number of outcomes that focus on increased community resilience.DfC has been operating community-based projects for EPPOC since 2017. These have gone through a number of iterations, drawing upon learning and evidence. The current DfC-led Developing Women in the Community Programme was first piloted in 2021. The target delivery areas for Phase 3 have been identified as the areas of most need – they reflect data (police and EPPOC project data) which points to high levels of paramilitary activity and harm. Some DfC areas overlap with CIT areas but some do not. This helps to ensure coverage is as wide as possible within the constraints of a limited budget. The target areas for Phase 3 are listed below: these are the same areas identified for Phase Two. North Down was identified as a target area for the pilot, Phase 2 and Phase 3. North Down also benefits from other EPPOC investment as well as wider statutory funding to address harm caused by paramilitarism. EPPOC has engaged extensively with both the PCSP and Council to support their efforts to address paramilitary harm in North Down.  Local Council AreaDEAAntrim & NewtownabbeyMacedonArds & North DownNewtownardsBelfastOldparkCourtBlack MountainTitanicCastleCollinCauseway Coast & GlensColeraineBallymoneyCausewayDerry & StrabaneThe MoorBallyarnettFoylesideWatersideSperrinMid & East AntrimKnockagh

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the criteria used to determine selection areas for Communities in Transition focused support.
answer_text: Communities in Transition is part of the Executive’s Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime, and since 2019 has delivered over £26 million of support across eight areas where there has been a significant history of paramilitary activity and coercive control.The CIT areas were identified by independent research commissioned by the Tackling Paramilitarism Programme Board and completed in 2017. Researchers identified areas that were at particular risk from on-going paramilitary activity by analysing a range of data sources including the media, data sets, academic literature, research reports and stakeholder meetings.Areas were scored against a number of indicators of paramilitary activity including verified and claimed incidents of paramilitary intimidation and paramilitary assaults and shootings.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail how the targeted delivery areas for Phase Three of the Developing Women in the Community Programme on behalf of the Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime were identified.
answer_text: ‘Developing Women in the Community’ (administered by the Department for Communities) is part of the NI Executive’s cross-departmental Programme on Paramilitarism & Organised Crime (EPPOC). The EPPOC Programme supports a range of projects using a public health approach.  In practice, this means directing limited EPPOC Programme resource where it will be most impactful, based on relevant information, data and evidence.  It also means that projects test and report on ‘what works’: Developing Women in the Community has had two independent evaluations carried out by Queens University Belfast (published on the Programme website www.endingtheharm.com). EPPOC adopts a portfolio investment approach, meaning that it uses a combination of different types of projects in different areas to achieve shared, measurable outcomes and aims to ensure the best possible spread of investment. This means that projects, and investment, should evolve to take account of learning. Investment is meant to provide additionality rather than to duplicate or backfill mainstream services or core funding. The target areas for Phase Three of this project were identified using the same methodology as the first two Phases.    It involves analysing a range of data from various sources (including PSNI and EPPOC project data) to identify the areas of most need, looking at prevalence based on incidents of paramilitary harm per ten thousand of population.  The areas identified for Phase Three were the same as those identified for Phase Two. We will continue to work closely with our partners to ensure that Programme investment reaches the areas of most need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given ongoing budget pressures in his Department, for his assessment of the action by Belfast Health and Social Care Trust to provide schools with substitute teacher cover to allow teachers to attend the RSE Ready? teachers’ professional learning programme.
answer_text: The practice of offering support to cover teachers to attend RSE Ready? Teachers’ Professional Learning programmes has been a longstanding support initiated to afford teachers and schools the opportunity to prioritise upskilling of staff around relationships and sexuality education, whilst not impacting on pupil learning objectives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps being taken by his Department to reduce waiting times for patients to see a consultant in relation to eating disorders.
answer_text: Based on available data, waiting times within both adult and youth eating disorder services are within the recommended timeframes as defined by the You in Mind Regional Care Pathway for the Treatment of Eating Disorders (2016).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans he has to improve resource allocation for the regional eating disorders service, in line with Action 29.d of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031.
answer_text: The Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 includes a commitment to enhance regional eating disorder services, and in line with the Mental Health Strategy Action 29 d, a Regional Clinical Lead and Manager for the Regional Eating Disorder Managed Care Network were appointed in late 2024. They are in the planning phase of establishing the Network and a business case to improve resource allocation, is in its final stages. A precise timescale for further implementation of Action 29d has not been determined, as it will depend upon the availability of funding. The Business Case related to Action 29d proposes to enhance the current community-based, evidence-based treatment model in line with National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidance; thereby increasing regional availability and accessibility of intensive community and day treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide a timescale for the implementation of Action 29.d of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031.
answer_text: The Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 includes a commitment to enhance regional eating disorder services, and in line with the Mental Health Strategy Action 29 d, a Regional Clinical Lead and Manager for the Regional Eating Disorder Managed Care Network were appointed in late 2024. They are in the planning phase of establishing the Network and a business case to improve resource allocation, is in its final stages. A precise timescale for further implementation of Action 29d has not been determined, as it will depend upon the availability of funding. The Business Case related to Action 29d proposes to enhance the current community-based, evidence-based treatment model in line with National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidance; thereby increasing regional availability and accessibility of intensive community and day treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the viability of intensive community and day treatments as an alternative to inpatient care for eating disorders; and (ii) whether he has any plans to expand the roll-out of these treatments across Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: The Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 includes a commitment to enhance regional eating disorder services, and in line with the Mental Health Strategy Action 29 d, a Regional Clinical Lead and Manager for the Regional Eating Disorder Managed Care Network were appointed in late 2024. They are in the planning phase of establishing the Network and a business case to improve resource allocation, is in its final stages. A precise timescale for further implementation of Action 29d has not been determined, as it will depend upon the availability of funding. The Business Case related to Action 29d proposes to enhance the current community-based, evidence-based treatment model in line with National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidance; thereby increasing regional availability and accessibility of intensive community and day treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust’s RSE Ready? teachers’ professional learning programme, to detail (i) who created the course; (ii) what other organisations or individuals were involved; (iii) who quality assured and approved the course; and (iv) why schools were not provided with detail of the course content with their invite.
answer_text: The Belfast Trust Sexual Health Training Team has had a responsibility to support and develop teacher competence and confidence in the delivery of RSE since 2002. The current Sexual Health Lead has had responsibility for this programme since 2018.  The course was developed through a collaborative partnership approach. Relevant stakeholder groups were involved in initial planning and development. Following this, teachers became the primary stakeholder group engaged with, supported by regular meetings with the Curriculum Development Team at CCEA, including participation and delivery within the RSE Stakeholder Forum hosted by CCEA.The programme is continuously quality assured against:Relationship and Sexual Education Training Programme for Post-Primary Schools in Northern Ireland: review of current programme status and exploration of approaches/ models for future programme development’ (July 2016)CCEA RSE Guidance for Post Primary Schools (2015) CCEA RSE Progression Framework (2024) CCMS RSE Guidance Doc for Post Primary Schools NI (2021)The programme is also informed through course participant evaluations, training needs assessment and regular content review. As and where appropriate, content is revised or updated in accordance with new guidance, data and emerging issues.The invitation letter to the programme advised invitees that the programme is designed to provide a range of RSE support for teachers who are actively engaged in the planning, co-ordination and/or delivery of RSE within Post Primary Schools, and set out the aim of the programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the tonnage of seed potatoes that Northern Ireland exported to Scotland in the last five years, broken down by seed classification.
answer_text: Following the coming into force of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the movement of seeds from Northern Ireland to Scotland no longer sits within my ministerial responsibilities and now comes under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the tonnage of seed potatoes that came into Northern Ireland from Scotland in the last five years, broken down by seed classification.
answer_text: Following the coming into force of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the movement of seeds into Northern Ireland from Scotland no longer sits within my ministerial responsibilities and now comes under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, given the poor state of the athletics track and field facility at Bangor Sportsplex, to detail any engagement his Department has had with (i) Ards and North Down Borough Council; and (ii) North Down Athletics Club in relation to a new track and field facility in North Down.
answer_text: I understand that Bangor Sportsplex is owned by Ards and North Down Borough Council, but I am not aware of either the Council nor North Down Athletics Club having made any approach to me or my Department specifically regarding track and field facilities in North Down.However, the Chief Executive of Sport NI has advised me of the following:-“The infrastructure team have engaged with Ards and North Down Borough Council over recent years, most notably in consultation on their new Leisure Strategy in August 2024 regarding their sports and recreation provision.“Sport NI provided a letter of support in February 2023 for a new cycling hub at Bangor Sportsplex, a project which has the support of Ards and North Down Borough Council.  The proposal seeks to provide a range of cycling facilities on the site, including a pump track, BMX track, mountain bike trail, and cycling skills area.  The project proposal will not impact the ‘athletics track’, but the infield area of the track, traditionally used for athletics field disciplines, and which also doubles up as a football pitch would be lost and replaced with a BMX track.   “In our letter of support, we strongly encouraged those involved with the proposal to consult with Athletics NI and local clubs to ensure that the proposed development is not to the detriment of athletics disciplines in Ards & North Down.  “Sport NI have not had any direct correspondence or discussions with North Down Athletics Club regarding Track & Field facilities in Ards & North Down.”At this time neither my Department nor Sport NI offers any capital works funding programmes to which either the Council or the North Down Athletics Club could apply.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the location of any social housing schemes in North Down that are (i) planned; and (ii) currently under construction.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:(i) “There are 81 social housing units programmed to start as part of the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) 2024/25 – 2026/27. Details attached in the table belowSocial housing units Programmed to Start in the North Down Parliamentary Constituency 2024/25 – 2026/27Housing AssociationScheme NameLocation TypeStrategic GroupUnitsOnsite YearCompletionYearChoiceWorcester AvenueBangorNew BuildUrban492025/262028/29ClanmilBangor Marina RegenerationBangorNew BuildUrban322025/262027/28(ii) There are currently 60 social housing units under construction. Details attached in table below.Social housing units under construction in the North Down Parliamentary ConstituencyHousing AssociationScheme NameLocation TypeStrategic GroupUnitsOnsite YearCompletionYearClanmilSavoy Redevelopment (Blocks B & C)BangorNew BuildUrban262021/222024/25WovenBalmoral Dale Phase 2BangorSocial Housing via Planning GainUrban172023/242024/25ArkDonaghadee RoadBangorCompetitive Land Design & BuildUrban82022/232025/26Ark12 Ballywalter RoadMillisleCompetitive Land Design & BuildRural92023/242025/26Please note that programmed schemes can be lost or slip to future programme years for a variety of reasons e.g. relating to delays in acquiring sites and/or failure to secure Planning Approval. Additional schemes can also be added to the SHDP ‘in-year’ through Competitive Land, Design & Build procurement route and/or the purchase of Existing Satisfactory / Off-the-Shelf properties.The SHDP 2025/26 – 2027/28 has been approved by the Housing Executive Board and has been submitted to the Department for Communities for approval. Schemes may be added/reprofiled for the SHDP in the North Down Parliamentary Constituency, subject to Departmental Approval”.  For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she is giving any consideration to increasing the intervention level for repairing potholes.
answer_text: My Department’s resource budget, which is used to deliver day-to-day maintenance activities such as patching and grass cutting, has been underfunded due to over a decade of austerity by the British Government.. As a result, my Department has had to reduce its routine road maintenance activities to a limited service, which prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety and protecting the transport network. Consequently, only the highest priority defects across the network are currently being repaired.The level of repairs that can be carried out in future financial years will depend on the 2025-26 final budget determination. Until such times as sufficient funding is made available, it will not be possible to change the current level of service. The importance of a well maintained road network cannot be understated but this requires investment. I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether there are any plans to relocate the Bangor Jobs and Benefits Office.
answer_text: There are no current plans to relocate the Bangor Jobs and Benefits office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether there are any plans to relocate the Bangor Housing Executive office.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows: “Through the Customer Outlet and Workspace Strategy, the Housing Executive is reviewing all accommodation requirements over a five-year period (23/24 – 28/29). Through this strategy the Housing Executive have determined that an office is still required in Bangor in order to meet the needs of its tenants. The Housing Executive are currently exploring options for office accommodation within the city centre.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she is giving any consideration to increasing the frequency of road gully cleaning, given ongoing flooding issues on local roads.
answer_text: My Department’s resource budget, which is used to deliver day-to-day maintenance activities such as gully cleaning, has been under pressure for the last ten years. As a result, my Department has had to reduce its routine road maintenance activities to a limited service, which prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety and protecting the transport network.My Department currently aims to inspect and carry out one clean, where necessary, on all gullies annually. The level of service that can be carried out in future financial years will depend on the 2025-26 final budget determination. Until such times as sufficient funding is made available, it will not be possible to change the current frequency of road gully cleaning. However, additional funds secured by my Department through the October Monitoring Round has been used to fund a £1m targeted intervention for gully cleaning and drainage maintenance by means of the cleaning and desilting of lines and manholes.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in relation to the Investment Strategy for Northern Ireland and improving connectivity on strategic corridors, to detail any engagement they have had with HM Government regarding the price of transport links between Northern Ireland and Great Britain.
answer_text: The draft Investment Strategy identifies priorities in respect of improving strategic transport corridors and recognises the opportunity to improve connectivity to our ports and airports in the long term. It takes account of the findings of the All-Island Strategic Rail Review and the Union Connectivity Review.  The price of onward links to Great Britain is beyond the scope of the Investment Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether his Department has adopted Colostomy UK stoma-friendly accessible toilets across departmental office estates.
answer_text: My Department’s Properties Division is responsible for the Civil Service Office Estate, providing office accommodation to departments and dealing with any building fabric issues that arise. The majority of our offices have an accessible toilet provided for use by individuals with a disability.  Responsibility for commissioning refurbishments of this kind in individual buildings resides with the respective department’s premises teams. Properties Division wrote to Departmental Accommodation Representatives on 28 June 2024, providing links to the latest guidance on the Colostomy UK Website, attaching technical guidance and advising that the following essential features should be in place in every toilet: a hook on the door, shelf space, a mirror and a disposal bin. I have asked for a review of the buildings where DoF has premises responsibility to ensure these toilets are stoma-friendly and for the guidance to be reissued to all departmental accommodation teams.  Properties Division is also responsible for the grounds of the Stormont Estate and all accessible public toilets on the Estate, including the Changing Place Toilet, are stoma friendly. Full length mirrors, hanging hooks and a shelf are available, and laminate signs and stickers provided by Colostomy UK have been installed.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether it has adopted Colostomy UK stoma-friendly accessible toilets in Parliament Buildings.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission is very aware of the need to ensure that Parliament Buildings is accessible for everyone and endeavours to be proactive in terms of accessibility adaptations. As such I can confirm that all of our disabled toilets are equipped with colostomy bag shelves to ensure comfort and dignity for individuals and we strive to make our spaces welcoming and accommodating for everyone.The Assembly Commission will continue to work closely with local organisations and give due consideration to opportunities to improve the facilities in Parliament Buildings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when his Department will progress legislation to (i) enable the Charity Commission to clarify the financial and reporting obligations for Section 167 charities registered elsewhere on these islands; and (ii) remove barriers to local funding for these charities.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my answer to AQW 20814/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when an adult ADHD specialist consultant will be in place in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: As many Members will know, there is currently no commissioned adult ADHD service in Northern Ireland. There is some provision available across the Health and Social Care system, but I recognise that provision is limited and waiting times are far too excessive.As the adult ADHD service is not a commissioned service, and is without funding, the Trust have confirmed the recruitment of a consultant cannot presently move forward.However, all Trusts are committed to work closely and collaboratively with commissioners to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based services that can meet the needs of adults with ADHD within Trust areas. My Department has commissioned a research exercise into the current and projected demand with a view to bringing forward recommendations to inform a commissioned ADHD service, subject to future budget availability. The exercise will (amongst other things) seek to establish the prevalence of ADHD (for both children and adults) in Northern Ireland, examine best practice from other jurisdictions and identify challenges and barriers in relation to ADHD diagnosis and treatment. Importantly, it will be expected to make recommendations on what any future commissioned service might look like in terms of, for example, costs, staffing profiles and recommended care pathways.An anticipated completion date is set for end June 2025.Whilst Members understandably call for greater services, there needs to be a recognition that new services will inevitably require resource. Unfortunately, such recognition has been limited in recent times in this chamber. Therefore, I would also welcome your support in the coming months as I make the case for an allocation that would allow me to protect, maintain and commission important services

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on when the digital platform for Neighbourhood Renewal funding will be live.
answer_text: The Digital Grant Management system being piloted for the Neighbourhood Renewal and Areas At Risk funding programmes opened on Friday 31 January 2025.The portal remained open for funding applications until Wednesday 19 February at 5pm.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail when the policy framework for infrastructure for the voluntary and community sector will be (i) published; and (ii) open for applications.
answer_text: My Department has undertaken extensive engagement and consultation on a draft Voluntary and Community Sector Infrastructure Support Policy Framework.  Final proposals for a new Policy Framework will be published in due course and will inform future investment in sectoral infrastructure support.  Communication and engagement will continue with Voluntary and Community organisations to inform them of opportunities and support them with the application process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail each occasion that the Community Investment Fund was open for new applications since 2009.
answer_text: Since 2005, my Department’s Community Investment Fund has provided a stable basis for funding of community development activity to strengthen communities, tackle disadvantage and ensure quality public services to local communities.Since 2009, there have been no open calls for new applications for the Community Investment Fund.  However, funding for two infrastructure support organisations, Resurgam Trust and Rathlin Development & Community Association, was approved by the Minister for Social Development in 2015/16.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of specialist adult attention deficit hyperactivity disorder consultants in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: There is currently no commissioned service for Adult ADHD. Northern, South Eastern, Southern and Western Health and Social Care Trusts currently have no specialist adult ADHD consultants.  Belfast Health and Social Care Trust reconfigured a small resource from within adult teams to process Adult ADHD referrals this is a 0.5 whole time equivalent consultant physiatrist. Regarding provision of service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD), demand for assessment and diagnosis has grown across Trusts.All Trusts are committed to work closely and collaboratively with commissioners to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based services that can meet the needs of adults with ADHD within Trust areas. This work would include the development of clinical pathways.The Department is commissioning a research exercise into the current and projected demand with a view to bringing forward recommendations to inform a commissioned ADHD service, subject to future budget availability. The exercise will (amongst other things) seek to establish the prevalence of ADHD (for both children and adults) in Northern Ireland, examine best practice from other jurisdictions and identify challenges and barriers in relation to ADHD diagnosis and treatment. Importantly, it will be expected to make recommendations on what any future commissioned service might look like in terms of, for example, costs, staffing profiles and recommended care pathways.A research lead has now been identified and work on the research has commenced, with an anticipated completion date of end June 2025.Any future commissioned service will be subject to both budget availability and evidenced demand, but the research, once completed, will help provide a firm evidence base to inform a future model.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action her Department takes to ensure that utility companies replace paving in public realm areas in town and city centres with like for like replacements in a timely manner.
answer_text: The Street Works (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 (the Order), forms the legislative framework for street works by statutory undertakers. The Order requires the undertaker, when reinstating a street, to comply with such requirements as may be prescribed in relation to the specification of materials to be used and the standards of workmanship to be observed. These requirements have been prescribed in the Specification for the Reinstatement of Openings in Roads (2nd Edition), a code of practice approved by the Department under Article 31 of the Order. Permanent reinstatements are normally carried out using the same materials and in some cases it may be possible to reuse some or all of the modules. Interim reinstatements may be carried out using different materials prior to permanent reinstatement. If an interim reinstatement is used, permanent reinstatement must be completed within six months unless agreed with the Department.The Department has powers to carry out investigatory works to check whether or not a utility company has complied with its duty to reinstate the street. These are detailed in the Code of Practice for Inspections. Where a utility company fails to reinstate to an acceptable standard, the reinstatement defects are notified to the utility company responsible for the repair or replacement. Where necessary, additional inspections are carried out by my officials, at the utility company’s expense, to ensure the defects have been corrected.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action being taken by her Department and NI Water to urgently address the sewer problem on the A2 Bangor Road which has resulted in long traffic delays in the area.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for NI Water however I have requested the relevant information and have been advised that NI Water currently has an emergency lane closure in place on A2 Bangor Road at the junction of Ballygrainy Road. The lane closure is for Belfast bound traffic. This is to facilitate emergency sewer repairs to replace 30m of collapsed pipework. The closure has been in place since Friday 31st January, and it was hoped that works would be completed during the same weekend.  However, on the commencement of the works it became apparent that there was a gas main running parallel to the foul sewer and also a collapsed storm sewer.  This has resulted in the contractor having to take additional time to complete the repairs. I am advised that work is expected to complete on Friday 7th February 2025.For any further operational matters you can contact NI Water directly through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will progress the installation of Speed Indicator Devices in North Down.
answer_text: Speed Indicator Devices (SIDs) are not considered to be traffic signs and do not fall under the remit of my Department but are the responsibility of Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs), funded by the Department of Justice.I recognise the road safety benefits SID signs can bring and my Department works in partnership with the above mentioned bodies to ensure their safe and managed placement through my Department’s policy Speed Indicator Devices DEM 191/23.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail why her Department has allowed NIE Networks to not replace public realm paving in Main Street, Bangor, several months after the utility works have taken place.
answer_text: Utility companies are responsible for the permanent reinstatement of roads and footways to an agreed specification.  It is common practice for Utility companies to lay a temporary repair to ensure the surface remains safe and allows any settlement to occur which is then followed up with a permanent repair a number of months later.  My Department is aware that NIE is currently sourcing ‘like for like’ material to permanently repair the temporary reinstatement, where possible, on the Main Street in Bangor.I appreciate that the use of temporary bituminous surfacing within areas where public realm improvement can be visually unappealing. However, a full reinstatement using materials sympathetic to the original scheme will be completed in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the installation of a pedestrian crossing on the East Circular Road, at the Ballymaconnel Road junction, Bangor.
answer_text: A new controlled pedestrian crossing on the A2 East Circular Road, Bangor was planned for completion during the 2024/25 financial year; regrettably due to a lack of contractor capacity the scheme was delayed and will now be considered for delivery in the 2025/26, subject to the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any financial support his Department provides to organisations that train guide dogs.
answer_text: My Department does not provide any financial support to organisations that train guide dogs.Furthermore, my officials previously contacted a range of voluntary and community organisations, including Guide Dogs Northern Ireland, informing them of the application process for my Department’s Core Grant Scheme for the 2023/24 financial year. The Department did not receive an application from Guide Dogs NI for the scheme in either 2023/24 or 2025/26.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities why the Housing Executive has delayed bathroom and kitchen replacements in the Breezemount Estate, Bangor
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Breezemount Bathroom Kitchen Rewire scheme (23:70:1013) was first identified through the roll-on process in 2023 and included within the 2024/25 planned programme for delivery.Unfortunately, the allocated contractor declined an extension to their service period, subsequently we have reallocated the works to an alternative contractor but this has caused slippage to our planned programme and the works will now be delivered in 25/26 financial year.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to ensure hospital staff continue to be able to access parking when charges are removed.
answer_text: As a fair recognition of the hard work and dedication of HSC staff, all staff parking permits became free of charge from 12 May 2024.  There is insufficient capacity for all staff to be provided with a parking permit.To ensure that those staff with the greatest need are provided with a free on-site parking permit, the Department has requested that all HSC Trusts implement an assessment of demonstrable “need” for designated staff car parking spaces / permits. The assessment criteria takes into consideration individual circumstances, operational requirements, and the individual’s ability to access alternative and sustainable forms of transport.When car parking charges are removed in May 2026, HSC Trusts will operate Automatic Number Plate Recognition (ANPR) technology to ensure that effective parking enforcement can be applied in the absence of charging. ANPR will be able to determine if a vehicle has stayed in a car park beyond an allotted time frame or parked incorrectly; and to identify and facilitate parking of registered staff permit holders. Staff without a parking permit can avail of a range of supports in place to access parking including free off-site staff parking spaces with free staff shuttle buses to and from work, cycle to work and car share schemes, free evening and weekend parking passes for parking out of hours, as well as the provision of bespoke Personal Travel Plans.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work being undertaken to improve the efficiency of patient discharge from hospitals to alleviate bed blocking.
answer_text: My department is working with Trusts to take a number of actions to improve the efficiency of patients discharges from hospital, when they are assessed as being medically fit.  It has been widely referenced in recent months that there is continued pressure with hospital flow due to delays in discharging individuals secondary to awaiting social care.  My Department has established a Social Care Collaborative Forum to provide vision, along with strategic advice and guidance on social care via workstreams to address delays.  Care Home Contract A series of workshops to consider various facets of the Regional Care Home Contract were held during September to December 2024.  This will contribute towards addressing hospital delays associated with the cohort of individuals considered to have enhanced and complex needs. Regional Home Care ContractI have committed to introducing the “Real Living Wage” for social care within this calendar year which will go some way to assist with challenges in increasing capacity in home care services.   A revised regional home care contract is key to ensuring equity and consistency and this work is due to commence in 2025/2026.Early Review TeamA pilot model was developed in October 2023 to ensure early and timely review of new and increased home care packages within 2-8 weeks of the support being implemented.  The home care hours saved can then be recycled back into the system for those awaiting care.  There will be a focus in 2025/2026 to roll out this service across all programmes of care in trusts.  Care Line LiveCare Line Live is a digital system which houses schedules for trust-led home care. It provides oversight of the capacity on schedules, therefore optimising the number of hours that can be delivered within existing staffing levels.  Three trusts have implemented this system and two have plans to go live in 2025/2026.  Trusted Assessor The underlying principle of the Trusted Assessor is to promote safe and timely discharges from hospitals when individuals are transitioning to care homes.  Waiting for the pre-admission assessment of an individual by a care home is reported regionally as one cause of delay in hospital discharges.  A review of this pilot and model is currently being undertaken. Care Home Availability Digital AppIt is identified that a digital solution to accessing information on the availability of care home beds on a daily basis would be advantageous.  When implemented, this will ensure trusts having regional access to one system and care homes submitting one return to cover all trusts they contract with.  This is a current priority to implement in quarter 1 of 2025/2026.  Direct PaymentsDirect Payments and Emergency Direct Payments are being promoted which enables a package of care to be provided through the employment of a Personal Assistant.Whilst the steps being taken above are having a position impact; in order to fully spread and imbed, additional investment is required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the autism support that is available for those aged under 18 across each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) provide support for children and young people diagnosed with autism aligned to their assessed needs. As with non-autistic children and young people, not all assessed need will meet the threshold for statutory intervention and may best be provided through community and voluntary organisations. Below is a brief synopsis of the support available in each HSC Trust.BHSCT: Provides targeted Tier 2 post-diagnostic support using a neuro-affirmative approach, focusing on emotional regulation, identity, toileting, and feeding difficulties. The Autism service (CAAS) works closely with CAMHS and voluntary organisations. Further information is available at: https://view.pagetiger.com/directoryofservices/1https://view.pagetiger.com/prediagnosticresources/1NHSCT: Offers pre- and post-diagnostic support via telephone consultations, PACE programmes, and individualised intervention packages. Extensive digital resources, including podcasts, webinars, and sensory videos are available. Further information is available at:	https://www.northerntrust.hscni.net/services/Autistic-spectrum-disorder-asd-services/paediatric-asd-services/asd-services-childrenSEHSCT: Provides structured pre-diagnostic support with a Parent Pack, 1st Contact Call, and monthly email updates. Post-diagnostic support includes webinars, emotional regulation training, and transition programmes for primary-to-secondary students. Further information is available at:https://setrust.hscni.net/service/neurodevelopmental-serviceSHSCT: Uses a tiered intervention pyramid, allowing children to move between intervention levels based on assessed needs. Individualised and group therapeutic support with specialist input from OT and Psychological Therapies are available. Further information is available at:https://hcsnisoutherntrust.pagetiger.com/autismservices/1?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=social&utm_content=ap_6x76zo5iuyWHSCT: Implements a stepped care model (Tier 1-3) with multi-disciplinary interventions covering social communication, sensory differences, behaviour, sleep, and emotional regulation. Provides early intervention services for those awaiting assessment. Further information is available at:https://westerntrust.hscni.net/services/childrens-services/children-and-young-peoples-autism-service/

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the autism support that is available for adults across each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) are responsible for the delivery of Autism support for adults, aligned to assessed needs. Service delivery is in keeping with the Autism Strategy 2023-2028, and associated Delivery Plan for 2023-2025. Both are available to access on the Department of Health website. They outline the key commitments and actions needed to support autistic people and their families.Within each of the Health and Social Care Trusts in Northern Ireland a robust diagnostic assessment can be accessed, followed by consultation and signposting to available services. Therapeutic intervention is also offered either through individual or group sessions. These sessions focus on social skills, emotional awareness, self-esteem, living skills and employment support. These interventions are provided within Trusts or are procured through the community and voluntary sector.Each of the Trusts have advisory services available. These services provide individual support and signposting in relation to employment, education, benefits, housing and Autism services.  These advisory services also provide educational awareness sessions. Support can be accessed through the Belfast, Western, Northern and South Eastern Autism Advisory Services, or within the Southern Trust, services are provided by Bolster Community.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any support available for football clubs, at junior level, seeking facilities for additional evening training during the season.
To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of Invest NI’s performance in relation to selling and developing its land for economic development.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has considered introducing legislation or regulations to ban the use of electric scooters given safety concerns, especially among young people.
answer_text: It is currently illegal to use e-scooters on public roads and public spaces here and I have no plans to change that position. Anyone using an e-scooter illegally may be subject to enforcement action by the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has had any discussions with UK Government ministers in relation to marine safety.
answer_text: I have met with an official from the Maritime and Coastguard Agency and staff from the RNLI to discuss water safety for both inland and marine waters. I have however had no discussions with UK Government ministers on this issue. My officials have also been engaging with officials from the Maritime and Coastguard Agency to ensure marine safety policies are included in the Marine Plan for Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount of investment from the Affordable Warmth Scheme that has been allocated to the North Down constituency in each of the last five years.
answer_text: I refer the member to my response to AQW 18221/22-27 which covers the Ards and North Down council area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on her discussions with the UK Government on the future of the United Kingdom Shared Prosperity Fund.
answer_text: The Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF) has been the topic for discussion and written correspondence between my predecessor and British Government ministers on several occasions. Most recently on 17 December 2024, my predecessor, Dr Caoimhe Archibald MLA, met with Alex Norris MP, Minister of Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG), to discuss the SPF, which was originally due to end on 31 March 2025. She also raised the successor to SPF with Darren Jones, Chief Secretary to the Treasury.At the meeting with Minister Norris, Dr Archibald pressed for clarity on allocations for the additional 2025/26 transition year, and for meaningful engagement on the longer-term replacement for SPF and the other Whitehall Local Growth funds. Furthermore, Dr Archibald stressed that the Labour manifesto commitment for future funding to be brought under devolved control must be delivered in full.I will be seeking an urgent meeting with Minister Norris to continue these discussions and press for progress on this important matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on when the Sydenham bypass resurfacing works will be complete.
answer_text: Currently, the anticipated completion date for the ongoing Sydenham Bypass resurfacing project is the end of February 2025, subject to favourable weather conditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the mutual aid provided by the rest of the UK following Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: My Department sought mutual aid from a range of bodies, including local councils, to enhance its capacity to respond to the impacts of Storm Éowyn. This included a bid through Whitehall for additional chainsaw operatives and specialist tree surgeons. Assistance from local councils was provided and deployed quickly, which was used to respond to the impacts across the road network. However, as work progressed, the additional resources from Whitehall were stood down as the Department had sufficient capacity to deal with the remaining impacts of the storm.NI Water also received c350,000 litres of bottled water from Welsh Water.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether there is any planned compensation scheme for residents and businesses affected by power outages following Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: Officials from my Department are engaging with senior representatives from the Utility Regulator and NIE Networks to determine what options are available to provide payments to electricity customers across the North who were most impacted by a loss of power due to Storm Éowyn. My officials will continue to work with the Utility Regulator and NIE Networks to identify the most appropriate option to provide payments to eligible electricity customers impacted by Storm Éowyn.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, given the volume of outages, to detail how restoration of electricity is prioritised following Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: Storm Éowyn was an unprecedented event, leaving almost one third of people (283,000 homes and businesses) without power. There were faults in over 5000 locations, with each of these requiring engineers to physically attend and repair. Multi agency co-ordination was stood up before, during and after the storm via Civil Contingencies mechanisms and NIE Networks should be praised for its tireless work throughout the event.NIE Networks was initially able to provide estimated restoration times for 90% of customers without power. There is a balance to be found when providing restoration timescales to consider customers’ needs, particularly if they need to plan alternative arrangements if they are likely to be off supply for many days.NIE Networks prioritise the repair of faults associated with large numbers of customers, which is focussed on the high voltage network. Full restoration on the low voltage network will take longer as this is typically more isolated or in rural areas and can bring different challenges for its engineers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the action his Department will be taking to secure Department-owned buildings and ensure public safety in Queen’s Parade, Bangor, following damage caused by Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: Departmental Officials have met on site with contractors to inspect, identify and resolve the issues across the Queen’s Parade site caused by Storm Éowyn.   There are currently significant demands on the construction industry due to the storm damage, but officials will continue to engage with contractors until any necessary repairs are completed.   However, in the short-term, the impacted areas have been cordoned off to ensure public safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how his Department is supporting schools affected by Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: Education Authority (EA) staff and contractors worked extremely hard to protect the schools’ estate and ensure those schools which incurred damage could reopen safely and as quickly as possible. Remedial works to repair damaged buildings will be ongoing in some schools for several weeks.The EA and the Department will continue to liaise with schools to provide support and ensure that any outstanding repairs or replacements are progressed as quickly as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail his animal welfare priorities for the remainder of the 2022-2027 mandate, including plans for engagement with animal welfare sector groups.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department is currently advancing preparations to bring forward a version of Lucy’s Law for Northern Ireland, and in the coming weeks, it is my intention to launch a full public consultation on mandatory CCTV in abattoirs. I am also actively considering my animal welfare priorities for the remainder of the mandate. To ensure the most pressing needs are addressed, I have been engaging in a series of meetings with stakeholders, which included Northern Ireland Companion Animal Welfare Group, to outline my proposed priorities and listen to their feedback before I publish an Animal Welfare Pathway, in the coming months. My priorities include; the prohibition of the use of aversive training devices, such as electric shock collars; the mandatory microchipping of pets cats; and the strengthening of dog microchipping requirements, by making it an offence not to update records. Work has also commenced to provide me with advice on the appointment of an expert group to review dog breeding regulations and provide an assessment of the need for regulation of canine fertility services.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how schools are facilitated in learning about the Holocaust and lessons from the Holocaust.
answer_text: Schools and teachers have the freedom to choose the contexts, topics and themes which are most appropriate to their learners.  Teaching about the Holocaust is not a statutory requirement in the Northern Ireland Curriculum.  However, there are opportunities for pupils to explore the Holocaust and related topics, particularly at Key Stage 2 via the ‘World Around Us’ and ‘Personal Development and Mutual Understanding’ areas of learning and at Key Stage 3 via ‘Learning for Life and Work’ and ‘Environment and Society’.In terms of statutory requirements, pupils must be provided with the opportunity to investigate the impact of significant events of the 20th Century on the world as part of the minimum content for History at Key Stage 3.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline his timeframe for the introduction of a licensing framework for animal welfare establishments, including dog pounds.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the actions his Department is taking to support tourism initiatives in North Down; and (ii) the amount spent in North Down on tourism initiatives in the last five years.
answer_text: the actions his Department is taking to support tourism initiatives in North Down.My Department is committed to achieving greater regional balance of tourism across the North. North Down forms part of a broader visitor destination which spans the Ards and North Down Borough Council (ANDBC) area with linkages to Belfast. Tourism NI collaborates with ANDBC and other partners as part of a shared approach to developing tourism in the area. Tourism NI is leading or supporting a range of initiatives to develop the area’s tourism potential including:Funding the development, build and initial licensing of a new consumer destination website for the area - www.visitardsandnorthdown.com.Supporting businesses to attend international events and trade shows and delivering Tourism NI led events across such as Meet the Buyer thereby connecting operators with international buyers. Over the two most recent financial years, ANDBC has participated in eight business development platforms, including World Travel Market,Generating opportunities for local businesses to market themselves and generate new revenues. Over the last two financial years, North Down has featured in six familiarisation trips, involving approximately 70 operators and buyers from out of state markets. Encouraging local businesses to become more innovative through participation in the Innovate Tourism initiative and the achievement of an Innovation Accreditation. Initiatives to expand the offer to visitors and help business to grow are complemented by Tourism NI’s suite of marketing, communications and PR activities. For instance, as part of Tourism NI’s seasonal marketing campaigns, there are weeks of coverage dedicated to the Ards and North Down area. The area benefits too from Tourism NI’s close partnership with Tourism Ireland encompassing the hosting of media and influencers from key international markets, and a wider programme of marketing and PR activity including social media, email marketing, and influencer marketing.  the amount spent in North Down on tourism initiatives in the last five years.           	See below a summary of Tourism NI spend over the last five financial years for those activities where spend is able to be directly allocated to the Ards and North Down Council area. 2019/20 – 500k2020/21 – 100k2021/22 – 600k2022/23 – 300k2023/24 – 100k*All figures rounded to the nearest £100,000.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any plans she has to introduce legislation to address the criminal use of Artificial Intelligence in circumstances such as the creation of deepfake media.
answer_text: I recognise the significant harm and distress that can be caused by the use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) both in terms of disinformation and also in the creation of false sexual imagery, such as deepfakes. Its use can destroy lives.Telecommunications and internet legislation are reserved matters.  However, online safety is also a cross-cutting departmental issue where a holistic approach needs to be taken to address the issue more widely.  This is demonstrated in the Executive’s Online Safety Strategy which has been established to assist children and young people to participate in the online world in a positive, safe and responsible way.    Where responsibility falls to my Department, I am committed to strengthening our laws to further protect our community from abhorrent behaviours associated with online abuse. I continue to consider all opportunities available to me to do so, including the extension of UK Government legislation as well as Assembly legislation.I have been in contact with the UK government on a range of proposals intended for its upcoming Crime and Policing Bill. This included a proposal to introduce a new offence of creating a sexually explicit deepfake image without consent, which I was keen to extend to Northern Ireland. However, this proposal has since been brought forward for England and Wales by way of an amendment to the current Data (Access and Use) Bill, tabled by the UK Government on 22 January 2025. As we were not made aware of this change of plan by the UK Government in advance, I am now considering the implications for Northern Ireland and I remain committed to strengthening the protection for victims of deepfake imagery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of priority referrals on the adult autism assessment waiting list.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) are responsible for the provision of Autism Services. HSCTs have provided the following responses regarding the number of priority referrals on the adult autism waiting list.Northern Health and Social Care Trust (NHSCT):The NHSCT advise the Adult Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) Service does not provide urgent diagnostic assessments.  Referrals are accepted in chronological order from date received.  Diagnostic assessment appointments are offered to service users based on length of time waiting.Western Health and Social Care Trusts (WHSCT):The WHSCT advise referrals to Adult Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) Service for diagnostic assessment are taken in chronological order from the date that they were received. Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT):The SHSCT advise Adult Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) Service referrals are prioritised by date of referral accepted onto the Adult ASD Diagnostic waiting list and assessed in chronological order based on those waiting the longest. Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT):The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust advise there are currently two priority referrals on the adult autism assessment waiting list. South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT):The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust advise after triage, referrals are placed on the waiting list by date of referral.  Referrals may then be prioritised according to clinical need. My Department has published an updated Autism Strategy 2023-2028 and associated Delivery Plan. The associated actions have been informed through extensive stakeholder engagement and consultation with people who have Autism, their families and organisations who represent them. Information regarding the Autism Strategy and Delivery Plan is available on the Department of Health Website Autism Strategy -2028 | Department of HealthAn Adult Autism Project Manager Lead has also been appointed and is scheduled to commence post within my Department (Strategic Planning and Performance Group), February 2025. The post-holder will work with salient stakeholders to support implementation of the Autism Strategy. Progress on implementation of the strategy will be reported on an annual basis. A further Delivery Plan is expected to be developed post 2025. My Department, working with other departments, HSCTs, agencies and stakeholders remains committed to ensuring adults with Autism and their Carers receive appropriate service provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount spent on adult autism assessments in the last three years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) are responsible for the provision of Autism Services. HSCTS have outlined the following approximate amount spent on adult autism assessments in the last three years.Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT):The SHSCT advise approximately £200K has been spent on Adult Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) assessments in the last 3 years to release appropriately trained staff from existing services. The Trust has however seen a steady growth in the referrals for adult ASD diagnostics and considers there is now a significant gap in the resources needed to deliver this service.Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT):The BHSCT Adult Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) Service notes the following spend is recorded against the ASD Service. BHSCT advises whilst the majority of this spend will be related to assessments, figures also include spend related to intervention and administration.  2022/2023- £355,9742023/2024- £334,1052024/2025 (to December 2024)- £331,483. BHSCT project full year spend of approximately £441,977Northern Health and Social Care Trust (NHSCT):The NHSCT Adult Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) Service offers both assessment and intervention. The NHSCT advises it is therefore not possible to provide an accurate figure regarding the amount spent from commissioned funding on ASD assessments only in the last three years. The NHSCT however advise the ASD Service have recently secured an additional £84,200 through the Trust’s Waiting List Initiative funding to outsource 58 diagnostic assessments, to be completed by independent sector providers between January 2025 and March 2025.South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT):The SEHSCT outlined the following costs to support assessment.2021/22-£261,594 - Staff costs & Waiting list initiative (due to unfilled post) to provide diagnostic assessments £14,4002022/23- £240,077 - Staff costs & Waiting list initiative (due to unfilled post) to provide diagnostic assessments £12,0002023/24- £246,663 – Staff costs2024/25- £219,546 - Staff costsThe SEHSCT advise the reduction in spend over the past few years can be accounted for by a number of staff vacancies, (i.e. Psychology vacancy and reduction in hours re two Autism Practitioners).Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT): The WHSCT note the total spend/cost (percentage of time spent on assessments) of Band 7 Occupational Therapist and Band 8C Consultant Clinical Psychologist as follows:2021/22    £102,7062022/23    £103,9402023/24    £102,883My Department, working with other departments, HSCTs, agencies and stakeholders remains committed to ensuring adults with Autism and their Carers receive appropriate service provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the rationale for his decision to withdraw staff overtime funding in relation to the (i) Fermanagh Classic; and (ii) Pike Classic.
answer_text: Budget pressures have required prioritisation of resources to deliver statutory obligations and the management of the Public Angling Estate. My Department will continue to support the Fermanagh Classic and the Pike Classic by making the Public Angling Estate available for use.My officials continue to have constructive engagement with Fermanagh and Omagh District Council over the Fermanagh Classic and I hope to have issues about support resolved soon. Officials will subsequently engage with the Pike Classic event organisers.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans he has to reduce the adult autism assessment waiting list.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) are responsible for the provision of Autism Services. HSCTS have outlined the following plans to reduce adult assessment waiting lists.Northern Health and Social Care Trust (NHSCT):The NHSCT advise the Adult Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) Service recently completed a capacity/demand analysis which highlighted current capacity in the service does not meet demand and cited the importance of further investment.  At Trust level, Waiting List Initiative Funding has been provided in 24/25 to contract with the independent sector to provide 58 diagnostic assessments which are being offered to those waiting the longest.  In addition, the service is reviewing its referral acceptance criteria.Western Health and Social Care Trusts (WHSCT):WHSCT advise within the last three years the number of referrals received is approximately 2.5/3 times the capacity of the Adult Autism Services, given the funding and staffing available. The Trust advises a comprehensive triage system for all referrals received for diagnostic assessment is implemented. Over the past number of years, when financially possible, the WHSCT have employed extra staff contracted into the Trust to assist with diagnostic assessments and have considered waiting list initiatives in an attempt to use all available resources to make use of extra clinics; including weekend clinics to assist in the reduction of waiting times. The WHSCT report that investment is required to support enhanced service provision.Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT):SHSCT advise the Adult Autism Diagnostic Service has increased assessment clinics from once per month to weekly in an effort to address demand and capacity issues.
