From January 2025 an additional weekly diagnostic assessment clinic and a new neuro developmental screening clinic have been added to help cope with the increasing demand.Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT):BHSCT advise waiting lists have arisen from demand being greater than funded capacity and challenges in maintaining staffing at full capacity in a specialist service (impacted by challenges in recruitment and difficulties backfilling posts e.g. maternity leave). The Trust further note newly appointed staff require training before being able to work at full capacity given the specialist nature of the posts. In order to support increased capacity when there are vacancies, the Trust has in place a contract with an independent sector provider.South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT):SEHSCT advise various approaches have been implemented to address capacity issues and growing demand including updating the short-term intervention service model to a predominately group-based intervention model, to enable capacity to be redirected to completing diagnostic assessments. SEHSCT also note their funding of an external provider to deliver a group work programme and redirect additional capacity to complete diagnostic assessments. SEHSCT have rolled o

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current waiting list for adult autism assessments, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) are responsible for the provision of Autism Services. HSCTS have outlined the following approximate current waiting list for adults awaiting assessment for an Autism diagnosis.Northern Health and Social Care Trust: Due to the recent move to Encompass, the Trust advised current data regarding the waiting list for Adult Autism diagnostic assessment is not currently available. Data available as at October 2024 indicates 1479 adults awaiting assessment for an autism diagnosis.Southern Health and Social Care Trust: Currently 472 adults waiting for Autism diagnostic assessments.Belfast Health and Social Care Trust: Currently 608 adults awaiting Autism diagnostic assessment.Western Health and Social Care Trust: Currently 461 adults awaiting Autism diagnostic assessment.South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust: Currently 833 adults awaiting Autism diagnostic assessment.My Department, working with other departments, HSCTs, agencies and stakeholders remains committed to ensuring adults with Autism and their Carers receive appropriate service provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans his Department has to introduce traffic calming measures in Donaghadee.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users.  The Department considers all requests for improvements to the road network as part of the Local Transportation and Safety Measures (LTSM) programme.  Requests for traffic calming are considered in line with current policies and any viable schemes including those in Donaghadee will be considered for inclusion in the work programme subject to the availability of funding. Concerns regarding driver behaviour such as speeding should be directed to the PSNI using their non-emergency 101 number.One of the best ways to ensure the safety of our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury on roads by 2050 is by changing driver behaviour.  As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe.  The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans his Department has to introduce residents' parking schemes in North Down.
answer_text: My Department published The Residents’ Parking Review Report on 29 November 2024, which can be viewed at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/residents-parking-review-report. Following the publication of the report there has been a significant level of interest for the establishment of residents’ parking zones across the north. Given the level of demand, and ongoing budgetary and resource constraints, I have asked my officials to expedite the development of an application and assessment process for schemes to ensure that any requests can be appropriately considered. This process will take into account the findings and recommendations of the report and provide a streamlined approach for considering, assessing and prioritising requests for residents parking schemes going forward.It is my intention to make an announcement as to how future schemes will be assessed by my Department over the coming months.   In the meantime, my Department will continue to develop the schemes already commenced such as in the Bogside in Derry and Iveagh area of Belfast, and will give consideration to schemes to address the emerging pressures with on-street parking in the vicinity of the new Ulster University campus in Belfast and the Magee campus.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the measures his Department is putting in place to ensure basic dignity and toileting needs are being met for patients waiting in corridors across the hospital estate.
answer_text: HSC Trusts have confirmed they all have measures in place to ensure human dignity is maintained at all times.South Eastern TrustIn the South Eastern Trust, teams endeavour to ensure that the dignity and toileting needs of patients are being met while waiting in corridors in the Emergency Department and across hospital wards.  The experience of these patients is kept under constant review and there is work ongoing to improve patient experience by regularly auditing the space and facilities.It is important to acknowledge that non-designated beds are in place due to the escalated pressures and the need to balance risk between the Emergency Department and hospitals.  Teams continue to do their best in very challenging circumstances.  Belfast TrustAll patients are risk assessed to determine suitability to be cared for in a non-designated clinical space (corridor spaces).  These patients will be of lower acuity and have the ability to mobilise to toilet and bathroom facilities.  All wards have purchased additional portable screens to place around these care spaces. Western TrustAll escalation and undesignated beds used within the Western Trust are risk assessed for suitability of use, included access to toileting facilities, washing and for privacy. Individual patients are risk assessed for suitability to ensure their personal care needs can be appropriately met in the escalation or undesignated bed space. In addition to ensuring appropriate access to toileting facilities privacy screens are provided were required.Northern TrustIn NHSCT we do not have patients in ward corridors. Within the ED there are patients waiting in areas that are not designated bed spaces and we have aligned nursing staff to all these areas. We are currently operating our ED departments with staffing over funded levels. Intentional rounding of patients is carried out to ensure they are receiving care. This is however challenging within the ED dependant on the number of patients requiring care and the space available.Southern TrustDue to unprecedented demand for unscheduled care, it is acknowledged that patients may have to wait in corridors. Our estate and available facilities do provide a challenge for us. However, an escalation plan including clear criteria to manage additional patients is implemented and ensures patients with the lowest acuity are placed in corridors. Therefore, it is anticipated that these patients can be directed to walk to toilets as required. For those patients who require partial assistance they will be wherever possible accommodated close to a staff base to ensure they are able to speak with staff to request assistance to access bathrooms for toileting needs and privacy and dignity is maintained  Patients who require full assistance with toileting will be accommodated in an area with adequate screens or curtains to ensure privacy and maintain dignity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether he intends to increase the number of inflammatory bowel disease nurse specialists; and (ii) whether he has considered opportunities for personal development, such as Master's level qualifications.
answer_text: The remit of operational workforce planning which informs the number of Specialist Nursing roles required to meet population health needs is that of the HSC Trusts.Post-registration education and training programmes are accessible as part of the DoH Nursing and Midwifery Education Commissioning process and are available at a range of academic study levels, including master’s level. Commissioning of these programmes is informed by HSC Trusts training needs analysis that is undertaken on an annual basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of surface defects reported on footpaths in each of the last five years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Road and footway surface defect data dating back to 2008/2009 to date is regularly updated and publicly available on the OpenDataNI portal at https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/dataset/surface-defects.  The data is updated each evening and can be downloaded in various formats.  The information includes: spatial location details, road section name, local district council, surface defect type (e.g. potholes, cracking, depressions, rutting), date recorded, defect status, target response time, and actual completion date.   It is important to note that there can be a time lag between when each surface defect is repaired and when this is updated on the maintenance management system.  While many roads would undoubtedly benefit from investment, due to a limited budget, resurfacing work must be taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need of intervention. In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what consideration he has given to reopening the inpatient ward and the Minor Injuries Unit at Bangor Community Hospital to alleviate pressures in capacity within the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients currently waiting in corridor beds, broken down by (i) Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) hospital.
To ask the Minister of Health how he intends to use artificial intelligence technology to improve efficiency across the health service.
answer_text: Artificial Intelligence (AI), and Generative AI in particular, offers opportunities to improve both productivity of the existing HSC workforce and the quality-of-care services delivered. Anticipated benefits include freeing up capacity to enhance services through improved communications and tailored treatment plans.Future plans include testing AI algorithms to assist with referral/ decision making, scheduling and acquisition, reporting and triage of reporting, and results acknowledgement/alerts. However, the potential capabilities of this technology must be set against the potential risks – for instance in the areas of clinical efficacy and information governance. In these areas the Department of Health and the Health and Social Care (HSC) system needs to ensure that the tools provided are trustworthy and do not breach the privacy of patients or service users.Additionally, the wide adoption of advanced technologies like AI/GenAI require a number of underlying enablers – including good quality, codified data that describes a service users needs and historic episodes and treatments (for training purposes), the clinical safety and ethical governance structures that are required to oversee the safe introduction of these AI tools, and skills in cloud services engineering and security (recognising that the compute power required to run AI algorithms is often best provided from cloud technologies). The HSC has been considering how the emerging capabilities of AI could be utilised to improve efficiency in a range of activities through AI steering group, with a number of proof-of-value projects being delivered in a number of areas. My Department has established an AI Working group reviewing the potential benefits of the technology and the governance risks to be addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration he has given to increase support and resources allocated to essential inflammatory bowel disease advice line services to ensure (i) responses during a flare are received by the end of the next working day; and (ii) treatment initiation or escalation is available within 48 hours of review during a flare.
answer_text: A timely response to Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD) patients in need of medical support is of the utmost importance to patients and a priority for the IBD teams.  Each of the HSC Trusts provides an advice line for IBD patients that aims to (i) respond to a flare by the end of the next working day and (ii) initiate or escalate treatment within 48 hours.  Where possible, the Department and Trusts seek to secure additional funding to further extend services or optimise current resources.  Funding has recently been approved for each of the Trusts in relation to staffing infrastructure which will help meet demand for the service.  Trusts have been asked to submit their proposals for expenditure.Belfast HSC TrustThe Belfast Trust, is currently working to increase the number of funded IBD Nurses to improve response times to the Advice Line service in order to consistently meet the targets above.  This would, however, require additional resources and funding so that the Trust could expand its service and increase clinical activity to enhance the Flare Services and Treatment Escalation.Two Review Clinics have recently been established and the Flare Clinic was converted back to a Review clinic in response to reduced demand.  The service is currently working to reinstate this as an ad hoc service to patients as and when it is required.South Eastern HSC TrustPatients under the care of a consultant for Inflammatory Bowel Disease can contact their care team on the advice line.  Following a clinical discussion, a decision regarding care will be agreed and a response initiated within the above standards, where possible.  This may include attending ED, attending an ambulatory service, attending outpatients or providing advice.Northern HSC TrustThe Northern Trust strives to ensure all advice line calls are responded to within 48 hours.  This is, however, dependent on staffing availability.  Once a Specialist Nurse returns a patient’s call they will initiate treatment or escalation to Consultant level. Western HSC Trust(i)	Western Trust patients are advised to leave a message on the IBD advice line number and an IBD nurse specialist will aim to return their call as quickly as possible.For the majority of cases, return calls are completed by the next working day. Despite fluctuations in demand for the advice line, service needs and staffing pressures, patients are informed that calls are usually responded to within 48 hours.  The exception to this is at weekends and bank holiday when the service is not available.  (ii) 	It is not always possible for treatment to be escalated within 48 hours.  For various reasons treatment can be delayed as there is a requirement to confirm a flare with stool samples (faecal calprotectin).  In most cases results take between 7-14 days to come back.  There is also a need to liaise with Consultants about treatments which may take time.   Patients are asked to review literature about medications and this can also take time for them to

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of trained inflammatory bowel disease nurse specialists, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: IBD Clinical Nurse Specialists are an essential part of patient care in the hospital and community.  They provide expert knowledge, information, education, treatment and support for patients with IBD.The Trusts have returned details on the number of IBD nurse specialists as follows:Belfast HSC Trust 			4.3 WTENorthern HSC Trust		3.6 WTESouth Eastern HSC Trust		2.0 WTESouthern HSC Trust		4.4 WTEWestern HSC Trust		3.0 WTE

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current waiting time following referral for elective inflammatory bowel disease surgery, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Trusts do not record data in relation to patients waiting for surgery related to their Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD).  Patients are listed for surgery by the operation required and not their condition. There are also various different operations for IBD patients but these may not be exclusive to IBD meaning that is not possible to extrapolate the data by type of surgery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans his Department has to invest in high-profile public health campaigns to raise awareness of the seriousness of inflammatory bowel disease and its symptoms, such as the Crohn’s and Colitis UK Cut the Crap initiative, with support from dedicated experts and a focus on addressing inequalities in diagnosis.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration his Department has given to the implementation of National Primary Care Diagnostic Pathway for lower gastrointestinal symptoms across all Health and Social Care Trusts, in order to provide clarity for primary care professionals and ensure people living with gut-related symptoms get the right tests at the right time.
answer_text: The National Primary Care Pathway was launched at the end of 2024.  It provides guidance to healthcare professionals on the screening and testing of lower gastrointestinal (GI) symptoms across the UK.  Its development included wide consultation and the involvement of a number of professional bodies across the UK including the HSC as well as charities like the IBD UK partnership, Crohns and Colitis UK, Coeliac UK, Guts UK, IBS Network etc.  It has also been endorsed by the Royal College of General Practitioners (RCGP), the Royal Pharmaceutical Society (RPS) and the British Society of Gastroenterology (BSG). The paediatric pathway has been endorsed by the British Society of Paediatric Gastroenterology, Hepatology and Nutrition (BSPGHAN)The national lower GI pathway reflects a number of previous pathways that are now combined in one place so that Primary Care professionals can now refer from a single guide that advises on the diagnosis of Irritable Bowel Syndrome, Inflammatory Bowel Disease, Coeliac Disease and Bowel Cancer.   These pathways in the HSC are supported by a range of blood tests and stool tests.   The HSC provides GP direct pathology access to Calprotectin stool testing for IBD and quantitative Faecal Immunochemical Testing (qFIT) stool testing for suspected bowel cancer. All GP referrals to secondary care should be made with test results thereby enabling consultants to prioritise and triage patients appropriately.Work continues across the HSC to further enhance gastroenterology services within available resources and raise awareness among GPs.  This is being facilitated by the Department of Health through a Primary Care Benign GI Programme Team in partnership with Trusts, primary care representatives and Crohns and Colitis UK.  A pilot study is being explored whereby patients with lower GI symptoms can be referred directly to a community led Dietetic team with their test results rather than secondary care. The degree to which this pilot can be implemented and rolled out to other areas, is dependent on appropriate funding being available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of practical driving tests carried out in the last 12 months, broken down by test centre.
answer_text: Since September 2020, the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has published monthly provisional headline statistics in the publication ‘Driver and Vehicle Agency Monthly Tests Conducted Statistics’ which is available at Driver and Vehicle Agency Monthly Tests Conducted Statistics | Department for Infrastructure. The number of driving tests conducted, broken down by test centre, are detailed in table 2.3 of Section 2.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the waiting time for learner drivers to obtain a theory driving test.
answer_text: The theory test is delivered for the Driver and Vehicle Agency by a third-party supplier who is required to meet several Key Performance Indicators (KPI), including those related to waiting times.The waiting time KPI states that candidates will wait on average no more than 28 days from booking to their theory test appointment date.  The target level achieved in December 2024 was 23 days.The KPI relating to non-standard test bookings, where a candidate requires additional accommodations, states that 95% of candidates making theory test bookings with non-standard accommodations shall be offered an appointment within 28 days either side of their preferred date.  The target level achieved in December 2024 was 98.3%.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any discussions he has held with Translink in relation to the introduction of late night bus and train services on a permanent basis.
answer_text: My officials are currently working with Translink to review the data obtained from the Nightmovers service over the festive period and to consider the viability of the expansion of this service beyond the festive period.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of people awaiting an MOT test who are currently logged with the Driver and Vehicle Agency.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) publish monthly provisional headline statistics in the publication ‘Driver and Vehicle Agency Monthly Tests Conducted Statistics’ which is available at Driver and Vehicle Agency Monthly Tests Conducted Statistics | Department for Infrastructure. The number of vehicle tests conducted, broken down by test centre, are detailed in table 1.3 of Section 1 - Vehicle Testing. Unvalidated management information taken from the DVA’s booking system at 15:30 on 14 January 2025 shows that, up to 2 April 2025, there were 110,598 private cars booked for an MOT test.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of MOT tests carried out in the last 12 months, broken down by test centre.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) publish monthly provisional headline statistics in the publication ‘Driver and Vehicle Agency Monthly Tests Conducted Statistics’ which is available at Driver and Vehicle Agency Monthly Tests Conducted Statistics | Department for Infrastructure. The number of vehicle tests conducted, broken down by test centre, are detailed in table 1.3 of Section 1 - Vehicle Testing. Unvalidated management information taken from the DVA’s booking system at 15:30 on 14 January 2025 shows that, up to 2 April 2025, there were 110,598 private cars booked for an MOT test.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on discussions she has held with the Bar Council and the Criminal Bar Association aimed at resolving the current criminal barristers’ strike.
answer_text: My Department has been engaging with professions on an ongoing basis since the outset of the access to justice reform programme. I also met the representatives of the professional bodies in advance of my statement to the Assembly in December which highlighted my intention to implement an uplift in fees as soon as possible. Officials also met separately with the professional bodies in advance of my statement and met again with representatives of the Bar and Criminal Bar Association when the latest withdrawal of services was announced at the end of December. Engagement has been ongoing since in an effort to understand and address the concerns of the CBA and I will continue to make every effort to engage with the aim of bringing the withdrawal of services and the consequent disproportionate impact on victims and witnesses to an end, where it is in my gift and it is appropriate. I must however follow due process which takes time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 19085/22-27, to detail any discussions he has had with primary care providers in relation to the accessibility of their online services, in order to assist patients who may have difficulty in using digital platforms when booking appointments.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of access to GP services for patients and my Department has made funding available to improve telephony infrastructure and improve accessibility, such as the use of on-line systems for ordering repeat prescriptions, helping to free up telephone lines and staff time.As independent contractors, and clinical professionals, GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care and management of individual patients.  This also means that decisions about the day to day running of the surgery, including the use of technology, telephony and the appointment system, are determined locally by the GP practice within the constraints of their available workforce.While there is no contractual obligation on GPs to provide an online platform for patients, the majority do offer practice websites providing information on their services and appointments system. Some GP practices offer online services for booking GP appointments and ordering repeat prescriptions. For patients who may have difficulty in using online platforms they still have the option to access services via the telephoneAs part of supporting work in this area, my Department established a GP Access Working Group, which includes both GP and service-user representation, to explore issues around accessing services and to oversee work to improve the experience for patients.The GP Access Working Group is currently overseeing development of a suite of guidance to cover good practice in demand management, and workflow optimisation to maximise efficiency along with consideration of how technology can be best used to improve experience for both patients and practices.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the discussions that have taken place between his Department and the Irish Football Association in relation to the implementation of a Mental Health Action Plan for the local game.
answer_text: My Department has not been involved in any discussions with the Irish Football Association in relation to the implementation of a Mental Health Action Plan for the local game. Mental Health falls within the remit of the Department of Health and the Public Health Authority, as their Arms Length Body.However, I have asked Sport NI, as an Arms Length Body of my Department, to provide detail of any discussions or actions they have taken on this matter.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Sport NI have not had any engagement with the Irish Football Association in relation to the implementation of a Mental Health Action Plan for the local game.Sport NI promotes Mental Health and Wellbeing through the Sport NI website.  We have regular meetings with PHA (approx. every 6 weeks) to ensure that public health messaging on mental health is current and linked to PHA resources.https://www.sportni.net/mental-health-and-well-being/mental-health-support/Sport NI has developed an introductory course on mental health and wellbeing in sport, available through the Sport NI e-learning Hub:https://www.sportlearningni.com/product?catalog=1715792606TQrQq%20”

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 19535/22-27, whether he will consider a change in the criteria for the sale or development of this land to enable the economic development in the area.
answer_text: The availability of industrial land plays an important role in attracting new economic investment to drive business growth and productivity.  Invest NI’s Regional Property Programme 2024-2034 gives a clear commitment to partnership working at a local level to identify specific industrial land and property needs, Invest NI is happy to explore development options for Balloo South Business Park with stakeholders to maximise its economic potential.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of learner drivers awaiting a practical driving test.
answer_text: Unvalidated management information taken from the Driver & Vehicle Agency’s booking system at 12pm on 14 January 2025, shows the average waiting time for a Category B (private car) practical driving test was 19 days and 4,934 candidates have a booking to undertake a Category B practical driving test up to 29 April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the waiting time for learner drivers to obtain a practical driving test.
answer_text: Unvalidated management information taken from the Driver & Vehicle Agency’s booking system at 12pm on 14 January 2025, shows the average waiting time for a Category B (private car) practical driving test was 19 days and 4,934 candidates have a booking to undertake a Category B practical driving test up to 29 April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the action his Department has taken in relation to the recent slurry spill into the Cotton River, which flows onto Ballyholme Beach; and (ii) the steps his Department will take to ensure there are no further pollution incidents in order to improve the water quality in the area.
answer_text: On Thursday 9 January 2025, NIEA received and investigated a report of pollution in the Cotton (Ballyholme) River, Bangor. Investigations identified a farm source and evidence was gathered on site by NIEA with immediate action required of the site manager  to stop further discharge. A follow up site visit on the morning of 10 January 2025 confirmed that the discharge had stopped. A major fishkill was confirmed. However, as this is a live investigation, no further information can be provided at this time. The pollution incident was confirmed as High Severity due to the environmental impact on the waterway and on Ballyholme Bay. The Local Council was notified and an “advice against bathing” notice issued. DAERA’s bathing water team collected water quality samples at a number of locations on Friday 10 January 2025 to monitor any impact.  The results showed very low bacterial counts noted indicating that the pollution in Ballyholme Bay had dispersed by the morning of 10 January.  The counts in the Cotton River were still elevated.My Department takes pollution incidents very seriously and all reports are investigated with action taken against the polluter where a source is identified. The Environmental Improvement Plan (EIP) also lays out my commitment to improving water quality throughout Northern Ireland, including the requirement to publish a third cycle River Basin Management Plan (RBMP).  The RBMP will contain a Programme of Measures, setting out the actions required to protect and improve all water bodies, including those in North Down and I am working to bring this forward in the very near future. Delivery of the plan will require collective action across government, the public, the private sector and wider society.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how he will expand clinical trial access in respect of blood cancer.
answer_text: Blood cancers including leukaemia, lymphoma, myeloma, myelodysplastic syndromes (MDS) and myeloproliferative neoplasms (MPN) each present with different symptoms, treatments and prognoses. Some blood cancers may present with vague, non-specific symptoms such as: pain, fatigue and recurring infections. Blood cancers are among the most common cancers in children and young people. It is widely acknowledged that there is a need for more equitable access to clinical trials for patients across all tumour sites in Northern Ireland. We know that the number of patients being offered and participating in cancer clinical trials remains below that reported in other jurisdictions. Cancer clinical trials are essential, not only to allow patients access to new or novel treatments, but also for attracting and developing a world-class workforce.  Blood cancer research in particular is experiencing rapid progress and innovation that can also inform developments in other tumour sites. While our haematology services endeavour to participate in clinical trials where feasible, there have been challenges opening new haematology clinical trials in recent years, particularly for children and young people.   These challenges are partly due to delays in the approvals process, and wider staffing pressures within haematology teams and in the broader health care staff, as well as access to facilities necessary for trial implementation.  Haematology services in NI have been under growing pressures for some time amidst serious workforce concerns for haematological services UK wide.  All Trusts in NI report consultant and middle-tier medical vacancies and challenges with recruitment and retention. This impacts timely service delivery and places significant pressure on the existing workforce, affecting the capacity to undertake service development and improvement, including clinical trial participation.  We acknowledge that improving access to clinical trials may also help attract haematology professionals to work in to Northern Ireland. With current pressures on our service, there are competing demands on the infrastructure required to support and facilitate clinical trials. The complex multidisciplinary nature of clinical trial delivery requires buy-in across Trusts with enhanced access to radiology, pathology and pharmacy services. The opening of trials continues to be challenged due to workforce constraints across all disciplines including research nurses and medical physics experts. Additionally, there is a need to ensure reliable funding streams and protected time within job plans to support clinical research across the region.Improving access to trials is an action of the NI Cancer Strategy 2022-2032. Action 56 of the NI Cancer strategy states, ‘Increase the number of people taking part in clinical trials, including children and young people.’  Officials in my Department are currently developing a Cancer Research Strategy for Northern Ireland, which will seek to help addre

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the steps his Department is taking to mitigate the impact of blood cancer in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The NI Cancer strategy 2022-2032 has several actions related to enhancements in blood cancers services.  Earlier diagnosis of cancer is one of the strategic themes within the Cancer Strategy. Improved awareness of the potential signs and symptoms of cancer is essential to ensuring this.   For example, Myeloma is rarely seen in a primary care setting and is therefore unfamiliar to most GPs.  Patients may also hesitate to visit their GP with general symptoms which they may associate with ageing, or other less serious conditions. As such, public awareness, GP awareness and access to rapid diagnostics is important.Action 6 of the NI Cancer strategy is to ‘deliver regular, effective, targeted evidence-based ‘Be Cancer Aware’ campaigns harnessing the expertise in the community and voluntary sector’. In 2022 Cancer Research UK in partnership with Public Health Agency (PHA) and NI Cancer Network (NICaN) launched a Spotting Cancer Early Campaign intended to encourage those aged 50+ from deprived areas of NI to contact their GP if they have noticed any signs or symptoms. It was also supported by CRUK web resources specifically for NI at Spot cancer early | Cancer Research UK. The PHA Be Cancer aware website also includes specific information on Hodgkin’s and non-Hodgkin’s lymphoma. General signs and symptoms of cancer | Be Cancer Aware (becancerawareni.info)Monthly Primary Care Education Sessions aimed at GPs, nurses, pharmacists and Allied Health Professionals have also taken place since 2021 to help improve understanding and awareness of red flag symptoms.  These sessions are delivered in partnership with BHSCT, Macmillan GP facilitators and NICaN.  A number of these have focussed on haematological Cancers for example, in May 2021 Myeloma was covered, February 2023 Lymphoma and most recently March 2024 Chronic Leukaemia.  My department has also set up 2 rapid diagnostic centres (RDCs) for referral of patients with vague symptoms.  GPs across NI can now refer into the RDCs those patients who do not necessarily meet suspect cancer referral guidelines but for whom they have concerns. This service should also help rapid diagnosis of patients with vague symptoms who may have a blood cancer.Other NI Cancer Strategy actions which will help improve blood cancer services include: action 16. ‘develop a specialist integrated haematological diagnostics service for Northern Ireland’.It is acknowledged that haematology services in NI and across UK have been under significant pressure for some time due to workforce issues.  In 2023 the DoH NI Cancer Policy and Projects Team commenced a project and produced three care pathway reports for Myeloma, Lymphoma and Leukaemia to help address the haematology service pressures. My Department is working with Health & Social Care Trusts to implement the Myeloma, Lymphoma and Leukaemia report recommendations. While some recommendations are cost neutral, others require significant investment and resource to implement.  Some prog

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps being taken to improve the retention of senior staff in all Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: My Department is continuing to engage with Senior Executive and Trade Union representatives around developing a set of reform proposals in relation to Senior Executive pay scales which are intended to address recruitment and retention issues affecting these key leadership roles.Retention is also a key theme within the Health and Social Care Workforce Strategy 2026 which was published by my Department in May 2018. This contains a range of actions to support HSC employers with staff retention. The latest report on progress towards implementation of the Strategy is published on my Department's website at: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/HSC-Workforce-Strategy-2026-Summary-Report.pdfMy Department has also introduced legislation enabling members of the 1995 Section of the HSC Pension Scheme to avail of a number of flexibilities, including partial retirement, with the hope they may help encourage senior staff to remain working within the HSC. A regional group of HSC employers and HSC Pension Service developed the necessary processes ensuring they are delivered consistently across HSC.  Flexible Retirement Guidance and application forms, alongside information sessions, were made available online to HSC Pension Scheme members on 2 December 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the proposals outlined in the UK Blood Cancer Action Plan.
answer_text: Whilst my Department acknowledges the helpful recommendations outlined within the UK Blood Cancer Action Plan, it should be stated that it was not consulted or involved in the development of this Action Plan.However, the recommendations around the themes of workforce, diagnosis and care, access to clinical trials and data, are all areas we acknowledge require work and we have been working to address these in Northern Ireland.Haematology services in NI have been under growing pressures for some time amidst serious workforce concerns for haematological services UK wide.  In order to address the severe regional pressures within haematology services, in 2023 the DoH NI Cancer Policy and Projects Team initiated a project for the redesign of Haematology Treatment Pathways across NI to include the three main haematological pathways: Myeloma, Lymphoma and Leukaemia. The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) of my Department were asked to implement the recommended actions in April 2024 and have engaged with Trusts on the reports’ recommendations.  While some recommendations are cost neutral, others require investment, and resource to implement.  Some progress has been made on early stage actions including baseline assessment and identification of nursing workforce gaps, maximising virtual clinics, identifying appropriate time and process for holistic needs assessment, weekly multi-disciplinary Team (MDT) meetings, adoption of NICE guidance on bispecific antibodies in Multiple Myeloma and development of a haematology cancer nurse career pathway. Progression of the actions will be dependent on consultant capacity, and upon reform and greater skill mix within haematology services, including haematology specialist and advanced nursing roles. These roles are key to releasing consultant capacity and provide alterative options for managing complex patients. The scale and pace of progress will be dependent on investment in the nursing workforce (action 39 of the NI Cancer Strategy) and Advanced Nurse Practitioners (ANPs) to take forward a significant number of the recommended actions in the DoH Haematology Pathway Reports.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the impact of the introduction of the Electronic Travel Authorisation Scheme for non-European travellers on the tourism sector.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any grants and support available for the repair and maintenance of listed buildings.
answer_text: This information is detailed on the Historic Environment Funding and Grants page on the Department’s website https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/topics/historic-environment-funding-grants

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of traffic light faults that have occurred in North Down in the last 12 months.
answer_text: I have been advised there have been 615 traffic light faults reported in the North Down area over the last twelve months, with 99% of repairs completed within established timescales.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how often the desilting of road gullies occurs across North Down.
answer_text: My Department’s current Limited Service Policy for road maintenance requires one gully clean to be undertaken each year.  Further ad-hoc cleaning may also be undertaken to address any specific localised issues as required, within available budgets.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of streetlight faults reported across North Down; and (ii) the number of defects which have been repaired, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: My Department does not hold the requested information on a constituency basis.  However, I can confirm that in Ards and North Down Council area, there were 3,867 street lighting outages recorded in the last 12 months, with 3,528 repaired to date.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the steps being taken to update his Department’s road safety policy to improve the road safety of motorcyclists.
answer_text: You will be aware I launched the Road Safety Strategy to 2030 in September 2024 and the supporting 2024/25 Action Plan.  The Action Plan contains 10 Strategic Interventions and 35 in-year Actions.  The Strategic Interventions are identified as those actions that will have the biggest impact on Road Safety, longer term, and include a review of drink driving and drug driving legislation.  The in-year actions are led by my Department and Road Safety Partners including the PSNI, Fire and Rescue Service, Ambulance Service, DAERA and DoJ.  The Road Safety Strategy was the subject of wide consultation, including input from motorcyclists.  It aims to improve road safety for everyone; while there are no specific actions for motorcyclists, the actions target road users in general, including motorcyclists, and in some cases vulnerable road users specifically, for example, ‘Share the Road to Zero’ .

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has any plans to review the Highway Code for Northern Ireland to extend the 1.5 metres overtaking distance for cyclists to include motorcyclists.
answer_text: The current guidance in the Highway Code for Northern Ireland for the overtaking distance for cyclists and motorcyclists is the same and is contained in rule 163, which does not refer to 1.5m.  The guidance is that you should “give motorcyclists, cyclists and horse riders at least as much room as you would when overtaking a car”.The average width of a car is 1.8m and therefore the current guidance is to allow for a greater distance than 1.5m when overtaking. It is also easier for most drivers to visualise the size of a car rather than a distance such as 1.5m.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the legislation that he plans to introduce during the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: My officials are currently developing two bills planned for introduction in 2024/25, on School Uniforms and Clothing Allowance Awards. Any additional legislation or bills for introduction during the rest of the mandate cannot be introduced without the agreement of the Northern Ireland Executive. Any further updates on legislative priorities will be communicated to the Northern Ireland Assembly as soon as they have been agreed by the Northern Ireland Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the legislation that he plans to introduce during the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: The legislation I plan to introduce before the end of the mandate is as follows:Decarbonisation Utility Regulator Powers, Non-domestic RHI Scheme Closure, Onshore Petroleum Licensing, Insolvency (Amendment) Bill, Good Jobs (Employment Rights), Energy – Offshore Renewable Energy Installations, Energy – Renewable Electricity Support Scheme, Energy Bill, The Price Marking (Amendment) Order and Credit Union Bill.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the legislation that she plans to introduce during the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: As you will be aware, it was not possible to introduce any legislation or progress key policy decisions in the absence of Ministers, the Executive and NI Assembly during a significant proportion of this period.Having reviewed and re-prioritised my previous legislative plans upon my return as Minister of Justice earlier this year, I intend to bring forward three Assembly Bills – equating to one per year - during the time remaining in this significantly shortened mandate.They include:a mixed-content Justice Bill – which was introduced to the Assembly on Tuesday 17 September 2024;a Sentencing Bill – for planned introduction by Autumn 2025; anda Victims Bill – for planned introduction by March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps being taken to restore the funding available to organisations through the Core Grant Funding Scheme, to pre-2023 levels.
answer_text: Subject to budget availability, it is my intention to increase the budget for the Department’s Core Grant Funding Scheme in the coming years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps being taken to support community groups and charities that provide services that promote public health and social wellbeing.
answer_text: I am committed to supporting the community and voluntary sector as much as possible in the knowledge that it can support my Department to meet its key strategic aims. My Department's Core Grant Funding Scheme currently provides £1.8m of financial support to community and voluntary sector organisations each year, to help them to meet core operating costs. I recently launched a new scheme for 2025/26 which has been co-designed with community and voluntary sector organisations and with the assistance of NICVA and, subject to budget availability, I will increase the Scheme budget in future years.In addition, the Public Health Agency supports around 125 community and voluntary organisations across the region to deliver local and regional community-led projects. In total they support around 300 community-led projects (including enhancements to existing projects) to a total value of £22m.The Agency provides a range of support to communities to increase community capacity, improve collaboration across sectors and influence the development of regional policy. This is done in partnership with the community and voluntary sectors through the Strengthening Communities for Health Steering Group, which oversees the delivery of a range of actions including the Elevate training programme, with a focus on health inequalities and the role of community.Other parts of the health and social care system also utilise and support the wider community and voluntary sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 18465/22-27, to detail any action being taken to ensure that Northern Ireland achieves the recommended number of heart failure nurses.
answer_text: As part of the work of the Department’s Heart Failure Strategic Leadership Group (under the remit of the Cardiac Network), a proposed draft regionally standardised heart failure pathway has been developed. It identified regional variance in the heart failure nurse workforce levels and subsequently the need for a more detailed workforce analysis to be undertaken, which is to progress in 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has any plans to bring forward legislation to improve animal welfare during the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: I am actively considering priority measures to improve animal welfare for the remainder of this mandate.  As such, I have recently met with members of the Northern Ireland Companion Animal Welfare Group to outline my initial proposals.  I will hold further meetings in the New Year with other stakeholders, including local councils and veterinary associations.  Following this engagement, it is my intention to publish a programme of reforms which I wish to see delivered within the lifetime of this Assembly.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the actions she has taken to increase the number of police officers.
answer_text: Whilst police officer numbers are an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is operationally accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board, I am convinced that we urgently need to stabilise and improve current police numbers.I have repeatedly raised the need for a better financial settlement for my Department, so that PSNI has adequate funding to stabilise and recover numbers back to a sustainable position with the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland, as well as with the Finance Minister and other Executive colleagues.My officials are currently working through a business case from PSNI on stabilisation and recruitment and we continue to work closely with PSNI colleagues to prepare a robust business case for onward submission to the Department of Finance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has engaged with Ards and North Down Borough Council in relation to improving coastal defences at Donaghadee harbour.
answer_text: The Department, along with DAERA, Councils with coastlines and the National Trust (NI) are working collaboratively to progress coastal erosion / coastal management issues through a Coastal Forum.I can advise that within Donaghadee my Department is responsible for a short section of sea wall that supports the public road, and which is considered separate from the harbour. My department has no plans to construct flood defences but will continue the inspection and maintenance regime in relation to designated watercourses and their associated infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the action being taken to address persistent flooding affecting Balloo Avenue, Bangor.
answer_text: Road drainage systems are designed to cope with normal annual average rainfall and can become temporarily overwhelmed during periods of high intensity rainfall and their effectiveness is dependent on the water levels in the open water course into which they discharge.I am advised that my officials do plan to carry out a full survey of the road drainage system in Balloo Avenue, Bangor when resources permit to ascertain if any remedial action can be taken.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures he will be taking to ensure people have access to NHS dental services.
answer_text: I am aware of the challenges currently being experienced within General Dental Services and I am committed to taking meaningful action to ensure the service works better for both patients and dental professionals. This is why for many months now, the focus of my Department has been on improving access to General Dental Services, particularly for high-need patients, and establishing options to support the sustainability of dental practices – such as the £9.2m investment announced earlier this year to reestablish the Enhanced Child Examination (ECE), establish the Dental Access Service (DAS), and fund the 30% enhancement to the fees paid for fillings.The DAS commenced on 1st August 2024. There are currently 30 practices participating in the scheme, details of which (including participating practices) can be viewed at:  https://online.hscni.net/dental-access-scheme/. To date the scheme has enabled the treatment of more than 6670 patients with an urgent or pressing dental condition.From June to November 2024, 18,100 newly registered children have benefitted from the Enhanced Child Examination scheme.Early indications are that these measures have had a positive impact and my Department will be monitoring the results closely.While interventions such as the DAS and ECE have been necessary to stabilise the service, long-term change will require sustained effort, rather than a single, one-off intervention. My officials will be engaging with the British Dental Association on how best to achieve these strategic aims, and address their concerns, as a matter of priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of social housing properties in North Down that are wheelchair accessible; and (ii) the number of applicants in North Down on the waiting list for wheelchair accessible properties.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“(i) Work is ongoing to survey all NIHE properties across NI to classify them on an Accessible Housing Register. Regrettably in the absence of the full picture we are not in a position at this time to provide the requested data.(ii) The table below provides waiting list and allocations data, as at end September 2024, for wheelchair users in the North Down Parliamentary Constituency.Parliamentary ConstituencyWheelchair User ApplicantsWheelchair User HS Applicants (30 points or more)Allocations to Wheelchair UsersNorth Down 656410Supporting NotesApplicant Information (all applicants and applicants in housing stress) is as at 30th September 2024. Allocation information refers to the number of allocations in the preceding 12 month period. Social housing waiting list and allocations data held by the Housing Executive, form part of the Northern Ireland Housing Bulletin, which is published quarterly as a National Statistic by the Department for Communities. Therefore, in order to avoid breaches of the code of practice for statistics, the most recent data provided is based on the most recently published data (end Sept 2024).”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of the level of business rates paid by small businesses in Northern Ireland compared to those in the rest of the UK.
answer_text: In total £275 million of support is provided to businesses through the rating system.Over 30,000 properties here out of a taxbase of around 75,000 properties currently receive a Small Business Rate Relief (SBRR) of between 20% and 50% at a cost of £21.5M. Over 4,400 manufacturing firms here receive 70% rate support through Industrial Derating at a cost of £75M. That support this isn’t available in England, Scotland or Wales. Over 50% of those in receipt of Industrial Derating fall under the valuation threshold that applies for SBRR. In total 70% of local non-domestic occupiers are in receipt of rates support in one form or another. Rates are a devolved matter and the system also applies local property values. The nature of our taxbase here is also very different. Our economy has a heavy reliance on SMEs which means the capacity for using income from large corporate ratepayers to cross-subsidise support is less available to the Executive than in other jurisdictions. Another difference in our system is the fact that two taxes are levied as one – namely the “regional rate” (set by the Executive) and the “district rate” (set by local councils). That is not the case in England, Scotland or Wales.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 17679/22-27, to detail the amount of land (i) sold; and (ii) developed for business use at the Balloo South Industrial Estate, Bangor, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: In the last ten years no land has been sold nor developed at Balloo South.   Invest NI will continue to market the estate as an attractive investment location and pursue property interests from those businesses meeting its intervention criteria.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the level of competition in the domestic (i) electricity market; and (ii) gas market in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: (i) The domestic electricity market has been open to competition since 2007 and currently has six competing suppliers. Power NI remain the incumbent supplier and continue to hold a higher proportion of the market.I would encourage electricity consumers to shop around and switch to the best deal. Consumers can get information on switching supplier and how to obtain the best deals, from the Consumer Council NI’s website.(ii) While the natural gas market has been fully open to competition for several years, there are a limited number of suppliers for the domestic sector. In the Greater Belfast area, there are two suppliers (SSE Airtricity and firmus energy). The incumbent suppliers in the two gas areas outside Greater Belfast (Firmus Energy in the Ten Towns and SSE Airtricity in the West) each remain the sole supplier for domestic gas customers.For those domestic gas consumers currently unable to shop around, protection is provided by the Utility Regulator's price control process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps being taken to increase the mileage allowance available as part of the Disabled Students' Allowance for higher education.
answer_text: Officials are in the process of reviewing the mileage allowance rate payable as part of the Disabled Students' Allowance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether Health and Social Care Trusts plan to roll-out advanced clinical practitioners in Northern Ireland, similar to that in England and Scotland.
answer_text: Advanced Practitioners play a key role in developing and sustaining the capacity and capability of the health and care workforce of the future and offer a dynamic, effective approach to addressing some of the pressures facing healthcare here. My Department is working with Higher Education Institutions and Health and Social Care Trusts to ensure that the requisite post registration training is commissioned to allow further roll out for Advanced Practitioner roles within Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the support currently available for people wanting to install renewable energy at their property.
answer_text: As you may be aware, the Windsor framework enables the extension to the 	North of the VAT relief for energy-saving materials that was previously 	available only in Britain. That means that people here will also be able to apply 	zero rates of VAT to the installation of energy-saving materials, such as solar 	panels.		Homeowners may also wish to seek independent advice on solar panels and 	other energy efficiency matters from organisations such as NI Energy Advice, 	the Energy Saving Trust or Citizen’s Advice. These technologies have the 	potential to provide an attractive payback to homeowners without grant support.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of Housing Executive properties in North Down that are void; and (ii) the actions being taken to reduce that number.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“(i) As of 4th December 2024 there were 24 actionable and 10 non-actionable voids in the Ards and North Down Parliamentary Constituency. Actionable voids are properties which are vacant while in the process of being allocated / awaiting change of tenancy repairs. These may also include properties deemed difficult to let. Non-Actionable voids are properties that are not immediately available for allocation and are held vacant for operational reasons i.e. decanting purposes (where they are being used to house a tenant on a temporary basis pending works to their home), planned maintenance schemes, and properties which have been identified for sale/transfer or have approval for demolition. (ii) Effective voids management is a key responsibility and core function of the Housing Executive. At a time when there is a critical shortage of housing stock available, the Housing Executive’s twin goals are to have the lowest possible number of void properties and to re-let good quality, secure and affordable housing as quickly and efficiently as possible. As an organisation, we have a target to relet properties within an average of 30 days; the Ards and North Down Area Office average was 23 days as of November 2024.  To achieve this performance the local office monitors both allocations, to ensure they are made in a timely manner, and change of tenancy repair work progress with contractors on a weekly basis, analysing voids, turnaround times and trends to identify and remove obstacles to service delivery. There is also an investment scheme planned in 2025 to reconfigure a small number of long-term voids at Ballyferris Walk Flats in Bangor.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she is seeking to reduce Corporation Tax in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: ANSWERI refer the Member to the answer to AQW 18801/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the current number of empty homes in North Down.
answer_text: The Land & Property Services (LPS) rating system does not hold information at a constituency level and does not maintain a register of 'empty homes'.For the purposes of this response, 'empty homes' is defined as properties included in the housing stock and recorded as vacant, on the LPS rating system.Please note, information in the rating system on domestic properties recorded as vacant may not be current or complete.
