question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the membership of the Northern Ireland Search and Rescue Practitioners Group; and (ii) the services each group provides.
answer_text: Membership of the Northern Ireland Search and Rescue (NISAR) Practitioners Group comprises representatives from both Statutory Agencies and Voluntary Organisations. It is chaired by an independently appointed chair. The members of the NISAR Practitioners Group being;Police Service for Northern Ireland - PSNI Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service - NIFRSNI Ambulance Service - NIASMaritime and Coastguard Agency - MCABelfast Harbour Police - BHPDepartment of Health - Medical Representative - Air Ambulance NIRoyal National Lifeboat Institute - RNLIBritish Red Cross Society - BRCCommunity Rescue Service - CRSFoyle Search and Rescue - FSARIrish Cave Rescue Organisation – ICROLagan Search and Rescue - LSARLough Neagh Rescue - LNRMourne Mountain Rescue Team - MMRTNorth West Mountain Rescue Team - NWMRTSearch and Rescue Dogs Ireland North – SARDA INSky Watch NI – SWNIThe services provided by Voluntary sector organisations encompass a wide range, including lowland search and rescue, mountain rescue, cave rescue, acute water rescue, lifeboat services, and dog and drone search and rescue operations.  Further details can be found on the NISAR page on the department’s website, https://www.justice-ni.gov.uk/articles/northern-ireland-search-and-rescue which includes links to the organisations’ websites.  These websites provide further information on the services offered, and organisations can be contacted directly for further inquiries.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she will be providing funding for local councils to cover the planned increase in employer National Insurance contributions.
answer_text: I refer the member to the response issued for AQW 18327 22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether there are any plans to regulate management companies to ensure that service charges are fair to residents.
answer_text: Management companies must act in accordance with existing companies legislation, which falls outside the remit of my Department. However, I also recognise that shared property management also attracts my Department’s interest in substantive land law, and I have been keen to make progress in examining this issue.I have engaged with officials to ensure the appropriate expertise in this complex policy area is secured. Resource has been identified and work will shortly commence on a study of the issues relating to the operation of property management companies and agents. That work will include the issue of service charges on residents. I will consider the outcome of that work, which I have asked to be completed by Easter, before determining the way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of pensioner households that are expected to receive the £100 winter fuel payment in the North Down constituency.
answer_text: Based on Winter 2023 data, it is estimated that approximately 13,290 households would have received the £100 fuel payment in the North Down constituency had the same rules applied last year.	Source Northern Ireland Winter Fuel Payment 2023/24

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department will be providing additional funding to the PSNI to cover the planned increase in employer National Insurance contributions.
answer_text: Discussions are underway between the local administration and HM Treasury regarding the impact of the increase in employer National Insurance contributions on Northern Ireland Departments.  My Department looks forward to the outcome of these discussions and, following this outcome, my Department can consider the funding allocations to individual business areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Housing Supply Strategy.
answer_text: The Housing Supply Strategy was agreed at the Executive meeting on 5 December 2024 and I announced its publication to the Assembly on 9 December. The Strategy can be accessed at the following link; https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/housing-supply-strategy-2024-2039

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting time for an attention deficit hyperactivity disorder diagnosis appointment at Scrabo Children’s Centre, Newtownards.
answer_text: The average wait time for an ADHD diagnosis appointment in Scrabo Children’s Centre is 442 days. My Department completed an assessment of paediatric ADHD pathways in Autumn 2023. This work has identified a number of inconsistencies and gaps across the region including different pathways to accessing services. Provision of service for ADHD assessment and diagnosis has developed across Trusts in response to population demand and available expertise within Health & Social Care teams.The limited capacity available in Trusts to meet demand and the level of referrals for ADHD assessment is leading to longer waiting times for assessment and support than advised in NICE guidelines. Children’s ADHD, and ASD, assessment and diagnostic pathways, and interventions will be addressed through the implementation of the HSC Children’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework. Departmental officials met with colleagues from the Public Health Agency and Child Health on 2nd December 2024 to inform the development of a draft inclusive neurodevelopmental pathway for consultation, that will include pathways for ADHD and ASD.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans he has to support primary care to ensure patients can appropriately speak to GPs and practice staff to properly register health concerns for triage and direction.
answer_text: My Department is committed to ensuring that patients will be able to continue to access high quality, sustainable GP services now and in the future and is taking forward a wide range of work with stakeholders across the system to help achieve that.In recent years, significant funding has been made available to meet the growing demands for primary care services and enable practices to improve their telephony systems, improve accessibility and increase staffing levels to ensure telephony demand is managed effectively.My Department has also established a GP Access Working Group to explore issues relating to access to services and to oversee work to improve the experience for patients. The Group includes representatives from General Practice and service users among its membership and one of its main aims will be to develop effective guidance documents and resources to help general practice manage demand more effectively, optimise workflow and standardise processes, with a view to increasing efficiency and improving access for patients.The Multi-Disciplinary Teams (MDT) model remains the key to stabilising and strengthening vital primary care services to ensure they can continue to provide high quality care, now and into the future.  Accordingly, a bid for transformation funding to support the further rollout of the MDT Programme across the five-year span of the transformation fund has been submitted.  To help primary care to deliver and grow, it is critical that we train, develop, and retain the GP workforce. The number of GP training places was previously increased to 121, an increase of 86% since 2015/16. The number of places will be retained at this level for 2024/25. My Department has worked with key stakeholders, to increase the exposure of undergraduates to General Practice; encourage more GP trainees; and ensure that there are sufficient GP training practices to meet demand.  GP Fellowship Schemes are supporting recently qualified GPs to transition from the training environment to independent practice, with opportunities for personal and professional development. The Attract, Recruit, Retain scheme supports recruitment of GPs in hard to recruit areas. Since its establishment in January 2023, 78 GP practices have applied and received approval in principle for funding under the scheme. 41 practices have applied and received final approval for payment in respect of a recruited GP. My Department has acted to streamline the recruitment processes for GPs who qualified in the ROI and we are currently updating the Northern Ireland Induction and Refresher schemes to align with the new General Medical Council (GMC) pathways for the recruitment of GPs from other countries joining the GMC GP register.  My Department has also introduced a Streamlined Returner process with the aim of removing unnecessary barriers to those wishing to return to the GP workforce.   In terms of the wider primary care workforce, we have continued to boost its capacity by in

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions being taken to help address childhood obesity.
answer_text: The current obesity prevention strategic framework, A Fitter Future for All, takes an integrated approach to obesity prevention and contains outcomes and actions across the life course for the entire population of Northern Ireland, including our children and young people.Work is underway to develop a replacement strategy, and a report on the consultation on ‘Healthy Futures – A Strategic Framework to Prevent the Harm caused by Obesity, and Improve Diets and Levels of Physical Activity in Northern Ireland’ is due to be published shortly. The Public Health Agency (PHA) invests in a number of obesity prevention programmes and work in partnership with statutory and non-statutory partners to promote activities that contribute to obesity prevention. Specifically, to help address childhood overweight and obesity, the PHA have developed and are funding the Early Years Obesity Prevention Programme (EYOPP) across NI. This targeted programme will address risk and protective factors for childhood obesity, adopting a holistic approach focusing on parenting skills, family lifestyle habits, diet and nutrition, physical activity, and emotional wellbeing.The PHA awarded a contract to HENRY (Health, Exercise & Nutrition for the Really Young - https://www.henry.org.uk/) in Feb 2020, to provide an early years obesity prevention training programme to Health and Social Care Trusts and SureStart staff across Northern Ireland. The programme aims to:equip health and early years practitioners with skills, knowledge, and confidence to tackle the issue of childhood obesity; and provide families & carers, with children aged 0-5, with a preventative group-based parent programme or a targeted 1-to-1 programme as appropriate.EYOPP coordinators and child health assistants are in post in each Trust to deliver programme to families. PHA - Raise, Engage, Refer training has been delivered as part of the EYOPP training package – this informs Allied Healthcare Professionals and others working in health on how to constructively raise the issue of obesity, engage with parents/carers and refer into the HENRY programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans he has to support GP practices' move towards a digital service to improve service delivery for patients.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of access to GP services for patients.  In response to increasing patient demand GPs are utilising a mixed consultation model with telephone consultations, video and face to face consultations as a means of managing the rising demand on services within the existing workforce while retaining responsiveness to those patients who need the most urgent attention and care. Currently around 50% of consultations are delivered face to face.As independent contractors, who provide primary medical services to their registered list of patients under the terms of the General Medical Services Contracts Regulations, and clinical professionals, GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care and management of individual patients.  This also means that decisions about the day to day running of the surgery, including the use of technology, telephony and the appointment system, are determined locally by the GP practice within the constraints of their available workforce.Additional measures have been put in place to support primary care to adapt flexibly to increasing demand.  These include focusing on increasing capacity to meet the demand from patients through, for example, reinforcing the GP workforce and increasing the range of healthcare professionals working in primary care, investment in general practice and the implementation of new and innovative ways of working, including making greater use of technology and telephony.  As part of supporting work in this area, my Department established a GP Access Working Group, which includes GP and service-user representation, to explore issues around accessing services and to oversee work to improve the experience for patients.  The Department continues to explore the role of technology in supporting better access for patients and a series of workshops are being planned to explore and scope priorities for digital development in primary care over the next 12-18 months and how this could improve patient experience.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he is taking to speed up the diagnosis for childhood attention deficit hyperactivity disorder and autism.
answer_text: Implementing the new regional crisis service is a commitment in the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031, under actions 12 and 27. The Department of Health Strategic Planning and Performance Group and the Public Health Agency are working in partnership to deliver on both of these actions and to develop a Regional Mental Health Crisis Service for all citizens of Northern Ireland. The components of the proposed regional mental health crisis service are: accessing mental health crisis services, primary care and interagency partnership, community crisis service, mental health liaison service and crisis resolution home treatment. A mapping report of current crisis services including community and voluntary sector is currently been finalised. Phase 2 future state work has commenced across lifespan services including scoping what a good service would look like. A Regional Mental Health Crisis Service workshop took place on the 11th October 2024 with relevant stakeholders across lifespan services. A draft Regional Mental Health Crisis Service Lifespan Pathway was presented. Stakeholders provided feedback on the draft pathway, discussed gaps within services and how best to take forward the next phase of work of the Regional Mental Health Crisis Service model. My Officials are currently reviewing the feedback in order to progress the Phase 2 future state work.In line with the usual process for commissioning of services, any decision to commission mental health crisis services which will include de-escalation services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of future budget availability. The needs of those in Belfast would be considered alongside those of other areas in the region

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps being taken to improve palliative care in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I am very aware of the importance of palliative and end of life care in supporting people with palliative care needs to live well and achieve the best quality of life as their illness progresses.The Palliative Care in Partnership Programme, co-led by my Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group and the Public Health Agency, continues to oversee the development and improvement of palliative and end of life care for adults across Northern Ireland.  The Partnership consists of members from across the HSC Trusts, NI Ambulance Service, hospice and independent palliative care providers, community and voluntary sector, primary care, pharmacy, and service users and carers.  The Partnership’s remit includes:ensuring the delivery of key priorities in palliative and end of life care both regionally and locally;ensuring what is designed and developed is person-centred; andadvising on and sharing best practice already in place to support people with palliative care needs.The Regional Palliative Care Work Plan 2023-26 sets out 4 Priority areas and associated workstreams: Early identification of and timely intervention for people with palliative care needs; Co-ordination & Quality Improvement in palliative care services; Palliative care education and training; and A public health approach to palliative care. A range of workstreams have been identified and are in place to support these priority areas.  These priorities and associated workstreams will inform and support the Palliative Care in Partnership Programme and its member organisations in taking forward a range of actions to further improve palliative and end of life care for adults. Other work being taken forward which supports palliative care is through the Department’s Cancer Strategy.  The Cancer Strategy Implementation Plan includes three actions on palliative care.  These are:Action 47 - Deliver integrated, co-ordinated and personalised palliative and end of life care to people with non-curative cancer when and where they need it.Action 48 - All people with non-curative cancer will have access to a palliative care key worker.Action 49 - Extend palliative and end-of-life support and continuity of care to seven-day working for all people with non-curative cancer.These actions are being taken forward through the PCiP workplan.Recurrent funding has been made available to support the implementation of the three palliative care actions.  In terms of palliative care for children and young people, the Strategy for Children’s Palliative and End-of-Life Care, published in 2016, sets out 23 objectives across the spectrum of paediatric palliative care from diagnosis through to bereavement care.In 2019 a Regional Paediatric Palliative Care Network was established and is chaired by a Paediatric Palliative Clinical Care Lead.  Membership comprises key clinical and nursing leads from each Health and Social Care Trust as well as the Northern Ireland Children’s Hospice.  The Network works closely toget

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when his Department's ten-year capital plan will be completed and published.
answer_text: The Department’s capital programme aims to facilitate the delivery of modern, fit for purpose services through the creation or maintenance of sustainable models of care that support communities and contribute to improved health, well-being and quality of life, and the provision of appropriate physical environments. In the absence of certainty of future revenue and capital budgets the Department’s capital plan remains under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has plans for Northern Ireland to join the NHS App, or to set up an equivalent NHS App.
answer_text: The NHS App cannot be used directly by Northern Ireland Health and Care organisations because of differences in the organisational structures and technical architectures between England and Northern Ireland. In Northern Ireland the HSC encompass programme is deploying the EPIC Electronic Patient Record solution in the Acute, Community Care and Mental Health sectors. This will provide patients access to their health and care information through a dedicated patient portal called MyCare which uses a website or a mobile phone app. MyCare does support proxy access for parents and those who care for others. The MyCare app supplements the Apps already in use to access the information and services provided by NI GPs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 17567/22-27, to detail any action the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust is taking to address the waiting list.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the support his Department will give to local councils to provide new or upgraded household recycling centres that can cater for modern recycling separation requirements.
answer_text: My Department’s Household Waste Recycling Collaborative Change Programme (HWRCCP) financially supports local councils to further increase both the quantity of and quality of recycling and reduce reliance on landfill, thereby realising the economic potential of recycling to the local economy. This includes support for transforming local council household waste recycling services including the provision of new or upgraded household recycling centres (HRCs) that can cater for modern recycling separation requirements.  To date, £8.3m has been made available for eleven projects, of which some £3.7m has been provided to four projects in three council areas for new HRCs or improvements to existing HRCs. 		Grants of up to 50% are available to support eligible projects, and I would encourage all Councils to submit applications to help further develop the local circular economy.   Further information on the HWRCCP and application process can be found on the DAERA website.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice why K9 Search and Rescue Northern Ireland has been denied membership of the Northern Ireland Search and Rescue Practitioners Group.
answer_text: The decision to decline the application from K9 Search and Rescue Northern Ireland for membership in the practitioners’ group was based on the agreed criteria set out in the application process.  Based on this process and an assessment of current service provisions, it was determined that there are already established canine search and rescue services in place, and no service gap in this area was identified that would necessitate additional resources.

To ask the Minister of Education whether the new delivery and implementation group, which is examining the development of a management authority to support controlled schools, will report by year end.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Fujitsu contract, including whether any payments have been made by the Education Authority to Fujitsu.
answer_text: The Education Authority and Fujitsu mutually agreed not to continue with the Strategic Partner / Schools Management System SPSMS Contract. The Education Authority has confirmed that no payments were made to Fujitsu under the SPSMS Contract.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 17564/22-27, to detail the action being taken to identify the source and bring this pollution to an end.
answer_text: Water pollution incidents investigated and confirmed in Ward Park, Bangor relate to discharges further upstream in the catchment, including domestic and industrial sources. The river catchment area above Ward Park extends to some nine square kilometres and this makes it extremely challenging to trace the source of pollution which becomes evident in the Park. Based on information from investigations, NIEA initiated a Proactive Pollution Prevention survey of priority sites in Balloo Industrial Estate in 2019. Forty priority sites were identified and inspected. Sixteen advisory and eight warning letters were issued under the Water (NI) Order 1999 which required actions to be taken. Twenty-one letters under the Control of Pollution (Oil Storage) Regulations 2010 were issued requiring businesses to take action to address non-compliance and reduce oil pollution and spillage risk. This initiative has reduced the number of water pollution incidents confirmed in Ward Park over the past five years from four to zero, with two reported again in 2024. All the confirmed incidents were assessed as being of Low Severity environmental impact. Whilst improvement has been noted, it remains the case that anyone observing water pollution should notify NIEA at the earliest opportunity, using the Incident Hotline, 0800 80 70 60.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current number of vacancies for heart failure nurses, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The table below details the current number of heart failure nurse vacancies and the current number of heart failure nurses employed, as provided by the Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts.HSC TrustHeart Failure Nurse Vacancies (WTE)Heart Failure Nurses Employed (WTE)Belfast1.009.30Northern1.604.50South Eastern08.60Southern08.00Western05.50The British Society of Heart Failure recommendation is for 2-4 heart failure nurses per 100,000 population. The nature of the NI HSC Trust system is that the patient’s Trust of residence may not necessarily correspond with the Trust in which the patient receives their treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of trainee places for heart failure nurses.
answer_text: The Department of Health does not commission trainee places specifically for Heart Failure Nurses. However, prospective candidates applying for heart failure specialist nursing roles are expected to either have or be willing to undertake a post-graduate training course in heart failure. The Department commissions a range of post-registration training programmes for cardiology nurses, which heart failure nurses can avail of. These include Specialist Practice Cardiology, which is required for all Specialist Nurses working within cardiology, and short courses. In the last four financial years 79 places have been commissioned on Cardiology Nursing Short Courses. Commissioning of these post-registration programmes is informed by HSC Trusts and Primary Care training needs analysis that is undertaken on an annual basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 17679/22-27, to detail the amount of (i) land sold; and (ii) developed for business use at the Balloo South Industrial Estate, Bangor, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: In the last five years no land has been sold nor developed at Balloo South.   Invest NI will continue to market the estate as an attractive investment location and pursue property interests from those businesses meeting its intervention criteria.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any actions being taken to acquire empty homes to improve the housing supply.
answer_text: My Department published a new Housing Supply Strategy on 9th December to address the wider challenges facing housing supply. It recognises the need to consider actions around the use of existing buildings to support housing supply. The detail of specific actions required will be considered as part of the action planning process.		I have also met with Chairs of the Registered Housing Associations to discuss voids and how they could potentially be used.  My Officials also continue to meet regularly with NIFHA to discuss this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of heart failure nurses; and (ii) the recommended number of heart failure nurses, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The table below details the current number of heart failure nurse vacancies and the current number of heart failure nurses employed, as provided by the Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts.HSC TrustHeart Failure Nurse Vacancies (WTE)Heart Failure Nurses Employed (WTE)Belfast1.009.30Northern1.604.50South Eastern08.60Southern08.00Western05.50The British Society of Heart Failure recommendation is for 2-4 heart failure nurses per 100,000 population. The nature of the NI HSC Trust system is that the patient’s Trust of residence may not necessarily correspond with the Trust in which the patient receives their treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the achievements of the Interim Regional Planning Commission to date.
answer_text: The interim Regional Planning Commission (iRPC) was established in early 2023 following the Public Accounts Committee report and recommendations on the planning system in the North.To date, the Commission has met on 8 occasions and discussed various issues including planning fees, training of new planning committee members, ongoing work to improve statutory consultees’ performance, and the progress of the Planning Improvement Programme.   It is also progressing a work programme to consider a number of thematic areas to understand the planning system in the North including best practice across the UK and Ireland, local council reviews, local development plans and front loading of planning applications. The minutes of the iRPC meetings are available to view on the Departments website and can be accessed here https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/interim-regional-planning-commission-minutes-meetings

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any consideration he has been given to granting local councils the power to vary business rates throughout their council area in order to help the regeneration of city and town centres.
answer_text: I have not given any consideration to this matter as the varying of Business rates within local Council areas does not fall within the remit of my department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has any plans allow departmental regeneration funding to be used to regenerate commercial and retail areas outside the core city or town centre boundaries, such as Ballyholme and Rathmore in Bangor.
answer_text: Over recent years the focus of regeneration activities has been on town and city centres given the significant issues that need to be addressed.  The operational area for urban regeneration programmes used by the Department is the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA) default classification that currently defines settlements of over 5,000 people as urban.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consideration he has given to allow council planners to condition developer contributions outside of the red line of an application.
answer_text: Developers may already be required to make contributions through the mechanism of a planning agreement to mitigate the impact of a proposal in order to overcome a policy or legislative barrier to the grant of planning permission. All such contributions must be reasonable and related to the development proposal in question and are not necessarily restricted to the red line application boundary.Planning agreements are, however, voluntary and, some councils may make more use of them than others.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action he has taken to speed up the response of statutory agencies to planning applications.
answer_text: There are many interconnected factors within the development management system that impact the performance of statutory bodies when responding to planning application consultation requests. These include the adequacy of available resources, the quality of applications entering the system and the number of consultation requests received by the various statutory bodies.  My Department works with statutory consultees and councils to help improve the planning consultation process through for example, the Planning Statutory Consultee Forum (PSCF). The PSCF meets on a regular basis to address issues impacting the effectiveness of the consultation process and to this end, has developed a best practice protocol for consultation for councils and consultees alike. See link attached.https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/consultations-planning-application-process-operating-principles-planning-authorities-and-consulteesTackling the cross-cutting issues that affect consultee response times is integral to the Planning Improvement Programme and my Department has developed legislation to allow for the introduction of validation checklists which should improve the quality of planning applications entering the system and thus reduce the amount of time that consultees spend on consultation and re-consultation responses.  My Department has also developed and monitors quarterly and annual performance reports for statutory consultations in the North - see link aside - https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/statutory-planning-consultations-quarterly-annual-reports	In terms of resources, my Department has made a bid to the Interim Public Sector Transformation Board to support the role of those statutory consultees within my Department. I am hopeful that this bid will ultimately be successful and will assist in increasing the capacity and efficiency of statutory consultee responses. 		My Department is working tirelessly to improve the planning statutory consultation process however, it must be recognised that improving this process is a collective issue, and all stakeholders must play their part in delivering the necessary changes and improvements to make the system more efficient and effective going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 15749/22-27 and AQW 17681/22-27, for his assessment of whether the figures for North Down brings into question his strategy to focus investment outside of the Greater Belfast area.
answer_text: There is a need to grow the economy across all areas of the North and this is one of my economic priorities. My Sub-Regional Economic Plan details how I plan to achieve this.Ards and North Down benefits from its proximity to Belfast. My Department and Invest NI also recognise the need to do more in Ards and North Down to improve the business base, to grow productivity performance and to encourage higher productivity businesses in the area. This imperative is reflected in the Sub-Regional Economic Plan and Invest NI’s draft 2024/25 – 26/27 Business Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail funding provided by the Arts Council in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Arts Council.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive.  Due to the volume of detail required to provide an answer to your question, I have arranged for this to be placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he plans to give local councils more powers to address the issue of bins left on public footways beyond their collection date.
answer_text: The Department has responded to previous queries on this matter, requesting that district councils demonstrate that such powers are necessary, and all other potential options have been given due consideration. This would be seen as a prerequisite before committing significant resource to amending primary legislation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the schools in North Down that will be included in the next roll-out of the part-time 20mph speed limit zoning.
answer_text: A review of the initiative introducing part-time 20mph speed limits at schools has been undertaken and I recently discussed the review report with my officials. The report has been shared with the Committee for Infrastructure and arrangements are currently underway to make it publicly available One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe.  The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the roll-out of LED street lighting in North Down.
answer_text: I can confirm that as of 19 November 2024 there are a total of 25,907 street lights in the North Down and Ards Council area of which 14,511 (56%) have now been upgraded to LED lanterns.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she plans to use any of the Barnett consequential of the 40 per cent rate relief scheme in England, announced by the Chancellor in the Autumn Budget 2024, to provide rates relief to independent retailers.
answer_text: I refer the member to my answer to AQW 17794/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the proposed new health and social care centre in Bangor.
answer_text: Plans to replace Bangor Health Centre and Bangor Hospital with a new Primary and Community Care Centre are included in the regional Primary Care Infrastructure Development Strategic Implementation Plan.  This project has also been identified as a key priority in the South Eastern Trust’s Capital Plan which has been submitted to the Department of Health.The South Eastern Trust awaits the outcome of the Department of Health’s Regional 10-year Capital Plan, which will identify when funding will be available to enable this project to commence.  In advance of this, an Outline Business Case is being developed by the Trust, which will include an assessment of potential locations for any development.The Department of Health remains committed to working with the Strategic Planning and Performance Group and the South Eastern Trust to progress this project as soon as possible to deliver new primary and community care infrastructure for the Ards and North Down population.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any action his Department will be taking to encourage footfall and to assist city centre retailers in Bangor whilst the Queen’s Parade development takes place.
answer_text: My officials in the Department’s Regional Development Office work closely with colleagues in Ards and North Down Borough Council to identify and deliver projects that will have a positive impact on the urban centres across the Borough. This work will naturally continue during the regeneration of Queen’s Parade and all parties will be mindful of the potential for disruption during any construction works. The Developer’s team will also be engaging with the local community to communicate plans and provide contact points. The regeneration of Queen’s Parade will be a truly transformational project for the people of Bangor.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether smart electricity meters will be rolled out in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My department has developed a design plan proposing principles for the roll-out of smart electricity meters in the North of Ireland. There is currently a public consultation on these design principles. The consultation will run until 12th December 2024.On closing of the consultation, the Department will analyse the responses and produce a decision paper.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the support provided by his Department to Ards and North Down Borough Council to meet the 70 per cent recycling target, as set out in the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: My department has a strong record of engagement with all eleven local councils to ensure we are well-equipped to meet the ambitious recycling targets set in legislation.  DAERA has recently collaborated with Ards & North Down Borough Council to model a range of potential waste collection scenarios, designed to optimise waste collections and enhance recycling in the borough. The Household Waste Recycling Collaborative Change Programme (HWRCCP) offers financial assistance to local councils to transform kerbside recycling and Household Waste Recycling Centre (HWRC) infrastructure and services. The programme is designed to support projects that enhance the quality and quantity of recycling and offers 50% match-funding to successful applications.DAERA provides funding to the Waste & Resources Action Programme (WRAP) to provide essential local authority support, including specialist technical guidance and the development of the necessary evidence base for implementing change. The programme also includes collaborative efforts with local councils on key national and regional behaviour change campaigns through the National Communications Advisory Panel (NCAP), for example Recycle Week and Food Waste Action Week, designed to enhance the quality and quantity of recycling.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on Reshaping Stroke Care.
answer_text: My Department published the Reshaping Stroke Care Stroke Action Plan in June 2022. The Action Plan, informed by a prior public consultation, sets out six priority areas to improve services both in our hospitals and in our communities. While capacity and funding constraints have impacted the pace of delivery, progress has nonetheless been made against those priorities. Progress includes the development of a regional model for TIA (mini-stroke) and subsequent Trust action plans; a costed model identifying the key requirements to expand thrombectomy to 24/7; a revised thrombectomy repatriation protocol; and service models for community stroke services and long term support. Artificial Intelligence software has been rolled out in six hospitals to provide advanced imaging and rapid information to support clinical decision making. In terms of stroke configuration, a Hyperacute Stroke Project Board  has been established and has agreed a new process to identify the optimal configuration for stroke services. This is being informed by updated modelling which is being taken forward by the University of Exeter. Members with lived experience of stroke joined the Project Board in June 2024 and a Clinical Advisory Task and Finish Group was established in November 2024 to advise the Project Board. In addition, preparatory work for a stroke workforce review is underway. It is intended that the review will commence early in 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the location of Nurture Groups in schools in North Down.
answer_text: The Department funds 62 Nurture Groups throughout Northern Ireland. There are three Nurture Groups located in Bangor, North Down: Bloomfield Primary School (establ. 2020), Clandeboye Primary School (establ. 2022) and Kilcooley Primary School (establ. 2016).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of recommendations from the report on the Review of the Implementation of the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 that have been carried out to date; and (ii) when this work will be completed.
answer_text: The report on the Review of the Implementation of the Planning Act (NI) 2011 made numerous recommendations/actions covering key elements of the planning system.  Good progress has already been made, including the implementation of a planning fee inflationary uplift; amendments to subordinate legislation enabling councils to bring forward planning application validation checklists which will improve the quality of applications entering the system; the review of the Planning (Development Management) Regulations (NI) 2015; and a review of the internal administration of the ‘call-in’ process. Enhanced use of digital technology in the planning system has also been aided with introduction of the Regional Planning IT System (Planning Portal).I will continue to review progress with my officials.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will be reviewing the intervention levels for repairing road defaults.
answer_text: My Department’s resource budget, which is used to deliver day-to-day maintenance activities such as patching and grass cutting, has been under pressure for a number of years. As a result, my Department has had to reduce its routine road maintenance activities to a limited service, which prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety and protecting the transport network. Consequently, only the highest priority defects across the network are currently being repaired.The level of repairs that can be carried out in future financial years will depend on the 2025-26 final budget determination. Until such times as sufficient funding is made available, it will not be possible to change the current level of service. The importance of a well maintained road network cannot be understated but this requires investment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans his Department has to expand park and ride facilities in North Down.
answer_text: Enhancing connectivity is one of my key priorities and my focus will be on building that connectivity through public transport and active travel, including opportunities for further park and ride schemes.My Department has identified a list of priority bus and rail-based Park and Ride sites which will be considered within the new Transport Strategy and emerging transport plans.While there are no plans at present to expand park and ride facilities in North Down, within the wider Ards and North Down area, potential Park and Ride sites have been identified in Newtownards and Comber. The development of these schemes is currently being progressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the current status of all School Enhancement Programme applications in North Down.
answer_text: There are five schools progressing under the School Enhancement Programme (SEP) in North Down.  Integrated Consultancy Teams have been appointed for each of these projects which are at various stages of design and planning as shown in the following table:School NameRIBA StageSt Columbanus’ College, BangorStage 2 – Concept DesignSullivan Upper School, Holywood Stage 2 – Concept DesignGlencraig Primary School, HolywoodStage 1 – Technical FeasibilityKilcooley Primary School, BangorStage 1 – Technical FeasibilityClifton Special School, BangorStage 1 – Technical Feasibility

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has engaged with the Chancellor following the Autumn Budget 2024 in relation to extending national insurance contributions relief to dentists, particularly NHS dentists, in order to save practices and prevent costs being passed on to patients.
answer_text: I have not engaged with the Chancellor on this matter as procedure would prevent that. Whilst supportive of the intent, it would be for the Minister of Finance to make any such engagement. It is not possible to provide a definitive assessment on our future funding position until a budget settlement for future years has been reached. Despite the positive news about the budget, it is still likely we will be facing a significant funding gap in Northern Ireland as a result of both inflationary pressures and increased demand.I acknowledge there are a number of healthcare providers that will be affected by the rise in employer National Insurance contributions announced in the Budget and I understand that as well as General Dental Practitioners, GPs, care homes and hospices have voiced concerns about the impact this will have on them. Recognising this and the valuable role played by those providers, these funding pressures will nevertheless need to be considered and prioritised alongside other priorities before I am in a position to make any commitments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any planned capital works to libraries in North Down.
answer_text: At present there are no capital works planned for the three libraries in the North Down constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his plan to adequately resource NI Water.
answer_text: NI Water’s investment needs, to deliver essential water and wastewater services, are defined in the independent Utility Regulator’s Price Control 21 (PC21) which covers a six-year investment period from 2021-27.  My Department has been operating in a challenging budgetary position for some time. Despite this, NI Water was fully funded for the first three years of PC21 and this year I have provided NI Water with a budget allocation of almost £0.5 billion for 2024-25. This represents just under 40% of the total non-ring-fenced budget for my Department. Furthermore, I am taking a three-pronged approach to this issue in: (i) continuing to work with my Executive colleagues to try and increase wastewater investment; (ii) by reviewing the policy and legislation around developer contributions; and (iii) I have secured Executive agreement for the Water, Flooding and Sustainable Drainage Bill to be introduced to the Assembly. This legislation will provide powers to encourage increased use of sustainable drainage systems which use nature to separate and release storm water more slowly into the system so that it does not overrun wastewater treatment works.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any actions being taken to replace lead water pipes from the water network.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised that NI Water is responsible for the portion of the service pipe from the watermain to the house/property boundary (known as the communication pipe), with the remainder of the pipe within private property, the responsibility of the property owner (known as the supply pipe). NI Water completes replacement of lead communication pipes through the following four streams of work:Lead communication pipes replaced because of customers notifying NI Water that they are replacing their lead supply pipe.Lead communication pipes replaced because of a water quality sample failure.Lead communication pipes replaced during watermain replacement work.Lead communication pipes replaced under the ‘pro-active lead replacement programme’.NI Water also provides advice and guidance for customers to check for lead pipework within their home if it was built before 1970 and currently has a 25-year programme to identify and replace lead pipes within its infrastructure.  In addition, through its Water Mains Rehabilitation Programme, NI Water informs customers when lead communication pipes have been replaced and encourage customers to replace any lead supply pipes within their private property.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether the PSNI will be provided with adequate capital budget to fulfil its plans following the acquisition of the former Kinnegar army base, Holywood.
To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the future of Rathgael House, Bangor and whether there are any plans to relocate Department of Education staff.
answer_text: The Department is considering sustainable long term accommodation options for NICS staff in the Bangor area, including in consultation with the local Council. Staff and Trade Union representatives will be consulted when the options have been confirmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the location of any Invest NI land in North Down.
answer_text: Invest NI holds 51 acres of land at its Balloo South Industrial Estate in Bangor.  Of this 19 acres remain available to support new investment, the remainder having been sold and developed for business use.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any planned action to expand the cycling network in North Down.
answer_text: My Department has developed a draft Active Travel Delivery Plan that includes an update of our policies and design guidance for Active Travel as well as network plans for our larger towns and cities, including Bangor, Donaghadee and Holywood. Public consultation on the draft Plan runs until 28 February 2025 and I would encourage all those with an interest in transport to engage with the consultation. When complete, the Active Travel Delivery Plan will complement the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan and the Strategic Plan for Greenways to provide a firm basis for the future prioritisation, design, and delivery of pedestrian and cycling improvements in our urban and rural settlements over the next ten years and beyond. My Department also continues to work with councils to better understand their five-year active travel programme and to develop an effective grant and support framework for the effective delivery of greenways and other measures to further promote and encourage active travel. Officials are working closely with colleagues in Ards and North Down Borough Council and I have approved funding of over £2.5 million towards delivery of the Bangor and Newtownards Greenway.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the new build for Crawfordsburn Primary School.
answer_text: The procurement process to appoint a building contractor is well underway. The call for tender is due to issue in mid-November and it is anticipated that the contract for the new build school will be awarded in March 2025.

answer_text: Invest NI reports its performance on a financial year basis with the year ending 31st March 2024 being the most recent available.  During the period 2019-20 to 2023-24, Invest NI provided support to businesses which will lead to the creation of 20,259 new jobs, the details of which are set out in the table below by Parliamentary Constituency and financial year.Invest NI New Jobs by Parliamentary Constituency Area (19-20 to 23-24)PCA19-2020-2121-2222-2323-24TotalBelfast East2081564113477616Belfast North7591752571336681,992Belfast South and Mid Down2,0027961,3499241,4906,561Belfast West1211415723262604East Antrim3931417173255East Londonderry25297668061556Fermanagh and South Tyrone23323810584119779Foyle187142463832711,029Lagan Valley1461887937432819Mid Ulster536161134991191,049Newry and Armagh17211285175109653North Antrim8144337555288North Down70207163126350South Antrim95812674244529South Down1115313676148524Strangford653681834114599Upper Bann187124797211511,262West Tyrone158107458033423NI Wide / Not Yet Determined3001866451201101,361Total5,7223,4063,4993,6593,96220,248Notes:Table totals may not add due to rounding.In addition to new jobs supported directly within businesses in the last five years, External Delivery Organisations or universities were offered support to create 11 new jobs through projects or initiatives that will benefit businesses across the whole of Northern Ireland.	In addition to financial assistance, Invest NI provided wider support to all businesses through advice, assistance or guidance from our staff and through nibusinessinfo.co.uk, our online business advice portal. 		For the Member’s future reference, Invest NI proactively publishes a full breakdown of financial support offered to every business over the past 5 years on the NI Open Data online portal which can be accessed from the following link: Invest NI Open Data.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any planned action to improve road safety on the A2 Bangor to Belfast road.
answer_text: Road safety remains an extremely high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users. Following the latest release of Road Traffic Collision data from the PSNI my officials are reviewing sites where there are clusters of collisions on the A2 to identify if there are any common causation factors which would benefit from the introduction of effective road safety measures. Any proposed engineering measures are proposed will be considered for inclusion in a future work programme, subject to available funding.Concerns regarding driver behaviour such as speeding should be directed to the PSNI using their non-emergency 101 number.One of the best ways to ensure the safety of our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury on roads by 2050 is by changing driver behaviour.  As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe.  The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he intends to review the High Hedges Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 to expand the Act's coverage beyond deciduous hedges and hedges comprising only one species, to all vegetation that block sunlight to property.
answer_text: The High Hedges Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 applies to hedges that are formed wholly or predominantly by a line of two or more evergreen or semi evergreen trees or shrub, that rise to a height more than two metres above ground level. I have no plans to review this at present.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the average waiting time; and (ii) the waiting time target for an urgent MRI Scan in (a) the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust; and (b) the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The average waiting time for an MRI scan in the Belfast HSC Trust is currently in the region of 12 weeks.  Within this, the average waiting time for an urgent MRI scan in the Belfast HSC Trust is 10 weeks.  As the tertiary centre, the Belfast HSC Trust responds not just to its local population requirements but also those from across the region, including from the South Eastern Trust.  The average waiting time for an MRI scan in the South Eastern HSC Trust is currently in the region of 1 year.  Within this, the average waiting time for an urgent MRI scan in the Southern Eastern HSC Trust is currently in the region of 15 weeks, although patients may be seen sooner based on their specific clinical requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether a child is eligible for a second year of funded pre-school education if a decision is taken to defer their school starting year.
answer_text: Should a parent of a child who has already availed of a pre-school place, choose to defer their child's start to Primary 1 they will have the opportunity to apply for a second year of pre-school education during the pre-school admissions process.  However, the law requires that children can only be prioritised as target age once within the pre-school admissions process.  Therefore, any child who has attended or currently attends a funded pre-school education place, as a final pre-school year child, and applies for a second year of funded pre-school education would only be considered at Stage Two of the admissions process and after all target age applications have been considered and no guarantee can be given that they would obtain a place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of reported water pollution incidents in Ward Park, Bangor in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The number of reported water pollution incidents in Ward Park, Bangor in the inclusive period 2019 to 2023 are detailed in the Table below. All confirmed incidents were classified as of low severity environmental impact.YearTotal ReportedUnconfirmed from investigation201962202052202154202232202322Total2112
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when he will make a decision in relation to residents’ parking schemes.
answer_text: The review of residents’ parking schemes is complete, and I have had the opportunity to consider the draft review report.  I have asked that the report be shared with the Infrastructure Committee and for it then to be published.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action he is taking to unlock building developments currently held up with waste and sewerage connection issues.
answer_text: I am taking a three-pronged approach to this matter:  (i) my officials are currently examining the legislative and regulatory frameworks to assess the feasibility of developers being able to make financial contributions to NI Water in order to improve waste and sewerage infrastructure and facilitate development; (ii) I am also bringing forward legislation to encourage increased use of nature-based solutions such as Sustainable Drainage Systems in developments to separate and release stormwater more slowly to the system so that it does not overrun wastewater infrastructure. This can also reduce flooding and pollution risks ; and (iii) I continue to work with Executive colleagues to try and increase wastewater investment. In addition, NI Water has established a Development Constraints Project Team, to help alleviate capacity issues and is also encouraging developers and wastewater management professionals to engage with it early, through the Pre- Development Enquiry process, to try and find a resolution to any issues at an early stage, to help facilitate development, where possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update the new build for Bangor Central Integrated Primary School.
answer_text: I announced Bangor Central Integrated Primary School to proceed to construction on 28 May 2024. However, following a review of the proposed project a reduction in the total enrolment numbers at the school in recent years was identified, which prompted my officials to engage with school representatives and the school’s Managing Authority regarding the implications of this reduction in enrolment.  I am happy to report that agreement regarding the size of the school and provision of Enhanced Learning Support has been reached between all parties and the Integrated Consultant Team are now updating the final design before procurement of an Integrated Supply Team is undertaken in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when his Department will introduce the necessary legislation to allow local councils to set their own tariffs for off-street parking.
answer_text: On 1 April 2015 the Off-street Parking (Functions of District Councils) Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 transferred responsibility for the majority of off-street car parks to local councils and since then my officials have been engaging with a number of councils in respect of them introducing their own off-street parking orders.  Some councils have raised queries concerning the outworkings of the legislation which transferred those powers to councils in 2015, and officials from my Department have been liaising with councils seeking to address those issues. Legal advice indicates it will necessitate a legislative solution that will be subject to the draft affirmative resolution procedure, fully divesting all councils from the legislative requirements of the Off-Street Parking Order (Northern Ireland) 2000. However, for this legislative solution to progress councils will need to have their own orders fully drafted and in a state of readiness for implementation. Until this work has been completed by councils, my Department is unable to implement its intended legislative solution. In the interim, my officials have by way of assistance provided councils with a comprehensive template for an order, as well as drafting guidance and advice and are continuing to engage with council representatives with a view to implementing the legislative solution as soon as practicable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action is he taking to address the lack of foster carers.
answer_text: Recruitment of new foster carers is a significant challenge across N Ireland specifically and across the UK generally. A number of underpinning factors are acknowledged as part of this challenge; the number of women working outside the home pursuing careers, a perception that Looked After Children have increasingly complex needs, concerns about the adequacy of financial support offered, particularly in the context of the rising cost of living and perceived lack of therapeutic support for the children and support generally for the carers.The Independent Review of Children’s Services in Northern Ireland report was published in June 2023 and amongst the recommendations made, there was an acknowledgement that foster carers should be recognised and positioned as valued members of the children’s social care workforce. The Department recognises that foster carers are an extremely important and a valued part of the child care system, providing care to some of the most vulnerable children in our society.Recognising the central role of foster carers as members of the Health and Social Care community, the Children’s Social Care Services Strategic Reform Board established a dedicated Fostering workstream focused on foster care recruitment and retention. The development of a recruitment and retention strategy is already underway, with options being considered to uplift foster care allowances; improve the practical, therapeutic and training supports available for foster carers; and embed engagement with foster carers as a routine part of the recruitment and retention process. Additionally, the Department is currently bringing forward new regulations for foster placements and fostering agencies.  The regulations aim to improve the assessment and approval process for foster parents, and will place new duties on Trusts and adoption agencies in respect of the information, training and support they provide to foster carers. It is intended that the regulations will be complemented by new standards which are being developed in partnership with stakeholders, including foster parents and those they care for.Over the past number of years, the Department has supported Foster Care Fortnight alongside The Fostering Network, which aims to raise the profile of fostering and show how foster care transforms lives.  It has also provided funding for the Foster Care Awards, which celebrates the contribution of all foster carers to the lives of those in their care, and presents specific awards for Foster Carer and Kinship Carer of the Year.HSC Trusts proactively recruit foster carers on an ongoing basis, with active recruitment events taking place throughout all Trusts every month.  This ongoing process of recruitment is supplemented by periodic targeted recruitment, often linked to main regional events such as the Belfast Mela Festival and Balmoral Show. Information for those interested in fostering is available on the HSC Adoption and Foster Care website, bolstered by daily upda

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether Translink plans to roll-out wheelchair seat belts on all buses.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and can confirm that Translink will comply with legislation on vehicle specification, including the fitment of seatbelts.  There are currently no plans to roll-out wheelchair seatbelts on all buses.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the installation of a controlled pedestrian crossing on East Circular Road, at the junction of Ballymaconnell Road, Bangor.
answer_text: Details of the planned programme of works for the 2024/25 financial year in the Ards and North Down Council area are included in my Department’s Annual Report presented to Council on 8 October 2024 published on my Department’s website; https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/annual-report-ards-and-north-down-borough-council-24-25.pdf Whilst a new controlled crossing on the A2 East Circular Road, Bangor was previously identified in the 23/24 Council report for work to be completed during the 24/25 financial year, delivery of this scheme has been delayed due to the availability of resources and contractor capacity. This scheme will therefore be considered for inclusion in future work programmes subject to the availability of resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Bangor Waterfront Belfast Region City Deal project.
answer_text: My officials are working collaboratively with colleagues in the Ards and North Down Borough Council to support the development and delivery of the £72.8m Bangor Waterfront Regeneration Programme, funded by the NI Executive through the Belfast Region City Deal. In line with the Outline Business Case, the Council is working to appoint the design teams for each of the five projects within the programme. To date, 2 design teams have been appointed, with a further 3 to follow in due course. My Officials are also working with Council colleagues on the development of a Social Value Action Plan for the project, which will further enhance the aspirational economic benefits.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the level of (i) domestic; and (ii) non-domestic rates unpaid in the Ards and North Down Borough Council area in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Land & Property Services produces annual statistics showing the amount of domestic and non-domestic rating debt and the count of occupancies with rating debt at the end of each rating year, by District Council. The statistics are available on Open Data NI and can be found at:https://admin.opendatani.gov.uk/dataset/annual-end-of-year-rating-debt-by-district-council-and-sector

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the controlled pedestrian crossing on Bloomfield Road, Bangor, will be operational.
answer_text: Unfortunately, due to an electrical supply issue, there has been a delay in making the controlled crossing on Bloomfield Road Bangor operational.  While I have no definitive date when the crossing will be opened for use, my officials are hopeful that this issue will be resolved in the coming months.  My officials are currently engaging with the nominated contractor with regard to a definitive timescale.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he was aware of any discussions between his former Special Adviser and the Vice Chancellor at Ulster University during the summer in relation to his former Special Adviser taking up a new position.
answer_text: I was not aware of any discussions between my former Special Adviser and the Vice Chancellor at Ulster University during the summer in relation to my former Special Adviser taking up a new position.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline when a permanent Public Sector Transformation Board will be put in place.

To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the timescale for providing the additional funding he recently announced for grassroots sports clubs, to mark the success of local Paris Olympic and Paralympic athletes.
answer_text: I announced the additional funding for grassroots sports clubs through an Olympic Medallists’ Fund at the Paris Celebration event on 3 October 2024 in the SSE Arena.Earlier that week, Sport NI, an arm’s length body of my Department, had approached the six medallists to advise them of the scheme. All were happy to participate.Sport NI is now working with the six medallists to identify suitable schemes to be supported by the Fund.  The CEO of Sport NI has advised me that a detailed delivery timescale is currently under development with all funding to be delivered by 31 March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) when his former Special Adviser informed him of her intention to resign; and (ii) when her employment was terminated.
answer_text: I received notification of my former Special Adviser’s resignation on 04 August 2024.  A notice period of less than 5 weeks was agreed by mutual consent and their last day of service was 13 August 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his former Special Adviser satisfied the terms and conditions in paragraph 30 of the Letter of Appointment to be Issued to Special Adviser, relating to her period of notice.
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer I gave on 16 October 2024 to AQW 16646/22-27.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of whether his former Special Adviser complied with the Code of Conduct for Special Advisers during her tenure.
answer_text: It is my assessment that my former Special Advisor complied with the Code of Conduct for Special Advisers during her tenure.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the recruitment process for the two appointments to co-lead the creation of the new Ulster University Strategic Policy Unit.
answer_text: Ulster University is an autonomous institution responsible their own policies and procedures including those which relate to recruitment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, in relation to the two appointments to co-lead the creation of the new Ulster University Strategic Policy Unit, to detail (i) how the positions were advertised; (ii) the number of applicants that applied for the posts; (ii) the number of applicants that were shortlisted for the posts; and (iv) the number of applicants that were interviewed for the posts.
answer_text: Ulster University is an autonomous institution responsible their own policies and procedures including those which relate to recruitment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of PSNI officers stationed in the North Down constituency (i) in September 2010; and (ii) in September 2024.
answer_text: PSNI resourcing is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board. I would suggest that you should direct your question to the Chief Constable for a detailed response.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail his Department's forecast for new foreign direct investment for the next (i) three years; and (ii) five years.
answer_text: Working with Invest NI, we have agreed ambitious targets to attract first time investors to the North. Invest NI’s Business Strategy is currently out for consultation. It sets out a target to attract 60 new first-time investors over the next 3 years. You will also be aware that I recently launched a Sub Regional Development Plan, focussed on increasing the percentage of all investments being made beyond the Belfast Metropolitan Area to drive opportunity and investment right across the North regional balance. New FDI and international business expansions will of course have an important part to play in delivering that objective.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department has carried out any analysis and identified any significant changes in the type of foreign direct investment that is likely to be attracted in the future.
answer_text: In 2022 my Department engaged Wavteq to assess the potential impact of post EU Exit trading arrangements on Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) into the North, including an assessment of trends and forecasts by sector and country. The analysis suggested that, in addition to building on our existing capabilities in high tech sectors, including IT and software, professional and business services, we should position the North to target renewable energy investments aligned with our engineering strengths and Net Zero research assets. A summary report is published on the Department’s website at >https://www.economy-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/economy/attracting-fdi-to-ni-in-context-post-eu-exit-trading-relationship.pdfBuilding on the Wavteq work, my Department has undertaken analysis of the post EU exit regulatory environment within individual sectors and therefore where dual market access opportunities could potentially be exploited, as a hook for FDI. The analysis is published on the Department’s website at >https://www.economyni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/economy/Northern-Ireland-Unique-Market-Access-Position.pdf
